### Step 3 — Calculate Available Throughput of the GPP

For the GPP, each MAC requires $CPI_{GPP} = 8$ cycles, so the time per MAC is:

$$
T_{MAC}^{GPP} = \dfrac{CPI_{GPP}}{f_{clk}} = \dfrac{8}{100 \times 10^{6}} = 80 \text{ ns/MAC}
$$

The maximum MAC throughput of the GPP is:

$$
\text{MAC}_{GPP} = \dfrac{1}{T_{MAC}^{GPP}} = \dfrac{1}{80 \times 10^{-9}} = 12.5 \text{ MMAC/sec}
$$

### Step 4 — Calculate Available Throughput of the DSP

For the DSP, each MAC requires $CPI_{DSP} = 1$ cycle, so:

$$
T_{MAC}^{DSP} = \dfrac{CPI_{DSP}}{f_{clk}} = \dfrac{1}{100 \times 10^{6}} = 10 \text{ ns/MAC}
$$

The maximum MAC throughput of the DSP is:

$$
\text{MAC}_{DSP} = \dfrac{1}{T_{MAC}^{DSP}} = \dfrac{1}{10 \times 10^{-9}} = 100 \text{ MMAC/sec}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Execution Time per Output Sample for Each Core

Time to process one output sample (64 MACs):

$$
T_{sample}^{GPP} = 64 \times 80 \text{ ns} = 5\,120 \text{ ns} = 5.12 \text{ \mu s}
$$

$$
T_{sample}^{DSP} = 64 \times 10 \text{ ns} = 640 \text{ ns} = 0.64 \text{ \mu s}
$$

### Step 6 — Verify the Real-Time Deadline

The time budget per sample (deadline) is:

$$
T_{deadline} = \dfrac{1}{f_s} = \dfrac{1}{48\,000} = 20.83 \text{ \mu s}
$$

Checking both cores:

$$
T_{sample}^{GPP} = 5.12 \text{ \mu s} \;\ll\; T_{deadline} = 20.83 \text{ \mu s} \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
T_{sample}^{DSP} = 0.64 \text{ \mu s} \;\ll\; T_{deadline} = 20.83 \text{ \mu s} \quad \checkmark
$$

### Step 7 — Compute the Speedup Factor

$$
S = \dfrac{T_{sample}^{GPP}}{T_{sample}^{DSP}} = \dfrac{5.12}{0.64} = 8.0\times
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** Both cores meet the deadline, but the **DSP delivers an 8× speedup** while consuming less energy. For battery-powered audio devices, the DSP wins. For a non-battery, cost-sensitive toy, the GPP may still be acceptable.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Amdahl's Law for Heterogeneous SoC Cores

**Problem Statement:**
A modern SoC uses a GPP for control tasks and a DSP for signal processing. The GPP code is 70% of total execution time; the DSP-accelerated part is 30%. If the DSP provides a 10× speedup on the accelerated part, what is the **overall system speedup**?

### Step 1 — State Amdahl's Law

$$
S_{total} = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Values

Here, $f = 0.30$ (fraction accelerated) and $n = 10$ (speedup of accelerated part).

$$
S_{total} = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.30) + \dfrac{0.30}{10}}
$$

### Step 3 — Simplify Numerator and Denominator

Numerator: $1$.

Denominator:

$$
(1 - 0.30) + \dfrac{0.30}{10} = 0.70 + 0.03 = 0.73
$$

### Step 4 — Final Calculation

$$
S_{total} = \dfrac{1}{0.73} = 1.3699 \approx 1.37\times
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** Even a 10× DSP speedup on 30% of the code gives only **1.37× total speedup** — this is **Amdahl's bottleneck**. To get more, we must accelerate a *larger fraction* of the workload.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Energy Efficiency Comparison

**Problem Statement:**
A DSP operates at $V_{dd} = 1.2 \text{ V}$, $f_{clk} = 200 \text{ MHz}$, with average switching activity $\alpha = 0.3$ and load capacitance $C = 50 \text{ pF}$. Compute the dynamic power and the Energy per Instruction (EPI), assuming $\text{IPC} = 1$.

### Step 1 — Compute the Dynamic Power

$$
P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}
$$

Substituting:

$$
P_{dyn} = 0.3 \times (50 \times 10^{-12}) \times (1.2)^{2} \times (200 \times 10^{6})
$$

### Step 2 — Numerically Evaluate

$$
P_{dyn} = 0.3 \times 50 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.44 \times 2 \times 10^{8}
$$

$$
P_{dyn} = 0.3 \times 50 \times 1.44 \times 2 \times 10^{-12+8}
$$

$$
P_{dyn} = 0.3 \times 144 \times 10^{-4} = 43.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ W} = 4.32 \text{ mW}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Energy per Instruction

Since IPC = 1, the number of instructions per second equals the clock rate. Energy per instruction:

$$
\text{EPI} = \dfrac{P_{dyn}}{f_{clk} \cdot \text{IPC}} = \dfrac{4.32 \times 10^{-3}}{200 \times 10^{6} \times 1} = 21.6 \times 10^{-12} \text{ J} = 21.6 \text{ pJ/instruction}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Note:** This is why reducing $V_{dd}$ (e.g., from 1.2 V to 0.8 V) **quadratically** reduces power — a major design lever in battery-powered embedded cores.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Core Inside an Embedded System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Application Layer] --> B[Embedded Software Firmware]
    B --> C[Core Processing Unit]
    C --> C1[ALU]
    C --> C2[Register File]
    C --> C3[Control Unit]
    C --> C4[Memory Cache]
    C --> D[On Chip Peripherals]
    D --> D1[Timers and PWM]
    D --> D2[ADC and DAC]
    D --> D3[UART SPI I2C]
    D --> D4[GPIO Ports]
    C --> E[External Bus Interface]
    E --> F[External Memory]
    E --> G[External Sensors and Actuators]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **Core Processing Unit** is the heart. Everything else (memory, peripherals, I/O) exists to *feed* it data and to *execute* the results of its computations.

---

## 4.2 Hierarchical Classification of Embedded Cores

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R[Embedded System Core] --> R1[General Purpose Processors]
    R --> R2[Domain Specific Processors]
    R1 --> R1A[Microprocessor]
    R1 --> R1B[Microcontroller]
    R1 --> R1C[Embedded Microprocessor]
    R2 --> R2A[Digital Signal Processor]
    R2 --> R2B[Application Specific Integrated Circuit]
    R2 --> R2C[Application Specific Instruction Processor]
    R2 --> R2D[Multi Core SoC]
```

---

## 4.3 Von Neumann vs Harvard Architecture — Block-Level Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VN[Von Neumann Architecture]
        V1[CPU] --> V2[Single Shared Bus]
        V2 --> V3[Memory Instructions and Data]
        V3 --> V2
    end
    subgraph HV[Harvard Architecture]
        H1[CPU] --> H2[Instruction Bus]
        H1 --> H3[Data Bus]
        H2 --> H4[Instruction Memory]
        H3 --> H5[Data Memory]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for DSP:** DSPs almost universally use **Modified Harvard** architecture because the **MAC unit needs a coefficient and a data sample in the same clock cycle** — impossible with a single shared bus.

---

## 4.4 GPP vs DSP — Decision Flow for an Embedded Designer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S[Start Design] --> Q1{Is the workload one narrow algorithm class?}
    Q1 -- No --> GPP[Choose General Purpose Processor]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is the volume high enough to amortise NRE cost?}
    Q2 -- Yes and algorithm fixed --> ASIC[Choose ASIC]
    Q2 -- Yes but some flexibility needed --> ASIP[Choose ASIP]
    Q2 -- No --> DSP[Choose Digital Signal Processor]
    GPP --> E[Validate against real time deadline]
    DSP --> E
    ASIP --> E
    ASIC --> E
    E --> Q3{Meets deadline T_exec less than T_deadline?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> DONE[Design Locked]
    Q3 -- No --> R[Revisit architecture or raise clock]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** This is the canonical **design-decision flowchart** for selecting a processor core in an embedded system. Memorise the decision branches — they appear directly in KTU exam questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **Differentiate between a General Purpose Processor and a Domain Specific Processor. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — allocate 1 mark per sub-point):**

A **General Purpose Processor (GPP)** is a CPU designed to execute a wide variety of computational tasks with a generic instruction set. Examples: Intel x86, ARM Cortex-M3.

A **Domain-Specific Processor (DSP)** is a CPU whose architecture and instruction set are tailored to efficiently execute algorithms of a specific application class (typically signal processing). Examples: Texas Instruments TMS320C6713, Analog Devices SHARC ADSP-21469.

**Key Differences (1 mark):**

| Aspect | GPP | DSP |
|---|---|---|
| Instruction Set | Generic, broad | Tailored (e.g., MAC, circular addressing) |
| Throughput on MAC | Low (multi-cycle) | High (single-cycle MAC) |
| Power Efficiency | Moderate | High |
| Flexibility | High | Lower |
| Cost | Low to medium | Medium to high |

---

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **What is a Microcontroller? Why is it considered the "core" of most embedded systems?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Microcontroller (µC)** is a single-chip computer that integrates a **CPU core, program memory (ROM/Flash), data memory (RAM), and peripherals** (timers, ADC, communication ports, GPIO) on a single silicon die (1 mark).

It is considered the **core of most embedded systems** because:

1. It provides a **complete computational platform** in a single IC, reducing the BOM (Bill of Materials), PCB area, and power consumption (1 mark).
2. It is **optimised for real-time deterministic control** tasks using on-chip timers, interrupts, and low-power sleep modes (1 mark).

Examples: 8051, PIC16F877, ATmega328 (Arduino Uno), STM32F407, MSP430G2553.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`
>
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **architecture of a Digital Signal Processor (DSP)**, highlighting its differences from a general-purpose Von Neumann processor. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare the **performance of a GPP and a DSP** for implementing a 128-tap FIR filter operating on audio sampled at 44.1 kHz. The GPP requires 6 cycles per MAC and runs at 120 MHz; the DSP requires 1 cycle per MAC and runs at 120 MHz. Compute the speedup and comment on real-time feasibility. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

A **Digital Signal Processor (DSP)** is a domain-specific microprocessor engineered for real-time numerical processing of discretised signals. Its architecture is a **Modified Harvard** design (1 mark) with the following features:

1. **Separate Program and Data Memories** — enables simultaneous instruction fetch and data load, eliminating the von Neumann bottleneck. (1 mark)
2. **Hardware Multiply-Accumulate (MAC) Unit** — performs a 16×16-bit (or 32×32-bit) multiply and accumulate in a **single clock cycle**, the workhorse of all DSP algorithms. (1 mark)
3. **Multiple Independent Memory Buses** — typically three buses (Program Memory Bus, Data Memory Bus, Coefficient Bus) to feed the MAC with operands concurrently. (1 mark)
4. **Hardware Zero-Overhead Looping** — dedicated loop counters and registers eliminate branch penalties for tight inner loops (typical of FIR/IIR/FFT kernels). (1 mark)
5. **Specialised Addressing Modes** — *circular (modulo) addressing* for FIFO buffers and *bit-reversed addressing* for in-place FFT. (1 mark)
6. **Saturating Arithmetic** — on overflow, the register saturates to the maximum representable value instead of wrapping, preserving signal integrity in audio/video. (1 mark)

**Contrast with Von Neumann GPP:** A von Neumann GPP uses a **single shared bus** for both instructions and data, cannot fetch both in the same cycle (the *von Neumann bottleneck*), and lacks specialised MAC hardware, requiring multiple sequential instructions (typically 4–8) to emulate a single MAC operation. Therefore, a GPP is orders of magnitude slower and less power-efficient for signal-processing workloads.

### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- FIR order $N = 128$
- Sample rate $f_s = 44.1 \text{ kHz}$
- GPP: $CPI_{GPP} = 6$, $f_{clk} = 120 \text{ MHz}$
- DSP: $CPI_{DSP} = 1$, $f_{clk} = 120 \text{ MHz}$

**Step 1 — Compute the Required MAC Throughput (1 mark)**

$$
\text{MAC/sec} = N \cdot f_s = 128 \times 44\,100 = 5\,644\,800 \text{ MAC/sec} \approx 5.645 \text{ MMAC/sec}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute GPP's Time per MAC (1 mark)**

$$
T_{MAC}^{GPP} = \dfrac{CPI_{GPP}}{f_{clk}} = \dfrac{6}{120 \times 10^{6}} = 50 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
\text{Throughput}_{GPP} = \dfrac{1}{50 \text{ ns}} = 20 \text{ MMAC/sec}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute DSP's Time per MAC (1 mark)**

$$
T_{MAC}^{DSP} = \dfrac{1}{120 \times 10^{6}} = 8.333 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
\text{Throughput}_{DSP} = 120 \text{ MMAC/sec}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute per-Sample Processing Time (2 marks)**

$$
T_{sample}^{GPP} = 128 \times 50 \text{ ns} = 6\,400 \text{ ns} = 6.4 \text{ \mu s}
$$

$$
T_{sample}^{DSP} = 128 \times 8.333 \text{ ns} = 1\,066.67 \text{ ns} \approx 1.067 \text{ \mu s}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Speedup and Verify Real-Time Feasibility (2 marks)**

Speedup:

$$
S = \dfrac{6.4}{1.067} = 6.0\times
$$

Real-time deadline:

$$
T_{deadline} = \dfrac{1}{f_s} = \dfrac{1}{44\,100} = 22.68 \text{ \mu s}
$$

Both cores satisfy the deadline ($T_{sample} \ll T_{deadline}$), but the **DSP delivers 6× speedup with 6× less execution time per sample**, making it the superior choice for power-constrained or multi-channel audio designs. The GPP is acceptable for low-cost mono applications only.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #1:** Students often forget to compare $T_{sample}$ against $T_{deadline}$ to confirm real-time feasibility. **Always end with a feasibility statement.**
> **Pitfall #2:** Forgetting to convert clock frequency to Hz (using MHz instead of Hz in denominator). **Always check units.**

---

> **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`
>
> **(a)** With neat block diagrams, explain the **Von Neumann and Harvard architectures**. Why does a DSP prefer the Modified Harvard variant? **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A wearable heart-rate monitor uses a microcontroller running at 32 MHz with $CPI = 1.2$ cycles/instruction. The firmware contains $4.5 \times 10^{6}$ instructions executed per sample, and the sensor samples at 100 Hz. Determine the **CPU utilisation** and whether the system meets the real-time deadline. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Von Neumann Architecture (3 marks):**

- Single shared memory stores **both instructions and data**.
- A **single bus** carries instructions and data sequentially.
- The CPU must therefore *fetch instruction → execute → fetch data* in alternating cycles.
- **Disadvantage:** The *Von Neumann bottleneck* — bandwidth of the single bus limits throughput. An instruction fetch and a data access cannot occur simultaneously.

**Harvard Architecture (2 marks):**

- Physically **separate memories** for instructions and data.
- **Two independent buses** (instruction bus + data bus) → simultaneous instruction fetch and data access.
- **Advantage:** Higher throughput, deterministic fetch timing. **Disadvantage:** More silicon area, more complex memory management.

**Why DSPs prefer Modified Harvard (2 marks):**

- DSP kernels (FIR, FFT) require **two data operands + one instruction per MAC cycle** (e.g., coefficient $h[k]$ and sample $x[n-k]$).
- A pure Harvard DSP exposes *three* buses — one for the program, two for data — to feed the MAC unit every cycle without stall.
- The "Modified" aspect allows occasional unified access (e.g., for self-modifying code or coefficient upload) while preserving the Harvard performance advantage.

### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $f_{clk} = 32 \text{ MHz}$
- $CPI = 1.2$ cycles/instruction
- $N_{inst} = 4.5 \times 10^{6}$ instructions per sample
- $f_s = 100 \text{ Hz}$ (sample rate)

**Step 1 — Compute Execution Time per Sample (2 marks)**

Using the fundamental CPU time equation:

$$
T_{exec} = \dfrac{N_{inst} \times CPI}{f_{clk}} = \dfrac{4.5 \times 10^{6} \times 1.2}{32 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
T_{exec} = \dfrac{5.4 \times 10^{6}}{32 \times 10^{6}} = 0.16875 \text{ seconds} = 168.75 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Deadline (1 mark)**

$$
T_{deadline} = \dfrac{1}{f_s} = \dfrac{1}{100} = 0.01 \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare and Determine CPU Utilisation (3 marks)**

CPU utilisation is:

$$
U = \dfrac{T_{exec}}{T_{deadline}} = \dfrac{168.75 \text{ ms}}{10 \text{ ms}} = 16.875 = 1687.5\%
$$

Since $U \gg 100\%$, the CPU is **drastically overloaded**.

**Step 4 — Conclusion (1 mark)**

The system **fails the real-time deadline** by a factor of $\approx 16.9\times$. The designer must either (i) reduce $N_{inst}$ by optimising the algorithm, (ii) lower the sampling rate, or (iii) upgrade to a faster core (e.g., 600 MHz microcontroller).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #3:** A common error is to use $f_{clk}$ in MHz without multiplying by $10^{6}$. Always write units explicitly. **Pitfall #4:** Computing $T_{exec}$ without multiplying by CPI (forgetting that the CPU takes *more than one cycle per instruction*).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — General Purpose & Domain Specific Processors**

- ☐ The **"core"** of an embedded system = the processor/CPU that interprets firmware and orchestrates peripherals.
- ☐ **GPP** = generic, flexible, software-driven. Sub-classes: **µP, µC, embedded µP**.
- ☐ **Domain-Specific Processor** = hardware-optimised for a narrow workload. Sub-classes: **DSP, ASIC, ASIP, SoC**.
- ☐ **Von Neumann** = single bus, bottleneck, cheaper, used in 8051.
- ☐ **Harvard** = separate buses for code/data, faster, used in AVR, PIC, ARM Cortex-M.
- ☐ **Modified Harvard** (used in DSPs) = three buses to feed the MAC every cycle.
- ☐ **Key DSP features**: single-cycle MAC, zero-overhead looping, circular addressing, saturating arithmetic, dual-memory fetch.
- ☐ **Key GPP features**: generic ALU, large register file, cache hierarchy, MMU/MPU (in higher-end ones).
- ☐ **CPU Time Formula** (must memorise): $T_{exec} = N_{inst} \times CPI / f_{clk}$.
- ☐ **MIPS Formula**: $\text{MIPS} = f_{clk} / (CPI \times 10^{6})$ — use only for comparing CPUs with the **same ISA**.
- ☐ **Power Formula**: $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V_{dd}^{2} f_{clk}$ — voltage reduction is the most powerful lever.
- ☐ **Amdahl's Law**: $S = 1 / [(1-f) + f/n]$ — diminishing returns on partial acceleration.
- ☐ **Real-time feasibility rule**: $T_{exec} \leq T_{deadline}$ must always hold.
- ☐ **Design heuristic** — pick **GPP** for flexibility + low NRE; pick **DSP** for deterministic real-time signal processing; pick **ASIC** for ultra-high volume where per-unit cost dominates.
- ☐ **Examples to remember**: 8051, ARM Cortex-M0/M3/M4, PIC16F, MSP430, ATmega328 (GPP/microcontroller family); TMS320C6000, SHARC, CEVA-XC (DSP family).
- ☐ **Modern trend**: heterogeneous **multi-core SoCs** (CPU + DSP + GPU + NPU) dominate IoT/edge-AI embedded designs.
- ☐ Always state **assumptions** (clock frequency in Hz, CPI averaged, instruction mix) before applying formulas in the exam.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 2 Notes — Core of the Embedded System: General Purpose and Domain Specific Processors.** Best of luck for your KTU 2024 Scheme examinations! 🎓

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ASICs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET695 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** ASICs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **ASIC (Application-Specific Integrated Circuit)** is a microchip that is custom-designed and manufactured to perform a dedicated function for a particular application, product, or system, as opposed to a general-purpose IC (like a standard microcontroller or microprocessor) which is designed to handle a wide variety of tasks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** An ASIC is an IC whose final mask layers are customized to implement a unique function for a specific end-application. It trades flexibility (the hallmark of microprocessors) for **higher performance, lower unit cost (at volume), lower power consumption, and smaller physical footprint**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the silicon chip ecosystem like **kitchen appliances**:

- **General-Purpose Microprocessor (MPU)** → A Swiss Army Knife. It can cut, saw, open bottles, etc. It is incredibly *flexible* because of software, but is *average* at every single task and consumes more "energy" (power) per task.
- **ASIC** → A dedicated, custom-built industrial pasta machine. It only makes pasta, but it makes it *10x faster, uses less electricity, and takes up less counter space* than the Swiss Army Knife trying to simulate pasta-making through software.

When a company like Apple or Samsung needs a chip exclusively for **Face ID**, **image signal processing**, or **neural network inference** in millions of phones, they do **not** use a general-purpose CPU. They design an ASIC.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **NRE Cost (Non-Recurring Engineering Cost):** The one-time fixed cost of designing the ASIC and fabricating the photolithographic masks. Typically ranges from **\$50,000 to several million USD** depending on process node (e.g., 7nm vs 180nm).
- **Per-Unit Cost:** The cost to manufacture *one* chip after masks are ready (depends on die size, wafer cost, packaging).
- **Die Yield (Y):** The percentage of functional dies on a wafer. Industry-standard: **Y = 0.7 to 0.95** for mature nodes.
- **Performance Density:** Measured in **MHz, mW, and Gates/mm²**.
- **Time-to-Market:** Typically **6 to 18 months** for ASICs vs. instant for software on a microprocessor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU Module-2 framework specifically asks students to distinguish between *Full-Custom*, *Semi-Custom*, and *Programmable* ASICs. Memorize the trade-off triangle: **Cost vs. Performance vs. Time-to-Market**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Classification of ASICs

ASICs are taxonomically classified into **three primary branches** based on how much of the chip is custom-designed versus pre-fabricated:

**A. Full-Custom ASIC**
- Every transistor, every interconnect, and every layout polygon is hand-crafted by layout engineers.
- Highest performance, lowest power, smallest die area.
- Used in analog/RF front-ends, critical datapaths, CPUs (e.g., Apple M-series cores).
- *Downside:* Astronomical NRE cost and longest design time.

**B. Semi-Custom ASIC**
The silicon wafer is **pre-fabricated** with a library of pre-designed logic primitives. Only the final metal interconnect layers (the "custom masks") are unique to the application.
- **Gate Array (GA):** Pre-fabricated array of uncommitted NAND/NOR gates. Only metal interconnect is customized.
- **Standard Cell (SC) / Cell-Based ASIC:** Uses a library of pre-characterized logic cells (AND, OR, Flip-Flops, Adders). Cells are placed and routed automatically by EDA tools. Better density than Gate Array.

**C. Programmable ASIC (the boundary case)**
- The chip is *fully fabricated* and *off-the-shelf*. Customization happens by "blowing fuses" or loading a configuration bitstream.
- **PROM, PAL, PLA** → Simple fuse-based logic.
- **CPLD (Complex PLD)** → Array of PAL-like blocks with central interconnect.
- **FPGA (Field-Programmable Gate Array)** → The most modern variant: an array of configurable logic blocks (CLBs), programmable interconnects, and dedicated hardware blocks (DSP slices, BRAM, transceivers).

### 2.2 The ASIC Design Flow — Stepwise Logic

The end-to-end flow is **waterfall-style**, with each stage feeding the next:

1. **System Specification** — Define functionality, performance, power, cost, I/O.
2. **RTL Design** — Describe hardware using a Hardware Description Language (**VHDL** or **Verilog**).
3. **Functional Simulation** — Verify the RTL matches specification (using ModelSim/QuestaSim).
4. **Logic Synthesis** — Convert RTL into a gate-level netlist (technology-mapped to a standard-cell library).
5. **Floorplanning & Placement** — Decide physical locations of macros and standard cells on the die.
6. **Routing** — Connect placed cells using metal layers.
7. **Physical Verification** — **DRC (Design Rule Check)**, **LVS (Layout vs. Schematic)**, **ERC (Electrical Rule Check)**.
8. **Tape-Out** — Send final GDSII file to the foundry (TSMC, Samsung Foundry, GlobalFoundries).
9. **Fabrication & Packaging** — Wafer fab, dicing, wire-bonding/flip-chip, packaging.
10. **Testing** — ATE-based functional and parametric testing.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{total} = C_{NRE} + (C_{unit} \times V)$ | Total cost of ownership: NRE plus per-unit cost times production volume | USD |
| 2 | $C_{unit} = \dfrac{C_{wafer}}{Y \cdot D}$ | Per-unit die cost (where $D$ = dies per wafer) | USD/die |
| 3 | $D = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (R/2)^2}{A_{die}} - \dfrac{\pi \cdot R}{\sqrt{2 \cdot A_{die}}}$ | Dies per wafer (approximation; $R$ = wafer diameter, $A_{die}$ = die area) | dies/wafer |
| 4 | $Y = e^{-D_0 \cdot A}$ | Murphy's Yield Model (simple exponential) | dimensionless (0-1) |
| 5 | $Y = \left(\dfrac{1 - e^{-D_0 \cdot A}}{D_0 \cdot A}\right)^2$ | Bose-Einstein Yield Model (more realistic, accounts for clustering) | dimensionless (0-1) |
| 6 | $V_{breakeven} = \dfrac{C_{NRE}}{C_{unit,sw} - C_{unit,asic}}$ | Volume above which ASIC becomes cheaper than software-on-microcontroller | units |
| 7 | $P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{dd}^2 \cdot f$ | Dynamic power dissipation (the reason ASICs are low power) | Watts |

> **Notation Guard:** $A$ denotes die area (in $\text{cm}^2$); $D_0$ denotes defect density (defects per $\text{cm}^2$); $C_L$ denotes load capacitance; $\alpha$ denotes switching activity factor. **Do not** confuse the yield $Y$ with the volume $V$ in the cost equations.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

ASICs are the invisible backbone of modern civilization. They power:
- **Smartphones** (Application Processors, modem chips, PMICs).
- **Automotive** (ECUs for airbag, ABS, engine control — ISO 26262 safety-critical).
- **Cryptocurrency mining** (Bitcoin ASICs from Bitmain, achieving TH/s).
- **AI/ML Inference** (Google TPU, Apple Neural Engine, NVIDIA GPUs partly use ASIC techniques).
- **IoT Edge Devices** (Ultra-low-power ASICs for sensor fusion).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** The Module-2 question "Compare ASIC and FPGA" appears every year. The single most-likely KTU answer demands three axes: **NRE cost, performance, time-to-market, and reprogrammability**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Breakeven Volume for ASIC vs. Microcontroller

**Problem Statement:** A team is choosing between (a) running their algorithm on a general-purpose microcontroller costing **\$2 per unit**, and (b) building an ASIC that costs **\$5 million in NRE** and **\$0.50 per unit** to manufacture. At what production volume $V$ does the ASIC become the cheaper option?

**Step 1 — Write the total cost of the microcontroller-based solution.**
Since the microcontroller needs no NRE (it is an off-the-shelf part), the total cost is purely volume-based:
$$C_{sw}(V) = C_{unit,sw} \times V$$

Substituting the given values:
$$C_{sw}(V) = 2 \cdot V$$

**Step 2 — Write the total cost of the ASIC solution.**
The ASIC has both a fixed NRE component and a per-unit variable cost:
$$C_{asic}(V) = C_{NRE} + C_{unit,asic} \times V$$

Substituting:
$$C_{asic}(V) = 5{,}000{,}000 + 0.5 \cdot V$$

**Step 3 — Set the two cost equations equal to find the crossover.**
The breakeven volume $V_{breakeven}$ is the point where total costs are identical:
$$C_{sw}(V) = C_{asic}(V)$$
$$2 \cdot V = 5{,}000{,}000 + 0.5 \cdot V$$

**Step 4 — Isolate the variable $V$ on one side.**
Subtract $0.5V$ from both sides:
$$2V - 0.5V = 5{,}000{,}000$$
$$1.5V = 5{,}000{,}000$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $V$.**
$$V = \dfrac{5{,}000{,}000}{1.5}$$
$$V = 3{,}333{,}333.33 \text{ units}$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the engineering conclusion.**
For any production volume **above 3.34 million units**, the ASIC is the financially superior choice. Below that threshold, the software-on-microcontroller approach wins because the high NRE cost of the ASIC cannot be amortized across a small production run.

### 3.2 Derivation: Die-per-Wafer Approximation

**Problem Statement:** A 300 mm wafer ($R = 300$ mm) is used to manufacture dies of area $A_{die} = 50 \text{ mm}^2$. Estimate the number of dies per wafer.

**Step 1 — Recall the die-per-wafer formula.**
$$D = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (R/2)^2}{A_{die}} - \dfrac{\pi \cdot R}{\sqrt{2 \cdot A_{die}}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the wafer area term.**
The wafer has area $\pi r^2$ where $r = 150$ mm:
$$\pi \cdot (150)^2 = \pi \cdot 22{,}500 = 70{,}685.83 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the gross die count (first term).**
$$\dfrac{70{,}685.83}{50} = 1{,}413.72 \text{ dies}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the edge-loss correction (second term).**
$$\sqrt{2 \cdot 50} = \sqrt{100} = 10$$
$$\dfrac{\pi \cdot 300}{10} = \dfrac{942.48}{10} = 94.25 \text{ dies}$$

**Step 5 — Subtract edge losses.**
$$D = 1{,}413.72 - 94.25 = 1{,}319.47 \text{ dies}$$

**Step 6 — Round down and interpret.**
Approximately **1,319 gross dies per wafer**. The $\approx 6.7\%$ loss represents dies that straddle the wafer edge and are therefore non-rectangular/unusable.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: ASIC Class Trade-Offs

| Parameter | Full-Custom | Standard Cell | Gate Array | FPGA (Programmable) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **NRE Cost** | Very High (\$M) | High (\$100k+) | Medium (\$50k–\$500k) | Zero (off-the-shelf) |
| **Per-Unit Cost (Volume)** | Lowest | Low | Medium | Highest |
| **Performance (MHz)** | Highest | High | High | Lowest (FPGA fabric overhead) |
| **Power Consumption** | Lowest | Low | Low-Medium | Highest |
| **Time-to-Market** | Longest (12–24 mo) | Medium (6–12 mo) | Medium (4–8 mo) | Fastest (days–weeks) |
| **Reprogrammability** | None (mask-locked) | None | None | Yes (in-field bitstream reload) |
| **Risk** | Highest (respin = \$M) | High | Medium | Lowest (reprogrammable) |
| **Best For** | CPUs, GPUs, RF | SoCs, networking chips | Legacy designs, medium volume | Prototyping, low-volume |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Notice the **inverse relationship between NRE and Time-to-Market**. FPGAs have *zero* NRE because the chip is pre-fabricated. The cost of programmability is paid in silicon area, latency, and power — not in upfront engineering hours.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ASIC Classification Tree (Mermaid Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[ASIC Classification] --> rootB[Full Custom]
    rootA --> rootC[Semi Custom]
    rootA --> rootD[Programmable]

    rootB --> rootB1[All layers custom]
    rootB --> rootB2[Handcrafted layout]

    rootC --> rootC1[Standard Cell]
    rootC --> rootC2[Gate Array]
    rootC --> rootC3[Cell library based design]

    rootD --> rootD1[FPGA]
    rootD --> rootD2[CPLD]
    rootD --> rootD3[PROM PAL PLA]

    rootC1 --> rootC1a[Pre designed logic cells]
    rootC1 --> rootC1b[Automated place and route]

    rootC2 --> rootC2a[Prefabricated transistor array]
    rootC2 --> rootC2b[Only metal masks custom]

    rootD1 --> rootD1a[Configurable Logic Blocks]
    rootD1 --> rootD1b[Programmable interconnect]
    rootD1 --> rootD1c[DSP and BRAM blocks]
```

### 4.2 ASIC Design Flow (Mermaid Pipeline)

```mermaid
graph LR
    flowA[System Specification] --> flowB[RTL Design in VHDL or Verilog]
    flowB --> flowC[Functional Simulation]
    flowC --> flowD[Logic Synthesis]
    flowD --> flowE[Floorplanning]
    flowE --> flowF[Placement]
    flowF --> flowG[Routing]
    flowG --> flowH[Physical Verification DRC LVS]
    flowH --> flowI[Tape Out GDSII to Foundry]
    flowI --> flowJ[Wafer Fabrication]
    flowJ --> flowK[Packaging and Testing]
    flowK --> flowL[Production Silicon]
```

### 4.3 NRE vs. Volume Trade-off Curve (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph TD
    chartStart[Cost Analysis] --> chartA[Microcontroller path is flat linear in volume]
    chartStart --> chartB[ASIC path has high NRE offset then becomes flatter]
    chartA --> chartA1[Slope equals C unit sw]
    chartB --> chartB1[Y intercept equals C NRE]
    chartB1 --> chartC[Intersection point is V breakeven]
    chartA1 --> chartC
    chartC --> chartD[Beyond intersection: ASIC is cheaper]
```

### 4.4 FPGA Internal Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
graph TB
    fpgaIn[FPGA Chip] --> fpgaIO[IO Banks and PADs on perimeter]
    fpgaIn --> fpgaCLB[Array of Configurable Logic Blocks]
    fpgaIn --> fpgaSwitch[Programmable Switch Matrix Interconnect]
    fpgaIn --> fpgaDSP[DSP Slices for math]
    fpgaIn --> fpgaBRAM[Block RAM memory tiles]
    fpgaIn --> fpgaClock[Clock Management Tiles]
    fpgaCLB --> fpgaLUT[Lookup Tables implement logic]
    fpgaCLB --> fpgaFF[Flip Flops for sequential state]
    fpgaSwitch --> fpgaRoute[Wires and PIPs route signals]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define an ASIC. List the three major categories of ASICs.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An **Application-Specific Integrated Circuit (ASIC)** is an integrated circuit custom-designed to perform a specific function for a particular application, in contrast to general-purpose ICs. The three major categories are:
1. **Full-Custom ASIC** – All mask layers are uniquely designed.
2. **Semi-Custom ASIC** – Standard Cell based and Gate Array based; only some mask layers are customized.
3. **Programmable ASIC** – Off-the-shelf, customized via programming (FPLA, PAL, CPLD, FPGA).

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for listing, 1 mark for the brief sub-classification.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is meant by the term "NRE cost" in ASIC design? Why is it considered a one-time cost?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**NRE (Non-Recurring Engineering) cost** is the one-time upfront expense incurred in designing an ASIC and fabricating its photolithographic masks, before any production silicon is manufactured. It includes RTL design, synthesis, physical layout, verification, mask fabrication, and tooling.
It is called "non-recurring" because the masks and design database, once paid for, can be reused to manufacture *an unlimited number of units* without repeating these costs. NRE contrasts with the per-unit recurring cost of each manufactured die.

*(1 mark: definition; 1 mark: examples; 1 mark: justification of "one-time".)*

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Rule (Mandatory):** Answer **ONE** of the following: either **Question A** *or* **Question B**. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

#### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **complete ASIC design flow** from system specification to fabrication. For each stage, briefly state its purpose.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

1. **System Specification (0.5 mark):** Defines the chip's function, I/O, performance, power, area, and cost targets. The contractual baseline for all downstream stages.
2. **RTL Design (1 mark):** Hardware is described in a Hardware Description Language (**VHDL** or **Verilog**) at the Register-Transfer Level. The designer expresses what the chip computes, not how.
3. **Functional Simulation (0.5 mark):** Testbenches are run to verify the RTL matches specification. Catches algorithmic/logic bugs *before* synthesis.
4. **Logic Synthesis (1 mark):** EDA tools (e.g., Synopsys Design Compiler) convert RTL into a **gate-level netlist**, mapping logic onto a target technology library (e.g., 28nm standard cells).
5. **Floorplanning (0.5 mark):** Major blocks (CPU core, memory, I/O pads) are assigned regions on the die. Power planning and clock tree strategy are decided.
6. **Placement (0.5 mark):** Each standard cell from the netlist is given an exact (x, y) coordinate on the die.
7. **Routing (0.5 mark):** Metal interconnect layers are drawn to electrically connect placed cells per the netlist.
8. **Physical Verification (1 mark):** **DRC (Design Rule Check)** ensures layout obeys foundry rules. **LVS (Layout vs. Schematic)** ensures the layout still matches the netlist.
9. **Tape-Out (1 mark):** The final layout is converted to **GDSII** format and sent to the foundry for mask fabrication. This is the point of no return — any error requires a costly re-spin.
10. **Fabrication, Packaging, Testing (1 mark):** Wafer is processed, diced, packaged (QFN, BGA), and tested on Automated Test Equipment (ATE).

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 200 mm wafer ($R = 200$ mm) is processed to manufacture ASIC dies of area **$A = 1 \text{ cm}^2$**. Assume a defect density of **$D_0 = 1 \text{ defect/cm}^2$**.
(i) Calculate the **gross die count per wafer** using the standard formula.
(ii) Using **Bose–Einstein's yield model**, calculate the percentage yield.
(iii) Comment on how yield affects unit cost.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**(i) Gross Die Count (3 marks):**

The die-per-wafer formula is:
$$D = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (R/2)^2}{A_{die}} - \dfrac{\pi \cdot R}{\sqrt{2 \cdot A_{die}}}$$

Converting units: $A_{die} = 1 \text{ cm}^2 = 100 \text{ mm}^2$, $R = 200$ mm.

**First term — gross die count:**
$$\dfrac{\pi \cdot (100)^2}{100} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 10{,}000}{100} = 100\pi = 314.16 \text{ dies}$$

**Second term — edge loss correction:**
$$\sqrt{2 \cdot 100} = \sqrt{200} \approx 14.14$$
$$\dfrac{\pi \cdot 200}{14.14} \approx 44.43 \text{ dies}$$

**Net dies per wafer:**
$$D = 314.16 - 44.43 \approx 269.73 \approx 270 \text{ gross dies}$$

**(ii) Bose–Einstein Yield (2 marks):**
$$Y = \left(\dfrac{1 - e^{-D_0 \cdot A}}{D_0 \cdot A}\right)^2$$

Substituting $D_0 \cdot A = 1 \cdot 1 = 1$:
$$Y = \left(\dfrac{1 - e^{-1}}{1}\right)^2 = (1 - 0.3679)^2 = (0.6321)^2 \approx 0.3995$$

**Yield ≈ 39.95 % (≈ 40 %).**

**(iii) Effect on Unit Cost (2 marks):**
With a yield of only 40%, every functional die effectively "pays" for itself plus ~1.5 scrapped dies. This **triples the effective per-unit silicon cost** before packaging/testing. To improve yield, the designer can (a) shrink die area, (b) use a more mature process node, or (c) add redundancy. Higher yield directly translates to lower cost-per-good-die, which is critical for consumer ASICs.

---

#### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **Full-Custom ASIC, Standard-Cell ASIC, Gate-Array ASIC, and FPGA** along the following axes: NRE cost, performance, time-to-market, and reprogrammability. State one engineering scenario where each is preferred.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

| Class | NRE Cost | Performance | Time-to-Market | Reprogrammable? | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Full-Custom** | Highest (millions \$) | Highest (GHz) | 12–24 months | No | CPU cores, RF/analog blocks |
| **Standard Cell** | High (\$100k–\$1M) | High | 6–12 months | No | SoCs, networking ASICs |
| **Gate Array** | Medium (\$50k–\$500k) | High | 4–8 months | No | Legacy glue logic, medium volumes |
| **FPGA** | **Zero** | Lowest (fabric overhead) | Days–weeks | **Yes** | Prototyping, low-volume, in-field updates |

**Engineering Scenarios (1 mark each):**
- **Full-Custom:** The ARM Cortex-A core in a flagship smartphone SoC — performance-per-watt is paramount.
- **Standard Cell:** A network switch ASIC for a 5G base station — needs high speed, low power, but not at extreme volumes.
- **Gate Array:** A legacy industrial controller needing fast turnaround at low-to-medium volume.
- **FPGA:** A satellite payload requiring in-orbit firmware updates via radiation-tolerant reprogrammable logic.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A startup is choosing between an **FPGA-based prototype** and a **direct ASIC tape-out** for an audio processing product. They expect a lifetime production volume of **200,000 units**.
- FPGA module cost: **\$40 per unit**, NRE for FPGA firmware: **\$10,000**.
- ASIC per-unit cost: **\$5**, NRE (mask, design): **\$1,500,000**.
- Compute the **total cost** for each path and recommend the best choice.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**FPGA path total cost (3.5 marks):**
$$C_{fpga} = C_{NRE,fpga} + (C_{unit,fpga} \times V)$$
$$C_{fpga} = 10{,}000 + (40 \times 200{,}000)$$
$$C_{fpga} = 10{,}000 + 8{,}000{,}000 = 8{,}010{,}000 \text{ USD}$$

**ASIC path total cost (3 marks):**
$$C_{asic} = C_{NRE,asic} + (C_{unit,asic} \times V)$$
$$C_{asic} = 1{,}500{,}000 + (5 \times 200{,}000)$$
$$C_{asic} = 1{,}500{,}000 + 1{,}000{,}000 = 2{,}500{,}000 \text{ USD}$$

**Recommendation (0.5 mark):**
The **ASIC path is cheaper by \$5,510,000** for 200,000 units. Therefore, the startup should proceed with the ASIC tape-out — *provided* they have the engineering budget to absorb the upfront \$1.5M NRE before revenue starts. (In practice, they might still use an FPGA for the first 1,000 units as a market-validation prototype before tape-out.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Common Mistake Alert):**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Students often mix **mm²** and **cm²** in the die-per-wafer formula. $1 \text{ cm}^2 = 100 \text{ mm}^2$. **Always convert to consistent units before plugging in.**
> 2. **Confusing Yield Models:** Murphy's model is $Y = e^{-D_0 A}$, but Bose–Einstein's is the **squared** version. KTU specifically tests Bose–Einstein in Module-2 numericals.
> 3. **Forgetting the edge-loss term:** Students frequently compute only $\pi r^2 / A_{die}$ and forget to subtract the second term. You will lose **1 full mark** for this.
> 4. **Misclassifying FPGA as "pure ASIC":** FPGAs are technically "**programmable** ASICs" but they are not *mask-programmed*. State this distinction explicitly.
> 5. **Breakeven pitfall:** When asked for "cheaper option", you must compare **TOTAL cost** at the given volume, not just per-unit cost. Examiners deduct marks when students only write the per-unit price.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **ASIC** = Application-Specific Integrated Circuit. A chip custom-built for one job.
- ✅ **Three classes:** Full-Custom, Semi-Custom (Standard Cell + Gate Array), Programmable (PLD/CPLD/FPGA).
- ✅ **NRE cost** = one-time design + mask cost. **Per-unit cost** = recurring wafer/die cost.
- ✅ **Total cost equation:** $C_{total} = C_{NRE} + C_{unit} \cdot V$.
- ✅ **Breakeven volume** = point where the two cost lines cross. Above it, ASIC wins.
- ✅ **Die-per-wafer formula** has **two terms** — gross dies minus edge losses. Don't forget the second term.
- ✅ **Yield models:** Murphy ($e^{-D_0 A}$) vs. Bose–Einstein (squared). KTU prefers Bose–Einstein.
- ✅ **FPGA advantages:** zero NRE, reprogrammable, fast prototyping.
- ✅ **FPGA disadvantages:** high per-unit cost, lower performance, higher power than ASIC.
- ✅ **Design flow order:** Spec → RTL → Sim → Synth → Floorplan → Place → Route → Verify → Tape-out → Fab → Test.
- ✅ **Key HDLs:** VHDL and Verilog (SystemVerilog in modern SoCs).
- ✅ **Physical verification:** DRC, LVS, ERC — must all pass before tape-out.
- ✅ **Unit conversions:** $1 \text{ cm}^2 = 100 \text{ mm}^2$; $1 \text{ inch} \approx 25.4$ mm; $1 \mu m = 10^{-6}$ m.
- ✅ **Time-to-market** order (fastest → slowest): FPGA → Gate Array → Standard Cell → Full-Custom.
- ✅ **Performance** order (lowest → highest): FPGA → Gate Array → Standard Cell → Full-Custom.
- ✅ **Reprogrammability** is **unique** to programmable ASICs (FPGA, CPLD). Others are mask-locked.
- ✅ **Real-world power formula:** $P_{dynamic} = \alpha C_L V_{dd}^2 f$ — explains why ASICs save power.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PLDs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET695 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** PLDs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Programmable Logic Devices (PLDs) — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Programmable Logic Devices (PLDs)** are general-purpose, off-the-shelf **digital integrated circuits** whose internal logic architecture (AND/OR gate arrays, interconnect network, and I/O blocks) can be **electrically configured by the end user** to implement an arbitrary combinational or sequential Boolean function — without going through a custom mask/fabrication step.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Embedded System Design (PEAET695)** syllabus, PLDs form the **hardware heart of every non-microprocessor-based embedded controller**. They act as the bridge between:

1. **Discrete TTL/CMOS logic** (too bulky, high power, rigid).
2. **Full-custom ASICs** (low NRE, but extremely expensive for low-volume embedded products).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – PEAET695):** Students must differentiate between **SPLDs (PROM, PAL, PLA)**, **CPLDs**, and **FPGAs**, understand their **internal AND/OR programmable planes**, and recognize the **technology trade-offs (fuse, antifuse, EPROM, EEPROM, SRAM)** that govern their use in embedded products.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Configurable LEGO Brick"

Imagine you walk into a hardware store and buy a single black box. Inside the box is a maze of wires, switches, and gates, but **nothing is connected**. The box comes with a "configurator" tool (a programmer). You hand the tool a recipe — *"connect wire 7 to gate 12, blow fuse F23"* — and instantly the same black box becomes a **traffic light controller**.

- The **black box** = the PLD chip.
- The **maze** = the fixed silicon AND/OR array.
- The **switches** = programmable fuses / antifuses / SRAM cells.
- The **recipe** = your Boolean equations (the *netlist*).

> Every PLD — from the humble 1980s **PAL16L8** to a modern Xilinx **Zynq UltraScale+ FPGA** containing billions of transistors — is fundamentally this same idea: **"silicon that you rewire after fabrication."**

## 1.3 Classification of PLDs (KTU Taxonomy)

PLDs are organised by **integration density** along a clear evolutionary ladder:

| Generation | Acronym | Full Form | Typical Gate Count | Use in Embedded Systems |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1st Gen SPLD | **PROM** | Programmable Read-Only Memory | < 100 | Fixed lookup tables, code storage |
| 1st Gen SPLD | **PAL** | Programmable Array Logic | < 100 | Glue logic, state decoders |
| 1st Gen SPLD | **PLA** | Programmable Logic Array | < 100 | Random two-level logic |
| 2nd Gen | **CPLD** | Complex PLD | 100 – 10 000 | Boot loaders, address decoding |
| 3rd Gen | **FPGA** | Field-Programmable Gate Array | 10 000 – 10 000 000+ | SoC prototypes, DSP, AI accelerators |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Expectation:** When asked to "compare" PLD families, examiners expect you to mention **architecture (AND/OR plane programmability)**, **non-volatility (volatile vs non-volatile)**, **in-system programmability (ISP)**, and **reprogrammability (OTP vs many-time).**

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Every KTU Student Must Memorize

- **Logic Capacity** is expressed in **Gate Equivalents (GE)** — 1 GE ≈ one 2-input NAND gate (4 transistors).
- **Propagation Delay ($t_{pd}$)** is measured in **nanoseconds (ns)**.
- **Fuse Programming** uses **antifuse ON-resistance ≈ 50–500 $\Omega$** (closed state).
- **SRAM cell** occupies **6 transistors** per configuration bit.
- Modern FPGAs operate at **clock speeds up to 500 MHz – 1 GHz** on dedicated fabric.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Universal PLD Architecture (AND–OR Plane Model)

Every classic SPLD — whether PROM, PAL, or PLA — is constructed from the **SOP (Sum of Products)** building block. The SOP of an *n*-variable Boolean function is:

$$F(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n) = \sum_{i=0}^{2^n - 1} m_i \cdot P_i$$

where $m_i$ is the *i*-th minterm and $P_i$ is the product term.

### 2.1.1 Three Flavours of SPLD — The Programmable Planes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **only structural difference** between PROM, PAL, and PLA is **WHICH of the two planes (AND, OR) is user-programmable**.

| Device | AND Plane | OR Plane | Output Structure |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PROM** | **Fixed** (decoder generates all $2^n$ minterms) | **Programmable** | Combinational only |
| **PAL** | **Programmable** | **Fixed** | Combinational + optional registered output |
| **PLA** | **Programmable** | **Programmable** | Combinational; most flexible |

### 2.1.2 Why PLA is the Most General but Most Expensive

In a PLA, the designer freely chooses:
- Which **product terms** are generated (AND plane fuses).
- Which of those product terms are OR'd into each output (OR plane fuses).

This dual flexibility lets a single PLA implement **shared sub-expressions** across multiple outputs, something a PAL cannot do because each output has its own dedicated OR gate fed by a fixed number of AND gates.

## 2.2 Programming Technologies — The "Memory" Behind the Fuse

| Technology | Volatile? | Re-programmable? | Node Size | Typical User |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bipolar Fuse (NiCr)** | No | **One-Time** (OTP) | 1 transistor + fuse | Old PALs |
| **Antifuse (ONO dielectric)** | No | **One-Time** (OTP) | Very small (~1/9 of SRAM) | Actel/Microsemi FPGAs |
| **EPROM (UV-erasable)** | No | Yes (with UV light) | Large | Old Altera MAX 5000 |
| **EEPROM / Flash** | No | Yes (in-circuit, 100k cycles) | 2 transistors | CPLDs (MAX II, MAX V) |
| **SRAM** | **Yes** | Yes (unlimited) | **6 transistors** | SRAM-based FPGAs (Xilinx 7-series, Intel Cyclone) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering trade-off:** SRAM is volatile and uses 6 transistors per bit, but it is **infinitely reconfigurable** in milliseconds — ideal for prototypes and reconfigurable computing. Antifuse is one-time but **radiation-hard**, ideal for aerospace.

## 2.3 CPLD Architecture — The "Macro Cell" Concept

A **CPLD** is essentially several PAL-like blocks (called **Logic Array Blocks — LABs** or **macro cells**) stitched together by a **central programmable interconnect matrix (IM / PIM)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Each macro cell** typically contains:
> 1. An **AND–OR plane** (e.g., 36 inputs × 16 product terms).
> 2. An **XOR gate** (for polarity control / arithmetic).
> 3. A **D flip-flop** (for sequential logic).
> 4. A **tri-state output buffer** (for bidirectional I/O).
> 5. **Feedback paths** to the interconnect.

CPLDs are characterised by:
- **Predictable, deterministic timing** (no routing-dependent delay — "zero-worst-case").
- **Non-volatile** configuration (typically Flash or EEPROM).
- **Instant-on** behaviour (no external boot PROM required, unlike SRAM FPGAs).
- Typical propagation delays of **4 – 10 ns** across the entire device.

## 2.4 FPGA Architecture — The "Sea of Configurable Logic"

An **FPGA** replaces the rigid AND–OR plane with **fine-grained reconfigurable blocks**.

### 2.4.1 Five Core Building Blocks of an FPGA

1. **Configurable Logic Block (CLB / LE / ALM)** — the AND/OR equivalent, containing a small **LUT (Look-Up Table)**, a carry chain, and a flip-flop.
2. **Programmable Interconnect Network** — switch matrices using **SRAM-controlled pass transistors** or muxes.
3. **Block RAM (BRAM)** — dedicated dual-port memory (typically 18 Kbit or 36 Kbit per block).
4. **DSP Slices** — hard-wired multipliers/accumulators.
5. **I/O Blocks (IOB)** — support LVCMOS, LVDS, SSTL, PCIe, SERDES, etc.

### 2.4.2 Look-Up Table (LUT) — How It Replaces AND–OR Logic

An *n*-input LUT is essentially a **$2^n \times 1$ SRAM**. The *n* inputs act as an address, and the SRAM cell at that address holds the **desired output bit (0 or 1)** of the Boolean function.

> A 6-input LUT can implement **any** Boolean function of 6 variables — the configuration SRAM simply stores the 64-bit truth table.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **only reference sheet** you need on the day of the KTU exam for PLD numerical/design questions.

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of product terms for SOP function** | $P = \sum_{i=1}^{k} (\text{unique minterms of output } F_i)$ | Each unique product = 1 AND gate |
| **PLA size** | $(\text{Inputs } n, \text{Product terms } P, \text{Outputs } m)$ | Denoted $(n, P, m)$ |
| **Gate equivalent of LUT** | $1 \text{ LUT-}n \approx 2^{n-1}$ GE | Approximation for area estimation |
| **Configuration memory size (SRAM FPGA)** | $M = N_{\text{LUT}} \times 2^n + N_{\text{FF}} + N_{\text{BRAM bits}}$ | bits |
| **CPLD macro cell count** | $\text{Macro cells} = 2 \times \text{equivalent PAL16L8}$ | Rough rule of thumb |
| **tpd (PAL 16L8 typical)** | $t_{pd} \approx \mathbf{25 \text{ ns}}$ | Datasheet value |
| **tpd (Modern CPLD, e.g. MAX V)** | $t_{pd} \approx \mathbf{4 - 7 \text{ ns}}$ | Datasheet value |
| **Antifuse ON resistance** | $R_{\text{on}} \approx \mathbf{50 - 500 \ \Omega}$ | Closed state |
| **SRAM cell size** | **6 transistors** per config bit | Standard 6T SRAM |
| **Flash CPLD endurance** | $\approx \mathbf{100\,000 \text{ erase/write cycles}}$ | Datasheet |
| **FPGA BRAM density** | $\mathbf{18 \text{ Kbit}}$ or $\mathbf{36 \text{ Kbit}}$ per block | Typical |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Application Domain | Preferred PLD | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Aerospace / Defence (radiation-hard) | **Antifuse FPGA** | Non-volatile, immune to SEUs |
| High-volume consumer (e.g., router glue) | **CPLD** (e.g., MAX II) | Cheap, instant-on, deterministic |
| ASIC prototyping | **SRAM FPGA** | Unlimited re-spin cycles |
| AI accelerator / DSP prototyping | **FPGA + DSP slices** | Massive parallelism |
| Automotive ECU glue logic | **Flash CPLD** | AEC-Q100 qualified, non-volatile |
| Smart sensor nodes | **Tiny FPGA / SoC FPGA (Zynq, Cyclone V)** | CPU + fabric + peripherals on one die |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Implementing a Boolean Function on a PLA

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2022, Modified]**  
> Implement the following two Boolean functions on a **PLA**:
> $$F_1(A,B,C) = \overline{A}\,B + A\,\overline{C}$$
> $$F_2(A,B,C) = A\,B + \overline{A}\,\overline{B}\,C + A\,\overline{C}$$

### Step 1 — Identify Common Product Terms

List the product terms (P-terms) needed by **both** functions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= \overline{A}\,B \\
P_2 &= A\,\overline{C} \\
P_3 &= A\,B \\
P_4 &= \overline{A}\,\overline{B}\,C
\end{aligned}
$$

Notice that $P_2 = A\,\overline{C}$ is **shared** between $F_1$ and $F_2$. This is the unique advantage of a PLA.

### Step 2 — Identify the OR-Plane Connections

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= P_1 + P_2 \\
F_2 &= P_2 + P_3 + P_4
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the PLA Size Tuple $(n, P, m)$

- Inputs $n = 3$ (variables A, B, C — and their complements are auto-generated internally).
- **Unique product terms** $P = 4$ ($P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4$).
- Outputs $m = 2$ ($F_1, F_2$).

$$
\boxed{\text{PLA Size} = (n, P, m) = (3, 4, 2)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU marking insight:** A common student mistake is to count $P_2$ twice (once for each function). The **correct count** is the number of **unique** product terms across the entire chip — not per output. Examiners explicitly award partial credit for this.

### Step 4 — Compare With PAL

A PAL would need **two separate AND–OR planes**, one per output, because the OR plane is fixed. Thus the PAL would need **5 product-term slots** (2 for $F_1$, 3 for $F_2$) instead of 4 — a **20% area penalty** for this small example. The penalty grows exponentially for larger circuits.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Determining Number of Product Terms on a PROM

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
> How many product terms are required to implement $F(A,B,C,D) = \Sigma m(0,3,5,7,10,11,12,15)$ on a **PROM**? Justify your answer.

### Step 1 — Recall the PROM Architecture

A PROM has a **fully decoded AND plane**, meaning **every** minterm of the input space is generated automatically. There is no need (and no ability) to minimise.

### Step 2 — Compute the Total Number of Minterms

For $n$ input variables, the AND plane generates **all** $2^n$ minterms.

$$
2^n = 2^4 = 16 \text{ minterms}
$$

### Step 3 — Determine the OR Plane Programming

Of these 16 minterms, the user programs the OR gate of $F$ to sum **only the minterms listed in the function**:

$$
P_{\text{PROM}} = 2^n = 16 \text{ minterms in the AND plane (fixed)}
$$

Only **8 of these 16 minterms** are connected to the OR gate (m0, m3, m5, m7, m10, m11, m12, m15), but the AND plane itself still has **all 16 fuses/connections reserved**.

$$
\boxed{\text{PROM AND-plane size} = 2^n = 2^4 = \mathbf{16 \text{ product terms}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU board examiner pitfall:** Students often write "$P = 8$" (the count of minterms in $F$). The **correct answer for a PROM is always $2^n$**, regardless of how few minterms are actually used, because the AND plane is *physically present* on silicon.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — LUT Capacity Estimation

> A 4-input Boolean function is to be implemented on a **LUT-based FPGA**. How many configuration bits are required for the LUT alone? What is the equivalent gate count?

### Step 1 — LUT Memory Size

A LUT-$n$ stores $2^n$ truth-table entries, each entry being one bit (the function output).

$$
\text{Configuration bits} = 2^n = 2^4 = 16 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 2 — Equivalent Gate Count (GE)

Using the rule of thumb (a LUT-$n$ implements any function of $n$ variables, equivalent to the average area of a NAND-NAND implementation):

$$
\text{GE} \approx \frac{2^n}{2} = \frac{16}{2} = \mathbf{8 \text{ GE}}
$$

(More precisely, the **lower bound** is $2^{n-1}$ GE and the **upper bound** is $(2^n + 2n)$ GE, depending on the function.)

### Step 3 — SRAM Configuration Memory Cost

Each LUT-4 bit is stored in **6-transistor (6T) SRAM**. Therefore the LUT alone consumes:

$$
\text{Transistors} = 16 \times 6 = 96 \text{ transistors}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** This is why LUTs are **never made larger than 6 or 7 inputs** in modern FPGAs — beyond 7 inputs, the SRAM overhead ($2^7 \times 6 = 768$ transistors per LUT) outweighs the logic gain. Instead, designers use **fracturable LUTs** or **multiple smaller LUTs combined**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — CPLD Macro Cell Sizing

> An embedded board needs a CPLD to perform **address decoding** for 4 memory-mapped peripherals and **glue logic** for 8 control signals. Each peripheral decoder requires 4 product terms on average. Each glue function requires 6 product terms. How many macro cells are required?

### Step 1 — Product-Term Budget

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{decode}} &= 4 \text{ decoders} \times 4 \text{ P-terms} = 16 \text{ P-terms} \\
P_{\text{glue}} &= 8 \text{ functions} \times 6 \text{ P-terms} = 48 \text{ P-terms} \\
P_{\text{total}} &= 16 + 48 = 64 \text{ P-terms}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Macro Cell Count

Assume each macro cell provides **16 product terms** (typical for MAX II CPLDs):

$$
\text{Macro cells} = \frac{P_{\text{total}}}{16} = \frac{64}{16} = \mathbf{4 \text{ macro cells}}
$$

Round up to the nearest available device — a **MAX II EPM240 (240 LE)** would be selected.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam takeaway:** Always round **UP** to the next available density, and add a **20 % headroom** for design changes and ECO (Engineering Change Orders).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — When to Use What

| Parameter | SPLD (PAL) | CPLD | FPGA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Logic density** | < 100 gates | 100 – 10 000 gates | 10K – 10M+ gates |
| **Architecture** | AND/OR plane | Multiple PAL-like macro cells | LUT + interconnect fabric |
| **Configuration memory** | Fuse / EPROM | EEPROM / Flash | SRAM (mostly) |
| **Volatility** | Non-volatile | Non-volatile | **Volatile** (needs boot PROM) |
| **Power consumption** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Deterministic timing** | Yes | Yes (predictable) | No (routing-dependent) |
| **Re-programmability** | OTP / limited | 100k cycles | Unlimited |
| **In-system programming (ISP)** | No | Yes | Yes |
| **Cost per chip (low vol.)** | Very low | Low | Medium–High |
| **Cost per chip (high vol.)** | Low | Medium | Higher than ASIC |
| **Best use case** | Decoder, glue | Address decode, boot loader | DSP, SoC prototype, AI |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Universal PLD Block Diagram — AND / OR Plane

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **canonical internal structure** of an SPLD, with programmable planes shaded to indicate user-configurable fuses.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT BUFFERS + INVERTERS"]
        A_in["A"] --> A_buf["Buffer"]
        B_in["B"] --> B_buf["Buffer"]
        C_in["C"] --> C_buf["Buffer"]
        A_buf --> A_inv["NOT A"]
        B_buf --> B_inv["NOT B"]
        C_buf --> C_inv["NOT C"]
    end

    subgraph AND_PLANE["AND PLANE  (Programmable in PAL/PLA)"]
        P1["Product Term 1"]
        P2["Product Term 2"]
        P3["Product Term 3"]
        P4["Product Term 4"]
    end

    subgraph OR_PLANE["OR PLANE  (Programmable in PROM/PLA)"]
        OR1["OR Gate for F1"]
        OR2["OR Gate for F2"]
    end

    OUTPUT1["Output F1"] --> OUTBUF1["Tri-state Buffer"]
    OUTPUT2["Output F2 (registered)"] --> OUTBUF2["Tri-state Buffer"]

    A_inv --> P1
    B_inv --> P1
    A_buf --> P2
    C_inv --> P2
    A_buf --> P3
    B_buf --> P3
    A_inv --> P4
    B_buf --> P4
    C_buf --> P4

    P1 --> OR1
    P2 --> OR1
    P2 --> OR2
    P3 --> OR2
    P4 --> OR2

    OR1 --> OUTPUT1
    OR2 --> OUTPUT2
```

## 4.2 PROM vs PAL vs PLA — Single Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PROM["PROM"] -->|"AND plane: FIXED (decoder)"| PROM_NOTE["All 2^n minterms generated automatically"]
    PROM -->|"OR plane: PROGRAMMABLE"| PROM_USE["Used as lookup table / code memory"]

    PAL["PAL"] -->|"AND plane: PROGRAMMABLE"| PAL_NOTE["User chooses which product terms to generate"]
    PAL -->|"OR plane: FIXED"| PAL_USE["Each output has dedicated OR gate"]

    PLA["PLA"] -->|"AND plane: PROGRAMMABLE"| PLA_NOTE["User chooses product terms"]
    PLA -->|"OR plane: PROGRAMMABLE"| PLA_USE["User chooses which products feed which outputs"]

    PROM_USE --> COMPARE["PLA = most flexible, but slower and larger"]
    PAL_USE --> COMPARE
    PLA_USE --> COMPARE
```

## 4.3 CPLD Internal Architecture (Macro Cell Array)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PIM["Central Programmable Interconnect Matrix PIM"]
        PIMnode["Switch Matrix"]
    end

    subgraph LAB1["Logic Array Block 1  Macro Cell 1"]
        ANDOR1["AND OR Plane 36x16"]
        XOR1["XOR for polarity"]
        FF1["D Flip Flop"]
        TS1["Tri state Output Buffer"]
        ANDOR1 --> XOR1 --> FF1 --> TS1
    end

    subgraph LAB2["Logic Array Block 2  Macro Cell 2"]
        ANDOR2["AND OR Plane 36x16"]
        XOR2["XOR for polarity"]
        FF2["D Flip Flop"]
        TS2["Tri state Output Buffer"]
        ANDOR2 --> XOR2 --> FF2 --> TS2
    end

    subgraph LAB3["Logic Array Block 3  Macro Cell 3"]
        ANDOR3["AND OR Plane 36x16"]
        XOR3["XOR for polarity"]
        FF3["D Flip Flop"]
        TS3["Tri state Output Buffer"]
        ANDOR3 --> XOR3 --> FF3 --> TS3
    end

    PIMnode --- LAB1
    PIMnode --- LAB2
    PIMnode --- LAB3

    IO["I O Pads"] --- PIMnode
```

## 4.4 FPGA Architecture — Five Building Blocks

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FPGA["FPGA Chip"]
        subgraph CLB["1. Configurable Logic Blocks CLB"]
            LUT["LUT n SRAM"]
            FF["Flip Flop"]
            MUX["Carry MUX"]
            LUT --> FF --> MUX
        end

        subgraph INT["2. Programmable Interconnect"]
            SM["Switch Matrices"]
            WIRE["Horizontal and Vertical Routing Channels"]
            SM --- WIRE
        end

        subgraph BRAM["3. Block RAM"]
            BR["18 Kbit Dual Port"]
            BR2["36 Kbit True Dual Port"]
        end

        subgraph DSP["4. DSP Slices"]
            MULT["Hardware Multiplier"]
            ACC["Accumulator"]
            MULT --> ACC
        end

        subgraph IOB["5. I O Blocks"]
            LVDS["LVDS"]
            CMOS["LVCMOS"]
            PCIE["PCIe SERDES"]
        end

        CLB --- INT
        CLB --- BRAM
        CLB --- DSP
        CLB --- IOB
    end
```

## 4.5 PLD Design Flow — From Specification to Silicon

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SPEC["Design Specification"] --> HDL["HDL Coding VHDL or Verilog"]
    HDL --> SIM["Functional Simulation"]
    SIM --> SYNTH["Logic Synthesis"]
    SYNTH --> MAP["Technology Mapping"]
    MAP --> PLACE["Placement"]
    PLACE --> ROUTE["Routing"]
    ROUTE --> BITGEN["Bitstream Generation"]
    BITGEN --> PROG["Program the PLD Device"]
    PROG --> TEST["Hardware in the loop Test"]
    TEST -->|"Failure"| HDL
    TEST -->|"Pass"| PROD["Production Deployment"]
```

## 4.6 Programming Technology Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START["Choose Programming Technology"] --> Q1{"Re programming needed?"}
    Q1 -->|"No One time"| Q2{"Radiation hardened?"}
    Q2 -->|"Yes Aerospace"| ANTIFUSE["Antifuse FPGA Actel Microsemi"]
    Q2 -->|"No Consumer"| BIFUSE["Bipolar Fuse OTP PAL"]
    Q1 -->|"Yes"| Q3{"Instant on required?"}
    Q3 -->|"Yes Boot ROM not allowed"| FLASH["Flash CPLD MAX II or MAX V"]
    Q3 -->|"No Boot ROM acceptable"| SRAM["SRAM FPGA Xilinx 7 series or Intel Cyclone"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
**Define the term Programmable Logic Device. List any three types of PLDs in increasing order of complexity.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **Programmable Logic Device (PLD)** is an integrated circuit whose internal logic structure can be electrically configured by the user after manufacture to implement arbitrary Boolean functions.

The three types in increasing order of complexity are:

1. **SPLD** (Simple PLD) — PROM, PAL, PLA
2. **CPLD** (Complex PLD) — multiple macro cells + interconnect matrix
3. **FPGA** (Field-Programmable Gate Array) — LUT-based fabric with millions of gates

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for listing + 1 for correct order of complexity)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**  
**Differentiate between volatile and non-volatile PLDs with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Volatile PLD | Non-volatile PLD |
|---|---|---|
| **Configuration retention after power-off** | Lost | Retained |
| **External boot memory** | Required (PROM/Flash) | Not required |
| **Re-programmability** | Unlimited, in-system | Limited (Flash: ~100k cycles) |
| **Example** | SRAM-based **FPGA** (Xilinx Spartan-6) | Flash-based **CPLD** (Altera MAX II) |

*(3 marks: 1 mark per correct point)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2022]**  
**(a)** Explain the internal architecture of a **PROM** and a **PAL**. Compare them in terms of AND plane, OR plane, flexibility, and speed. **(7 marks)**  
**(b)** A PLA is to be used to implement the following Boolean functions:

$$F_1(A,B,C) = \overline{A}\,B + B\,\overline{C}$$

$$F_2(A,B,C) = A + \overline{B}\,\overline{C}$$

Determine the PLA size $(n, P, m)$, draw the PLA programming table, and comment on the number of fuses saved compared to a PAL implementation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**PROM Architecture (3.5 marks):**

- PROM = **Programmable Read Only Memory**.
- AND plane = **fixed binary decoder** that generates all $2^n$ minterms of the $n$ input variables.
- OR plane = **user-programmable**, allowing any subset of minterms to be summed at each output.
- Operates as a **lookup table** — the input combination acts as an address, and the corresponding memory cell stores the output bit.
- Inherently **combinational only** (no flip-flops inside a basic PROM).

**PAL Architecture (2 marks):**

- PAL = **Programmable Array Logic**.
- AND plane = **user-programmable**, generates selected product terms.
- OR plane = **fixed**, each output has a dedicated OR gate fed by a fixed number (e.g., 3 or 7) of AND gates.
- Some PAL outputs include a **registered feedback** path, enabling sequential logic.

**Comparison (1.5 marks):**

| Parameter | PROM | PAL |
|---|---|---|
| AND plane | Fixed | Programmable |
| OR plane | Programmable | Fixed |
| Flexibility | Low (limited product sharing) | Medium |
| Speed | Fast (only OR fuses) | Fast |
| Best use | Lookup tables / code | Custom combinational logic |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Expand each function (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \overline{A}\,B + B\,\overline{C} \\
    &= \overline{A}\,B(C+\overline{C}) + (A+\overline{A})\,B\,\overline{C} \\
    &= \overline{A}\,B\,C + \overline{A}\,B\,\overline{C} + A\,B\,\overline{C} + \overline{A}\,B\,\overline{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

Simplify (note that $\overline{A}\,B\,\overline{C}$ appears twice):

$$
F_1 = \overline{A}\,B\,C + \overline{A}\,B\,\overline{C} + A\,B\,\overline{C}
$$

$$
F_2 = A + \overline{B}\,\overline{C}
$$

**Step 2 — List the product terms:**

- $P_1 = \overline{A}\,B\,C$
- $P_2 = \overline{A}\,B\,\overline{C}$
- $P_3 = A\,B\,\overline{C}$
- $P_4 = A$
- $P_5 = \overline{B}\,\overline{C}$

**Step 3 — Identify the PLA size (1 mark):**

- $n = 3$ inputs (A, B, C)
- $P = 5$ unique product terms
- $m = 2$ outputs (F1, F2)

$$
\boxed{(n, P, m) = (3, 5, 2)}
$$

**Step 4 — PLA programming table (3 marks):**

| Product Term | A | B | C | Output F1 | Output F2 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| $P_2$ | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| $P_3$ | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| $P_4$ | 1 | – | – | 0 | 1 |
| $P_5$ | – | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 |

(Where "1" = true, "0" = complement, "–" = no connection / don't care.)

**Step 5 — Fuse count comparison with PAL (1 mark):**

In a PAL, the OR plane is fixed. Since $F_2$ alone needs **5 product terms** ($A + \overline{B}\,\overline{C}$ expanded to $A\,B\,C + A\,B\,\overline{C} + A\,\overline{B}\,C + A\,\overline{B}\,\overline{C} + \overline{A}\,\overline{B}\,\overline{C}$ = 5 terms), each output PAL block would need 5 OR inputs. The **PAL implementation** requires:

$$
\text{PAL product-term slots} = 5 + 3 = \mathbf{8 \text{ slots}}
$$

The **PLA** needs only:

$$
\text{PLA product terms} = 5 \text{ unique}
$$

**Fuses saved = 8 − 5 = 3 product-term slots**, demonstrating the PLA's superiority when product terms are **shared** across outputs.

---

### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *ALTERNATIVE CHOICE*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
**(a)** Describe the internal architecture of a **CPLD**. Explain the role of the **Programmable Interconnect Matrix (PIM)** and the **macro cell** with the help of a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**  
**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **five core building blocks of an FPGA**. Compare SRAM-based and Antifuse-based FPGAs in terms of volatility, reprogrammability, and application areas. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition (1 mark):**  
A **Complex Programmable Logic Device (CPLD)** is a PLD that integrates several PAL-like blocks (called **Logic Array Blocks, LABs** or **macro cells**) onto a single chip, interconnected by a **central programmable switch matrix**.

**Macro Cell Architecture (3 marks):**

A macro cell consists of:
1. **AND–OR plane** — typically 32–36 inputs producing 16 product terms.
2. **XOR gate** — provides output polarity control (active-high vs active-low) and helps implement arithmetic (addition/subtraction).
3. **D flip-flop** — converts combinational logic into registered (sequential) logic, with clock-enable and reset.
4. **Tri-state output buffer** — drives the I/O pad and provides bidirectional capability.
5. **Feedback path** — routes the registered or combinational output back into the interconnect matrix, allowing internal state to be reused.

**Programmable Interconnect Matrix (2 marks):**

The **PIM** sits at the heart of the CPLD. It is a **uniform, full-crossbar** switch matrix:
- Every macro cell output can connect to every macro cell input.
- Any I/O pin can be routed to any macro cell.
- Routing delay is **constant and predictable**, irrespective of the connection chosen — this is the famous **"zero-worst-case"** property of CPLDs.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to the macro cell diagram shown in **Section 4.3** above.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**FPGA Five Building Blocks (3.5 marks):**

1. **Configurable Logic Block (CLB / Logic Element / ALM):** the basic logic unit containing a Look-Up Table (LUT), carry logic, and a flip-flop.
2. **Programmable Interconnect Network:** switch matrices and routing channels that wire CLBs together; controlled by SRAM bits.
3. **Block RAM (BRAM):** dedicated dual-port SRAM blocks (typically 18 Kbit or 36 Kbit) used for FIFOs, buffers, register files.
4. **DSP Slices:** hard-wired multipliers and MAC units for high-speed arithmetic.
5. **I/O Blocks (IOB):** configurable transceivers supporting standards like LVCMOS, LVDS, SSTL, PCIe, SERDES.

**Comparison Table (3.5 marks):**

| Property | SRAM-based FPGA | Antifuse-based FPGA |
|---|---|---|
| **Volatility** | Volatile (needs boot PROM) | Non-volatile |
| **Reprogrammability** | Unlimited, in-system | **One-time** (OTP) |
| **Programming time** | Milliseconds | Seconds (high-voltage pulse) |
| **Configuration cell size** | 6 transistors/bit (large) | ~1/9 the size of SRAM cell |
| **Power consumption** | Higher (always-on SRAM) | Lower |
| **Radiation tolerance** | Susceptible to SEUs | **Excellent** (no charged nodes) |
| **Application areas** | Prototyping, DSP, AI, video | Aerospace, military, medical implants |
| **Examples** | Xilinx 7-series, Intel Cyclone | Actel/Microsemi RTG4, Igloo |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Confusing PAL and PLA:** Students frequently write "PAL has programmable AND and OR planes" — this is the definition of **PLA**, not PAL. **PAL has only a programmable AND plane.**
> 2. **Forgetting complements:** Every PLD input auto-generates both true and complement forms inside the chip. Students who list only "A, B, C" without mentioning internal inverters lose a mark.
> 3. **PROM product-term count:** The number of product terms on a PROM is **always** $2^n$, never the number of minterms in the function. This is the single most common mistake in numerical answers.
> 4. **LUT size:** A 4-input LUT is *not* equivalent to 4 AND gates. It is a $16 \times 1$ SRAM that can implement **any** 4-variable function — be precise in your language.
> 5. **CPLD "instant-on" property:** Students often confuse the **boot time** of SRAM FPGAs (which need external Flash) with CPLDs, which boot instantly from internal Flash. Examiners specifically look for the phrase **"non-volatile, instant-on"** in CPLD answers.
> 6. **No diagrams in Part A:** Short-answer questions **must** include at least a labelled block diagram for full marks. A 3-mark answer with only text typically scores 2/3.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **PLD** is a user-configurable digital IC; the user programs the AND/OR planes or LUTs to implement custom Boolean functions.
- ✅ **PROM**: fixed AND (decoder), programmable OR → used as lookup table.
- ✅ **PAL**: programmable AND, fixed OR → glue logic; output may have a flip-flop.
- ✅ **PLA**: both AND and OR programmable → most flexible; best for sharing product terms.
- ✅ PLA size is denoted as **$(n, P, m)$** where $n$ = inputs, $P$ = unique product terms, $m$ = outputs.
- ✅ **CPLD** = macro cells (PAL-like) + central **PIM**; **non-volatile, instant-on, predictable timing**.
- ✅ **FPGA** = LUT-based fabric + interconnect + BRAM + DSP + IOB.
- ✅ A LUT-$n$ is a $2^n \times 1$ SRAM storing the truth table of any $n$-input Boolean function.
- ✅ **SRAM FPGA** = volatile, unlimited reprogrammability, largest config cell (6T/bit).
- ✅ **Antifuse FPGA** = non-volatile, OTP, radiation-hard, smallest config cell.
- ✅ **Flash CPLD** = non-volatile, ~100 000 erase cycles, no boot ROM needed.
- ✅ Propagation delay: PAL ≈ 25 ns, CPLD ≈ 4–7 ns, FPGA = routing-dependent (typically 5–10 ns inside a CLB).
- ✅ **Antifuse ON-resistance** ≈ 50–500 Ω.
- ✅ Modern FPGA densities: from **10 000 to 10 million+** system gates.
- ✅ FPGAs are used as **ASIC prototypes, DSP engines, AI accelerators, and SoC substrates** (Zynq, Cyclone V SoC).
- ✅ CPLDs are used for **address decoding, bus arbitration, glue logic, boot loaders**.
- ✅ For KTU exams, always mention **AND-plane, OR-plane programmability, volatility, and reprogrammability** when comparing PLD families.
- ✅ Use neat labelled diagrams (block diagrams of AND/OR plane, macro cell, FPGA building blocks) — they constitute **20–30% of the marks** in a 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Commercial Off-The-Shelf Components (COTS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET695 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Commercial Off-The-Shelf Components (COTS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Commercial Off-The-Shelf (COTS)** components are **pre-designed, pre-fabricated, and pre-validated hardware or software building blocks** that are mass-produced, readily available on the open commercial market, and can be procured and integrated into an embedded system design **without any custom modification** by the system developer.

In the strict KTU/embedded systems lexicon, a COTS component is defined by **three mandatory attributes**:

1. **Commercial availability** — sold through standard distribution channels (distributors, OEMs, online vendors).
2. **Standardised interface & packaging** — conforms to industry-standard pinouts, buses, or APIs (e.g., ARM Cortex, USB, I²C, SPI).
3. **No bespoke engineering required** — the developer treats it as a **black box** and integrates it via datasheet specifications.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** COTS is studied under *Hardware/Software Trade-offs in Embedded Design*. KTU expects you to reason about *when* COTS is preferable over custom ASIC/ASIP design and to justify the choice using cost, time-to-market, and reliability metrics.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "LEGO Block" View

Imagine you are building a **custom race car**.

- **Custom design** = hand-forging every bolt, every gear, every piston from raw steel. It is *perfect for your race*, but takes 18 months and ₹50 lakhs.
- **COTS approach** = buying a **standard Honda engine**, a **Brembo brake caliper** off the shelf, and bolting them into your chassis. It is **80% as good**, but ready in 2 weeks for ₹2 lakhs.

The embedded engineer who picks COTS is essentially saying:
> *"I will not re-invent the wheel. I will buy the wheel, the brake, and the ECU — and spend my engineering effort on the **system-level integration** that gives me a competitive product."*

---

## 1.3 Physical/Engineering Metrics at a Glance

| Attribute | Typical COTS Value | Custom Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Unit Cost (high volume) | **\$1 – \$500** | **\$5,000 – \$500,000** (NRE dominated) |
| NRE Cost | **\$0** | **\$100k – \$10M** |
| Lead Time | **Days – Weeks** | **6 – 18 months** |
