| Operating Temperature | **0 °C – 70 °C** (commercial) or **−40 °C – 85 °C** (industrial) | Tailored (often **−55 °C – 125 °C**) |
| MTBF | **100,000 – 1,000,000 hrs** | Application-specific |
| Lifecycle | **3 – 7 years** (then EOL) | Project-lifetime |

> [!WARNING]
> **COTS Lifecycle Trap:** A COTS chip can be **End-of-Life (EOL)** by the manufacturer within **3–5 years**, forcing a costly board re-spin if your embedded product has a 10–15 year field life (e.g., industrial PLCs, avionics).

---

## 1.4 Categories of COTS in Embedded Systems

1. **Hardware COTS:**
   - Microcontrollers (STM32, NXP LPC, PIC)
   - Microprocessors (ARM Cortex-A series, RISC-V SoCs)
   - FPGAs (Xilinx, Intel/Altera, Lattice)
   - Sensors (Bosch BMP280, MPU6050 IMU)
   - Wireless modules (ESP32, nRF24, LoRa)
   - Power regulators, memory chips, displays

2. **Software COTS:**
   - Real-Time Operating Systems (FreeRTOS, VxWorks, embOS)
   - TCP/IP stacks (lwIP)
   - File systems (FatFs, LittleFS)
   - Communication protocol stacks (CAN, Modbus, BLE)
   - Cryptographic libraries (mbedTLS)

3. **Hybrid / SoC COTS:**
   - System-on-Chip (SoC) integrating CPU + GPU + DSP + RF (e.g., NXP i.MX8, Texas Instruments Sitara)
   - Single Board Computers (Raspberry Pi CM4, BeagleBone)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why and How of COTS — The Decision Logic

The adoption of COTS is not arbitrary; it follows a structured **Make-or-Buy** reasoning rooted in **systems engineering economics**.

### Step-by-step logic a KTU student must memorise:

1. **Volume Projection:** If the expected production volume is **high (>10,000 units/yr)**, custom ASIC NRE amortisation becomes viable. Below this threshold, COTS dominates.
2. **Time-to-Market Pressure:** Consumer products demand launch windows of **6–12 months**. COTS is the **only feasible path**.
3. **Risk Profile:** COTS components are **pre-validated** by the vendor — reducing **design risk** but introducing **supply-chain risk**.
4. **Differentiation Focus:** If your product's competitive advantage is in **application software** (UI, analytics, AI), then underlying hardware should be COTS.
5. **Certification Burden:** In safety-critical domains (DO-178C, ISO 26262), COTS may still need **qualification evidence** from the vendor.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations form the **quantitative spine** of any COTS analysis question worth 7–14 marks in KTU ESE.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total Cost of Ownership** | $C_{TCO} = C_{NRE} + (N \cdot C_{unit}) + C_{integration} + C_{maintenance} + C_{risk}$ | True life-cycle cost, not sticker price. |
| 2 | **Break-Even Volume** | $N_{BE} = \dfrac{C_{NRE,cust} - C_{NRE,COTS}}{C_{unit,COTS} - C_{unit,cust}}$ | Units beyond which custom design is cheaper. |
| 3 | **Cost per Unit (amortised)** | $C_{unit,eff} = C_{unit} + \dfrac{C_{NRE}}{N}$ | Includes NRE spread over volume. |
| 4 | **Mean Time Between Failures (system)** | $\dfrac{1}{MTBF_{sys}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{\lambda_i}{1} = \sum \lambda_i$ | Failure rates of COTS parts add in series. |
| 5 | **System Reliability (series)** | $R_{sys}(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_{sys} \cdot t}$ | Reliability degrades exponentially with part count. |
| 6 | **Power Budget** | $P_{sys} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} V_{i} \cdot I_{i} \cdot d_i$ | Sum of active-mode powers of all COTS ICs. |
| 7 | **Obsolescence Risk Index** | $O_{risk} = \dfrac{T_{product\_life}}{T_{COTS\_availability}}$ | If $> 1$, re-design is mandatory. |
| 8 | **Development Time Reduction** | $T_{COTS} \approx 0.2 \cdot T_{custom}$ to $0.5 \cdot T_{custom}$ | Empirical industry rule-of-thumb. |

> [!WARNING]
> In markdown tables, the pipe symbol `|` is **forbidden** inside cells. The notation $\vert x \vert$ above uses **LaTeX `\vert`** to avoid breaking the table grid.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace (NASA, ISRO):** Use radiation-hardened COTS (RH-COTS) to cut cost while tolerating space radiation.
- **Automotive (ECU clusters):** Infineon Aurix, NXP S32K — automotive-grade COTS ASIL-D MCUs.
- **IoT Edge Nodes:** ESP32, STM32WL — sub-\$5 COTS SoCs with integrated RF.
- **Defence:** The US DoD's *COTS Initiative* (1994) mandated that **all new military systems use commercial components** wherever possible to leverage the semiconductor industry's R&D investment.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Break-Even Volume for Make-vs-Buy Decision

This is the **most frequently asked 7-mark analytical problem** in KTU ESE Module 2.

### Problem Setup
A startup is designing a smart energy meter.
- **Option A (COTS module):** Buy an STM32-based reference module at **₹400/unit**, zero NRE.
- **Option B (Custom ASIC):** Develop a proprietary mixed-signal chip. NRE = **₹80,00,000**, unit cost = **₹120/unit** (at high volume).

Find the **break-even production volume** $N_{BE}$ at which both options have equal Total Cost of Ownership (ignoring integration & maintenance for simplicity).

### Step 1: Write the cost equations

Total cost of the **COTS** option:
$$C_{TCO}^{COTS} = N \cdot 400$$

Total cost of the **Custom** option:
$$C_{TCO}^{cust} = 80{,}00{,}000 + N \cdot 120$$

### Step 2: Equate the two costs at break-even

At break-even volume $N_{BE}$, the two costs are equal:
$$N_{BE} \cdot 400 = 80{,}00{,}000 + N_{BE} \cdot 120$$

### Step 3: Isolate $N_{BE}$

Subtract $N_{BE} \cdot 120$ from both sides:
$$N_{BE} \cdot (400 - 120) = 80{,}00{,}000$$

$$N_{BE} \cdot 280 = 80{,}00{,}000$$

### Step 4: Solve for $N_{BE}$

$$N_{BE} = \frac{80{,}00{,}000}{280}$$

$$N_{BE} = 2{,}85{,}714.28 \approx 2{,}85{,}715 \text{ units}$$

### Step 5: Engineering Interpretation

For production volumes **below 2.85 lakh units**, the COTS module is cheaper.
For volumes **above 2.85 lakh units**, the custom ASIC pays back its NRE.

If the startup projects a lifetime volume of **only 50,000 units**, COTS saves them:
$$\Delta C = (80{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}000 \cdot 120) - (50{,}000 \cdot 400)$$
$$\Delta C = (80{,}00{,}000 + 60{,}00{,}000) - 2{,}00{,}00{,}000$$
$$\Delta C = 1{,}40{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 = -60{,}00{,}000$$

**Negative means COTS saves ₹60 lakhs.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation: System Reliability of a COTS-Based Board

A board uses **4 COTS ICs** in series (failure of any one fails the board):
- IC1 (MCU): $\lambda_1 = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ /hr
- IC2 (Sensor): $\lambda_2 = 8 \times 10^{-6}$ /hr
- IC3 (Power regulator): $\lambda_3 = 3 \times 10^{-6}$ /hr
- IC4 (Memory): $\lambda_4 = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ /hr

Find the system MTBF at operating time $t = 10{,}000$ hrs.

### Step 1: Sum failure rates (series model)

$$\lambda_{sys} = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3 + \lambda_4$$
$$\lambda_{sys} = (5 + 8 + 3 + 2) \times 10^{-6}$$
$$\lambda_{sys} = 18 \times 10^{-6} \text{ failures/hr}$$

### Step 2: Compute System MTBF

$$MTBF_{sys} = \frac{1}{\lambda_{sys}} = \frac{1}{18 \times 10^{-6}}$$
$$MTBF_{sys} \approx 55{,}556 \text{ hrs} \approx 6.34 \text{ years}$$

### Step 3: Compute System Reliability at $t = 10{,}000$ hrs

$$R_{sys}(10{,}000) = e^{-\lambda_{sys} \cdot t} = e^{-18 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 10{,}000}$$
$$R_{sys}(10{,}000) = e^{-0.18}$$
$$R_{sys}(10{,}000) \approx 0.8353 \text{ or } 83.53\%$$

**Interpretation:** Even with quality COTS parts, the *cumulative* part count pulls reliability below 84% after one year of continuous operation. **Mitigation:** use parts with lower $\lambda$, add redundancy, or use higher-grade (automotive/military) COTS.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Advantages vs Disadvantages of COTS

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is **high-yield** — expect a 7-mark question asking you to compare COTS with custom design using these exact points.

| Dimension | COTS Advantage | COTS Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|
| **Cost (low volume)** | ✅ Very low NRE | ❌ Higher per-unit cost |
| **Cost (high volume)** | ❌ NRE of custom amortises | ✅ Custom becomes cheaper |
| **Time-to-Market** | ✅ Days–weeks | ❌ None (advantage is huge) |
| **Performance** | ❌ Generic, may be over/under-spec | ✅ Custom can be tuned |
| **Power** | ❌ Includes unused features | ✅ Custom optimised |
| **Reliability** | ✅ Vendor-validated | ❌ Vendor controls qualification |
| **Lifecycle** | ❌ 3–7 yr EOL | ✅ Project-controlled |
| **IP / Security** | ❌ Public datasheet = known attack surface | ✅ Custom hidden |
| **Supply Chain** | ❌ Dependent on one vendor | ✅ In-house fabrication control |
| **Certification** | ✅ Vendor provides docs | ❌ May need extra qualification |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow: COTS Selection Decision Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: New Embedded Product] --> A1{Define Requirements}
    A1 --> A2[List Performance, Power, Cost, I-O Needs]
    A2 --> A3{Production Volume Forecast}
    A3 -->|Low < 10k units per yr| A4[Choose COTS Path]
    A3 -->|High > 100k units per yr| A5[Evaluate Custom ASIC]
    A3 -->|Medium 10k to 100k| A6[Check Break Even Volume]
    A4 --> A7{Time to Market Critical?}
    A7 -->|Yes within 12 months| A8[Adopt COTS Module or SoC]
    A7 -->|No more than 24 months allowed| A9[Consider COTS with FPGA Co Processor]
    A6 --> A10[Compute NBE Using Equation]
    A10 --> A11{NBE less than Forecast?}
    A11 -->|Yes| A5
    A11 -->|No| A8
    A8 --> B1[Vendor Selection Phase]
    B1 --> B2{Required Operating Temperature?}
    B2 -->|0 to 70 C| B3[Commercial Grade]
    B2 -->|minus 40 to 85 C| B4[Industrial Grade]
    B2 -->|minus 55 to 125 C| B5[MIL or Automotive Grade]
    B3 --> C1[Check Lifecycle and EOL Policy]
    B4 --> C1
    B5 --> C1
    C1 --> C2{Lifecycle covers Product Life?}
    C2 -->|Yes| C3[Approve and Procure]
    C2 -->|No| C4[Plan Redesign Buffer or Second Source]
    A5 --> D1[Custom ASIC Flow]
    D1 --> D2[Spec to GDSII 12 to 18 months]
    D2 --> C3
    C3 --> E1[Integration and Validation]
    E1 --> E2[Field Deployment]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Architecture of a Typical COTS-Based Embedded System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POW[Power Subsystem COTS]
        POW1[Buck Regulator TPS5430]
        POW2[LDO AMS1117]
    end
    subgraph CORE[Processing Core COTS]
        CORE1[MCU STM32F407]
        CORE2[FPGA Spartan 6]
    end
    subgraph SENS[Sensor Front End COTS]
        SENS1[IMU MPU6050]
        SENS2[Temp Sensor BMP280]
    end
    subgraph COM[Comms Module COTS]
        COM1[BLE Module nRF51822]
        COM2[WiFi ESP8266]
    end
    subgraph MEM[Memory COTS]
        MEM1[SPI Flash W25Q64]
        MEM2[SDRAM W9825G6KH]
    end
    SENS1 --> CORE1
    SENS2 --> CORE1
    CORE1 <-->|SPI Bus| CORE2
    CORE1 <--> MEM1
    CORE1 <--> MEM2
    CORE1 --> COM1
    CORE2 --> COM2
    POW1 --> CORE1
    POW1 --> CORE2
    POW2 --> SENS1
    POW2 --> SENS2
    POW2 --> COM1
    POW2 --> COM2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Every block (MCU, sensor, regulator, memory, wireless module) is a **separate COTS chip** procured from a different vendor. The **embedded system** is essentially the **PCB that ties them together** plus the **firmware** running on the MCU. The engineer's value-add is in the **system integration**, not in fabricating silicon.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]**
Define the term **Commercial Off-The-Shelf (COTS)** component. List **any two examples** of hardware COTS used in embedded systems.

**Model Answer:**
COTS components are **pre-designed, pre-tested, mass-produced hardware/software modules** that are readily available in the commercial market and can be integrated into an embedded system without custom fabrication.

**Examples (any two):**
1. **STM32F407** microcontroller (ARM Cortex-M4 based)
2. **ESP32** Wi-Fi + BLE SoC
3. **MPU6050** 6-axis IMU sensor

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
State **two advantages** and **one disadvantage** of using COTS components in an embedded product.

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. **Reduced time-to-market** — components are pre-built and validated.
2. **Lower NRE cost** — no silicon fabrication or custom IP development needed.

**Disadvantage:**
1. **Limited product lifecycle** — COTS parts typically have an **End-of-Life (EOL)** of 3–7 years, shorter than industrial/medical product lifetimes, forcing costly board re-designs.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Make-or-Buy decision framework** for selecting between a **COTS module** and a **custom ASIC** in an embedded system. Derive the **break-even volume formula** and apply it to a scenario where:
- COTS unit cost = ₹500
- Custom NRE = ₹1,00,00,000
- Custom unit cost = ₹150

Determine the break-even volume and state which option is preferable at a forecast volume of 50,000 units.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss **five major risks** associated with COTS adoption in long-lifecycle embedded products (e.g., industrial PLCs, defence systems). Suggest **two mitigation strategies** for each.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The **Make-or-Buy framework** evaluates whether to purchase a COTS component or invest in custom silicon based on **cost**, **volume**, **time-to-market**, and **differentiation**.

**Derivation:**
Let $N$ = production volume, $C_{unit}^{C}$ = COTS unit cost, $C_{NRE}^{A}$ = ASIC NRE, $C_{unit}^{A}$ = ASIC unit cost.

Equating total costs:
$$N \cdot C_{unit}^{C} = C_{NRE}^{A} + N \cdot C_{unit}^{A}$$
$$N_{BE} \cdot (C_{unit}^{C} - C_{unit}^{A}) = C_{NRE}^{A}$$
$$N_{BE} = \frac{C_{NRE}^{A}}{C_{unit}^{C} - C_{unit}^{A}}$$

**Substituting values:**
$$N_{BE} = \frac{1{,}00{,}00{,}000}{500 - 150} = \frac{1{,}00{,}00{,}000}{350}$$
$$N_{BE} = 2{,}85{,}714 \text{ units}$$

**At N = 50,000:**
Since 50,000 < 2,85,714, the **COTS module is more economical**.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

| # | Risk | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Component Obsolescence (EOL)** within 3–5 yrs | (a) Maintain **Last-Time-Buy** stockpile. (b) Design with **pin-compatible second source** COTS parts. |
| 2 | **Vendor Lock-in** / single supplier | (a) Multi-vendor sourcing agreements. (b) Use **standard bus interfaces** (I²C/SPI) so firmware portability is preserved. |
| 3 | **Supply Chain Disruption** (geopolitics, pandemics) | (a) Maintain **6-month safety stock**. (b) Qualify **alternate-region vendors**. |
| 4 | **Security Vulnerabilities** in publicly documented COTS | (a) Add **hardware root-of-trust** wrappers. (b) Implement **secure boot + encrypted firmware**. |
| 5 | **Certification Gaps** (DO-178, ISO 26262) | (a) Demand **vendor qualification documents**. (b) Conduct **in-house delta-qualification** testing. |
| 6 | **Performance ceiling** — cannot meet exotic specs | (a) Use **COTS + co-processor (FPGA/ASIC)** hybrid. (b) Request vendor for **semi-custom variant**. |

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of a typical COTS-based embedded system**. Identify and label at least **four distinct COTS sub-systems**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the **system MTBF** and the **reliability at t = 5000 hours** for a board composed of:
- MCU (λ = 4 × 10⁻⁶/hr)
- Sensor (λ = 6 × 10⁻⁶/hr)
- Regulator (λ = 2 × 10⁻⁶/hr)
- Memory (λ = 1 × 10⁻⁶/hr)

Comment on whether this board is suitable for a **5-year continuous-operation industrial application**.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The COTS-based embedded system has the following architecture (refer to Section 4.2 diagram):

1. **Power Subsystem (COTS):** Buck regulator + LDO provide regulated rails.
2. **Processing Core (COTS):** MCU executes application firmware; optional FPGA for parallel DSP.
3. **Sensor Subsystem (COTS):** IMU, temperature, pressure sensors connect via I²C/SPI.
4. **Communication Module (COTS):** BLE/Wi-Fi/LoRa radio enables connectivity.
5. **Memory (COTS):** External SPI Flash + SDRAM.

**Value-add of the engineer:** PCB layout, firmware development, and system-level integration.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: Sum failure rates (series model):**
$$\lambda_{sys} = 4 + 6 + 2 + 1 = 13 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /hr}$$

**Step 2: System MTBF:**
$$MTBF_{sys} = \frac{1}{13 \times 10^{-6}} = 76{,}923 \text{ hrs}$$

**Step 3: Reliability at t = 5000 hrs:**
$$R_{sys}(5000) = e^{-13 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 5000} = e^{-0.065} \approx 0.9370$$

**Step 4: 5-year operation = 5 × 8760 = 43,800 hrs:**
$$R_{sys}(43800) = e^{-13 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 43800} = e^{-0.5694} \approx 0.566$$

**Comment:**
At 43,800 hrs (5 years), the reliability drops to **~56.6%**, meaning roughly **43% of boards will fail** before 5 years. **This is unsuitable** for a 5-year continuous-operation industrial product unless **redundancy**, **higher-grade parts**, or **predictive replacement** is added.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** *unit cost* with *total cost*. Always include **NRE** in your Make-or-Buy equations. Many students lose **3 marks** here.
> 2. **Always state units** in numerical answers (e.g., "₹", "hours", "%"). Unlabelled numbers fetch **zero** in valuation.
> 3. For **reliability questions**, students forget to convert years → hours using **8760 hrs/yr**. This causes a **10× error**.
> 4. COTS lifecycle (EOL) risk must be **explicitly mentioned** when answering long-lifecycle domain questions (defence, medical).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **COTS** = pre-built, pre-tested, commercially available components integrated as black boxes.
- ✅ Three pillars of COTS identity: **commercial availability + standard interface + no custom fab**.
- ✅ **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** = NRE + (N × unit) + integration + maintenance + risk.
- ✅ **Break-even volume** $N_{BE} = \dfrac{C_{NRE}^{custom}}{C_{unit}^{COTS} - C_{unit}^{custom}}$ — the single most important formula.
- ✅ COTS dominates at **low-to-medium volume** and **short time-to-market** products.
- ✅ Custom ASIC dominates at **high volume** (>100k units) when NRE is amortised.
- ✅ System MTBF for series COTS parts: $MTBF = \dfrac{1}{\sum \lambda_i}$.
- ✅ System reliability: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda_{sys} \cdot t}$ — degrades **exponentially** with part count.
- ✅ Major COTS risks: **EOL/Obsolescence, Vendor Lock-in, Supply Chain, Security, Certification gaps**.
- ✅ Mitigations: **second-source design, last-time-buy stock, secure boot, qualification evidence**.
- ✅ Real-world grades: **Commercial (0–70°C)**, **Industrial (−40–85°C)**, **Automotive/MIL (−40–125°C)**.
- ✅ Key engineering domains adopting COTS: **aerospace (RH-COTS), automotive ECUs, IoT, defence, industrial PLCs**.
- ✅ Engineer's value-add in COTS designs is in **system integration, firmware, and PCB design — not silicon fabrication**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory: ROM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET695 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Memory: ROM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Read-Only Memory (ROM)** is a non-volatile semiconductor memory device used in embedded systems to permanently store firmware, boot loaders, microcode, lookup tables (LUTs), and constant data that must be retained even when the system power is removed. ROM is *programmed* (written) once — either at the fabrication plant or via a special programming procedure — and thereafter supports only **read** operations during normal execution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *ROM is a class of non-volatile memory in which data is permanently or semi-permanently written, and during normal operation the processor can only fetch (read) data from it. Variants include Mask ROM, PROM, EPROM, EEPROM, and Flash memory.*

**Key Properties (Bolded Standard Metrics):**

- **Non-volatility:** Data retention without power for **>10 years** (typical).
- **Access time:** **25 ns – 250 ns** depending on technology.
- **Density range:** **4 Kbit to 4 Gbit** per chip (Flash being the densest).
- **Endurance (write cycles):** Mask ROM = **0 writes**, PROM = **1 write**, EPROM ≈ **100 cycles**, EEPROM ≈ **10⁶ cycles**, Flash ≈ **10⁵ cycles**.
- **Bus interface:** Parallel (8/16/32-bit) or Serial (SPI, I²C).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Printed Book in a Library**
> Imagine ROM as a **printed reference book** kept inside an embedded device.
> - The book is written *once* by the manufacturer (author) and **published** (fabricated).
> - The reader (CPU/MCU) can **open any page (address)** and **read its content (data)** infinitely.
> - The reader can **never erase or rewrite** a page in the printed book — just like the CPU cannot write into a Mask ROM during runtime.
> - An **EEPROM**, however, is like a **book with pencil-erasable pages** — you can scribble new content, but slowly and only a few thousand times.
> - **Flash memory** is like a **chalkboard section** that can be erased in bulk and rewritten in blocks — fast for whole chapters, slow for individual words.

This analogy clarifies why ROM is the *firmware vault* of every embedded product: the code never changes during the product's lifetime.

## 1.3 Why ROM is Essential in Embedded Systems

| Reason | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|
| Bootstrapping | CPU must execute code at reset; volatile RAM loses contents. |
| Determinism | Lookup tables (e.g., sine/cosine, CRC) guarantee cycle-accurate reads. |
| Security | Firmware cannot be tampered with easily (Mask ROM / OTP ROM). |
| Cost | For high-volume products, Mask ROM is the **cheapest** memory per bit. |
| Reliability | No batteries or refresh circuits required. |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Organization of a ROM Chip

A generic ROM IC is organized as a **2D matrix of memory cells** with three functional blocks:

1. **Address Decoder (Row/Column Select):** Decodes the *n*-bit address bus into one-hot row/column lines.
2. **Memory Cell Array:** Stores a hard-wired bit at each intersection (Mask ROM) or a charge on a floating gate (EPROM/EEPROM/Flash).
3. **Output Buffer / Sense Amplifier:** Drives the data bus when the chip is selected (CS̅ active).

For a ROM of capacity **C bits**, organized as **W words × B bits/word**:
$$n = \log_2 W \quad \text{(number of address lines)}$$
$$\text{Total pins (parallel)} = n + B + \text{Control pins (CS, OE, CE)}$$

## 2.2 Read Cycle Timing — The Most Tested Concept

The **read access time** ($t_{AA}$) is the delay between a stable address input and a valid data output. For any KTU exam, three timing parameters must be memorized:

| Parameter | Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Address Access Time | $t_{AA}$ | Time from valid address → valid data | **100–250 ns** (EPROM) |
| Chip Enable Access Time | $t_{ACE}$ | Time from CS̅ low → valid data | **100 ns** |
| Output Enable Access Time | $t_{OE}$ | Time from OE̅ low → valid data (after CS̅ already active) | **30–50 ns** |

The relationship is governed by the inequality:
$$t_{AA} \geq t_{ACE} + t_{OE} \text{ (additive path model)}$$

The CPU must wait at least $t_{AA}$ nanoseconds before sampling the data bus — this is enforced via **wait states** in the bus interface unit.

## 2.3 Evolution of ROM Technologies (KTU High-Yield Table)

| ROM Type | Write Mechanism | Erase Mechanism | Volatility | Endurance | Write Time |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mask ROM** | Done at fab via lithographic mask | Not erasable | Non-volatile | **0 (factory only)** | N/A |
| **PROM** | Fuse/antifuse blown by user | Not erasable | Non-volatile | **1 (OTP)** | ~ms |
| **EPROM** | Hot-electron injection (Fowler-Nordheim) | **UV light (20–30 min)** | Non-volatile | **~100 cycles** | ~ms |
| **EEPROM** | Fowler-Nordheim tunneling | **Electrically, byte-wise** | Non-volatile | **10⁶ cycles** | ~1–10 ms |
| **Flash** | Fowler-Nordheim tunneling | **Electrically, block-wise** | Non-volatile | **10⁵ cycles** | ~µs/block |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite Question:**
> *"Differentiate between EPROM and EEPROM."* — Always mention the **erase granularity**: EPROM erases the *whole chip* under UV; EEPROM erases *one byte* electrically.

## 2.4 Floating-Gate Transistor — The Physics Behind Modern ROM

Modern ROMs (EPROM, EEPROM, Flash) store a bit as **electrical charge trapped on a floating gate** of a MOSFET. Two quantum-mechanical mechanisms are used:

**1. Hot-Electron Injection (HEI)** — used for *programming*:
$$I_{inj} \approx K \cdot e^{-\Phi_b / V_{ds}} \quad \text{(channel hot electrons surmount Si–SiO}_2 \text{ barrier } \Phi_b \approx 3.2 \text{ eV)}$$

**2. Fowler-Nordheim Tunneling** — used for *erase* and modern *programming*:
$$J_{FN} = A \cdot E_{ox}^2 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{-B}{E_{ox}}\right)$$
where $E_{ox}$ is the oxide field (≈ **10 MV/cm**), and $A, B$ are tunneling constants. The thin tunnel oxide (≈ **7–10 nm**) allows electrons to *quantum-mechanically tunnel* onto/off the floating gate.

> [!NOTE]
> The **threshold voltage** $V_{TH}$ of the cell transistor shifts based on stored charge:
> - Charged floating gate → high $V_{TH}$ → cell reads as **'0'** (erased state).
> - Discharged floating gate → low $V_{TH}$ → cell reads as **'1'** (programmed state, for convention).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Address lines required | $n = \lceil \log_2 W \rceil$ | bits |
| Total ROM capacity | $C = W \times B$ | bits |
| Data bus width | $B$ (typically 8 / 16 / 32) | bits |
| Read access time constraint | $t_{CPU\_wait} \geq t_{AA}$ | ns |
| Energy per bit (read) | $E_{read} = C_{bit} \cdot V_{DD}^2$ | Joules |
| Charge retention (Arrhenius) | $t_{ret} = t_0 \cdot \exp(E_a / k_B T)$ | seconds |
| Floating-gate charge decay | $Q(t) = Q_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau_{leak}}$ | Coulombs |
| Endurance (avg) | $N_{cycles} = \log_{10}(\text{variant})$ | cycles |

> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** ROMs underpin every IoT device, automotive ECU (firmware for engine control), washing-machine controller (program flow), smart card (cryptographic keys), and satellite avionics (radiation-hardened Mask ROM).

## 2.6 ROM Interface to a Microcontroller

A typical parallel ROM (e.g., 27C256 — 32 K × 8 EPROM) is interfaced as follows:

- **Address Bus A0–A14** → ROM A0–A14 (15 lines for 32 K locations).
- **Data Bus D0–D7** ← ROM O0–O7 (8 lines, bidirectional for MCU).
- **CS̅ (Chip Select)** ← MCU address decoder (e.g., 74HC138) output.
- **OE̅ (Output Enable)** ← MCU read strobe (RD̅).
- **VPP** → +12.75 V during programming only (float or GND during read).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **memory-mapped I/O**, ROM occupies a fixed region of the CPU's address space. The address decoder ensures the ROM is selected *only* when the CPU places an address within this window. This avoids bus contention.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Address Lines for a Given ROM Size

**Problem (typical KTU 2-mark question):**
*A ROM has a capacity of 64 K × 16. Calculate (i) total bit capacity, (ii) number of address lines, (iii) number of data lines, (iv) total memory-mapped pins excluding Vcc/GND.*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify word and bit counts.**

Given: $W = 64\text{ K words} = 64 \times 1024 = 65{,}536$ words, $B = 16$ bits/word.

**Step 2 — Total bit capacity.**
$$C = W \times B = 65{,}536 \times 16$$

$$\begin{aligned}
C &= 65{,}536 \times 16 \\
  &= 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ bits} \\
  &= 1{,}048{,}576 \;/\; 1024 \\
  &= 1024 \text{ Kbits} \\
  &= 1 \text{ Mbit}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Number of address lines** (must satisfy $2^n \geq W$).
$$n = \log_2(65{,}536) = \log_2(2^{16}) = 16$$

Therefore, **16 address lines** (A0–A15).

**Step 4 — Number of data lines.** Since $B = 16$, we need **16 data lines** (D0–D15).

**Step 5 — Total memory pins (excluding Vcc/GND).**
$$P = n + B + \text{Control pins (CS, OE, CE)}$$

Assuming 3 control pins (CS̅, OE̅, CE̅):
$$\begin{aligned}
P &= 16 + 16 + 3 \\
  &= 35 \text{ pins}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** Capacity = **1 Mbit**, address lines = **16**, data lines = **16**, total memory pins = **35**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Determining Address Decoder Range for Memory Mapping

**Problem:**
An 8051-family MCU has 16 address lines (A0–A15, i.e., 64 KB address space). Design an address map that places a 32 KB ROM at address **0000H–7FFFH** and a 32 KB RAM at **8000H–FFFFH**. Determine the logic for CS̅ of ROM.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Express the address range in binary.**

ROM range:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{0000H} &= \underbrace{0\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000}_{16 \text{ bits}} \\
\text{7FFFH} &= \underbrace{0\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1111}_{16 \text{ bits}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the constant bit (decoder input).**

Across the entire ROM range, the **most significant bit A15 = 0** for all addresses from 0000H to 7FFFH. The remaining 15 bits (A0–A14) vary freely.

For RAM range (8000H–FFFFH), A15 = 1.

**Step 3 — Derive the CS̅ logic for ROM.**

ROM must be selected when A15 = 0:
$$\overline{CS}_{ROM} = \overline{\overline{A_{15}}} = A_{15}'$$

In practice, using an active-low decoder (e.g., 74HC138 with Y0 output):
$$Y_0 = \overline{\overline{G_2A} \cdot \overline{G_2B} \cdot G_1 \cdot \overline{CBA}}$$

By tying C=B=A=0 and feeding A15 to G1 (with proper inversion logic), we obtain:
$$CS_{ROM} = \overline{A_{15}}$$

**Final Answer:** ROM is selected when A15 = 0 → $CS_{ROM} = \overline{A_{15}}$; RAM is selected when A15 = 1 → $CS_{RAM} = A_{15}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Insight:** A 32 KB boundary aligns perfectly on a 15-bit address boundary ($2^{15} = 32{,}768$), so a single inverter on A15 cleanly separates the two halves.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: KTU Comparison Questions

**Q: Compare Mask ROM, PROM, EPROM, EEPROM, and Flash across 8 parameters.**

| Parameter | Mask ROM | PROM | EPROM | EEPROM | Flash |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Programming** | At fab | User (once) | User (electrical) | User (electrical) | User (electrical) |
| **Erasing** | Not possible | Not possible | UV (whole chip) | Electrical (byte) | Electrical (block) |
| **Erase time** | — | — | 20–30 min | 1–10 ms / byte | 1 s / block |
| **Endurance** | 0 | 1 | ~100 | ~10⁶ | ~10⁵ |
| **Write time** | N/A | ms | ms | ms | µs/block |
| **Density** | Very high | Low–Med | Med | Med | Very high |
| **Cost/bit** | Lowest (high vol.) | High | Med | High | Low |
| **Typical use** | Game cartridges, TV firmware | OTP prototypes | Dev boards | Config registers | SSD, MCU firmware |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "which ROM is best for in-field firmware updates?", the answer is **Flash** — it offers electrical, block-wise erase with reasonable density and cost.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Internal Block Diagram of a Generic ROM IC

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Address Bus A0 to An-1] --> B[Address Decoder n to 2n]
    B --> C[Row Select Lines]
    C --> D[Memory Cell Matrix 2n x B]
    E[CS Chip Select active low] --> F[Output Enable Logic]
    G[OE Output Enable active low] --> F
    F --> D
    D --> H[Sense Amplifiers and Output Buffers]
    H --> I[Data Bus D0 to D-1]
    J[VDD and VSS] -.-> D
```

**Caption:** Functional architecture of a parallel ROM. Address lines are decoded to select a row; the cell array drives the sense amplifiers, gated by CS̅ and OE̅, onto the data bus.

---

## 4.2 ROM Read Operation — Timing Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as CPU or MCU
    participant DEC as Address Decoder
    participant ROM as ROM Chip
    participant BUS as Data Bus

    CPU->>DEC: Place address on A0 to An-1
    CPU->>ROM: Assert CS low and OE low
    ROM->>ROM: Internal access time tAA elapses
    ROM->>BUS: Drive valid data onto D0 to D-1
    CPU->>CPU: Sample data bus after tAA
    CPU->>ROM: Deassert OE and CS
```

**Caption:** Sequence of events during a CPU read from ROM. Note the **access time $t_{AA}$** between address valid and data valid.

---

## 4.3 ROM Programming Flow (for EPROM/EEPROM)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Place ROM in Programmer Socket] --> B[Apply VPP 12.75 V for EPROM]
    B --> C[Issue Program Command per Address]
    C --> D{All Bytes Programmed}
    D -- No --> C
    D -- Yes --> E[Verify against checksum]
    E --> F[Remove VPP and power off]
    F --> G[For EPROM: UV erase 20 to 30 min]
```

---

## 4.4 Memory Map of an Embedded System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ADDR["64 KB Address Space 0000H to FFFFH"]
        D0["0000H - 7FFFH ROM 32 KB"]
        D1["8000H - FFFFH RAM 32 KB"]
    end
    A15["Address line A15"] -->|"A15 = 0"| D0
    A15 -->|"A15 = 1"| D1
```

---

## 4.5 Floating-Gate MOSFET Cross-Section (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    G1[Control Gate] --> G2[Thin Tunnel Oxide approx 7 to 10 nm]
    G2 --> G3[Floating Gate stores charge]
    G3 --> G4[Thicker Gate Oxide approx 30 to 40 nm]
    G4 --> G5[Channel of P-substrate]
    S[Source] --> G5
    D[Drain] --> G5
    G5 --> B[Bulk P-substrate]
```

**Caption:** The floating gate is **electrically isolated** by two oxide layers. Electrons tunnel through the thin oxide to alter the threshold voltage $V_{TH}$, encoding a bit.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Q: Differentiate between volatile and non-volatile memory. Give two examples of each.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Volatile memory loses stored data when power is removed, whereas non-volatile memory retains data even without power.

| Volatile | Non-Volatile |
|---|---|
| SRAM (e.g., cache) | Mask ROM |
| DRAM (e.g., main RAM) | Flash memory |
| Used for temporary data | Used for firmware, boot code |

ROM is **non-volatile** because it stores firmware permanently.

---

### Question 2
**Q: List any three features of EPROM.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
1. **Erasable** using UV light through the quartz window (takes 20–30 minutes).
2. **Re-programmable** up to ~100 cycles.
3. **Non-volatile** — data retention > 10 years.
4. Uses **floating-gate MOSFETs** for storage.
5. Requires a **high programming voltage VPP ≈ +12.75 V**.

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module 2 Internal Choice)

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the internal architecture of a Read-Only Memory (ROM) chip. Discuss the function of each block. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare Mask ROM, PROM, EPROM, and EEPROM in terms of programming mechanism, erasability, endurance, cost, and typical applications. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(a)

A ROM chip consists of three major blocks:

1. **Address Decoder:** Receives *n*-bit address lines (A0–Aₙ₋₁) and activates exactly one of $2^n$ word lines. Implemented using AND/NAND gates or a binary decoder IC (e.g., 74LS154).

2. **Memory Cell Array:** A 2D matrix of memory cells. Each cell stores one bit via:
   - A fuse/antifuse (PROM),
   - A transistor presence/absence (Mask ROM), or
   - A floating-gate charge (EPROM/EEPROM).

3. **Output Buffer with CS̅ and OE̅ Control:** The output buffer is tri-state and is enabled only when both **CS̅ (chip select)** and **OE̅ (output enable)** are active. This allows multiple ROM/RAM chips to share a common data bus.

**Operation:**
- CPU places the address → decoder activates one word line → corresponding cells drive internal bit lines → sense amplifiers output valid data after $t_{AA}$.
- The data bus is tri-stated when CS̅ is high, preventing bus contention.

**Block Diagram (described):** Address bus → [Address Decoder] → Word lines → [Memory Cell Array] → Bit lines → [Sense Amplifiers + Output Buffer] → Data bus, gated by [CS̅ + OE̅ control logic].

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(b)

| Feature | Mask ROM | PROM | EPROM | EEPROM |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Programming | At fabrication | User, fuse blow | User, electrical | User, electrical |
| Erasing | Impossible | Impossible | UV (whole chip) | Electrical (byte) |
| Erase time | N/A | N/A | 20–30 min | 1–10 ms/byte |
| Endurance | 0 | 1 (OTP) | ~100 cycles | ~10⁶ cycles |
| Cost per bit (low vol.) | Lowest | High | Medium | High |
| Bit density | Highest | Low–Med | Medium | Medium |
| Write/Program time | N/A | ~ms | ~ms | ~ms |
| Typical application | Game cartridges, TV firmware | OTP prototypes, serial numbers | Dev boards, prototype firmware | BIOS chips, config storage |

**Conclusion:** Mask ROM is cheapest for high-volume fixed firmware; EEPROM offers byte-level electrical rewrite but at higher cost; EPROM is suited for prototyping.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the operation of a floating-gate MOSFET used in EPROM/EEPROM cells. How is data written and erased? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An embedded system uses an 8051 MCU (16 address lines). Design the memory map such that a 16 KB ROM occupies 0000H–3FFFH and a 16 KB RAM occupies 4000H–7FFFH. Provide the CS̅ logic for each. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(a)

A **floating-gate MOSFET** is an MOS transistor with an *extra gate (the floating gate)* sandwiched between the control gate and the channel, electrically isolated by two thin SiO₂ layers.

**Structure:**
- Source, Drain, Channel (P-substrate) — as in a normal MOSFET.
- **Floating gate** — completely surrounded by oxide; no electrical connection.
- **Control gate** — connected externally for programming/reading.

**Programming (Writing) — Hot-Electron Injection or Fowler-Nordheim Tunneling:**
- A high voltage (≈ 12 V) is applied between control gate and drain.
- Electrons gain kinetic energy in the channel; some surmount the Si-SiO₂ barrier (≈ 3.2 eV) and get trapped on the floating gate.
- The stored charge raises the transistor's threshold voltage $V_{TH}$.
- A cell with high $V_{TH}$ conducts weakly → reads as **'0'** (or '1', depending on convention).

**Erasing:**
- **EPROM:** UV light photons generate electron-hole pairs in the oxide, neutralizing the trapped charge. Whole chip erased in 20–30 min.
- **EEPROM:** Reverse bias across the thin tunnel oxide causes electrons to tunnel *off* the floating gate (Fowler-Nordheim tunneling), restoring low $V_{TH}$. Done electrically, byte-by-byte, in 1–10 ms.

**Reading:**
- Apply $V_{CC}$ (≈ 5 V) to control gate; sense whether the cell transistor conducts. The stored charge alters $V_{TH}$, so the cell reads as either '0' or '1'.

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Given:** 8051 MCU with 16-bit address bus (A0–A15, addressing 64 KB).
**Required:** ROM at 0000H–3FFFH (16 KB), RAM at 4000H–7FFFH (16 KB).

**Step 1 — Convert ranges to binary (analyzing A15 and A14):**

| Range | Hex | Binary A15 A14 ... A0 |
|---|---|---|
| Start of ROM | 0000H | 0 0 0000 0000 0000 |
| End of ROM | 3FFFH | 0 0 1111 1111 1111 |
| Start of RAM | 4000H | 0 1 0000 0000 0000 |
| End of RAM | 7FFFH | 0 1 1111 1111 1111 |

**Step 2 — Identify constant bits.**
- **ROM** region: A15 = 0, A14 = 0.
- **RAM** region: A15 = 0, A14 = 1.

**Step 3 — Derive CS̅ logic using a 74HC139 (2-to-4 decoder) or 74HC138.**

Using A15 and A14 as decoder inputs (with A0–A13 being the 14 internal address lines within each 16 KB block):

$$\begin{aligned}
CS_{ROM} &= \overline{\overline{A_{15}} \cdot \overline{A_{14}}} \quad \text{(active when A15=0, A14=0)} \\
CS_{RAM} &= \overline{\overline{A_{15}} \cdot A_{14}} \quad \text{(active when A15=0, A14=1)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Memory Map.**

| Address Range | Device | A15 | A14 | CS Logic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0000H–3FFFH | ROM (16 KB) | 0 | 0 | $\overline{A_{15}} \cdot \overline{A_{14}}$ |
| 4000H–7FFFH | RAM (16 KB) | 0 | 1 | $\overline{A_{15}} \cdot A_{14}$ |
| 8000H–FFFFH | Unused (A15 = 1) | 1 | x | No chip selected |

**Conclusion:** Two address lines (A15 and A14) suffice to decode four 16 KB regions of the 64 KB space, cleanly mapping ROM and RAM.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the active-low convention:** CS̅ and OE̅ are *active LOW*. Students often draw them as active HIGH, losing 2 marks.
> 2. **Wrong base in log₂:** When computing $n = \log_2 W$, students use $W = 64$ (not $64 \times 1024$) and get $n = 6$ instead of $n = 16$. Always check if the question states 64 KB or 64 K words.
> 3. **Confusing PROM with EPROM:** PROM is **one-time programmable (OTP)** with NO erase mechanism. EPROM has UV erase. Marks deducted if these are mixed.
> 4. **Ignoring VPP:** For EPROM/EEPROM programming, **VPP** must be specified; otherwise the answer is incomplete.
> 5. **Bus contention:** Students forget that the output buffer must be tri-stated when CS̅ is deasserted. Mention "tri-state outputs" explicitly.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **ROM = Read-Only Memory** — non-volatile, programmed once (or rarely), read many times.
- ✅ Five major variants: **Mask ROM → PROM → EPROM → EEPROM → Flash**.
- ✅ **Mask ROM**: cheapest per bit, programmed at fab, used for high-volume fixed firmware.
- ✅ **PROM**: one-time programmable by user via fuse/antifuse.
- ✅ **EPROM**: erasable by **UV light (20–30 min)**, programmable electrically, ~100 cycles.
- ✅ **EEPROM**: electrically erasable **byte-by-byte**, ~10⁶ cycles, used for configuration storage.
- ✅ **Flash**: electrically erasable **block-by-block**, ~10⁵ cycles, highest density (used in SSDs, MCU firmware).
- ✅ Modern ROMs use **floating-gate MOSFETs** storing charge via **Fowler-Nordheim tunneling**.
- ✅ Number of address lines: $n = \log_2 W$ where $W$ = number of words.
- ✅ Total capacity: $C = W \times B$ bits.
- ✅ Read timing parameters: $t_{AA}$, $t_{ACE}$, $t_{OE}$ — must satisfy $t_{AA} \geq t_{ACE} + t_{OE}$.
- ✅ ROM interfacing requires **address decoder** (e.g., 74HC138) for CS̅ generation.
- ✅ **CS̅** enables the chip; **OE̅** enables the output buffer — both **active LOW**.
- ✅ Memory mapping uses constant bits (e.g., A15 = 0 selects lower half) to generate CS̅.
- ✅ **VPP** (12.75 V for EPROM) required during programming only; GND or float during read.
- ✅ KTU favourite topics: comparison tables, address line calculations, CS̅ logic derivation, floating-gate explanation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory According to the Type of Interface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET695 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Memory According to the Type of Interface

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the architecture of microprocessors and microcontrollers, **Memory Interface** defines the electrical and logical convention used by the CPU/MCU to read from and write to memory (and peripheral) devices. According to the **type of interface**, memory in an embedded system is broadly classified into two categories:

1. **Memory-Mapped Interface (Memory-Mapped I/O)** — Where memory devices and I/O registers reside in the *same unified address space*. The CPU uses the same instructions (e.g., `LDR`, `STR` in ARM, or `MOV` in 8051) to access both memory and peripheral registers.
2. **I/O-Mapped Interface (Port-Mapped I/O / Isolated I/O)** — Where memory and I/O devices occupy *two physically and logically separate address spaces*, addressed using dedicated control signals (e.g., `MEMR/`, `MEMW/` vs. `IOR/`, `IOW/` in x86, or `RD/`, `WR/` with separate enable in some MCUs).

Additionally, memories themselves are classified by their **physical/logical interface protocol** into **Parallel Interface Memory** and **Serial Interface Memory**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):**
> The KTU PEAET695 Module 2 emphasizes how embedded memory subsystems are accessed by the processor. The two interface paradigms — *Memory-Mapped* and *I/O-Mapped* — directly influence **instruction selection**, **address decoding complexity**, **bus utilization**, and **system throughput**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large residential apartment complex with two different postal delivery systems:

> **Analogy — The "Dual-Pin-Code City":**
> - In **Memory-Mapped I/O**, the city uses *one combined pin-code system* for both houses (memory) and post offices (I/O devices). The postman uses the same vehicle, the same letterbox key, and the same address format to deliver everywhere. You write a letter to either using the same syntax.
> - In **I/O-Mapped I/O (Isolated I/O)**, the city has *two completely separate addressing systems*: one for residential houses and a totally different one (like a separate PO Box number) for post offices. The postman has **two different keys**, **two vehicles** (or routes), and the sender must explicitly specify which system they are using.
>
> The "combined" system is simpler but wastes a portion of the address space on offices. The "isolated" system preserves the entire address range for houses but requires extra logic to differentiate.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bold)

The following engineering metrics are universally used when classifying memory by interface type:

- **Address Space Size = 2ⁿ locations**, where **n = number of address lines**.
- **Memory Capacity = 2ⁿ × W bits**, where **W = data bus width in bits**.
- **Memory Access Time (tₐ)** — measured in nanoseconds (ns) for SRAM, microseconds for Flash/EEPROM.
- **Bus Width** — typically **8-bit, 16-bit, 32-bit, or 64-bit**.
- **I/O Pin Count** — number of physical pins required on the MCU/processor to interface the memory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Memory-Mapped I/O:** A system architecture in which I/O devices are assigned addresses within the same memory address space as program/data memory, so the same load/store instructions access them.
>
> **Definition — I/O-Mapped I/O:** A system architecture in which I/O devices have a separate, dedicated address space accessed via special instructions and dedicated bus control signals.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Classification of Memory by Interface Type

#### 2.1.1 Memory-Mapped Interface (Unified Address Space)

**Operational Logic:**
- The processor generates an address on the address bus.
- A single decoder enables either memory or I/O depending on the address range.
- The **same control signals** (`RD/`, `WR/` in a generic MCU) are used for both.
- The full address space (e.g., **2¹⁶ = 65,536 locations** for 16 address lines) is **shared** between memory and I/O.

**Why this approach:**
- Simplifies instruction set — only one addressing mode needed.
- Allows any data manipulation instruction (arithmetic, logical, bitwise) to act on I/O registers.
- Standard in **ARM Cortex-M, 8051, AVR, PIC** architectures.

**How it works (steps):**
1. CPU places an address on the address bus.
2. Address decoder (logic gates / decoder IC like **74LS138**) activates either the memory chip-select (`CS`) or the peripheral chip-select.
3. Data is transferred over the shared data bus.
4. The same `RD/` and `WR/` signals govern both transfers.

#### 2.1.2 I/O-Mapped Interface (Isolated Address Space)

**Operational Logic:**
- Two distinct address spaces exist: **Memory Address Space** and **I/O Address Space**.
- Dedicated control lines like **`M/IO#`** or **`IOR#/IOW#`** (x86 terminology) tell external logic whether the current cycle is a memory cycle or an I/O cycle.
- Special instructions (e.g., `IN`, `OUT` in x86; `INP`, `OUTP` in Z80) are required.
- Full address range (e.g., **64 KB**) is available for memory, and a separate range (e.g., **port address space**) is available for I/O.

**Why this approach:**
- **Preserves the entire memory address range** for memory — no wastage on I/O addresses.
- Faster I/O instruction decoding (often single-byte opcodes).
- Used in legacy **Intel 8086, 8085 (with partial), Z80, Motorola 6800**.

#### 2.1.3 Parallel vs Serial Interface Memory

| Aspect | Parallel Interface Memory | Serial Interface Memory |
|---|---|---|
| Data transfer | Multiple bits simultaneously (4, 8, 16 bits at a time) | One bit at a time (or a few bits) |
| Pin count | **High** (e.g., 28+ pins for 8-bit SRAM) | **Low** (4–8 pins typical) |
| Speed | **High throughput** | Lower throughput but adequate |
| Examples | SRAM (62xxx), DRAM, Parallel NOR Flash, EEPROM | SPI Flash (25xxx), I²C EEPROM (24xxx), QSPI |
| Used in | External memory expansion, data buffers | Small form-factor embedded boards |

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are fundamental to **memory interfacing** numerical problems expected in KTU ESE.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression / Value | Engineering Unit | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Memory locations from address lines | $N = 2^{n}$ | locations | n = number of address lines |
| 2 | Total memory capacity | $C = 2^{n} \times W$ | bits | W = data width in bits |
| 3 | Memory capacity in bytes | $C_{bytes} = \dfrac{2^{n} \times W}{8}$ | Bytes | Divide by 8 |
| 4 | Address range (decimal) | $\text{End} = \text{Start} + 2^{n} - 1$ | decimal | Inclusive range |
| 5 | Address lines required | $n = \log_{2}(N)$ | lines | N = number of locations |
| 6 | Decoder inputs needed | $d = \lceil \log_{2}(m) \rceil$ | inputs | m = number of chips |
| 7 | Memory-Mapped I/O addr range | $\text{Mem} \cup \text{I/O} \leq 2^{n}$ | locations | Shared |
| 8 | I/O-Mapped I/O isolation | Separate decoder enabled by $\overline{M/\overline{IO}}$ | — | Control signal |
| 9 | Address decoding (partial) | Uses **A₁₅ … A_(n–k)** | lines | Top k lines |
| 10 | Address decoding (full) | Uses **all n lines** | lines | No address aliasing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Aliasing Rule (KTU favorite):** When fewer address lines are used for decoding than total available (partial decoding), the same memory location appears at multiple addresses — this is **address mirroring/aliasing** and **loses marks** in valuation if not explicitly stated.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Memory-Mapped I/O** dominates modern **ARM Cortex-M, RISC-V, ESP32** designs because it simplifies compilers and allows peripheral registers to be treated as `struct` fields in C.
- **I/O-Mapped I/O** is still found in legacy industrial controllers, automotive ECU pre-processors (e.g., older Motorola HC11/HC12), and certain DSPs.
- **Serial Interface Memory** is the **backbone of IoT devices** — virtually every ESP32/STM32 design uses SPI Flash for firmware storage and I²C EEPROM for parameter retention.
- Choosing the right interface impacts **PCB area, power consumption, BOM cost, and firmware complexity**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Memory-Mapped vs I/O-Mapped Comparison

The following matrix is the **single most important analytical tool** for answering KTU theory questions on this topic.

| Parameter | Memory-Mapped I/O | I/O-Mapped I/O (Isolated) |
|---|---|---|
| Address space | Unified (single) | Dual (separate) |
| Instructions used | Same as memory (`LDR`, `STR`, `MOV`) | Special (`IN`, `OUT`) |
| Control signals | Same `RD/`, `WR/` | Separate `IOR/`, `IOW/` (or `M/IO#`) |
| Address range wastage | Yes (some range given to I/O) | No (full range for memory) |
| Memory capacity | Reduced | Full |
| Arithmetic on I/O data | Yes (direct) | No (must move to memory first) |
| Flexibility | High | Low |
| Decoder complexity | One decoder for both | Two decoders or gated decoder |
| Speed of I/O | Slightly slower (memory cycle) | Faster (dedicated cycle) |
| Examples | ARM, 8051, AVR, PIC | 8086, Z80, 8085 (partial) |
| Address pins | All n pins used | All n pins used (same) |
| Typical use case | Modern embedded MCUs | Legacy CPUs, DSPs |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Calculating Memory Capacity from Address Lines

#### Problem Statement
An 8051-based embedded system uses **external memory interfacing**. The system has **16 address lines (A₀–A₁₅)** and an **8-bit data bus**. Calculate the total memory capacity if the system uses **memory-mapped I/O**, and 25% of the address space is allocated to I/O devices.

#### Step 1 — Identify Given Parameters

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of address lines, } n &= 16 \\
\text{Data bus width, } W &= 8 \text{ bits} \\
\text{I/O allocation} &= 25\% \text{ of total address space}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2 — Compute Total Addressable Locations

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{total} &= 2^{n} \\
          &= 2^{16} \\
          &= 65536 \text{ locations}
\end{aligned}
$$

The total addressable locations are **65,536**.

#### Step 3 — Compute Memory Capacity in Bits (Full Space)

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{full} &= N_{total} \times W \\
         &= 65536 \times 8 \\
         &= 524288 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — Convert to Bytes (Division by 8)

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{full\_bytes} &= \dfrac{524288}{8} \\
                &= 65536 \text{ Bytes} \\
                &= 64 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5 — Compute Effective Memory (after removing 25% for I/O)

Since 25% is allocated to I/O, **75% remains for memory**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{memory} &= C_{full\_bytes} \times 0.75 \\
           &= 65536 \times 0.75 \\
           &= 49152 \text{ Bytes} \\
           &= 48 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 6 — Compute Effective I/O Space

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{IO} &= C_{full\_bytes} \times 0.25 \\
       &= 65536 \times 0.25 \\
       &= 16384 \text{ Bytes} \\
       &= 16 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 7 — Compute the Memory Address Range

If memory occupies the **lower 75%** of the address space:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Start address} &= 0x0000 \\
\text{End address}   &= (C_{memory} - 1) \\
                     &= 49152 - 1 \\
                     &= 49151 \\
                     &= \text{0xBFFF}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 8 — Compute the I/O Address Range

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Start address} &= 0xC000 \\
\text{End address}   &= 0xFFFF \\
\text{Range size}    &= 0xFFFF - 0xC000 + 1 \\
                     &= 16384 \\
                     &= 16 \text{ KB} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: Address Lines Required for a Given Memory Size

#### Problem Statement
Design an embedded memory subsystem with **32 KB of SRAM** and **8 KB of ROM** using a **16-bit data bus** with memory-mapped I/O. Determine the number of address lines required.

#### Step 1 — Compute Locations per Chip

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{SRAM} &= \dfrac{32 \text{ KB} \times 8}{W} = \dfrac{32768 \times 8}{16} = 16384 \text{ locations} \\
N_{ROM}  &= \dfrac{8 \text{ KB} \times 8}{W} = \dfrac{8192 \times 8}{16} = 4096 \text{ locations}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2 — Compute Address Lines per Chip

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{SRAM} &= \log_{2}(16384) = \log_{2}(2^{14}) = 14 \\
n_{ROM}  &= \log_{2}(4096)  = \log_{2}(2^{12}) = 12
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Compute Total Address Lines

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{total} &= \log_{2}(N_{SRAM} + N_{ROM}) \\
          &= \log_{2}(16384 + 4096) \\
          &= \log_{2}(20480) \\
          &\approx 14.32
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — Round Up to Next Integer

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{total} &= \lceil 14.32 \rceil = 15 \text{ address lines}
\end{aligned}
$$

So **15 address lines (A₀–A₁₄)** are needed, with one line used in chip selection logic.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Memory-Mapped I/O Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU["CPU / MCU Core"] -->|Address Bus| DEC["Address Decoder 74LS138"]
    DEC -->|CS1| MEM1["Program Memory ROM"]
    DEC -->|CS2| MEM2["Data Memory SRAM"]
    DEC -->|CS3| IO1["Peripheral I/O 1 UART"]
    DEC -->|CS4| IO2["Peripheral I/O 2 GPIO"]
    CPU -->|Shared Data Bus| MEM1
    CPU -->|Shared Data Bus| MEM2
    CPU -->|Shared Data Bus| IO1
    CPU -->|Shared Data Bus| IO2
    CPU -->|RD WR Control| MEM1
    CPU -->|RD WR Control| MEM2
    CPU -->|RD WR Control| IO1
    CPU -->|RD WR Control| IO2
```

> **Interpretation:** A single decoder (`74LS138`) decodes the high-order address lines. Each output line (`CS1`, `CS2`, `CS3`, `CS4`) activates either a memory chip or an I/O peripheral. **The same `RD/` and `WR/` control signals** drive all four. This is **memory-mapped I/O**.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — I/O-Mapped I/O (Isolated) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU2["CPU with M/IO pin"] -->|Address Bus| LOG["Control Logic Gate"]
    LOG -->|MEM CS| MEMDEC["Memory Decoder 74LS138"]
    LOG -->|IO CS| IODEC["I/O Decoder 74LS138"]
    MEMDEC -->|CS1| MEM3["ROM Memory"]
    MEMDEC -->|CS2| MEM4["SRAM Memory"]
    IODEC -->|CS3| IO3["I/O Port A"]
    IODEC -->|CS4["CS4"]| IO4["I/O Port B"]
    CPU2 -->|IOR IOW signals| IODEC
    CPU2 -->|MEMR MEMW signals| MEMDEC
```

> **Interpretation:** The `M/IO#` line from the CPU **enables only one decoder** at a time. Memory and I/O share the address lines but **not the control signals** and **not the address space**. The I/O decoder is only active when `M/IO# = 0`.

### 4.3 Classification Tree of Memory by Interface Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT["Memory Classification by Interface"]
    ROOT --> A["Memory Mapped Interface"]
    ROOT --> B["I/O Mapped Interface Isolated"]
    ROOT --> C["Physical Interface Type"]
    A --> A1["Single Unified Address Space"]
    A --> A2["Same RD WR Control Signals"]
    A --> A3["ARM 8051 AVR PIC"]
    B --> B1["Separate Address Spaces"]
    B --> B2["Dedicated IOR IOW Signals"]
    B --> B3["8086 Z80 8085"]
    C --> C1["Parallel Interface"]
    C --> C2["Serial Interface"]
    C1 --> C1a["SRAM 62xxx Series"]
    C1 --> C1b["DRAM"]
    C1 --> C1c["Parallel NOR Flash"]
    C2 --> C2a["SPI Flash 25xxx"]
    C2 --> C2b["I2C EEPROM 24xxx"]
    C2 --> C2c["QSPI OSPI"]
```

### 4.4 Memory Interfacing Block Diagram — 8051 Style

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M["8051 MCU"] -->|P0 Address Data Multiplexed| LATCH["Address Latch 74LS373"]
    LATCH -->|A0 A7 Lower Address| ROM["ROM 8KB"]
    LATCH -->|A0 A7 Lower Address| RAM["SRAM 8KB"]
    M -->|P2 Upper Address A8 A15| ROM
    M -->|P2 Upper Address A8 A15| RAM
    M -->|ALE Signal| LATCH
    M -->|PSEN to ROM OE| ROM
    M -->|RD WR to SRAM| RAM
    IO["Peripheral I/O Mapped"] -.->|Memory Mapped Same Bus| LATCH
```

> **Interpretation:** In the 8051, the lower address and data are multiplexed on Port 0. The `ALE` signal latches the address into the `74LS373` latch. ROM uses `PSEN#` for read enable, while SRAM uses `RD#`/`WR#`. External peripherals are accessed via the same bus — this is the **classic memory-mapped architecture**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. Differentiate between memory-mapped I/O and I/O-mapped I/O. List any three points.**

**Model Answer:**
| Parameter | Memory-Mapped I/O | I/O-Mapped I/O |
|---|---|---|
| Address space | Single unified address space | Separate address space for I/O |
| Instructions | Uses same memory access instructions (`MOV`, `LDR`) | Uses special instructions (`IN`, `OUT`) |
| Control signals | Common `RD#` and `WR#` | Separate `IOR#`, `IOW#` and `MEMR#`, `MEMW#` |
| Memory address range | Reduced (shared with I/O) | Full memory range available |
| Arithmetic on I/O data | Possible directly | Not possible directly |
| Example architectures | ARM, 8051, AVR | 8086, Z80 |

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**Q2. Explain what is meant by "memory according to the type of interface." Mention the two broad classifications.**

**Model Answer:**
Memory in an embedded system is classified based on the type of interface used to connect it with the processor. The two broad classifications are:

1. **Memory-Mapped Interface (Unified Address Space):** Memory and I/O devices share the same address space and are accessed using the same set of instructions and control signals. Example: 8051, ARM Cortex-M.

2. **I/O-Mapped Interface (Isolated Address Space):** Memory and I/O devices have separate address spaces, accessed through dedicated control signals and special instructions. Example: 8086 microprocessor.

Memory can also be classified by its physical interface as **Parallel Interface Memory** (e.g., SRAM, DRAM) or **Serial Interface Memory** (e.g., SPI Flash, I²C EEPROM).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **memory-mapped I/O** scheme of interfacing memory and I/O devices with a microprocessor. List its **advantages and disadvantages**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **I/O-mapped I/O** scheme with a block diagram. Compare it with memory-mapped I/O on **five key parameters**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) Solution:**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Memory-mapped I/O is a scheme in which I/O devices are treated as memory locations. They are assigned addresses within the same memory address space.
- **Block diagram (2 marks):** CPU → Address Bus → Address Decoder → enables either Memory or I/O via separate chip-selects. Shared data bus and shared `RD#`/`WR#` signals.
- **Working (2 marks):** The CPU places the address on the bus. The decoder activates the appropriate chip-select based on the address range. The same `RD#` and `WR#` signals are used. Data is transferred on the shared data bus.
- **Advantages (1 mark):**
  1. Same instructions access memory and I/O — simple programming.
  2. Any data manipulation instruction can act on I/O registers.
  3. Full address range flexibility.
  4. No special I/O instructions needed.
- **Disadvantages (1 mark):**
  1. Memory address space is reduced (some range consumed by I/O).
  2. I/O addresses cannot be distinguished from memory addresses by software.

**Part (b) Solution:**
- **Definition (1 mark):** I/O-mapped I/O (isolated I/O) uses a separate address space for I/O devices, accessed through dedicated control signals and special instructions.
- **Block diagram (2 marks):** CPU with `M/IO#` line → Control Logic → enables either Memory Decoder OR I/O Decoder. Different control signal sets (`MEMR#`/`MEMW#` vs. `IOR#`/`IOW#`).
- **Five key comparisons (≈3 marks):** Cover (1) address space, (2) instructions used, (3) control signals, (4) memory wastage, (5) speed/flexibility.
- **Conclusion (1 mark):** I/O-mapped I/O is preferred in legacy CPUs like 8086, while memory-mapped I/O is standard in modern microcontrollers.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]`

**(a)** Explain the classification of **memory based on the type of interface**. Discuss in detail **parallel interface memory** and **serial interface memory** with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An embedded system uses a **16-bit address bus** and **8-bit data bus**. The system designer allocates **32 KB for ROM**, **16 KB for SRAM**, and the remaining address space for memory-mapped I/O devices. Calculate:
   (i) The starting and ending addresses of each memory region.
   (ii) The total number of address lines reserved for I/O.
   (iii) The percentage of address space used for I/O. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) Solution:**
- **Introduction (1 mark):** Memory in embedded systems is classified based on the physical/logical interface used to communicate with the processor.
- **Parallel Interface Memory (3 marks):**
  - Transfers multiple bits simultaneously over a parallel data bus.
  - Requires more pins (e.g., 28+ pins for 8-bit SRAM like 6264).
  - Examples: SRAM (62xxx series — 6264, 62256), DRAM, Parallel NOR Flash (29xxx series).
  - Advantages: High speed, simultaneous data transfer.
  - Disadvantages: Higher pin count, larger PCB area.
- **Serial Interface Memory (3 marks):**
  - Transfers one bit at a time over a serial protocol.
  - Requires fewer pins (e.g., 4–8 pins for SPI/I²C).
  - Examples: SPI Flash (W25Q series — 25xxx), I²C EEPROM (24Cxx series), QSPI Flash.
  - Advantages: Low pin count, smaller PCB, suitable for compact IoT devices.
  - Disadvantages: Lower throughput, requires protocol handling.

**Part (b) Solution:**

Given: Address bus = 16 lines → $N = 2^{16} = 65,536$ locations. Data bus = 8 bits.

**Step 1 — Convert Memory Sizes to Address Locations:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{ROM}  &= \dfrac{32 \text{ KB}}{1 \text{ Byte}} = 32768 \text{ locations} \\
N_{SRAM} &= \dfrac{16 \text{ KB}}{1 \text{ Byte}} = 16384 \text{ locations}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Address Ranges:**

| Region | Start Address | End Address | Size (Locations) |
|---|---|---|---|
| ROM | `0x0000` | `0x7FFF` | 32,768 |
| SRAM | `0x8000` | `0xBFFF` | 16,384 |
| I/O (Memory-Mapped) | `0xC000` | `0xFFFF` | 16,384 |

**Step 3 — Calculate Total I/O Locations:**
$$
N_{IO} = 65536 - 32768 - 16384 = 16384 \text{ locations} = 16 \text{ KB}
$$

**Step 4 — Address Lines for I/O:**
$$
n_{IO} = \log_{2}(16384) = \log_{2}(2^{14}) = 14 \text{ address lines}
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage of Address Space for I/O:**
$$
P_{IO} = \dfrac{16384}{65536} \times 100\% = 25\%
$$

**Final Answer:**
- ROM: `0x0000` to `0x7FFF`
- SRAM: `0x8000` to `0xBFFF`
- I/O: `0xC000` to `0xFFFF`
- Address lines reserved for I/O: **14**
- I/O percentage: **25%**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU Valuation Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to mention the control signal difference** between the two schemes. Examiners specifically look for `RD#`/`WR#` vs. `IOR#`/`IOW#` distinction.
> 2. **Confusing "memory-mapped I/O" with "memory-mapped memory"** — students often write only about memory chips. Always clarify that **both memory and I/O share the same address space**.
> 3. **Not stating the formula $N = 2^n$ explicitly** in numerical problems. Many students compute correctly but lose 1 mark for not showing the formula.
> 4. **Ignoring "address mirroring"** in partial address decoding. If the problem uses partial decoding, mention that the same memory may appear at multiple addresses.
> 5. **Mixing up parallel and serial interface memory examples.** SPI Flash is **serial**, SRAM 62xxx is **parallel** — students often swap these in Part A.
> 6. **Not converting KB to bytes** in numerical problems. Always show: 1 KB = 1024 Bytes = $2^{10}$ Bytes.
> 7. **Omitting the block diagram** in 7-mark questions. Even a hand-drawn-style ASCII/Mermaid diagram is **mandatory** for full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memory According to the Type of Interface**

- [x] Memory interface type classifies how CPU communicates with memory/I/O devices.
- [x] **Memory-Mapped I/O** = single unified address space, same instructions (`LDR`/`STR`/`MOV`), same control signals (`RD#`/`WR#`).
- [x] **I/O-Mapped I/O** = separate address spaces, special instructions (`IN`/`OUT`), separate control signals (`IOR#`/`IOW#`).
- [x] Memory-mapped I/O is used in **ARM Cortex-M, 8051, AVR, PIC**.
- [x] I/O-mapped I/O is used in **8086, Z80, 8085 (partial)**.
- [x] **Parallel Interface Memory** = high speed, more pins (e.g., 62xxx SRAM, 29xxx Flash).
- [x] **Serial Interface Memory** = fewer pins, lower speed (e.g., 25xxx SPI Flash, 24xxx I²C EEPROM).
- [x] **Key formula:** $N = 2^{n}$ locations; $C = 2^{n} \times W$ bits.
- [x] **Address decoding:** full decoding (no aliasing) vs. partial decoding (aliasing possible).
- [x] Memory-mapped I/O **reduces** memory address range; I/O-mapped I/O **preserves** it.
- [x] Arithmetic and logical operations on I/O data are **direct in memory-mapped**, **indirect in I/O-mapped**.
- [x] **Modern MCUs prefer memory-mapped I/O** for compiler simplicity and code reuse.
- [x] Always show **block diagrams + comparison tables** in 7-mark questions for full marks.
- [x] Remember 1 KB = 1024 Bytes = $2^{10}$ Bytes; 1 MB = $2^{20}$ Bytes.
- [x] The `74LS138` (3-to-8 decoder) is the most common address decoder IC in KTU numericals.
- [x] In 8051, **Port 0** carries multiplexed address/data, **Port 2** carries upper address; **ALE** latches the address.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory Shadowing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET695 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Memory Shadowing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Memory Shadowing** is a memory-mapping technique employed in embedded systems wherein a single physical memory address is intentionally aliased — that is, the same logical address range is made to refer to **two physically distinct memory devices** simultaneously. In the canonical implementation, the address space is shared between a non-volatile memory (typically **ROM / Flash**) and a volatile read-write memory (typically **RAM / SRAM**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Memory Shadowing is the architectural technique where identical addresses in the processor's memory map point to **both ROM (slow, non-volatile) and RAM (fast, volatile)**. During reset, the system executes from ROM; the boot/initialization routine then copies the contents from ROM into the overlaid RAM, and subsequent fetches are serviced from the faster RAM. The ROM "shadow" remains hidden, hence the name "shadowing."

The two memory devices occupy the **same address range** but are differentiated by the processor's bus cycle or memory access phase:
- **Reset/Boot Phase:** The memory controller routes read accesses to **ROM**.
- **Runtime Phase (after shadowing is established):** The memory controller routes read accesses to **RAM**, while the ROM remains connected but is effectively "shadowed" by the contents now loaded into RAM.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stencil & Chalkboard" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a professor who has prepared lecture notes on a **stencil (ROM — read-only, durable)**. Each morning, the teaching assistant **traces the stencil onto the classroom chalkboard (RAM — writable, fast)**. Once the chalkboard has the full copy, the professor delivers the lecture by reading from the **chalkboard** (fast, easy to annotate, easy to update) instead of the stencil. The stencil stays intact as a permanent master copy, but the **working copy on the chalkboard is what is actively used**.

In this analogy:
- **Stencil** = ROM/Flash (master, non-volatile, slow to access directly in some cases)
- **Chalkboard** = RAM (working copy, fast, volatile)
- **Tracing action** = boot-time copy routine (the shadowing operation)
- **Classroom** = the shared address window where both stencil and chalkboard coexist

### 1.3 Why Memory Shadowing is Used in Embedded Systems

| Motivation | Engineering Rationale |
|---|---|
| **Speed** | On-chip SRAM is typically **5x–10x faster** than external Flash/ROM at the same bus width. |
| **Code Modification** | RAM allows self-modifying code, patching, and runtime updates impossible in ROM. |
| **Power Optimization** | Fetching from low-power SRAM reduces dynamic power vs. active Flash reads. |
| **Determinism** | Zero-wait-state SRAM access yields **deterministic instruction fetch timing** for hard real-time tasks. |
| **In-System Programming (ISP)** | Shadow RAM can hold new firmware during in-field upgrades before commit to Flash. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Keyword:** The phrase *"the contents of ROM are copied into RAM at startup so that subsequent execution occurs from the faster RAM"* is worth **direct 2–3 marks** in the KTU ESE. Memorize this phrasing.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics (Bolded Constants & Numbers)

- Typical ROM-to-RAM copy happens within the first **100 µs – 5 ms** of boot, depending on image size.
- Common shadow window size in 8/16-bit MCUs (8051, HC11, MSP430): **4 KB – 64 KB**.
- In 32-bit ARM Cortex-M systems, shadow regions may cover **entire vector table + startup code** (often first **512 bytes – 4 KB**).
- Zero-wait-state SRAM access is typically **1 CPU clock cycle** vs. Flash's **2–4 wait states** at the same clock.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How Memory Shadowing Works

The mechanism can be decomposed into **five logical phases** that the KTU examiner expects you to enumerate:

**Phase 1 — Power-On Reset (POR)**
- The CPU's reset vector points to a location in the **ROM address space**.
- The memory controller is configured such that the shared address window is **decoded to ROM** by default.
- A hardware reset signal initializes the Program Counter (PC) to a fixed address (e.g., `0x00000000`).

**Phase 2 — Vector Table Initialization**
- The CPU reads the **initial stack pointer value** and the **reset handler address** from the vector table located in ROM.
- Execution begins at the reset handler entry point in ROM.

**Phase 3 — Shadow Copy (The Heart of the Technique)**
- The startup/boot code (often written in assembly for size efficiency) executes a **block-memory copy** loop.
- Source: ROM address range, e.g., `0x00000000` to `0x0000FFFF`.
- Destination: RAM address range, e.g., `0x20000000` to `0x2000FFFF`.
- After this copy, **the bit-for-bit image of the ROM code now exists in RAM**.

**Phase 4 — Memory Remap (Optional but Common)**
- A memory remap register (or hardware strap pin) is toggled.
- The shared address window is now **decoded to RAM** instead of ROM.
- From this point onward, every instruction fetch from this window returns the **RAM contents**, which are an exact replica of ROM.

**Phase 5 — Continued Execution from RAM**
- The CPU jumps (or simply continues) into the now-shadowed region.
- All subsequent instruction fetches are served from the **zero-wait-state SRAM**.
- The ROM remains physically connected and addressable, but its contents are never read again from the shadow window unless explicit remap occurs.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why copy at all?** Because we want the **speed of RAM** and the **durability of ROM** — shadowing gives us both simultaneously.
- **Why a block-copy and not DMA?** In small MCUs, DMA may not be available; even when it is, the copy must complete **before any code in the shadowed region is executed** — a simple polled loop guarantees ordering.
- **Why a memory remap?** Without remap, both ROM and RAM would drive the bus simultaneously, causing bus contention. Remap ensures **only one device responds** at any given cycle.

### 2.3 Variants of Memory Shadowing

> [!NOTE]
> **Variant A — Full Shadow:** The **entire ROM image** is copied to RAM. Common in 8051, ARM7, and many DSPs.
>
> **Variant B — Partial Shadow:** Only the **performance-critical routines** (e.g., interrupt service routines, inner loops) are shadowed. Common in automotive ECUs and bootloaders.
>
> **Variant C — Vector-Table Shadow:** Only the **vector table** (typically 256–1024 bytes) is copied to RAM for fast interrupt dispatch. Common in ARM Cortex-M systems.
>
> **Variant D — Dynamic Shadow:** Shadowing is **enabled/disabled at runtime** based on power mode or task priority.

### 2.4 Drawbacks and Engineering Trade-offs

| Drawback | Mitigation in Practice |
|---|---|
| Requires **extra SRAM** (often 1:1 with ROM size) | Use partial shadowing to minimize SRAM cost |
| **Boot time increases** due to copy operation | Optimize copy routine using word/dword-aligned DMA |
| **Code complexity** — startup code must be bug-free | Use vendor-supplied startup files as templates |
| **Power loss during copy** could leave inconsistent state | Copy critical sections first; use checksums |
| **Bus contention** if remap is forgotten | Hardware memory mapping units (MMU/MPU) prevent this |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Address mapping | $A_{ROM} = A_{RAM} = A_{shadow}$ | bytes | Same logical address |
| 2 | Copy time (polled loop) | $T_{copy} = \dfrac{N \cdot t_{byte}}{f_{CPU}}$ | seconds | $N$ = bytes to copy, $t_{byte}$ = cycles per byte |
| 3 | Copy time (DMA) | $T_{copy}^{DMA} = \dfrac{N}{B_{DMA} \cdot f_{DMA}}$ | seconds | $B_{DMA}$ = bytes per DMA cycle |
| 4 | Effective access time after shadowing | $T_{eff} = t_{SRAM} = 1$ wait state typically | cycles | Vs. ROM $T_{ROM} = 2$–$4$ wait states |
| 5 | Speedup factor | $S = \dfrac{T_{ROM}}{T_{SRAM}}$ | dimensionless | Typically $S \in [2, 8]$ |
| 6 | Memory overhead | $M_{overhead} = \dfrac{S_{RAM}^{shadow}}{S_{ROM}}$ | ratio | Often $M_{overhead} = 1$ for full shadow |
| 7 | Wait-state ratio | $WS_{ratio} = \dfrac{WS_{ROM}}{WS_{SRAM}}$ | dimensionless | Lower is better after shadowing |
| 8 | Power per fetch | $P_{fetch} = C \cdot V^{2} \cdot f$ | watts | SRAM has lower $C$ than Flash |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When a question says *"explain the role of memory shadowing in fast execution"*, your answer **must include** the word **"overlap"** or **"alias"** and reference the **boot-time copy** from ROM to RAM. This phrasing pattern has appeared in past KTU supplementary exams.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECUs (AUTOSAR):** The bootloader in Flash is shadowed to RAM to allow **fast and safe firmware updates** during vehicle operation.
- **Mobile SoCs (e.g., Qualcomm, Apple A-series):** Critical boot ROM portions are mirrored in on-chip SRAM for **secure boot** acceleration.
- **DSP Systems (TI TMS320, ADI SHARC):** Entire firmware is shadowed to internal SRAM because external memory is too slow for real-time DSP loops.
- **IoT Edge Devices (ARM Cortex-M0+):** Vector table + first-level interrupt handlers are shadowed to achieve **deterministic interrupt latency** under 10 µs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Boot-Time Copy Duration

This derivation is **directly applicable to numerical problems** in KTU ESE Module 2.

**Problem Setup (typical KTU 2-mark sub-question):**
> *"An embedded system has a 32 KB ROM and 16 KB RAM in a shadowed configuration. The CPU runs at 50 MHz and each ROM-to-RAM byte copy takes 4 clock cycles. Calculate the boot-time copy duration."*

**Given:**
- ROM image size: $N = 32 \text{ KB} = 32 \times 1024 \text{ bytes} = 32768 \text{ bytes}$
