- Clock frequency: $f_{CPU} = 50 \text{ MHz} = 50 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$
- Cycles per byte: $n_c = 4 \text{ cycles/byte}$

**Step 1 — Total clock cycles required for the copy operation.**

$$
C_{total} = N \cdot n_c
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
C_{total} = 32768 \text{ bytes} \cdot 4 \text{ cycles/byte}
$$

$$
C_{total} = 131072 \text{ cycles}
$$

This is the total number of clock cycles the CPU will spend executing the copy loop.

**Step 2 — Convert clock cycles to time using the CPU clock period.**

The clock period is the reciprocal of the frequency:

$$
T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{CPU}} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}} = 20 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply the total cycles by the clock period to get wall-clock copy time.**

$$
T_{copy} = C_{total} \cdot T_{clk}
$$

$$
T_{copy} = 131072 \cdot 20 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{copy} = 2621440 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{copy} = 2.62144 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \approx 2.62 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Final answer with appropriate units and engineering interpretation.**

> The boot-time ROM-to-RAM shadow copy takes approximately **2.62 ms**. Since the system boot must complete before any real-time task begins, this duration directly adds to the **cold-start latency** of the embedded device.

### 3.2 Derivation — Effective Speedup Due to Shadowing

**Problem Setup (typical KTU numerical):**
> *"After shadowing, an instruction fetch from ROM takes 3 wait states and from shadowed RAM takes 0 wait states. If the CPU runs at 100 MHz and a routine contains 10,000 instruction fetches, compute the time saved by shadowing."*

**Given:**
- CPU frequency: $f_{CPU} = 100 \text{ MHz}$
- ROM wait states: $WS_{ROM} = 3$
- SRAM wait states: $WS_{SRAM} = 0$
- Number of fetches: $F = 10000$
- Bus cycle = 1 clock cycle (assumption for simplicity)

**Step 1 — Compute the time per fetch from ROM (with wait states).**

Each fetch from ROM takes $1 + WS_{ROM}$ cycles:

$$
t_{ROM} = \frac{1 + WS_{ROM}}{f_{CPU}} = \frac{1 + 3}{100 \times 10^6} = 40 \text{ ns per fetch}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the time per fetch from shadowed SRAM.**

Each fetch from SRAM takes $1 + WS_{SRAM}$ cycles:

$$
t_{SRAM} = \frac{1 + WS_{SRAM}}{f_{CPU}} = \frac{1 + 0}{100 \times 10^6} = 10 \text{ ns per fetch}
$$

**Step 3 — Total time for 10,000 fetches from ROM (without shadowing).**

$$
T_{ROM} = F \cdot t_{ROM} = 10000 \cdot 40 \times 10^{-9} = 4.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 400 \text{ \mu s}
$$

**Step 4 — Total time for 10,000 fetches from shadowed RAM.**

$$
T_{SRAM} = F \cdot t_{SRAM} = 10000 \cdot 10 \times 10^{-9} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 100 \text{ \mu s}
$$

**Step 5 — Time saved and speedup factor.**

$$
\Delta T = T_{ROM} - T_{SRAM} = 400 \text{ \mu s} - 100 \text{ \mu s} = 300 \text{ \mu s}
$$

$$
S = \frac{T_{ROM}}{T_{SRAM}} = \frac{400}{100} = 4
$$

> The shadowing technique yields a **4× speedup** in this configuration, saving **300 µs** of execution time per 10,000 fetches.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table for KTU Long-Answer

| Parameter | Without Shadowing (ROM only) | With Shadowing (RAM after copy) |
|---|---|---|
| Device at fetch time | ROM / Flash | SRAM |
| Wait states | 2–4 typical | 0 typical |
| Access time (at 100 MHz) | 30–50 ns | 10 ns |
| Volatile? | No | Yes (loses data on power-off) |
| Writable? | No (in normal mode) | Yes |
| Power consumption per fetch | Higher (Flash charge pumps) | Lower (CMOS SRAM) |
| Suitable for ISR? | Often no (latency too high) | Yes (deterministic) |
| Code modification at runtime? | Impossible | Possible |
| Cost per byte | Lower for large Flash | Higher for SRAM |
| Used in which phase? | Boot only | Runtime |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Memory Shadowing Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    cpuA[CPU Core] --> addrBusA[Address Bus]
    cpuA --> ctrlBusA[Control Bus MREQ RD WR]
    addrBusA --> decoderA[Memory Address Decoder]
    ctrlBusA --> decoderA
    decoderA --> remapRegA[Remap Control Register]
    remapRegA --> decisionA{Shadow Enabled}
    decisionA -->|No - Boot Phase| romSelA[ROM Selected - CS LOW]
    decisionA -->|Yes - Runtime Phase| ramSelA[RAM Selected - CS LOW]
    romSelA --> romDevA[ROM or Flash 32 KB Non Volatile]
    ramSelA --> ramDevA[SRAM 32 KB Volatile Working Copy]
    romDevA -.->|Block Copy at Startup| ramDevA
    romDevA --> dataBusA[Shared Data Bus]
    ramDevA --> dataBusA
    dataBusA --> cpuA
    startupA[Boot ROM Code 0x0000] -.->|Copies contents| ramDevA
    vectorA[Vector Table] -.->|Also shadowed| ramDevA
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Boot-to-Runtime Transition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1[Step 1 Power On Reset] --> step2[Step 2 PC loads from ROM vector table]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3 Execute reset handler in ROM]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4 Initialize stack pointer SP]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5 Run shadow copy routine]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6 Copy N bytes from ROM to RAM]
    step6 --> step7[Step 7 Toggle remap register bit]
    step7 --> step8[Step 8 Bus now routes to RAM]
    step8 --> step9[Step 9 Branch to main in shadowed RAM]
    step9 --> step10[Step 10 All future fetches from SRAM]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Address Decoding Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stateInit[Initial State Remap Bit equals 0] --> checkAddrA{Address in Shadow Window}
    checkAddrA -->|Yes| selectROM[Activate ROM Chip Select]
    checkAddrA -->|No| checkAddrB{Normal Address Region}
    checkAddrA -->|Outside| selectPeriph[Activate Peripheral]
    checkAddrB --> selectROM
    checkAddrB --> selectOther[Activate External Memory or IO]
    selectROM --> execPhase[ROM Fetch Occurs]
    execPhase --> copyRoutine[Copy Routine Runs]
    copyRoutine --> setRemap[Set Remap Bit equals 1]
    setRemap --> stateNew[New State Remap Bit equals 1]
    stateNew --> checkAddrC{Address in Shadow Window}
    checkAddrC -->|Yes| selectRAM[Activate RAM Chip Select]
    checkAddrC -->|No| selectOther2[Other Devices Unaffected]
    selectRAM --> runtimeFetch[RAM Fetch Occurs at Zero Wait States]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Notes for Students:**
> - The **dashed arrows** in the block diagram represent the *one-time data flow* during the boot copy.
> - The **solid arrows** represent *continuous bus traffic* during normal operation.
> - The **remap register** is the single control bit that determines whether the bus sees ROM or RAM at the shared address — this is the conceptual "switch" of memory shadowing.

---

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**
**Define memory shadowing. Mention its role in embedded system boot-up.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Memory shadowing is a memory-mapping technique in which a single physical address range is shared between two memory devices — typically a **ROM (non-volatile)** and a **RAM (volatile)**. The same address can therefore refer to either device depending on the system phase.

**Boot-up role (1 mark):** During reset, the system executes initialization code from **ROM**, which is reliable and retains content without power. The boot routine then **copies the contents of ROM into the overlaid RAM** so that subsequent program execution occurs from the **faster zero-wait-state RAM**.

**Engineering benefit (1 mark):** This technique combines the **non-volatility of ROM** (for permanent code storage) with the **high speed of SRAM** (for execution), thereby improving instruction fetch performance and enabling self-modifying code, which would be impossible from ROM alone.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**List any three advantages of memory shadowing over direct ROM execution.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — one mark per advantage):**

1. **Faster Instruction Execution:** Since SRAM has zero wait states while ROM typically has 2–4 wait states, shadowed code in RAM executes significantly faster, improving overall system throughput.

2. **Code Modifiability:** RAM allows the processor to **write back to the instruction memory**, enabling self-modifying code, runtime patching, and bootloader operations that cannot be performed on ROM.

3. **Lower Dynamic Power Consumption:** Each SRAM access consumes less switching capacitance than a Flash read, so shadowing reduces per-fetch energy — beneficial in battery-powered IoT devices.

4. *(Optional bonus point)* **Deterministic Interrupt Latency:** Shadowing critical ISRs into RAM guarantees **fixed, predictable fetch times**, essential for hard real-time systems.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Memory Shadowing in 8051 Architecture**

**[KTU University Exam — Supplementary 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) Explain with a neat block diagram how memory shadowing is implemented in the 8051 microcontroller. Discuss the role of the EA-bar pin and the role of the boot ROM in this process. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** The 8051 microcontroller uses memory shadowing in its **external code memory expansion mode**. When the program code exceeds the internal 4 KB ROM, the 8051 must fetch code from external ROM, and a shadowing arrangement is used to combine internal and external code memory.

**Block Diagram Description (2 marks):**

The 8051 has a **16-bit program counter (PC)** and a 64 KB external code memory space. The lower 4 KB addresses (`0x0000`–`0x0FFF`) are shared between:
- **Internal ROM (4 KB)** — non-volatile, factory-masked or OTP
- **External ROM** — connected via the external data bus

The **EA-bar (External Access) pin** decides which device responds:
- If **EA-bar = 1** (logic high): internal ROM is selected for addresses `0x0000`–`0x0FFF`; external ROM responds only for `0x1000`–`0xFFFF`.
- If **EA-bar = 0** (logic low): internal ROM is **disabled**, and the entire 64 KB is fetched from external ROM.

**Role of the Boot ROM (2 marks):**

The 8051's internal ROM contains the **boot loader** and **interrupt vector table**. The reset vector at `0x0000` points to the initialization code in this internal ROM. This boot ROM is also responsible for the **shadow copy of interrupt service routines** into internal RAM (the 256-byte IRAM) so that ISRs can be patched or modified at runtime.

**Working Summary (2 marks):**

On reset, the 8051 fetches its first instruction from internal ROM at `0x0000`. The internal ROM contains routines that copy themselves and other critical code into the **internal data RAM**. Once the copy completes, the remap logic (or the linker script) ensures that all subsequent execution of those routines occurs from the faster internal RAM, while the original ROM continues to hold the master copy. This achieves the dual goals of **persistence** and **speed**.

---

**(b) A 16-bit embedded processor uses memory shadowing. The ROM-to-RAM copy of 48 KB takes 6 ms at 80 MHz. Each instruction fetch from ROM requires 3 wait states, while each fetch from shadowed RAM requires 0 wait states. (i) Compute the cycles per byte used in the copy routine. (ii) If a routine performs 25,000 instruction fetches, calculate the time saved by shadowing. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Image size: $N = 48 \text{ KB} = 48 \times 1024 = 49152 \text{ bytes}$
- Copy duration: $T_{copy} = 6 \text{ ms} = 6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$
- CPU frequency: $f_{CPU} = 80 \text{ MHz} = 80 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$
- ROM wait states: $WS_{ROM} = 3$
- RAM wait states: $WS_{SRAM} = 0$
- Number of fetches: $F = 25000$

**(i) Cycles per byte in the copy routine (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Total clock cycles in the copy duration.**

$$
C_{total} = T_{copy} \cdot f_{CPU} = 6 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 80 \times 10^6 = 480000 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 2 — Cycles per byte.**

$$
n_c = \frac{C_{total}}{N} = \frac{480000}{49152} \approx 9.77 \text{ cycles/byte}
$$

> **Result:** The copy routine uses approximately **9.77 cycles per byte**, which is reasonable for a 16-bit polled loop with address arithmetic and loop overhead.

**(ii) Time saved by shadowing for 25,000 fetches (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Clock period.**

$$
T_{clk} = \frac{1}{80 \times 10^6} = 12.5 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2 — Time per fetch from ROM.**

$$
t_{ROM} = (1 + WS_{ROM}) \cdot T_{clk} = (1 + 3) \cdot 12.5 \text{ ns} = 50 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 3 — Time per fetch from shadowed RAM.**

$$
t_{SRAM} = (1 + WS_{SRAM}) \cdot T_{clk} = (1 + 0) \cdot 12.5 \text{ ns} = 12.5 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 4 — Total time from ROM.**

$$
T_{ROM} = 25000 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-9} = 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1.25 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Total time from shadowed RAM.**

$$
T_{SRAM} = 25000 \cdot 12.5 \times 10^{-9} = 3.125 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.3125 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 6 — Time saved.**

$$
\Delta T = T_{ROM} - T_{SRAM} = 1.25 \text{ ms} - 0.3125 \text{ ms} = 0.9375 \text{ ms} \approx 937.5 \text{ \mu s}
$$

> **Result:** Shadowing saves **937.5 µs** of execution time for 25,000 instruction fetches, yielding a **4× speedup** in this scenario.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Memory Shadowing in ARM Cortex-M Systems**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) Describe the memory shadowing mechanism in an ARM Cortex-M3/M4 system. Explain how the vector table offset register (VTOR) interacts with shadowing of the vector table. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** In ARM Cortex-M3/M4 systems, memory shadowing is implemented in a **partial-shadow** fashion where the **vector table** is mirrored from Flash to SRAM at boot, and the **VTOR (Vector Table Offset Register)** is used to redirect all exception/interrupt vector fetches to the shadowed SRAM copy.

**Architecture Description (3 marks):**

The ARM Cortex-M3/M4 has a fixed internal bus matrix that can decode the **code region (`0x00000000`–`0x1FFFFFFF`)** from either Flash or SRAM depending on the **implementation-defined memory map** and **FLASH_OPTCR register** settings in vendor-specific devices (e.g., STM32).

On reset, the vector table is read from the **boot memory** (Flash or system memory, depending on BOOT0/BOOT1 pins). The startup code (`startup_stm32.s` in vendor libraries) then:
1. Copies the **vector table entries** (initial SP, Reset_Handler, NMI_Handler, etc.) from Flash to the start of SRAM (typically at `0x20000000`).
2. Updates the **VTOR** to point to the SRAM address.
3. The Cortex-M NVIC (Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller) now uses the SRAM-resident vector table for all subsequent exception dispatching.

**Role of VTOR (2 marks):**

The **Vector Table Offset Register (VTOR)** is a **32-bit system control register** accessible at address `0xE000ED08`. It contains the **base address** of the vector table used by the NVIC. By programming VTOR, the system designer can:
- Relocate the vector table to **SRAM** (shadow mode for fast ISR dispatch)
- Relocate the vector table to **external memory** (for bootloader handoff)
- Dynamically switch vector tables in **RTOS contexts** (e.g., FreeRTOS task-specific vectors)

**Engineering Benefit (1 mark):**

By shadowing the vector table in SRAM, the Cortex-M achieves **deterministic interrupt latency** independent of Flash wait states, which is critical for hard real-time applications such as motor control and automotive ECUs.

---

**(b) In a Cortex-M4 system, the Flash wait states are configured as 4 WS at 168 MHz, and the SRAM has 0 WS. The system uses partial shadowing where 8 KB of performance-critical code is copied from Flash to SRAM. Compute: (i) The instruction fetch time for both memories. (ii) If the critical loop executes 50,000 instruction fetches, find the total time saved per execution. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- CPU frequency: $f_{CPU} = 168 \text{ MHz} = 168 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$
- Flash wait states: $WS_{Flash} = 4$
- SRAM wait states: $WS_{SRAM} = 0$
- Number of fetches: $F = 50000$

**(i) Instruction fetch time for both memories (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Clock period.**

$$
T_{clk} = \frac{1}{168 \times 10^6} \approx 5.952 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2 — Fetch time from Flash (with wait states).**

$$
t_{Flash} = (1 + WS_{Flash}) \cdot T_{clk} = (1 + 4) \cdot 5.952 = 29.76 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 3 — Fetch time from shadowed SRAM.**

$$
t_{SRAM} = (1 + WS_{SRAM}) \cdot T_{clk} = (1 + 0) \cdot 5.952 = 5.952 \text{ ns}
$$

> **Result:** Flash fetch takes **29.76 ns**, SRAM fetch takes **5.952 ns**.

**(ii) Time saved for 50,000 fetches (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Total time from Flash (without shadowing).**

$$
T_{Flash} = 50000 \cdot 29.76 \times 10^{-9} = 1.488 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1.488 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Total time from shadowed SRAM.**

$$
T_{SRAM} = 50000 \cdot 5.952 \times 10^{-9} = 2.976 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.2976 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Time saved and speedup.**

$$
\Delta T = T_{Flash} - T_{SRAM} = 1.488 \text{ ms} - 0.2976 \text{ ms} = 1.1904 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
S = \frac{T_{Flash}}{T_{SRAM}} = \frac{1.488}{0.2976} = 5
$$

> **Result:** Shadowing saves **1.1904 ms** per execution of the critical loop and provides a **5× speedup** in instruction fetch performance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to add 1 to wait states:** A fetch with $WS = 3$ takes $(1 + 3) = 4$ clock cycles, not 3. Many students lose 1 mark here.
> 2. **Mixing up KB and Kb:** $32 \text{ KB} = 32 \times 1024 \text{ bytes}$, not $32 \times 1000$. KTU expects **binary multiples**.
> 3. **Wrong unit conversions:** $50 \text{ MHz} = 50 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$, not $50 \times 10^3$. Always write out powers of ten in calculations.
> 4. **Confusing the remap direction:** Some students write "remap from RAM to ROM" during runtime. The correct direction is "remap **from** ROM **to** RAM" after the copy completes.
> 5. **Omitting the role of the remap register:** A 7-mark question on shadowing **must** mention the memory remap mechanism — failure to do so caps marks at 5/7.
> 6. **Not stating the engineering rationale:** Writing "ROM is slow" without explaining **why** SRAM is faster (zero wait states) loses a mark for depth.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- **Definition:** Memory shadowing = two physical devices (ROM + RAM) sharing **one address range**.
- **Purpose:** Combine **non-volatility of ROM** with **speed of SRAM**.
- **Boot Sequence:** POR → Vector fetch from ROM → Reset handler → **Copy ROM to RAM** → Remap → Execute from RAM.
- **Remap Register:** A single bit/control register that **switches bus decoding** between ROM and RAM.
- **Variants:** Full shadow, Partial shadow, Vector-table shadow, Dynamic shadow.
- **Copy Time Formula:** $T_{copy} = \dfrac{N \cdot n_c}{f_{CPU}}$ where $N$ = bytes, $n_c$ = cycles/byte, $f_{CPU}$ = clock.
- **Wait-State Rule:** Each fetch takes $(1 + WS)$ clock cycles — **never forget the +1**.
- **Speedup Formula:** $S = \dfrac{1 + WS_{ROM}}{1 + WS_{SRAM}}$.
- **8051 Specific:** EA-bar pin controls internal vs. external ROM; lower 4 KB is the shadow window.
- **ARM Cortex-M Specific:** **VTOR register** at `0xE000ED08` relocates the vector table; shadowing the vector table gives deterministic ISR latency.
- **Common Mistake:** Confusing shadowing with caching — **shadowing is a physical copy at boot**, caching is a dynamic, hardware-managed mechanism.
- **Use Cases:** Bootloaders, automotive ECUs, real-time DSP, secure boot in mobile SoCs.
- **Trade-off:** Requires **extra SRAM** (often 1:1 with ROM size) and adds **boot-time latency**.
- **Key Exam Keywords:** *"alias," "overlap," "remap," "zero wait state," "boot-time copy," "non-volatile master copy," "volatile working copy."*
- **Typical KTU Marks Distribution:** 2 marks for definition, 3 marks for working with diagram, 4 marks for advantages/disadvantages, 2 marks for numerical example, 3 marks for application context.

---

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Memory Selection for Embedded Systems** is the systematic engineering process of evaluating, comparing, and choosing appropriate memory devices and architectures for an embedded product based on quantitative parameters such as **density (capacity)**, **access time (latency)**, **power dissipation**, **endurance (write cycles)**, **retention period**, **cost per bit**, and **physical form factor**. It involves constructing a logical **memory map**, performing **address decoding**, and balancing the **memory hierarchy** (Registers → Cache → Primary Memory → Secondary Storage) to meet real-time, power, and cost constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Students are expected to understand *why* a particular memory (e.g., **NOR Flash** for boot code, **SRAM** for caches, **DDR SDRAM** for frame buffers) is selected, the *trade-offs* involved, and the *mathematical logic of address decoding* to build a non-overlapping, contiguous memory map.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Workshop Storage Analogy"

Imagine an embedded system is a **mechanic's workshop (the CPU)**:

| Workshop Element | Embedded Equivalent | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| The workbench (where tools are immediately used) | **CPU Registers** | Fastest, smallest, most expensive |
| The tool rack beside the bench (1-step reach) | **Cache (L1/L2 SRAM)** | Very fast, limited size |
| The wall-mounted shelf (visible from desk) | **Primary Memory (SRAM / DRAM)** | Moderate speed, larger capacity |
| The warehouse at the back of the building | **Secondary Storage (Flash, SD Card, HDD)** | Slow, non-volatile, huge |
| The courier service that brings a part on demand | **DMA / External Bus (NAND Flash, EEPROM)** | Non-volatile, sequential access |

Just as the mechanic decides *which tool goes on the bench* vs *which goes in the warehouse* based on **frequency of use, size, and urgency**, an embedded engineer decides *which memory chip holds the bootloader vs application code vs sensor logs* based on **speed, volatility, write endurance, and cost**.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Standard Vocabulary)

> [!NOTE]
> **Must-know terms for KTU ESE (End Semester Examination):**

- **Volatile Memory** — Loses contents when power is removed (e.g., **SRAM, DRAM**).
- **Non-Volatile Memory (NVM)** — Retains data without power (e.g., **ROM, PROM, EPROM, EEPROM, Flash**).
- **Static RAM (SRAM)** — Uses 6 transistors per cell; no refresh needed; fast but bulky.
- **Dynamic RAM (DRAM)** — Uses 1 capacitor + 1 transistor per cell; needs periodic refresh; high density.
- **ROM / PROM / EPROM / EEPROM** — Read-Only variants with progressively more flexibility.
- **Flash Memory** — A modern EEPROM variant; **NOR Flash** (random access, XIP-capable) vs **NAND Flash** (block access, high density).
- **Memory Map** — A pictorial/logical assignment of physical address ranges to memory devices.
- **Address Decoding** — Logic that generates **Chip Select (CS)** signals for a specific address range.
- **Wait States** — Extra clock cycles the CPU inserts when the memory is slower than the bus.
- **Endurance** — Maximum guaranteed program/erase cycles (e.g., **100,000 cycles** for NOR Flash).
- **Retention** — Time data can be stored without power (e.g., **10 years** for Flash).

---

## 1.4 Why Memory Selection is *Critical* in Embedded Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Unlike a desktop PC (where memory is "abundant and swappable"), an embedded system has **fixed, pre-soldered memory**. A wrong selection can:
> - **Cause system boot failure** (wrong boot ROM type)
> - **Exceed power budget** (DRAM refresh drains batteries)
> - **Miss real-time deadlines** (too-slow memory → missed interrupt response)
> - **Reduce product lifespan** (Flash wear-out in a logging application)
> - **Blow the BOM (Bill of Materials) cost** (over-specifying SRAM)

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Memory Classification — A Structured Hierarchy

Memory for embedded systems is classified along **four orthogonal axes**:

### 2.1.1 By Volatility
- **Volatile:** SRAM, DRAM, SDRAM, DDRx, LPDDR
- **Non-Volatile:** Mask ROM, OTP ROM, PROM, EPROM, EEPROM, NOR Flash, NAND Flash, FRAM, MRAM, RRAM, Battery-backed SRAM (BB-SRAM)

### 2.1.2 By Access Pattern
- **Random Access (Byte/Word addressable):** SRAM, DRAM, NOR Flash, ROM
- **Sequential/Block Access (Page/Block addressable):** NAND Flash, HDD, SD card

### 2.1.3 By Write Capability
- **Read-Only:** Mask ROM, PROM, EPROM (erasable via UV), OTP
- **Read-Mostly (slow writes):** EEPROM, Flash
- **Read/Write (fast writes):** SRAM, DRAM, FRAM

### 2.1.4 By Physical Location
- **On-chip:** CPU registers, internal SRAM, internal Flash (e.g., on STM32, LPC, PIC)
- **Off-chip / External:** SDRAM chips, SPI Flash, parallel NAND

---

## 2.2 The Six Pillars of Memory Selection (KTU Hot Topic)

Every KTU question on this subject tests one or more of these criteria:

1. **Capacity (Density):** Total bits = `Locations × Word Size`. Example: `64K × 8` = **64 Kilobytes**.
2. **Access Time / Latency:** Time from valid address to valid data, in **nanoseconds (ns)**.
3. **Power Dissipation:** Measured in **mW (active)** and **µW (standby/sleep)**.
4. **Cost per Bit:** A major driver for consumer IoT products.
5. **Endurance (Write Cycles):** `P/E cycles` for Flash (typically **10⁴ – 10⁶**).
6. **Interface & Packaging:** Parallel vs Serial (SPI, I²C, QSPI, HyperBus), BGA vs TSOP.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Real-World Trade-off Rule:** No single memory is best on all 6 pillars. Selection is always a **Pareto optimization** — pick the best mix that satisfies *all hard constraints* (e.g., "must be non-volatile") and is *good enough* on the rest.

---

## 2.3 Address Decoding — The Mathematics of Memory Maps

**Address decoding** translates a CPU's address bus into individual **Chip Select (CS)** signals for each memory device.

### 2.3.1 Address Line Count Formula
If a memory has $N$ addressable locations, it requires $n$ address lines such that:

$$
2^n = N \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad n = \log_2(N)
$$

**Example:** A 32 KB memory requires
$$
n = \log_2(32 \times 1024) = \log_2(32768) = 15 \text{ address lines}
$$

### 2.3.2 Address Decoding Schemes

| Scheme | Description | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear Decoding** | One address line = one CS (e.g., A15 → CS1, A14 → CS2) | Simple, cheap | Incomplete decoding → **address aliases** (multiple addresses map to same location) |
| **Full Decoding** | All address lines used through a decoder (e.g., 74LS138 = 3-to-8) | No aliases, every address unique | More complex, extra gates |
| **Partial Decoding** | Subset of high-order lines decoded | Compromise | Aliases in un-decoded region |

### 2.3.3 The 3-to-8 Decoder (74LS138 / 74HC138) — A KTU Favorite

The 74×138 has:
- **3 input lines:** A, B, C
- **3 enable lines:** $\overline{G2A}$, $\overline{G2B}$, $G1$
- **8 active-low outputs:** $\overline{Y0}$ to $\overline{Y7}$

To fully decode a 16-bit address bus (A0–A15) into **8 chips of 2 KB each**:
- Lower **11 lines** (A0–A10) → address within chip
- Upper **3 lines** (A13, A14, A15) → inputs to 74×138
- Lines A11, A12 → tied to enable pins for proper gating

This is a **standard 14-mark KTU question** — see Section 5.

---

## 2.4 Wait States — When Memory is Slower than the CPU

If the CPU's bus cycle time is **faster** than the memory's access time, the CPU must insert **wait states**.

Let:
- $T_{\text{CPU}}$ = CPU clock period (or bus cycle time) in **ns**
- $T_{\text{MEM}}$ = Memory access time in **ns**
- $W$ = Number of wait states

$$
W = \left\lceil \frac{T_{\text{MEM}}}{T_{\text{CPU}}} \right\rceil - 1
$$

> **Alternative form using frequency $f$ (in MHz):**
> $$
> T_{\text{CPU}} = \frac{1000}{f_{\text{MHz}}} \text{ ns}
> $$
> $$
> W = \left\lceil \frac{T_{\text{MEM}} \cdot f_{\text{MHz}}}{1000} \right\rceil - 1
> $$

**Rule of thumb:** $W$ **must be ≥ 0**. A negative result means no wait state is needed (memory is fast enough).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference — Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE:**

| # | Concept | Formula | Unit / Typical Value | Variables |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Address lines for $N$ locations | $n = \log_2(N)$ | dimensionless | $N$ = locations |
| 2 | Memory capacity (bits) | $C = N \times w$ | bits, KB, Mb | $w$ = word size in bits |
| 3 | Capacity in bytes | $C_{\text{byte}} = (N \times w) / 8$ | bytes | — |
| 4 | CPU clock period | $T_{\text{CPU}} = 1 / f$ | ns, µs | $f$ = frequency in Hz |
| 5 | Wait states | $W = \lceil T_{\text{MEM}} / T_{\text{CPU}} \rceil - 1$ | integer ≥ 0 | $T_{\text{MEM}}$ = access time |
| 6 | Decoder outputs | $D = 2^{m}$ | chips selectable | $m$ = decoder input lines |
| 7 | Address range of a chip | $A_{\text{start}}$ to $A_{\text{end}}$ | hex or decimal | start = base addr |
| 8 | Chip size in bytes | $S = 2^{(n-m)} \times 2^m$ | bytes | $n$ = total addr lines, $m$ = decoder inputs |
| 9 | Power (active) | $P_a = V \times I_a$ | mW | $V$ in V, $I$ in mA |
| 10 | Energy per access | $E = P_a \times T_{\text{MEM}}$ | nJ | — |
| 11 | Flash endurance | $N_{\text{P/E}}$ | cycles (10⁴–10⁶) | per block |
| 12 | Cost per bit | $C_b = \text{Price} / (N \times w)$ | ₹/bit or ¢/bit | — |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Typical Memory Choice | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Automotive ECU** | NOR Flash (code) + ECC SRAM (data) | Deterministic boot, safety (ISO 26262) |
| **Smartphone SoC** | LPDDR5 (RAM) + UFS 3.1 (NAND) + eMMC | Power + speed + density |
| **Wearable / IoT sensor** | Internal MCU Flash + SPI FRAM | Ultra-low power, high endurance |
| **Industrial PLC** | Battery-backed SRAM + EEPROM | Instant save on power-loss |
| **Bootloader** | NOR Flash (XIP — Execute-In-Place) | Random read access, byte-addressable |
| **Mass storage / SSD** | NAND Flash (SLC/MLC/TLC/QLC) | Highest density per dollar |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Address Lines Required for a Given Memory Size

**Problem Statement:** A memory module has 65,536 locations, each storing 8 bits. How many address lines are required?

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Identify the parameter $N$.**
The number of addressable locations is:
$$
N = 65536 = 64 \times 1024 = 64\text{K}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the binary addressing principle.**
For $n$ address lines, the number of unique binary combinations is $2^n$. To uniquely select $N$ locations, we require:
$$
2^n = N
$$

**Step 3 — Take the base-2 logarithm of both sides.**
$$
n = \log_2(N) = \log_2(65536)
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate using known powers of 2.**
$$
2^{16} = 65536 \quad \text{(since } 2^{10} = 1024 \text{ and } 2^{16} = 2^{10} \cdot 2^6 = 1024 \cdot 64 = 65536 \text{)}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclude.**
$$
\boxed{n = 16 \text{ address lines}}
$$

> **Generalised Rule:** $\log_2(1\text{K}) = 10$, $\log_2(4\text{K}) = 12$, $\log_2(64\text{K}) = 16$, $\log_2(1\text{M}) = 20$, $\log_2(4\text{M}) = 22$, $\log_2(16\text{M}) = 24$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Wait State Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A microcontroller runs at $f = 25 \text{ MHz}$ and interfaces with an SRAM that has an access time of $T_{\text{MEM}} = 120 \text{ ns}$. Calculate the number of wait states required.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Compute the CPU clock period.**
$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{25 \times 10^6} = 40 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s} = 40 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the wait-state formula.**
$$
W = \left\lceil \frac{T_{\text{MEM}}}{T_{\text{CPU}}} \right\rceil - 1
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.**
$$
W = \left\lceil \frac{120 \text{ ns}}{40 \text{ ns}} \right\rceil - 1 = \lceil 3.0 \rceil - 1
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the ceiling function.**
$$
\lceil 3.0 \rceil = 3
$$

**Step 5 — Final result.**
$$
\boxed{W = 3 - 1 = 2 \text{ wait states}}
$$

**Interpretation:** The CPU must insert **2 extra clock cycles** for every memory read/write, so the effective memory access takes `1 (normal) + 2 (wait) = 3 clock cycles = 120 ns`. This matches the memory's capability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Edge case — what if $T_{\text{MEM}} \leq T_{\text{CPU}}$?**
> If $T_{\text{MEM}} = 30$ ns, then $W = \lceil 30/40 \rceil - 1 = \lceil 0.75 \rceil - 1 = 1 - 1 = 0$. So **no wait states** are required. Zero is the *minimum legal value*; a negative $W$ is meaningless and indicates a **mismatched formula application**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Memory Map Construction using 74×138 Decoder

**Problem Statement:** A 16-bit address bus (A0–A15) must be decoded into **four 16 KB × 8 memory chips** using a 3-to-8 decoder. Design the chip-select logic and produce the memory map.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Determine the number of address lines needed per chip.**
For 16 KB = $16 \times 1024 = 16384$ locations:
$$
n_{\text{chip}} = \log_2(16384) = \log_2(2^{14}) = 14 \text{ address lines}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the address lines used *within* the chip.**
These are the **lowest** 14 lines: **A0 through A13**.
$$
\text{Lines for intra-chip addressing: } A_0 \ldots A_{13} \quad (14 \text{ lines})
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the address lines used *for chip selection*.**
Total lines = 16, intra-chip = 14, so:
$$
n_{\text{CS}} = 16 - 14 = 2 \text{ lines}
$$

> But a 3-to-8 decoder has 3 inputs — we will tie one of them to a constant (to make $m = 2$ effective) **or** use the 3rd line as a *gating enable*. The KTU board expects the 3rd line to be tied to a constant for a 4-chip map.

**Step 4 — Connect decoder inputs.**
Let us connect:
- Decoder input **C** = A15
- Decoder input **B** = A14
- Decoder input **A** = A13
- All enables ($G1$, $\overline{G2A}$, $\overline{G2B}$) tied to **logic 1, 0, 0** respectively for permanent enable.

**Step 5 — Determine the base address of each chip.**

For each $\overline{Y_i}$ activated when `(CBA) = i`:

| Chip | A15 A14 A13 | Active Output | Address Range (Hex) |
|---|---|---|---|
| ROM-1 | 0 0 0 | $\overline{Y0}$ | `0x0000 – 0x3FFF` |
| ROM-2 | 0 0 1 | $\overline{Y1}$ | `0x4000 – 0x7FFF` |
| RAM-1 | 0 1 0 | $\overline{Y2}$ | `0x8000 – 0xBFFF` |
| RAM-2 | 0 1 1 | $\overline{Y3}$ | `0xC000 – 0xFFFF` |

**Step 6 — Verify the address range mathematically.**

For **ROM-1** ($\overline{Y0}$ active when A15 A14 A13 = 000):
- **Lowest address:** `A15..A13 = 000`, `A12..A0 = 00..0` → `0b0000_0000_0000_0000 = 0x0000`
- **Highest address:** `A15..A13 = 000`, `A12..A0 = 11..1` → `0b0011_1111_1111_1111 = 0x3FFF`
- **Range size:** $0x3FFF - 0x0000 + 1 = 0x4000 = 16{,}384 = 16 \text{ KB}$ ✓

For **ROM-2** ($\overline{Y1}$ active when A15 A14 A13 = 001):
- **Lowest address:** `0b0100_0000_0000_0000 = 0x4000`
- **Highest address:** `0b0111_1111_1111_1111 = 0x7FFF`
- **Range size:** $0x7FFF - 0x4000 + 1 = 0x4000 = 16 \text{ KB}$ ✓

**Step 7 — Memory Map Summary.**

$$
\text{Address Space} = \bigcup_{i=0}^{3} \text{Block}_i = [0\text{x}0000, 0\text{x}FFFF] = 64 \text{ KB} \quad \checkmark
$$

> The four blocks are **non-overlapping** and **contiguous** — this is the hallmark of a *correctly designed* full-decoding memory map.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cost-per-Bit and Density Comparison

**Problem Statement:** A designer must choose between a **256 Mbit SPI NOR Flash** costing **₹120** and a **1 Gbit parallel NAND Flash** costing **₹180**. Compare cost per bit and the number of address lines.

**Step 1 — Compute cost per bit for NOR Flash.**
$$
C_{\text{NOR}} = \frac{120 \text{ ₹}}{256 \times 10^6 \text{ bits}} = 4.6875 \times 10^{-7} \text{ ₹/bit}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute cost per bit for NAND Flash.**
$$
C_{\text{NAND}} = \frac{180 \text{ ₹}}{1 \times 10^9 \text{ bits}} = 1.8 \times 10^{-7} \text{ ₹/bit}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare.**
$$
\frac{C_{\text{NOR}}}{C_{\text{NAND}}} = \frac{4.6875}{1.8} \approx 2.6
$$
NAND is **~2.6× cheaper per bit**. This justifies NAND for mass storage (videos, large logs).

**Step 4 — Address lines comparison (for byte addressing).**
- **NOR 256 Mbit** = 32 MBytes → $\log_2(32 \text{ M}) = 25$ address lines (with chip-enable for byte vs word)
- **NAND 1 Gbit** = 128 MBytes → $\log_2(128 \text{ M}) = 27$ address lines

> NAND's block-based interface means it does **not** use 27 random-access lines — it uses a **page-based protocol** (e.g., 2 KB pages with 12-bit column + 17-bit row). KTU students must distinguish *interface* from *random-access addressing*.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Memory Technology at a Glance

| Parameter | SRAM | DRAM | NOR Flash | NAND Flash | EEPROM | FRAM | Mask ROM |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Volatile?** | Yes | Yes | No | No | No | No | No |
| **Cell size** | 6 T | 1 T + 1 C | 1 T (split-gate) | 1 T (floating-gate) | 2 T | 1 T + 1 C | 1 T (hard-wired) |
| **Density** | Low | High | Medium | Very High | Low | Low–Med | High |
| **Read speed** | Very Fast | Fast (w/ refresh) | Fast (XIP) | Slow (page-load) | Medium | Fast | Fast |
| **Write speed** | Very Fast | Fast | Slow (~ms) | Slow (~ms) | Slow (~ms) | Fast | N/A |
| **Endurance** | ∞ | ∞ | 10⁴–10⁶ | 10⁴–10⁵ | 10⁵–10⁶ | 10¹⁰–10¹⁴ | N/A |
| **Power** | High | High (refresh) | Low (read) | Low | Medium | Low | Lowest |
| **Cost/bit** | High | Medium | Medium | Lowest | High | Very High | Lowest |
| **Best use** | Cache | Main RAM | Boot code | Mass storage | Config bytes | Data logging | Finalised firmware |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Memory Hierarchy Pyramid

This diagram illustrates the **classic memory pyramid** — as we move up, speed and cost-per-bit increase; as we move down, capacity and non-volatility increase.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    regA["CPU REGISTERS - 32 to 64 x 32 bit - 1 cycle access - 100 percent fastest"]
    cacheA["LEVEL 1 CACHE L1 - 16 to 64 KB per core - 2 to 4 cycles - SRAM cells"]
    cacheB["LEVEL 2 CACHE L2 - 128 KB to 1 MB - 10 to 20 cycles - unified"]
    mainMem["PRIMARY MEMORY - 4 MB to 512 MB - 50 to 100 ns - SRAM or SDRAM"]
    internalFlash["INTERNAL FLASH - 64 KB to 2 MB - 50 to 100 ns read - Non volatile"]
    externalFlash["EXTERNAL FLASH - 4 MB to 256 MB - SPI QSPI or parallel - 50 to 200 ns"]
    massStore["MASS STORAGE - SD card eMMC NAND SSD - 1 GB to 1 TB - ms access"]
    archive["ARCHIVE STORAGE - Cloud USB HDD - TB scale - seconds access"]

    regA --> cacheA
    cacheA --> cacheB
    cacheB --> mainMem
    mainMem --> internalFlash
    internalFlash --> externalFlash
    externalFlash --> massStore
    massStore --> archive
```

> **Reading the pyramid:** The arrows represent the data movement between layers (cache fills, page faults, DMA transfers). Each *upward* movement is faster but smaller; each *downward* movement is slower but larger and persistent.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Memory Selection Decision Flowchart

Use this KTU-board-style flowchart to logically arrive at a memory choice.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["START - Identify data type to store"]
    q1{"Is data boot code or XIP code"}
    q2{"Is data frequently read and written"]
    q3{"Is data large and rarely changed"}
    q4{"Is data small config bytes"}
    q5{"Is ultra low power mandatory"}
    outA["Choose NOR Flash - XIP capable"]
    outB["Choose SRAM for cache or DRAM for main RAM"]
    outC["Choose NAND Flash or eMMC"]
    outD["Choose EEPROM or small serial Flash"]
    outE["Choose FRAM or BB SRAM"]

    startA --> q1
    q1 -- YES --> outA
    q1 -- NO --> q2
    q2 -- YES --> outB
    q2 -- NO --> q3
    q3 -- YES --> outC
    q3 -- NO --> q4
    q4 -- YES --> outD
    q4 -- NO --> q5
    q5 -- YES --> outE
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Address Decoding using 74HC138 (Full Decoding)

This is a block-level functional architecture showing how a 16-bit address bus is decoded into 4 chip-selects for a 64 KB address space split into 4 × 16 KB chips.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cpuA["CPU ADDRESS BUS - A0 to A15 - 16 lines total"]
    splitA["ADDRESS BUS SPLIT - Two groups formed"]
    lowAddr["LOW ADDRESS GROUP - A0 to A13 - 14 lines - used INSIDE each chip"]
    highAddr["HIGH ADDRESS GROUP - A15 A14 A13 - 3 lines - fed to 74HC138 inputs CBA"]
    decoderA["74HC138 3 TO 8 DECODER - 3 inputs and 8 active low outputs"]
    enA["ENABLE PINS - G1 tied to VCC plus G2A and G2B tied to GND - permanently enabled"]
    cs0["Y0 BAR - CS0 to ROM1 - 0x0000 to 0x3FFF - 16 KB"]
    cs1["Y1 BAR - CS1 to ROM2 - 0x4000 to 0x7FFF - 16 KB"]
    cs2["Y2 BAR - CS2 to RAM1 - 0x8000 to 0xBFFF - 16 KB"]
    cs3["Y3 BAR - CS3 to RAM2 - 0xC000 to 0xFFFF - 16 KB"]
    aliasNote["ALIASES - NONE - because ALL 16 address lines are used"]

    cpuA --> splitA
    splitA --> lowAddr
    splitA --> highAddr
    highAddr --> decoderA
    enA --> decoderA
    decoderA --> cs0
    decoderA --> cs1
    decoderA --> cs2
    decoderA --> cs3
    lowAddr --> cs0
    lowAddr --> cs1
    lowAddr --> cs2
    lowAddr --> cs3
    cs0 -.-> aliasNote
    cs1 -.-> aliasNote
    cs2 -.-> aliasNote
    cs3 -.-> aliasNote
```

> **Reading note:** Each chip receives *all 14 low-order address lines* (A0–A13) to address 16 KB internally, but its **chip-select** is activated *only* when its unique combination appears on the high-order lines.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Linear vs Full Decoding Comparison (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph linA["LINEAR DECODING BLOCK"]

    cpu1 --> c3
    c1 --> ali1
    c2 --> ali1
    c3 --> ali1

    cpu2 --> dec1
    dec1 --> d0
    dec1 --> d1
    dec1 --> d2
    d0 --> ali2
    d1 --> ali2
    d2 --> ali2
```
