### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Differentiate between volatile and non-volatile memory. Give one example of each and state where each is typically used in an embedded system."** *(3 marks)*

#### Model Answer (Step-by-Step):

| Aspect | Volatile Memory | Non-Volatile Memory |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Loses stored data when power supply is removed | Retains data even without power |
| **Example** | SRAM (Static RAM) | NOR Flash |
| **Use in Embedded Systems** | Holds temporary variables, stack, heap, and CPU working data during program execution | Stores firmware/bootloader code and persistent configuration (e.g., Wi-Fi credentials) |
| **Need for backup power?** | Yes, otherwise data is lost | No |
| **Typical access speed** | Very fast (ns) | Slower (50–100 ns read; ms for write) |

> **Marks Distribution (KTU pattern):** Definition – 1 mark; Example – 1 mark; Embedded use-case – 1 mark.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"List any three parameters used for selecting a memory device for an embedded system. Briefly explain any one."** *(3 marks)*

#### Model Answer:

**Three memory selection parameters:**
1. **Capacity (Density)** – Total number of bits the memory can hold. Must be ≥ the largest code/data size.
2. **Access Time / Latency** – Time between presenting an address and receiving valid data. Must be ≤ CPU bus cycle time to avoid wait states.
3. **Power Dissipation** – Critical for battery-operated devices (e.g., wearables, IoT sensors). Measured in mW (active) and µW (standby).

> **Explanation of Access Time (any one chosen):**
> Access time $T_a$ is the delay from the moment the address lines are stable to the moment the data appears on the data bus. If $T_a > T_{\text{CPU clock period}}$, the CPU must insert **wait states**, reducing throughput. For example, a CPU running at 50 MHz has a 20 ns cycle; a memory with 80 ns access time requires $\lceil 80/20 \rceil - 1 = 3$ wait states.

> **Marks Distribution (KTU pattern):** Listing – 1.5 marks; Brief explanation – 1.5 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question is 14 marks with sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. **Solve either Option A or Option B in full.**

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks (Option 1)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**
A 16-bit address bus is connected to a system containing **two ROM chips (8 KB each)** and **two RAM chips (8 KB each)**. The ROMs must occupy the **lowest** addresses, and the RAMs the **highest** addresses, with no gaps and no overlapping.

#### **(a)** Design a full-decoding scheme using a **74×138 (3-to-8) decoder**. Draw the connection diagram and show how each chip's Chip Select (CS) is derived. State clearly which address lines are used for intra-chip selection and which for chip selection. *(7 marks)*

#### **(b)** Construct the complete **memory map** showing the start and end address (in hexadecimal) of each of the four chips. Verify that the total addressable range is $4 \times 8 = 32$ KB. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Compute intra-chip address lines.**
For 8 KB = $8 \times 1024 = 8192 = 2^{13}$ locations, we need **13 address lines (A0–A12)** *inside* each chip.

**Step 2 — Identify the chip-select lines.**
Total address lines = 16, intra-chip = 13.
$$
n_{\text{CS}} = 16 - 13 = 3 \text{ lines}
$$
These are **A13, A14, A15** — exactly 3 lines, perfect for the 74×138.

**Step 3 — Connect the decoder.**
- Decoder input **C** ← A15
- Decoder input **B** ← A14
- Decoder input **A** ← A13
- Enable pins: $G1 = 1$, $\overline{G2A} = 0$, $\overline{G2B} = 0$ (permanently enabled)

**Step 4 — Assign outputs to chips.**

| Chip | A15 A14 A13 | Active Output | Address Range (Hex) |
|---|---|---|---|
| ROM-1 | 0 0 0 | $\overline{Y0}$ | `0x0000 – 0x1FFF` |
| ROM-2 | 0 0 1 | $\overline{Y1}$ | `0x2000 – 0x3FFF` |
| RAM-1 | 0 1 0 | $\overline{Y2}$ | `0x4000 – 0x5FFF` |
| RAM-2 | 0 1 1 | $\overline{Y3}$ | `0x6000 – 0x7FFF` |

**Step 5 — Connection diagram (described in text).**
All four chips receive address lines A0–A12 in parallel. Each chip's CS pin is connected to its respective active-low output of the 74×138. Outputs $\overline{Y4}$ to $\overline{Y7}$ are unused (left open or used for future expansion).

> **Marks split (7):** Calculation of n – 1 m; Identification of CS lines – 1 m; Decoder connection – 2 m; Output assignment table – 2 m; Diagram/description – 1 m.

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Memory Map (Hexadecimal):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROM-1: } & 0\text{x}0000 \;\;\text{to}\;\; 0\text{x}1\text{FFF} \\
\text{ROM-2: } & 0\text{x}2000 \;\;\text{to}\;\; 0\text{x}3\text{FFF} \\
\text{RAM-1: } & 0\text{x}4000 \;\;\text{to}\;\; 0\text{x}5\text{FFF} \\
\text{RAM-2: } & 0\text{x}6000 \;\;\text{to}\;\; 0\text{x}7\text{FFF}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification — Total addressable range:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total size} &= 4 \times 8 \text{ KB} = 32 \text{ KB} = 32 \times 1024 = 32768 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Highest address} &= 0\text{x}7\text{FFF} = 32767 \text{ (in decimal)} \\
\text{Lowest address} &= 0\text{x}0000 = 0 \\
\text{Range covered} &= 32767 - 0 + 1 = 32768 \text{ bytes} = 32 \text{ KB} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Checking non-overlap:** Each block ends at `0x1FFF`, `0x3FFF`, `0x5FFF`, `0x7FFF` and the next begins exactly one address higher → **contiguous and non-overlapping** ✓

> **Marks split (7):** Memory map presentation – 3 m; Hex calculations – 2 m; Verification – 2 m.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Option 2 — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
A microcontroller runs at a **clock frequency of 40 MHz**. It must interface with an **external SRAM** that has an access time of **150 ns**.

#### **(a)** Calculate the number of **wait states** required for reliable read/write operations. Show all steps. *(7 marks)*

#### **(b)** Justify the memory selection for the following embedded application: **"A battery-powered wildlife tracking collar that logs GPS coordinates every 5 minutes and operates for 2 years on a single coin cell."** Discuss: (i) the type of non-volatile memory, (ii) volatile memory (if any), (iii) the parameter that most influences your choice, and (iv) the trade-off. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Compute the CPU clock period.**
$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{40 \times 10^6} = 25 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s} = 25 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the wait-state formula.**
$$
W = \left\lceil \frac{T_{\text{MEM}}}{T_{\text{CPU}}} \right\rceil - 1
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values.**
$$
W = \left\lceil \frac{150 \text{ ns}}{25 \text{ ns}} \right\rceil - 1 = \lceil 6.0 \rceil - 1
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate.**
$$
\lceil 6.0 \rceil = 6
$$

**Step 5 — Final answer.**
$$
\boxed{W = 6 - 1 = 5 \text{ wait states}}
$$

**Interpretation:** The CPU must wait 5 extra clock cycles (effective memory access time = 6 × 25 ns = 150 ns).

> **Marks split (7):** $T_{\text{CPU}}$ calculation – 2 m; Formula – 2 m; Substitution & answer – 2 m; Interpretation – 1 m.

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Application:** Battery-powered GPS wildlife tracking collar, 2-year lifetime, logs every 5 minutes.

**Total writes in 2 years:**
$$
\text{Writes} = \frac{2 \times 365 \times 24 \times 60}{5} = 210{,}240 \text{ writes}
$$

**(i) Type of NVM — FRAM (Ferroelectric RAM) or high-endurance EEPROM/MCU Flash with wear-levelling.**
- **Why FRAM:** Endurance of 10¹⁰ to 10¹⁴ cycles → vastly exceeds the required 2.1 × 10⁵ writes.
- **Why not standard Flash:** NOR Flash endurance is typically 10⁴–10⁵ P/E cycles per block → would wear out within months without wear-levelling.
- **Why not NAND:** Block-oriented; not suitable for small, frequent byte-level writes.

**(ii) Volatile memory — Small internal SRAM of the MCU (e.g., 8–32 KB) is sufficient.**
- GPS coordinates are processed and quickly flushed to NVM; large DRAM is unnecessary and power-inefficient.

**(iii) Most influential parameter: Endurance (write cycles).**
- The 2-year operational life combined with frequent writes makes endurance the *limiting constraint*. Capacity (only a few bytes per write) and access speed are secondary.

**(iv) Trade-off:**
- **Cost:** FRAM is more expensive per bit than Flash.
- **Density:** FRAM is lower density than NAND Flash.
- **Justification:** The *ultra-low power* and *extreme endurance* outweigh cost and density penalties for this use-case.

> **Marks split (7):** NVM choice + reasoning – 2.5 m; Volatile RAM choice – 1 m; Dominant parameter – 1.5 m; Trade-off discussion – 2 m.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `-1` in the wait-state formula** — most students write $W = T_{\text{MEM}}/T_{\text{CPU}}$ and lose 1 mark. The correct formula subtracts 1 because the first clock cycle is the "normal" cycle.
> 2. **Confusing address lines with data lines** — A 16-bit address bus has 16 address pins, *not* 16 data pins. Each address pin can address $2^1$ unique cells.
> 3. **Not specifying active-low behaviour for 74×138 outputs** — the outputs are $\overline{Y_i}$ (active LOW). KTU board expects the overbar or the word "active-low" in the answer.
> 4. **Linear decoding accepted as "correct" in KTU but losing marks for "aliases"** — KTU rubric awards full marks for linear decoding *if* the question allows it; otherwise, a comment on the aliasing pitfall earns 1 extra mark.
> 5. **In memory map questions, students often write `0x0000–0xFFFF` for 64 KB without checking if it actually spans the full 16-bit address space** — always verify the upper bound.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read before entering the KTU exam hall:**

- **Memory map** is a logical assignment of address ranges to physical chips; it must be **contiguous, non-overlapping**, and **cover the entire address space** (or be explicitly decoded as "not present").
- **Number of address lines for $N$ locations:** $n = \log_2(N)$. Memorise: **$1\text{K} = 10$, $4\text{K} = 12$, $64\text{K} = 16$, $1\text{M} = 20$** address lines.
- **Memory capacity (bits) = $N \times w$**, where $w$ is word size in bits. Convert to bytes by dividing by 8.
- **Wait states:** $W = \lceil T_{\text{MEM}} / T_{\text{CPU}} \rceil - 1$. Always remember the `-1`! Result must be **≥ 0**.
- **Volatile memories** (SRAM, DRAM) lose data on power-off; **Non-volatile** (ROM, Flash, FRAM, EEPROM) retain data.
- **NOR Flash** = random access, XIP-capable, **slower writes**, used for **boot code**. **NAND Flash** = block/page access, **high density, low cost**, used for **mass storage**.
- **SRAM** = 6 transistors/cell, no refresh, fastest, low density. **DRAM** = 1 T + 1 capacitor, needs periodic refresh, high density.
- **74×138 (3-to-8 decoder):** 3 inputs, 8 active-low outputs, 3 enable pins. Used for **full decoding** of $n$-bit address bus.
- **Linear decoding** uses one address line per chip — simple but creates **address aliases** (multiple addresses → same cell).
- **Full decoding** uses all high-order address lines through a decoder — **no aliases**, every address is unique.
- **Endurance** (write cycles) is the **limiting parameter** for memory in *logging, wear-levelling, and over-the-air update* applications.
- **Cost per bit** = `Price / (Locations × Word Size)`. NAND is cheapest; SRAM is most expensive per bit.
- **Memory hierarchy (top → bottom):** Registers → L1 Cache → L2 Cache → Primary RAM (SRAM/DRAM) → Internal Flash → External Flash → Mass Storage.
- **Key real-world mapping:** Bootloader → NOR Flash; OS/RTOS & App → NAND/eMMC; Working variables → SRAM/DRAM; Config bytes → EEPROM/FRAM.
- **For 14-mark KTU Part B (memory map), always:** (1) Show the calculation of $n_{\text{chip}}$, (2) identify $n_{\text{CS}}$, (3) connect decoder, (4) tabulate address ranges, (5) verify total range.
- **For 14-mark Part B (wait states), always:** (1) Compute $T_{\text{CPU}}$ from $f$, (2) apply formula with `-1`, (3) interpret result, (4) state effect on throughput.
- **Active-low notation:** Chip Select (CS) and decoder outputs in 74×138 are **active-low**; use $\overline{\text{CS}}$, $\overline{Y_i}$ — this is the KTU-board convention.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensors and Actuators
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET695 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sensors and Actuators

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Sensor?

A **sensor** is a physical device, module, or subsystem whose purpose is to detect events or changes in its physical environment and convert that detected information into a form that an embedded controller (typically a microcontroller) can interpret — most commonly an **electrical signal** (analog voltage, current, frequency, or a digital word).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *A sensor is a transducer that converts a physical quantity (such as temperature, pressure, light, displacement, or chemical concentration) into an electrical signal suitable for measurement, display, or further processing by an embedded system.*

Formally, a sensor is the **input transducer** of an embedded system. It maps a physical stimulus from the "real world" into the "electrical world" of the processor.

## 1.2 What is an Actuator?

An **actuator** is the exact inverse of a sensor. It is a device that converts an **electrical control signal** from the embedded processor into a **physical action** (motion, heat, light, magnetic field, or fluid flow). Actuators are the **output transducers** of an embedded system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Terminology Bridge:** In KTU Module 2, the term *transducer* is used for any device that converts one form of energy to another. A **sensor** is an *input transducer*; an **actuator** is an *output transducer*.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine the **human body** as a giant embedded system:

| Human Body Part | Embedded System Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Eyes, ears, skin (touch), nose, tongue | **Sensors** | Detect light, sound, pressure, smell, taste |
| Nerves (afferent) | **Signal conditioning + ADC** | Convert analog sensation to electrical nerve impulses |
| Brain (cortex) | **Microcontroller / Processor** | Decides action based on input |
| Nerves (efferent) | **Driver / DAC / PWM controller** | Sends command signals to muscles |
| Muscles, hands, legs, vocal cords | **Actuators** | Produce motion, sound, force |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Exam:** *"Sensors are SENSES, Actuators are ACTIONS."* Senses bring data **in**; Actions push energy **out**.

## 1.4 Classification of Sensors

Sensors are classified along several axes relevant to the KTU 2024 syllabus:

### A. By Output Signal Type
- **Analog Sensors** — output is a continuous voltage/current proportional to the measurand (e.g., LM35 temperature, MPX pressure).
- **Digital Sensors** — output is a discrete digital word or pulse train (e.g., DHT11, DS18B20, MPU6050 via I²C).

### B. By Power Requirement
- **Active Sensors** — require external excitation (e.g., RTD, strain gauge, thermistor).
- **Passive Sensors** — self-generating (e.g., thermocouple, photodiode, piezoelectric).

### C. By Physical Phenomenon
- Thermal, Mechanical, Optical, Chemical, Magnetic, Acoustic, Biological.

## 1.5 Classification of Actuators

- **Electrical Actuators**: DC motors, stepper motors, solenoids, relays.
- **Hydraulic Actuators**: Cylinders driven by pressurized fluid.
- **Pneumatic Actuators**: Cylinders driven by compressed air.
- **Thermal Actuators**: Heaters, Peltier coolers.
- **Optical Actuators**: LEDs, laser diodes, LCD shutters.
- **Chemical / Biological**: pH pumps, drug delivery systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:** In the context of embedded systems, the three most-examined actuators are **(i) DC Motor, (ii) Stepper Motor, and (iii) Relay/Solenoid**, all controlled via **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** or **GPIO + H-Bridge** drivers.

## 1.6 Why Sensors & Actuators Matter in Embedded Systems

An embedded system without sensors is **"blind"**; without actuators it is **"mute"**. The Sensor → Processor → Actuator chain is the fundamental **perception–cognition–action** loop that defines every IoT device, robot, smart appliance, and industrial controller.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sensor Signal Chain (Input Path)

A physical signal does not directly reach a microcontroller. It passes through a well-defined chain:

$$\text{Physical Stimulus} \rightarrow \text{Sensor} \rightarrow \text{Signal Conditioning} \rightarrow \text{ADC} \rightarrow \text{MCU}$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **Sensing Element** — interacts with the measurand (e.g., a thermistor changes resistance with temperature).
2. **Transduction** — converts the physical change into an electrical change (resistance, voltage, capacitance).
3. **Signal Conditioning** — amplifies weak signals, filters noise, linearizes, and level-shifts.
4. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)** — converts the conditioned analog voltage into a discrete binary code.
5. **MCU Processing** — executes control algorithms and produces actuator commands.

## 2.2 The Actuator Signal Chain (Output Path)

$$\text{MCU} \rightarrow \text{DAC / PWM} \rightarrow \text{Driver / Amplifier} \rightarrow \text{Actuator} \rightarrow \text{Physical Action}$$

**Why a driver is needed:** Microcontroller GPIO pins typically source/sink only **3.3 V at 4–20 mA**. Real-world actuators demand **hundreds of mA to several amps** at higher voltages, so a **driver stage** (transistor, MOSFET, H-bridge, relay, or motor driver IC like L293D) is mandatory.

## 2.3 Static Characteristics of Sensors (KTU Favourite)

These define how accurately a sensor maps input to output:

| Characteristic | Definition | Ideal Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Range** | Min & max input the sensor can measure | Application-specific |
| **Span** | (Max input) − (Min input) | Application-specific |
| **Sensitivity (S)** | Ratio of output change to input change | Constant (for linear sensor) |
| **Linearity** | How closely output vs input fits a straight line | R² ≈ 1 |
| **Accuracy** | Closeness of output to true value | ±0.1 % FS |
| **Precision (Repeatability)** | Ability to reproduce same output for same input | High |
| **Resolution** | Smallest detectable change in input | Smallest LSB |
| **Drift** | Change of output with time/temperature | Zero |
| **Hysteresis** | Difference in output for rising vs falling input | Zero |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **must-memorize** for KTU University Exam. The table uses `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sensor Sensitivity (linear) | $S = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta X_{in}}$ | V/unit | Slope of transfer curve |
| 2 | Linear transfer function | $V_{out} = S \cdot X + V_{offset}$ | V | Y = mX + c form |
| 3 | ADC Resolution (voltage) | $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}-1}$ | V | Smallest detectable step |
| 4 | ADC Digital Output | $D = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^{n}-1) \right\rfloor$ | counts | Floor of full-scale ratio |
| 5 | Input Reconstructed from ADC | $X = \dfrac{D \cdot V_{LSB} - V_{offset}}{S}$ | unit of X | Inverse of transfer fn |
| 6 | RTD Resistance (linear approx.) | $R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha \left( T - T_0 \right) \right]$ | Ω | α = temperature coefficient |
| 7 | Thermistor (B-parameter) | $R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B\left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$ | Ω | T in Kelvin |
| 8 | Thermocouple (Seebeck) | $V = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} S(T)\,dT \approx S \cdot \Delta T$ | V | S in µV/°C |
| 9 | Strain Gauge | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon$ | unitless | GF ≈ 2.0 (typical) |
| 10 | Gauge Pressure | $P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | Pa | Hydrostatic column |
| 11 | LDR (approx. empirical) | $R = A \cdot L^{-\gamma}$ | Ω | γ ≈ 0.7–0.9 |
| 12 | Photo-diode photocurrent | $I_{ph} = R_\lambda \cdot \Phi$ | A | Rλ = responsivity (A/W) |
| 13 | PWM Duty Cycle | $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{period}} \times 100\,\%$ | % | 0–100 |
| 14 | DC Motor Speed (approx.) | $N = V_{applied} - I_a R_a$ proportional | rpm | Back-EMF governed |
| 15 | L293D Logic Supply | $V_{s} = 4.5$ to $36$ V | V | Per datasheet |
| 16 | LM35 Sensitivity | $10$ mV/°C | mV/°C | Linear, 0–150 °C |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU, examiners love to combine the **ADC formula (Row 4)** with the **sensor transfer function (Row 2)**. The typical question asks: *"Given an LM35 connected to a 10-bit ADC with Vref = 5 V, what digital code corresponds to 35 °C?"*

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Sensor | Actuator | Embedded Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Automotive** | MAP sensor, oxygen sensor, ABS wheel speed | Fuel injector, brake actuator | ECU control loops |
| **Healthcare** | ECG electrodes, pulse oximeter, temperature | Insulin pump, ventilator valve | Patient monitoring |
| **Industrial** | Pressure transmitter, flow meter, RTD | Pneumatic valve, servo motor | PLC / SCADA |
| **Consumer IoT** | DHT22 (temp+humidity), PIR, LDR | Buzzer, LED, relay | Smart home |
| **Robotics** | IMU, encoder, ultrasonic, LiDAR | DC/servo/stepper motor, gripper | Mobile robot control |

## 2.6 The "Why" of Signal Conditioning

> [!NOTE]
> **Why can't the sensor output connect directly to the MCU?**
> 1. **Level mismatch** — sensor output may be **mV** while MCU needs **0–3.3 V**.
> 2. **Noise** — small signals are buried in EMI/thermal noise.
> 3. **Impedance** — high-impedance sources load down with the MCU's input impedance.
> 4. **Non-linearity** — sensors like thermistors are exponential; need linearization.

Common conditioning blocks: **Instrumentation amplifier (e.g., INA128)**, **active low-pass filter**, **Wheatstone bridge** (for strain gauges), **cold-junction compensation** (for thermocouples).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: ADC Resolution and Quantization Step

**Problem Setup:** An *n*-bit ADC converts an analog voltage between 0 and $V_{ref}$ into a digital code from 0 to $2^n - 1$.

### Step 1 — Define the Number of Quantization Levels

An *n*-bit ADC has $2^n$ discrete codes. Since 0 is one of them, the number of *intervals* is:

$$N_{levels} = 2^{n} - 1$$

### Step 2 — Define the Resolution (Size of One LSB)

Each step (Least Significant Bit) corresponds to a voltage increment of:

$$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref} - 0}{N_{levels}} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1}$$

### Step 3 — Digital Code for an Input Voltage

The digital output is the integer count of LSBs that fit into $V_{in}$:

$$D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{LSB}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in} \cdot (2^{n} - 1)}{V_{ref}} \right\rfloor$$

### Step 4 — Reconstructing the Input

Inversely, given digital code $D$:

$$V_{in} \approx D \cdot V_{LSB} = D \cdot \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1}$$

**Example Worked Out:** 10-bit ADC, $V_{ref} = 5$ V, find the digital code for $V_{in} = 2.5$ V.

$$D = \left\lfloor \frac{2.5 \times (2^{10} - 1)}{5} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{2.5 \times 1023}{5} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 511.5 \right\rfloor = 511$$

So $D = 0b\,0111111111 = 511_{10}$ — exactly the half-scale point, as expected.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: LM35 Temperature → Digital Code

**Problem Setup:** LM35 has sensitivity $S = 10$ mV/°C, connected to a 12-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V. Find the digital code for $T = 45$ °C.

### Step 1 — Sensor Output Voltage

$$V_{out} = S \times T = 10\,\text{mV/°C} \times 45\,°\text{C} = 450\,\text{mV} = 0.450\,\text{V}$$

### Step 2 — ADC Resolution

$$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1} = \frac{3.3}{2^{12} - 1} = \frac{3.3}{4095} = 8.058 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{V} \approx 0.8058\,\text{mV}$$

### Step 3 — Digital Code

$$D = \left\lfloor \frac{0.450}{8.058 \times 10^{-4}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 558.5 \right\rfloor = 558$$

### Step 4 — Verification (Reverse Mapping)

$$V_{in} \approx 558 \times 0.8058\,\text{mV} = 449.6\,\text{mV} \quad\Rightarrow\quad T = 449.6 / 10 = 44.96\,°\text{C}$$

The small 0.04 °C error is the inherent **quantization error** of the ADC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quantization Error Rule:** The worst-case quantization error is $\pm \frac{1}{2} V_{LSB}$. Always mention this in long-answer solutions for full marks.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Strain Gauge in a Wheatstone Bridge

**Problem Setup:** A strain gauge of nominal resistance $R = 120\ \Omega$ and gauge factor $GF = 2.1$ is bonded to a steel beam. The strain is $\varepsilon = 800\ \mu\varepsilon$ (microstrain). Find $\Delta R$.

### Step 1 — Apply the Strain Gauge Equation

The fundamental strain gauge equation is:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

### Step 2 — Solve for $\Delta R$

$$\Delta R = GF \cdot \varepsilon \cdot R = 2.1 \times (800 \times 10^{-6}) \times 120$$

$$\Delta R = 2.1 \times 9.6 \times 10^{-2} = 0.2016\ \Omega$$

### Step 3 — Bridge Output Voltage

Placed in one arm of a Wheatstone bridge with excitation $V_{ex} = 5$ V:

$$V_{out} \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{5}{4} \times (2.1 \times 800 \times 10^{-6}) = 2.1 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V} = 2.1\,\text{mV}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 2.1 mV is a problem:** This is far too small for a direct ADC. An **instrumentation amplifier with gain ≈ 1000** is required to bring it to ≈ 2.1 V. This is exactly why signal conditioning is non-optional.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: PWM Duty Cycle and Average Motor Voltage

**Problem Setup:** A DC motor is driven by a PWM signal with $T_{on} = 1.8$ ms and $T_{period} = 4$ ms, supply $V_s = 12$ V. Find the average motor voltage.

### Step 1 — Duty Cycle

$$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{period}} = \frac{1.8}{4.0} = 0.45 = 45\,\%$$

### Step 2 — Average Voltage (ideal, ignoring ripple)

$$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s = 0.45 \times 12 = 5.4\,\text{V}$$

### Step 3 — Motor Speed (assuming linear)

$$N = N_{max} \cdot D = N_{max} \times 0.45$$

So the motor runs at **45 % of its no-load maximum speed** with **45 % of rated torque capacity**.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Sensor Selection by Application

| Measurand | Recommended Sensor | Output | Range | KTU Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature (–55 to 150 °C) | LM35 | Analog 10 mV/°C | –55 to 150 °C | Embedded thermostat |
| Temperature (digital, high accuracy) | DS18B20 | 1-Wire digital | –55 to 125 °C | Weather station |
| Humidity | DHT22 | Digital (single-wire) | 0–100 % RH | IoT climate monitor |
| Pressure (gas) | MPX5700AP | Analog 0–5 V | 0–700 kPa | Tyre pressure |
| Pressure (precision) | Strain gauge + bridge | mV differential | ±5000 µε | Load cell |
| Light (intensity) | LDR / photodiode | Resistance / current | Wide | Automatic streetlight |
| Distance (0.02–4 m) | HC-SR04 ultrasonic | Digital pulse | 2–400 cm | Obstacle avoidance |
| Position / angle | Rotary encoder | Quadrature pulses | 360° | Robotic arm |
| Acceleration / tilt | MPU6050 | I²C/SPI digital | ±16 g | Self-balancing bot |
| Gas concentration | MQ-2/MQ-135 | Analog + digital | ppm | Air-quality monitor |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Complete Sensor–Actuator Loop (Block Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **perception–cognition–action** loop of a generic embedded system and the role of sensors/actuators within it.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PHEN[Physical World Stimulus] --> SENSBLK
    SENSBLK[Sensor Module] --> SCOND[Signal Conditioning Amplifier Filter]
    SCOND --> ADC[ADC Converter]
    ADC --> MCU[Microcontroller Decision Algorithm]
    MCU --> DRV[Driver Stage H-Bridge MOSFET Relay]
    DRV --> ACT[Actuator DC Motor Stepper Relay LED]
    ACT --> PHENACT[Physical Action on World]
    PHENACT --> PHEN

    classDef sensor fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A06000,color:#000
    classDef compute fill:#A0CFFF,stroke:#003F7F,color:#000
    classDef actuator fill:#C5F0B0,stroke:#2F6F00,color:#000
    classDef world fill:#F0F0F0,stroke:#555,color:#000

    class PHEN,PHENACT world
    class SENSBLK,SCOND,ADC sensor
    class MCU compute
    class DRV,ACT actuator
```

## 4.2 Sensor Subsystem Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENSE_UNIT [Sensing Element]
        SE[Physical Interaction e.g. bimetallic strip thermistor membrane]
    end
    subgraph TRANS_UNIT [Transduction Element]
        TR[Convert Physical to Electrical Resistance Voltage Capacitance]
    end
    subgraph COND_UNIT [Signal Conditioning]
        AMP[Instrumentation Amplifier]
        FIL[Low Pass Filter]
        LIN[Linearization LUT or Equation]
        LSH[Level Shifter]
    end
    subgraph DIG_UNIT [Digitization]
        ADC12[ADC n bit]
        BUS[Parallel I2C SPI Output]
    end

    SE --> TR --> AMP --> FIL --> LIN --> LSH --> ADC12 --> BUS
```

## 4.3 Actuator Subsystem Architecture (with Driver Isolation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MCUPIN[GPIO or PWM Pin 3.3 V Low Current] --> DRVIC[Driver IC L293D ULN2003 MOSFET]
    PWR[External Power Supply 5V 12V 24V] --> DRVIC
    DRVIC --> ACTUATOR[Actuator Motor Solenoid Heater]
    ACTUATOR --> FB[Feedback Sensor Encoder Thermistor]
    FB --> MCUPIN
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Notice the **feedback loop** at the bottom — real embedded systems are **closed-loop**. A DC motor is paired with an **encoder**; a heater is paired with a **thermistor**. The MCU reads the encoder/thermistor and adjusts the PWM duty cycle continuously. This is the essence of a **PID controller** in KTU Module 3.

## 4.4 Common Sensor Interfaces (One-View Summary)

| Interface | Signal Type | Example Sensor | Pins Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| Analog voltage | Single-ended | LM35, LDR via divider | 1 data + GND |
| Resistive | Bridge / divider | Thermistor, RTD, LDR | 1 ADC pin + 2 reference |
| I²C | 2-wire digital | MPU6050, BMP280 | SDA, SCL + 2 power |
| SPI | 4-wire digital | MAX6675 thermocouple, ADXL345 | MOSI, MISO, SCK, CS |
| UART | Asynchronous serial | GPS, Bluetooth modules | TX, RX + 2 power |
| 1-Wire | Single-wire digital | DS18B20 | DQ + GND |
| PWM output | Pulse train | HC-SR04 echo pin | 1 GPIO |
| Frequency/Counter | Pulse rate | Flow sensor | 1 GPIO with timer |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between a sensor, a transducer, and an actuator. Give one example of each used in an embedded system.**

**Model Answer:**

- A **transducer** is the generic term for any device that converts one form of energy into another.
- A **sensor** is a type of transducer that converts a *physical stimulus* into an *electrical signal* — i.e., it is an **input transducer**. *Example: LM35 converts temperature to voltage.*
- An **actuator** is the opposite — it converts an *electrical control signal* into a *physical action* — i.e., an **output transducer**. *Example: DC motor converts PWM voltage to rotation.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation cue (1 mark):** Stating the direction of energy conversion (physical → electrical vs electrical → physical) is what fetches the differentiation mark. Examples are mandatory for full 3 marks.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is the function of signal conditioning in a sensor interface? List any two conditioning blocks.**

**Model Answer:**

Signal conditioning prepares the **raw sensor output** for the microcontroller's ADC by performing four functions:
1. **Amplification** — boost weak signals (mV) to ADC range (0–3.3 V).
2. **Filtering** — remove high-frequency EMI and 50/60 Hz mains hum.
3. **Linearization** — correct the non-linear transfer function of sensors like thermistors.
4. **Level shifting / isolation** — protect the MCU from over-voltage and ground loops.

**Two conditioning blocks:** Instrumentation amplifier (e.g., INA128) and active low-pass filter (op-amp + RC network).

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Pattern:** KTU Module 2 frequently tests *why* each conditioning block is needed, not just *what* it is. Always pair a block with the problem it solves.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete sensor signal chain** from physical stimulus to digital value inside a microcontroller. Discuss the role of each block in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An **LM35** temperature sensor is connected to the **10-bit ADC** of an ATmega328P microcontroller. The ADC reference voltage is $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V, and the LM35 output sensitivity is $10$ mV/°C. Compute the digital output code when the temperature is $32$ °C, and the **minimum temperature resolution** the system can detect. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

The sensor signal chain is the **input pipeline** of any embedded system. The blocks, in order, are:

| Block | Function | Example IC / Component |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensor** | Converts physical quantity → electrical signal | LM35, MPX5700 |
| **Signal Conditioner** | Amplifies, filters, linearizes | INA128, op-amp filters |
| **Multiplexer** | Selects one of N analog inputs | CD4051, internal in MCU |
| **Sample-and-Hold** | Holds input stable during conversion | SHA inside ADC |
| **ADC** | Converts analog voltage to digital code | 10-/12-bit SAR ADC |
| **MCU Register** | Stores the digital code | ADC data register |

**Explanation of each block:**

1. **Sensor:** The transduction element reacts to the measurand (e.g., LM35 produces a voltage proportional to its case temperature).
2. **Signal Conditioning:** Real sensor outputs are weak (mV-level) and noisy. An instrumentation amplifier (gain 10–1000) raises the signal; a low-pass filter removes RF noise; linearization (LUT) compensates for non-linear sensors.
3. **Multiplexer:** Most MCUs have a single ADC but multiple analog pins (e.g., ATmega328P has 8). The analog MUX selects which pin is currently digitized.
4. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H):** The ADC needs a stable input during conversion (a few µs). The S/H capacitor captures the instantaneous voltage and isolates it from input transients.
5. **ADC:** Performs quantization — maps the continuous 0–$V_{ref}$ range into $2^n$ discrete levels using successive approximation (SAR) or dual-slope techniques.
6. **MCU Register:** The digital code is stored in a memory-mapped register, read by firmware, and processed by the control algorithm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Block diagram** (a Mermaid version): see Section 4.2 of these notes. Drawing this earns the remaining 1 mark.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute LM35 output voltage at 32 °C:**

$$V_{out} = S \times T = 10\,\text{mV/°C} \times 32\,°\text{C} = 320\,\text{mV} = 0.320\,\text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute one LSB (resolution in voltage):**

$$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1} = \frac{5.0}{2^{10} - 1} = \frac{5.0}{1023} = 4.887 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V} \approx 4.887\,\text{mV}$$

**Step 3 — Compute digital code $D$:**

$$D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{out}}{V_{LSB}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{0.320}{4.887 \times 10^{-3}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 65.48 \right\rfloor = 65$$

**Step 4 — Minimum detectable temperature (temperature resolution):**

One LSB corresponds to a voltage step of $4.887$ mV. Since LM35 produces 10 mV per °C:

$$\Delta T_{min} = \frac{V_{LSB}}{S} = \frac{4.887\,\text{mV}}{10\,\text{mV/°C}} = 0.4887\,°\text{C} \approx 0.49\,°\text{C}$$

**Final Answers:** $D = 65$ (binary $0001000001$); $\Delta T_{min} \approx 0.49\,°\text{C}$.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the working of a **strain gauge** sensor. Derive the expression for gauge factor and show how it is interfaced using a **Wheatstone bridge** with the help of a circuit diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **DC motor** used in a robotic arm is driven via an **L293D H-bridge driver** from the PWM output of an ARM Cortex-M0 microcontroller. The motor is rated 12 V, 1.5 A, and the PWM frequency is 1 kHz with 60 % duty cycle. Calculate the **average voltage applied to the motor**, and explain how the **direction of rotation** is controlled by the L293D. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Working Principle of a Strain Gauge:**

A strain gauge is a **passive resistive sensor** consisting of a thin metallic foil (or semiconductor) pattern bonded to a flexible backing. When the host structure (beam, shaft, diaphragm) is stressed, the gauge deforms by a strain $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$. This geometric change alters the gauge's resistance by $\Delta R$, given by:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \frac{\Delta L}{L} - \frac{\Delta A}{A} = (1 + 2\nu)\varepsilon + \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}$$

where $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio and $\rho$ is the resistivity. Defining the **Gauge Factor (GF)**:

$$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$

For metallic foils, $GF \approx 2.0$; for semiconductors, $GF$ can be 100–200.

**Wheatstone Bridge Interface:**

A single $\Delta R$ is too small to measure directly. The strain gauge is placed in one arm of a Wheatstone bridge, with three fixed precision resistors. The bridge output voltage is:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4} - \frac{R_g + \Delta R}{R_g + \Delta R + R_1} \right)$$

For a balanced bridge (all $R$ equal initially, $V_{out} = 0$), the small change simplifies to:

$$V_{out} \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

The differential output (in mV) is then amplified by an **instrumentation amplifier** before being digitized by the ADC.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Average Voltage:**

$$V_{avg} = D \times V_{supply} = 0.60 \times 12\,\text{V} = 7.2\,\text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Average Current (assuming resistive model for simplicity):**

$$I_{avg} \approx D \times I_{rated} = 0.60 \times 1.5\,\text{A} = 0.9\,\text{A}$$

**Step 3 — Direction Control with L293D:**

The L293D contains **two H-bridges** (4 half-H drivers per chip). Each H-bridge has four transistors arranged so that the motor terminals can be connected to either $V_s$ or GND. The direction is set by two logic inputs per channel:

| IN1 | IN2 | Function |
|---|---|---|
| HIGH | LOW | Motor spins **forward** (current flows left → right) |
| LOW | HIGH | Motor spins **reverse** (current flows right → left) |
| LOW | LOW | Motor **coasts** (freewheels, no braking) |
| HIGH | HIGH | Motor **brakes** (both terminals grounded) |

The third pin **EN (enable)** is tied to the MCU's PWM output. By varying the duty cycle on EN, the **average voltage — and hence the speed — is controlled**, while IN1/IN2 set the direction. This separation of *speed* (PWM) and *direction* (logic) is the standard pattern for all motor-driver ICs (L293D, L298N, DRV8871, etc.).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting the enable pin's role** in PWM speed control (–2 marks).
> 2. **Confusing IN1/IN2 with EN** — IN pins select direction, EN modulates speed. Examiners will deduct 1–2 marks if you swap them.
> 3. **Not mentioning the freewheeling diodes** inside the L293D that protect against back-EMF from the motor's inductance.
> 4. **Omitting the unit** of the duty cycle in the final answer.
> 5. **Not connecting back to "embedded"** — always close with how the MCU firmware sets the PWM timer register (e.g., TIM2->CCR1 = 60 % of ARR).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Module 2:**

- [ ] **Sensor** = input transducer (physical → electrical). **Actuator** = output transducer (electrical → physical). *Mnemonic: Senses in, Actions out.*
- [ ] **Three static characteristics** most tested: **Range, Sensitivity, Linearity**. Three dynamic: **Bandwidth, Response time, Settling time**.
- [ ] **ADC Resolution formula:** $V_{LSB} = V_{ref} / (2^n - 1)$. **Digital code:** $D = \lfloor V_{in} (2^n - 1) / V_{ref} \rfloor$.
- [ ] **LM35 sensitivity** = $10$ mV/°C, linear, 0–150 °C. **LM335** = 10 mV/K, Kelvin scale.
- [ ] **RTD equation:** $R_T = R_0 [1 + \alpha(T - T_0)]$. Platinum PT100 has $\alpha = 0.00385$.
- [ ] **Thermistor:** B-parameter exponential; **non-linear**; needs linearization LUT.
- [ ] **Strain Gauge:** $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$. Need **Wheatstone bridge + INA**.
- [ ] **LDR** is non-linear ($R = A L^{-\gamma}$); use it in a **voltage divider** not as a direct ADC input.
- [ ] **HC-SR04** distance = $(\text{echo pulse width} \times 0.034) / 2$ metres.
- [ ] **PWM Duty Cycle** = $T_{on}/T_{period} \times 100\,\%$. Frequency choice: **1–25 kHz for motors** (above audible 20 kHz preferred to avoid whining).
- [ ] **L293D** = dual H-bridge, **600 mA continuous per channel**, 1.2 A peak; needs **separate logic (5 V) and motor supply**.
- [ ] **Direction control** is via IN1/IN2 logic levels; **Speed control** is via PWM on EN pin.
- [ ] **MPU6050** = 6-axis IMU (3-axis accel + 3-axis gyro), I²C address 0x68 (AD0=0) or 0x69 (AD0=1).
- [ ] **DHT22** uses a proprietary single-wire protocol (not Dallas 1-Wire) — frame is 40 bits.
- [ ] **Signal conditioning** has four pillars: **Amplify, Filter, Linearize, Isolate**.
- [ ] **Active sensors** need excitation; **passive sensors** generate their own voltage/current.
- [ ] Always quote **units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners will deduct ½ mark per missing unit.
- [ ] The **perception → cognition → action** loop is the **central dogma of every embedded system** — repeat it in any descriptive answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Communication Interface: Onboard and External Communication Interfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET695 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Communication Interface: Onboard and External Communication Interfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Communication Interface in Embedded Systems?

In the context of **Embedded System Design (PEAET695)**, a **Communication Interface** is a standardized hardware and software protocol mechanism that enables the transfer of data (bits, bytes, frames, or packets) between two or more entities within an embedded product or between the product and the outside world. The entities may be microcontrollers, sensors, actuators, memory chips, displays, or external computing devices.

A communication interface defines **four essential engineering layers**:

1. **Physical Layer** – voltage levels, pin counts, connector types, and wiring topology.
2. **Data Link Layer** – framing, addressing, error detection, and synchronization.
3. **Transport/Protocol Layer** – packet format, handshaking, and flow control.
4. **Application Layer** – how the firmware interprets the payload.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** A *Communication Interface* is a set of well-defined electrical and logical rules governing the exchange of binary data between Integrated Circuits (ICs) on a single PCB, or between the embedded device and peripheral networks/instruments.

## 1.2 Classification: Onboard vs. External Interfaces

KTU Module 2 categorizes communication interfaces into two broad families based on the **physical reach** and **use case**:

| Family | Reach | Typical Use | Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Onboard Communication Interfaces** | Centimeters (intra-PCB, intra-chassis) | Chip-to-chip / board-level data exchange | UART, SPI, I²C, CAN, 1-Wire, Parallel Bus |
| **External Communication Interfaces** | Meters to Kilometers (off-board) | Device-to-device / device-to-cloud / device-to-instrument | USB, Ethernet, RS-485, RS-232, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, ZigBee, LoRa, CAN-FD, SPI over fiber |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **modern office building**:

- **Onboard Communication** is the **internal telephone exchange / intercom system** that connects rooms on the same floor. It is fast, short-distance, low-power, and uses simple wiring. The "rooms" are the **microcontroller, ADC, EEPROM, and sensor** sitting on the same PCB.
- **External Communication** is the **public telephone network, broadband internet, or radio towers** that connect the building to the outside world. It needs more sophisticated protocols, error handling, and regulatory compliance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** The same *protocol* (e.g., UART or SPI) can be onboard or external depending on the **physical layer driver** used. A UART with TTL levels is onboard; the same UART with RS-232 line drivers (±12 V) is treated as external.

## 1.4 Foundational Vocabulary Students MUST Memorise

- **Baud Rate (Bd):** Number of signal changes (symbols) per second. Each symbol may carry 1 or more bits. **Unit: symbols/second (baud)**.
- **Bit Rate (bps):** Number of bits transmitted per second = Baud Rate × bits per symbol.
- **Throughput:** Useful data rate *after* overheads (start/stop bits, addressing, acknowledgements). **Unit: bytes/sec or bits/sec**.
- **Full-Duplex:** Both directions simultaneously (e.g., SPI, UART with two lines).
- **Half-Duplex:** One direction at a time (e.g., I²C, 1-Wire).
- **Simplex:** One direction only (e.g., broadcast radio).
- **Master/Slave (or Controller/Peripheral):** Bus arbiter terminology in I²C, SPI, UART-multidrop.
- **Synchronous:** A shared **clock line** accompanies data.
- **Asynchronous:** No shared clock; both sides agree on a **pre-configured timing** and resync using start bits.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Communication Interfaces

Communication interfaces are classified along **three orthogonal axes** that the examiner frequently tests:

### Axis A — Topology

1. **Point-to-Point (P2P):** Exactly two endpoints. e.g., full-duplex UART, full-duplex SPI, USB device-to-host.
2. **Multi-Drop / Multi-Master Bus:** More than two devices share the same physical wires. e.g., I²C, CAN, RS-485.
3. **Daisy-Chain:** Devices connected in series; data passes from one to the next. e.g., SPI with multiple slaves, JTAG.

### Axis B — Clocking Strategy

1. **Synchronous:** A dedicated **SCLK** (Serial Clock) line drives the bit transitions. The master provides the clock. Examples: SPI, I²C, I²S, QSPI, JTAG.
2. **Asynchronous:** No clock line. Both endpoints must be pre-configured to the **same baud rate** and resynchronize on a **start bit**. Examples: UART (RS-232, RS-485), LIN, IrDA.
3. **Isochronous:** Mixed – fixed bandwidth with relaxed timing, used in streaming (e.g., USB, CAN, audio I²S).

### Axis C — Data Direction

1. **Simplex** – one direction only.
2. **Half-Duplex** – alternating direction, one shared data line.
3. **Full-Duplex** – simultaneous, two data lines (MOSI + MISO, TX + RX).

## 2.2 Onboard Communication Interfaces — Engineering Deep-Dive

### 2.2.1 UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter)

- **Topology:** Point-to-point, asynchronous, full-duplex.
- **Lines:** TXD, RXD, (optional) RTS/CTS for hardware flow control.
- **Frame Format:** `START bit (0) | DATA (5–9 bits, LSB first) | [Parity] | STOP bit(s) (1)`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Fact:** UART is *asynchronous* — both devices must agree on **baud rate, parity, stop bits, and data length** in advance. Any mismatch causes **framing errors**.

### 2.2.2 SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface) — Motorola Standard

- **Topology:** Master–Slave(s), synchronous, full-duplex.
- **Lines (4-wire standard):** SCLK, MOSI (Master-Out-Slave-In), MISO (Master-In-Slave-Out), SS/CS (Slave Select, active low).
- **Clock Modes (CPOL, CPHA):** 4 modes (0, 1, 2, 3) defined by clock polarity and phase.

> [!NOTE]
> SPI has **no formal standard** (unlike I²C). Every vendor picks CPOL/CPHA; the firmware must match the slave's datasheet.

### 2.2.3 I²C (Inter-Integrated Circuit) — NXP/Philips Standard

- **Topology:** Multi-master, multi-slave, half-duplex, synchronous.
- **Lines (only 2!):** SDA (Serial Data), SCL (Serial Clock).
- **Addressing:** 7-bit (or 10-bit) slave address. **Total address space: 2^7 = 128 devices** (7-bit) or **2^10 = 1024 devices** (10-bit). Some addresses are reserved.
- **Open-Drain Bus:** Both SDA and SCL use open-drain with pull-up resistors → **wired-AND logic** → supports multi-master arbitration.
- **Speeds:** Standard-mode **100 kHz**, Fast-mode **400 kHz**, Fast-mode Plus **1 MHz**, High-speed **3.4 MHz**, Ultra-fast **5 MHz** (unidirectional).

### 2.2.4 CAN (Controller Area Network) — Bosch Standard

- **Topology:** Multi-master, differential (CAN-H, CAN-L), asynchronous NRZ with bit stuffing.
- **Speeds:** Classic CAN up to **1 Mbps** (40 m bus), CAN FD up to **5 Mbps** (data phase).
- **Frame:** SOF, Arbitration (11-bit or 29-bit ID), Control, Data (0–8 bytes classic), CRC, ACK, EOF.
- **Priority by ID:** Lower ID = Higher priority due to wired-AND arbitration.
- **Why it matters:** Dominant in automotive ECUs.

### 2.2.5 Parallel Bus (e.g., 8080, 6800, EBI, FMC, FSMC)

- Used for high-speed on-board devices: **external SRAM, NOR Flash, LCD TFT, FPGA**.
- 8/16/32 data lines + address lines + control lines (CS, WE, OE, RD).
- Bandwidth scales linearly with bus width.

## 2.3 External Communication Interfaces — Engineering Deep-Dive

### 2.3.1 RS-232 (Recommended Standard 232)

- **Origin:** 1960s, designed for modem/terminal communication.
- **Voltage levels:** Logic 1 = **−3 V to −25 V**, Logic 0 = **+3 V to +25 V** (inverted from TTL!).
- **Max cable length:** ~15 m at 19.2 kbps; degrades with baud.
- **Topology:** P2P, full-duplex (TX, RX, GND + handshakes).
- **Use today:** Debug console, industrial instruments, legacy equipment.

### 2.3.2 RS-485

- **Voltage levels:** Differential **±1.5 V minimum** across a twisted pair.
- **Max cable length:** up to **1200 m** (with repeaters: more).
- **Max devices:** 32 unit loads (256 with high-impedance transceivers).
- **Modes:** Half-duplex (2-wire) or Full-duplex (4-wire).
- **Use today:** Industrial networks (Modbus RTU), building automation.

### 2.3.3 USB (Universal Serial Bus)

- **Topology:** Host–Device star, with hubs. **Polled** by host.
- **Speeds:** Low-Speed **1.5 Mbps**, Full-Speed **12 Mbps**, High-Speed **480 Mbps**, SuperSpeed **5 Gbps** and above.
- **Power delivery:** up to 100 W (USB-PD).
- **Classes:** HID, CDC, MSD, Audio, Video, Vendor — each defines a protocol layer.

### 2.3.4 Ethernet (IEEE 802.3)

- **Topology:** Star with switches; point-to-point link with PHY.
- **Speeds:** 10 Mbps, 100 Mbps, 1 Gbps, 10 Gbps, 25/40/100 Gbps.
- **Layers:** PHY + MAC + TCP/UDP/IP.
- **Connector:** RJ-45 with magnetics; uses **differential pairs** (Cat5e/Cat6).

### 2.3.5 Wireless (IEEE 802.15 / Cellular)

- **Wi-Fi (802.11):** Up to ~1 Gbps, ~50 m indoor. Heavy protocol stack (TCP/IP).
- **Bluetooth / BLE (802.15.1):** 1–3 Mbps classic, 2 Mbps BLE. PAN.
- **ZigBee (802.15.4):** 250 kbps, mesh, low-power, used in sensor networks.
- **LoRa (proprietary LPWAN):** 0.3 – 50 kbps, **up to 15 km** range, sub-GHz ISM.
- **NB-IoT / LTE-M:** Cellular, narrowband, mMTC.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The vertical bar `|` is used as a column separator in markdown tables. To avoid breaking tables, absolute-value bars are typeset with the LaTeX command `\vert` (or `\mid`).

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Standard Value | Unit | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Bit rate (synchronous) | $f_{bit} = f_{SCLK}$ | bps | SPI, I²S, parallel |
| 2 | UART bit time | $T_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{Baud}$ | s | UART timing |
| 3 | UART frame time | $T_{frame} = (1 + n_{data} + n_{parity} + n_{stop}) \cdot T_{bit}$ | s | One byte duration |
| 4 | Effective UART throughput | $\eta_{UART} = \dfrac{n_{data}}{1+n_{data}+n_{parity}+n_{stop}}$ | fraction | Useful data / total bits |
| 5 | I²C pull-up resistor | $R_{pull} = \dfrac{V_{DD} - V_{OL}}{I_{OL}}$ (also check $t_{r}$) | Ω | Bus design |
| 6 | I²C max bus capacitance | $C_{bus} \le 400\,pF$ (Standard/Fast) | F | Fan-out limit |
| 7 | SPI throughput | $R_{SPI} = f_{SCLK} \times 8 \text{ (per byte, 1 bit/clock)}$ | bps | Sensor data rate |
| 8 | CAN max bus length (rule of thumb) | $L_{max} \approx \dfrac{40\,m \cdot 1\,Mbps}{f_{baud}}$ | m | At lower baud, longer bus |
| 9 | RS-232 max cable (rule) | $L_{max} \approx \dfrac{25\,m}{Baud/9.6kbps}$ (approx.) | m | Practical guideline |
| 10 | RS-485 max cable | **$L_{max} = 1200\,m$** at ≤ 100 kbps | m | Industrial spec |
| 11 | Shannon–Hartley channel capacity | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | bps | Theoretical wireless limit |
| 12 | Bit-rate ↔ byte-rate | $R_{bytes} = \dfrac{R_{bits}}{8}$ | Bps | Conversion |
| 13 | Duty cycle of clock | $D = \dfrac{t_{high}}{t_{high}+t_{low}} \times 100\%$ | % | SPI modes |
| 14 | Power-delay product | $PDP = P \times t_{delay}$ | J | Low-power design metric |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** CAN bus for ECU-to-ECU, LIN for low-cost sub-bus, FlexRay/Ethernet for ADAS and infotainment.
- **IoT:** BLE for personal-area sensors, Wi-Fi for high-throughput streaming, LoRa for remote telemetry, NB-IoT for cellular M2M.
- **Industrial PLCs:** RS-485 + Modbus RTU is still the de-facto field-bus because of its robustness and long cable runs.
- **Consumer Electronics:** I²C links the touch-screen controller, audio codec, PMIC, and camera modules to the application processor. SPI connects the display and the flash memory.
- **Medical Devices:** SPI for ADC front-end (e.g., ECG), USB for data dump, Bluetooth for patient telemetry.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section derives the most frequently asked **numerical** relations used in KTU 2024 Scheme papers. Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly. Multi-line equations are wrapped in a single `\begin{aligned} ... \end{aligned}` block and are sandwiched by exactly one blank line.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: UART Frame Time and Effective Throughput

**Problem setup.** Configure a UART channel at **115 200 baud**, with **8 data bits, no parity, 1 stop bit** (the most common "8-N-1" configuration). Find (a) the bit period, (b) the frame time per byte, (c) the effective throughput in bytes per second, and (d) the **overhead fraction** lost to start/stop bits.

### Step 1 — Identify the frame structure

The standard "8-N-1" frame consists of:

- 1 start bit (always logic 0),
- 8 data bits (LSB first),
- 0 parity bits,
- 1 stop bit (always logic 1).

Therefore the total number of bit-periods per character is

$$ n_{frame} = 1 + 8 + 0 + 1 = 10 \text{ bits} $$

### Step 2 — Compute the bit period

The bit period is the reciprocal of the baud rate (in asynchronous serial, 1 symbol = 1 bit).

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{bit} &= \frac{1}{Baud} \\
&= \frac{1}{115\,200} \text{ seconds} \\
&\approx 8.6805 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} \\
&\approx 8.68\,\mu s
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Frame time per byte

The frame time is the total bit-periods multiplied by the bit period.

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{frame} &= n_{frame} \times T_{bit} \\
&= 10 \times 8.6805 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} \\
&\approx 86.805 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} \\
&\approx 86.81\,\mu s
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Maximum byte throughput

The reciprocal of the frame time gives the maximum number of complete bytes that can be transmitted per second.

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{byte} &= \frac{1}{T_{frame}} \\
&= \frac{1}{86.805 \times 10^{-6}} \text{ B/s} \\
&\approx 11\,520 \text{ B/s}
\end{aligned}$$

This is exactly **Baud ÷ 10** in "8-N-1" mode — a **classic KTU result**.

### Step 5 — Overhead (efficiency) calculation

Efficiency is the ratio of useful data bits to total transmitted bits per frame.

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{UART} &= \frac{n_{data}}{n_{frame}} \\
&= \frac{8}{10} \\
&= 0.8 \;\; (\text{or } 80\%)
\end{aligned}$$

So **20% of every frame is overhead** for a 115 200-baud link, giving a useful bit rate of

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{useful} &= \eta_{UART} \times Baud \\
&= 0.8 \times 115\,200 \\
&= 92\,160 \text{ bps}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum I²C Pull-Up Resistor Sizing

**Problem setup.** A **3.3 V** I²C bus operates in **Fast-Mode (400 kHz)**. The bus capacitance is **C_bus = 200 pF** (typical for a few devices on a short trace). Find the maximum pull-up resistor that still allows a clean rising edge.

### Step 1 — Required rise time for Fast-Mode

The I²C specification (UM10204) demands a maximum rise time of

$$ t_{r,max} = 300\,ns \quad \text{(Fast-Mode)} $$

### Step 2 — RC charging relation

For an open-drain line pulled high through a pull-up resistor $R_{pull}$, the rise time (10 % to 90 %) is approximately

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{r} &\approx 2.2 \cdot R_{pull} \cdot C_{bus}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $R_{pull}$

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{pull,\,max} &= \frac{t_{r,max}}{2.2 \cdot C_{bus}} \\
&= \frac{300 \times 10^{-9}}{2.2 \times 200 \times 10^{-12}} \\
&= \frac{300 \times 10^{-9}}{440 \times 10^{-12}} \\
&= 682\,\Omega
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Engineering interpretation

A pull-up smaller than **≈ 680 Ω** is safe in Fast-Mode for 200 pF. Smaller R → faster rise → lower value of $t_r$ → safer margin, but **higher current consumption** when the device pulls the line low. The **current sunk** by a device pulling low is

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{sink} &= \frac{V_{DD} - V_{OL}}{R_{pull}} \\
&= \frac{3.3 - 0.4}{682} \;\text{(typical }V_{OL} = 0.4 V\text{)} \\
&\approx 4.25\,mA
\end{aligned}$$

This must be within the sink-current rating of the I/O pin (typically 3 mA or 6 mA). **A standard KTU trick question** tests whether the student picks a $V_{OL}$ that matches the I/O spec.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: CAN Bus Length vs. Bit Rate Trade-off

**Problem setup.** A classic CAN bus uses a propagation delay of approximately **5 ns/m** and a one-way cable delay of $L \cdot t_{prop}/m$. The bit time must be at least **5 × propagation delay** to allow propagation, arbitration, and processing.

### Step 1 — Propagation delay along cable

For a bus of length $L$ metres,

$$ t_{prop} = 5\,ns \times L $$

### Step 2 — Required bit time

Empirically, the maximum bus length follows

$$ L_{max} \approx \frac{40\,m}{Baud_{Mbps}} $$

### Step 3 — Plug in 1 Mbps

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{max} &\approx \frac{40\,m}{1} \\
&= 40\,m
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Plug in 125 kbps

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{max} &\approx \frac{40\,m}{0.125} \\
&= 320\,m
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **inverse-proportional trade-off** the examiner loves: lower baud → longer bus.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Onboard vs. External Interfaces (the ultimate comparison table)

| Parameter | UART | SPI | I²C | CAN | RS-232 | RS-485 | USB 2.0 | Ethernet 100BASE-TX |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Synchronous?** | No | Yes | Yes | No (bit-stuff) | No | No | Yes (SOF/EOP) | Yes (clock in line) |
| **Wires** | 2 (TX, RX) | 4 (SS, SCLK, MOSI, MISO) | 2 (SDA, SCL) | 2 (CAN-H, CAN-L) | 2 + GND | 2 (half) / 4 (full) | 2 (D+, D−) | 4 (2 pairs) |
| **Duplex** | Full | Full | Half | Half | Full | Half/Full | Half (with PIPE) | Full |
| **Max devices** | 2 | Many (via CS) | 127 (7-bit) | Many (bus) | 2 | 32 UL | 127 (hub) | Many (switch) |
| **Speed class** | Low–Mid | Mid–High | Low–Mid | Mid | Low | Low–Mid | High | Very High |
| **Max rate** | ~1 Mbps typical | ~100 Mbps (some) | 5 MHz | 1 M / 5 M (FD) | ~115 kbps typical | 10 Mbps | 480 Mbps | 100 Mbps |
| **Max distance** | ~15 m | < 1 m | < 1 m | 40 m @ 1 M | ~15 m | 1200 m | ~5 m | 100 m |
| **Driver cost** | Very low | Low | Low | Mid | Low | Low–Mid | Mid | Mid–High |
| **Typical role** | Debug, GPS, BT-modem | Flash, ADC, Display | Sensors, RTC, EEPROM | Automotive ECU | Legacy console | Industrial | PC peripherals | Networked MCU |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory aid for the exam:** *UART is for the "outside world" (console), SPI is for the "fast peripherals" (display, flash), I²C is for the "low-speed sensor farm" (temp, RTC, GPIO-expander), CAN is for the "automotive backbone", and Ethernet/USB is for "high-bandwidth PC/cloud".*

## 3.5 Derivation 4: SPI Throughput with Multi-I/O (QSPI / OSPI)

**Problem setup.** A modern MCU drives a Quad-SPI NOR flash at **SCLK = 50 MHz**, with **4 data lines (IO0–IO3)**. Find the throughput in **MB/s** when reading sequentially.

### Step 1 — Bits per clock

With Quad-SPI, every clock edge carries 4 bits.

$$ n_{bits/clk} = 4 $$

### Step 2 — Bit rate

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{bit} &= f_{SCLK} \times n_{bits/clk} \\
&= 50 \times 10^6 \times 4 \\
&= 200 \times 10^6 \text{ bps} = 200\,Mbps
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to bytes per second

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{byte} &= \frac{R_{bit}}{8} \\
&= \frac{200 \times 10^6}{8} \\
&= 25 \times 10^6 \text{ B/s} = 25\,MB/s
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the reason modern MCUs prefer QSPI / OSPI for **execute-in-place (XIP) boot** — a 25 MB/s data rate is enough to stream code from a small external flash without any internal copy.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**Differentiate between synchronous and asynchronous serial communication. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Synchronous | Asynchronous |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Clock line | Shared clock transmitted alongside data | No clock line; sender and receiver use pre-agreed timing |
| Synchronization | Continuous, at every bit | Re-synchronized at the start bit of every frame |
| Framing overhead | Small (1-byte packets or no overhead) | High (start + stop bits per byte) |
| Speed capability | High (tens of MHz to GHz) | Low–mid (typically ≤ 1 Mbps) |
| Examples | SPI, I²C, I²S, QSPI, USB, Ethernet | UART (RS-232, RS-485), LIN, IrDA |

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

**List the four signals of a standard 4-wire SPI bus. Explain the role of CPOL and CPHA.**

**Model Answer:**
The four signals are:
1. **SCLK** – Serial Clock, generated by the master.
2. **MOSI** – Master Out, Slave In.
3. **MISO** – Master In, Slave Out.
4. **SS / CS** – Slave Select, active low; tells the slave to listen.

- **CPOL (Clock Polarity)** – defines the idle level of SCLK (0 = low idle, 1 = high idle).
- **CPHA (Clock Phase)** – defines which edge of SCLK is used to sample data (0 = leading edge, 1 = trailing edge).

The combination (CPOL, CPHA) gives **4 SPI modes: 0, 1, 2, 3**. Master and slave must be configured to the **same mode** to communicate.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### 🔹 Question A — 14 Marks  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an **I²C bus**. Discuss the role of the open-drain drivers, the START/STOP conditions, and the ACK bit. What is the maximum number of devices that can be connected to a standard 7-bit-addressed I²C bus?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **UART, SPI and I²C** in terms of (i) number of wires, (ii) speed, (iii) duplex mode, (iv) addressing, and (v) typical applications. Recommend a suitable interface to connect (1) a temperature sensor, (2) a 1.8-inch TFT display, and (3) a GPS module to an STM32-class MCU. Justify each choice.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **I²C bus** uses only **two bidirectional open-drain lines**:

- **SCL** – Serial Clock, generated by the master.
- **SDA** – Serial Data, open-drain to allow **wired-AND** behavior.

**Block diagram (textual):**

```
                Vdd
                 |
              [ Rp ]   <-- Pull-up resistor
                 |
   ---SCL--------+------- to all devices (open-drain)
   ---SDA--------+------- to all devices (open-drain)
       |         |
   Master      Slave 1
   (MCU)       (e.g., 0x50 EEPROM)
                Slave 2
                (e.g., 0x68 RTC)
                Slave 3
                (e.g., 0x20 IO expander)
```

**Role of open-drain drivers:**
- Any device can pull the line **LOW** (active), but **no device can drive HIGH**. The HIGH level is set externally by the pull-up resistor.
- This **wired-AND** behavior implements **arbitration** for multi-master operation: if two masters transmit simultaneously, the one sending a HIGH while the other sends a LOW loses the bus (because the line stays LOW).

**START and STOP conditions:**
- **START (S):** SDA falls while SCL is HIGH. Initiates a transfer.
- **STOP (P):** SDA rises while SCL is HIGH. Terminates a transfer.
- A **REPEATED START (Sr)** is a START issued without a preceding STOP; used to change direction without releasing the bus.

**ACK bit:**
- After every byte, the **receiver** pulls SDA LOW during the 9th clock pulse to acknowledge. Master transmits 1 byte → receiver ACKs. Master reading from slave → master ACKs every byte except the last (NACK).
- If the line stays HIGH during the 9th pulse, this is a **NACK** (no acknowledge) — used to signal end-of-read or address-not-present.

**Maximum devices on 7-bit I²C:**
- 7 bits → 2⁷ = **128 possible addresses**. However, addresses 0x00 (general call) and 0x78–0x7F (reserved by NXP for future use) cannot be used, leaving **112** usable addresses. Standard answer expected by KTU: **127 devices** (accept either 128 or 112 depending on the rubric).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | UART | SPI | I²C |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Number of wires | 2 (TX, RX) | 4 (SCLK, MOSI, MISO, CS) | 2 (SDA, SCL) |
| Speed (typical) | ≤ 1 Mbps | ≤ 50 MHz (some 100 MHz) | ≤ 1 MHz (Fast-mode+) |
| Duplex | Full | Full | Half |
| Addressing | None (point-to-point) | None (uses CS) | 7-bit or 10-bit |
| Multi-device | No | Yes (via CS lines) | Yes (built-in) |
| Typical use | Debug console, GPS, BT | Flash, ADC, display | Sensors, RTC, EEPROM |

**Recommendations:**

1. **Temperature sensor** → **I²C**. Justification: low data rate, low pin budget, multiple sensors can share one bus at distinct addresses (e.g., LM75 @ 0x48, MCP9808 @ 0x18). I²C’s open-drain design is also robust on PCB traces.

2. **1.8-inch TFT display** → **SPI** (4-wire). Justification: TFT requires continuous high-throughput pixel streams; SPI’s full-duplex synchronous bus can deliver 10–40 Mbps easily. Many ST7789/ILI9341 displays accept SPI mode 0. Alternative: parallel 8080 bus for very high refresh, but SPI saves pins.

3. **GPS module** → **UART**. Justification: GPS modules (u-blox NEO-6/7/8, AT6558R) typically expose a UART with NMEA 0183 sentences at 9600 baud. UART is standard, point-to-point, easy to configure (8-N-1).

### 🔹 Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **CAN bus** frame format in detail. Discuss the **arbitration** mechanism, the **role of bit stuffing**, and the reason CAN uses **differential signalling** on CAN-H and CAN-L. State the maximum number of nodes allowed in a standard 11-bit CAN 2.0A network.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An embedded data-acquisition board must send **1 million 16-bit samples per second** to a remote host located 800 m away. Choose between RS-485 and Ethernet. Justify the choice with a calculation of minimum bandwidth requirement, and describe the hardware (transceivers, connectors, magnetics) and protocol stack needed.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — CAN Bus Frame Format

A standard **CAN 2.0A data frame** has the following fields (in order):

| Field | Length | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **SOF** (Start of Frame) | 1 bit (dominant 0) | Marks start of frame |
| **Arbitration Field** | 11-bit ID + 1 RTR bit | Identifier + request type |
| **Control Field** | IDE, r0, 4-bit DLC | DLC = data length code (0–8) |
| **Data Field** | 0–8 bytes | Payload |
| **CRC Field** | 15-bit CRC + delimiter | Error detection |
| **ACK Field** | 2 bits | Acknowledgement slot |
| **EOF** | 7 recessive bits | Marks end of frame |
| **IFS** | 3 bits | Inter-frame space |

**Arbitration mechanism:**
- The bus uses **wired-AND** of dominant (0) and recessive (1) bits. A dominant bit **overwrites** a recessive one.
- Each transmitter monitors the bus while sending. If it sends recessive (1) but reads dominant (0), it has lost arbitration and **backs off**, allowing the lower-ID message to win.
- **Lower 11-bit ID = higher priority.** This is a *non-destructive* arbitration (no time is lost by the winning message).

**Bit stuffing:**
- After 5 consecutive identical bits, the transmitter **inserts a stuff bit** of opposite polarity. The receiver removes it.
- Purpose: ensures enough edges on the bus for **synchronization** using a PLL at the receiver (NRZ encoding otherwise has no edges for clock recovery).

**Differential signalling on CAN-H / CAN-L:**
- Dominant: CAN-H ≈ 3.5 V, CAN-L ≈ 1.5 V → differential ≈ **2 V**.
- Recessive: CAN-H ≈ CAN-L ≈ 2.5 V → differential ≈ **0 V**.
- Common-mode noise (e.g., from the alternator in a car) appears equally on both lines and is **rejected by the differential receiver**, giving CAN excellent **EMC immunity**.

**Max nodes on 11-bit CAN 2.0A:** Practically, the bus supports **as many nodes as the electrical loading allows** (each transceiver ≈ 1 unit load; standard says **up to 32 nodes** at 1 Mbps on a 40 m bus, and **more** at lower bit rates). The 11-bit ID gives **2¹¹ = 2048 unique message IDs**, not a node limit.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Bandwidth Calculation and Interface Choice

**Step 1 — Compute required raw data rate**

1 million samples/sec × 16 bits/sample = **16 × 10⁶ bps = 16 Mbps** of payload.

**Step 2 — Account for protocol overhead**

- **RS-485** with Modbus RTU at 8-N-1 → 20% overhead → ≥ 20 Mbps. But RS-485’s electrical spec only guarantees **up to 10 Mbps**, and **only at very short distances** (< 30 m at 10 Mbps). At 800 m, the recommended rate is **≤ 100 kbps**. **RS-485 cannot carry 16 Mbps over 800 m.** ❌
- **Ethernet 100BASE-TX** operates at 100 Mbps and is spec'd to **100 m per segment**. Using an **Ethernet switch + fiber media converter** (or two copper switches at 100 m + fiber backbone) can easily cover 800 m. **Ethernet is the correct choice.** ✅

**Step 3 — Hardware and stack**

- **PHY chip:** e.g., LAN8720A, DP83848, KSZ8041 — connects MCU's RMII/MII to the line.
- **Magnetic transformer + RJ-45 jack** (with integrated magnetics, e.g., Würth 7490110124).
- **MAC:** integrated in the MCU (STM32, ESP32, i.MX RT, etc.) or external.
- **Protocol stack:** LwIP or uIP for TCP/UDP/IP. For real-time data, use **UDP** (low overhead) over IP, or **TCP** if reliability is required.
- **At 800 m, use fiber:** 100BASE-FX (1300 nm) with SC connectors, plus a media converter at each end. The total budget is ample for 16 Mbps.
- **Alternative:** use an **RS-485 daisy chain with multiple nodes** *only* if the data rate is pre-aggregated and decimated. With 1 M samples/s × 16 bits, **RS-485 is not feasible.**

**Conclusion:** Use **100BASE-TX Ethernet with fiber extender** for 800 m at 16 Mbps.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes reported by KTU valuators:**
> 1. Confusing **UART as synchronous** because some diagrams show a "clock" — that clock is for the **internal baud-rate generator**, not a shared wire.
> 2. Saying **"I²C supports 128 devices"** without mentioning the 16 reserved addresses (0x00 general call + 0x78–0x7F NXP reserved). Always state **112 usable** in formal answers.
> 3. Spelling **MOSI / MISO** as **Mosi / Miso** — case-sensitivity not graded, but **misdefining them** (e.g., "MISO = Master In Slave Out" → ❌ "Slave In Master Out" loses a mark).
> 4. Drawing **SPI** with one data line (single MOSI/MISO) instead of two — the **4-wire standard** is the expected default.
> 5. **RS-232 voltage levels inverted** by students who think it uses 0/3.3 V TTL. Examiner specifically docks 1 mark if the answer says "RS-232 uses TTL levels".
> 6. Forgetting **ACK bit position** in I²C (it is the **9th** clock pulse, not part of the 8 data bits).
> 7. In CAN, writing **"higher ID = higher priority"** — it is the **lower ID that wins arbitration**.
> 8. Computing **UART throughput** as `Baud / 8` instead of `Baud / 10` for 8-N-1.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-revision checklist — print this before entering the exam hall:**

- [ ] **UART** is asynchronous, full-duplex, 2 wires (TX, RX), frame = 1 start + 8 data + parity + stop, baud rate must match both ends.
- [ ] **SPI** is synchronous, full-duplex, 4 wires (SCLK, MOSI, MISO, CS), 4 modes (CPOL × CPHA), no formal standard, fastest among simple onboard buses.
- [ ] **I²C** is synchronous, half-duplex, 2 wires (SDA, SCL), open-drain, multi-master with arbitration, 7-bit (112 usable) or 10-bit addressing, speeds 100 kHz / 400 kHz / 1 MHz / 3.4 MHz.
- [ ] **CAN** is differential, dominant-recessive arbitration, lower-ID-wins, classic 1 Mbps up to 40 m, message IDs 2¹¹ or 2²⁹, bit stuffing every 5 bits.
- [ ] **RS-232** uses ±3 to ±25 V (inverted from TTL), max ~15 m, point-to-point, still common on debug consoles.
- [ ] **RS-485** is differential, up to 1200 m at ≤ 100 kbps, 32 unit loads, used in Modbus RTU industrial networks.
- [ ] **USB** is host-driven, polled, hierarchical star, 480 Mbps (HS), plug-and-play with classes (HID, CDC, MSD, Audio).
- [ ] **Ethernet** is 100 m per copper segment, switched, full-duplex; use fiber for > 100 m; speeds 10/100/1000 Mbps and beyond.
- [ ] **Wireless families:** Wi-Fi (high throughput, IP stack), BLE (low power PAN), ZigBee (mesh sensor), LoRa (long range LPWAN), NB-IoT/LTE-M (cellular M2M).
- [ ] **Synchronous → clock line present**; **Asynchronous → start/stop bits + agreed baud**.
- [ ] **Open-drain / open-collector** is essential for I²C and CAN, enabling **wired-AND arbitration**.
- [ ] **Pull-up resistor sizing** for I²C: $R_{pull,max} = t_{r,max} / (2.2 \cdot C_{bus})$.
- [ ] **UART throughput** at 8-N-1 = **Baud/10** bytes per second; overhead = **20%**.
- [ ] **Bit stuffing in CAN** ensures enough edges for clock recovery.
- [ ] **Differential signalling** (CAN, RS-485, Ethernet, USB) rejects common-mode noise.
- [ ] **Multi-device buses** → I²C (addressing), SPI (chip select), CAN (ID), RS-485 (unique address).
- [ ] **Speed-distance trade-off** → lower baud → longer cable (RS-232, RS-485, CAN).
- [ ] **QSPI / OSPI** deliver **4× / 8×** the bit rate of single-SPI at the same SCLK.
- [ ] **Always quote the KTU-recommended max numbers:** I²C = 112/127 devices, CAN 2.0A = 11-bit ID (2¹¹), RS-485 = 32 UL, RS-232 = 15 m.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RTOS Based Embedded System Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET695 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** RTOS Based Embedded System Design # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is an RTOS? A **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)** is a specialized operating system designed to manage hardware resources, execute multiple concurrent tasks, and process data within **strictly bounded response times** (deterministic deadlines) for embedded and time-critical applications. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > *"A Real-Time Operating System is an operating system intended to serve real-time applications that process data as it comes in, typically without buffer delays. The key characteristic is determinism — the time taken to respond to an event must be guaranteed within a specified deadline."* ### Formal Characteristics of an RTOS: - **Determinism** — Guaranteed worst-case response time - **Responsiveness** — Fast interrupt handling and context switching - **Reliability** — Failsafe behavior in mission-critical systems - **Concurrency** — Multi-tasking through preemptive scheduling - **Small Footprint** — Optimized for resource-constrained embedded hardware ### Comparison: RTOS vs GPOS (General-Purpose OS) | Parameter | RTOS | GPOS | |---|---|---| | Primary Goal | Predictable timing | High throughput | | Scheduling | Priority + Preemptive | Fair share / Time-sharing | | Latency | Microsecond-level | Millisecond-level | | Kernel Size | Few KB to MB | Hundreds of MB | | Use Case | Embedded control, IoT | Desktops, Servers | --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Hospital Emergency Room 🏥 > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — Understanding RTOS Intuition** Imagine two kitchens: 🍳 **Home Kitchen (GPOS-like):** The cook prepares dishes one at a time, optimizes for the *average* serving speed, and doesn't care if your soup arrives 10 minutes late. 🚑 **Hospital Emergency Room (RTOS-like):** A heart-attack patient **must** be attended to within *exactly* 3 minutes. Triage prioritization ensures the most critical case gets handled first — predictability over fairness. In the **ER analogy**: - **Patients** = Tasks/Threads - **Triage Nurse** = Scheduler - **Doctors** = CPU execution units - **ICU Beds** = Critical resources (Mutex protected) - **Patient Transfers** = Inter-Process Communication (IPC) An RTOS is the **"Triage Nurse"** of embedded systems — it decides *what runs next* and *for how long*, ensuring critical deadlines are never missed. --- ## 1.3 Types of Real-Time Systems > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three classes of real-time systems (mandatory for KTU exams):** ### 1. Hard Real-Time System - Missing a deadline = **System Failure** (catastrophic) - Maximum tolerable latency is **strictly bounded** - **Examples:** Airbag deployment, Anti-lock Braking System (ABS), Pacemaker, Nuclear reactor control ### 2. Soft Real-Time System - Missing a deadline = **Degraded Quality** (acceptable but not ideal) - Latency is bounded but occasional misses tolerable - **Examples:** Video streaming, Audio playback, Web servers, VoIP ### 3.
