Firm Real-Time System - Missing a deadline = **Result is useless**, but no system damage - Late results are discarded - **Examples:** Financial trading systems, Video frame processing --- ## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Bold for Memorization) > [!NOTE] > **Critical RTOS Metrics every KTU student must know:** - **Response Time** = Time from event occurrence to task execution start - **Throughput** = Number of tasks completed per unit time - **Jitter** = Variation in response time across multiple executions - **Context Switch Time** = Time to save/restore CPU state (typically **< 10 μs**) - **Interrupt Latency** = Time from interrupt signal to first instruction of ISR - **Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET)** = Maximum time a task requires CPU # 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 RTOS Architecture (Layered Model) The RTOS architecture is structured in well-defined layers, each with a specific responsibility: ### Layer 1: Hardware Layer - Microcontroller/Microprocessor - Memory (ROM, RAM) - I/O peripherals - Timers, Interrupt controllers ### Layer 2: Kernel (Microkernel vs Monolithic) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Kernel Types — High-Yield KTU Topic** | Feature | Monolithic Kernel | Microkernel | |---|---|---| | Architecture | All services in kernel space | Minimal services in kernel space | | Size | Large (100s of KB) | Small (10-50 KB) | | Performance | Fast (no mode switches) | Slower (more context switches) | | Reliability | Lower | Higher (fault isolation) | | Examples | Linux, μC/OS-II | QNX, VxWorks, FreeRTOS | ### Layer 3: System Services - Task management - Memory management - Interrupt handling - IPC mechanisms ### Layer 4: Application Layer - User-defined tasks - Middleware - Device drivers --- ## 2.2 Tasks and Task States A **Task (Thread)** is the basic unit of execution in an RTOS. Each task has its own: - Stack - Program counter (PC) - Registers - Task Control Block (TCB) ### Task States (KTU Favorite Question ⭐) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The 5 Standard Task States:** 1. **Ready** — Task is ready to run, waiting for CPU 2. **Running** — Task is currently executing on CPU 3. **Blocked (Waiting)** — Task is waiting for an event/resource 4. **Suspended** — Task is explicitly put on hold 5. **Terminated (Deleted)** — Task has completed or been killed ### State Transitions: - `Ready → Running` (Scheduler dispatches) - `Running → Ready` (Preempted by higher priority) - `Running → Blocked` (Waiting for event) - `Blocked → Ready` (Event occurred) - `Running → Terminated` (Task completed) --- ## 2.3 Task Scheduling Algorithms ### 2.3.1 Non-Preemptive Scheduling - Once a task starts, it runs to completion - Simple but unpredictable - **Disadvantage:** High-priority tasks can be starved ### 2.3.2 Preemptive Scheduling ⭐ - Higher-priority tasks can interrupt running tasks - **Context switching** occurs at priority boundaries - **Standard in real-time systems** ### 2.3.3 Priority-Based Scheduling - Each task assigned a static (or dynamic) priority - Highest priority ready task always runs ### 2.3.4 Round-Robin Scheduling - Equal time quantum for all same-priority tasks - **Used in:** Time-sharing systems - **Equation:** Time Quantum = Total Time / Number of Tasks ### 2.3.5 Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) ⭐ - Static priority = Inverse of period - Shorter period → Higher priority - Optimal for **static, periodic** task sets ### 2.3.6 Earliest Deadline First (EDF) ⭐ - Dynamic priority based on absolute deadline - Optimal dynamic scheduling algorithm - Can achieve **100% CPU utilization** --- ## 2.4 Inter-Process Communication (IPC) Mechanisms > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 3 — IPC is a frequently asked topic** ### 1. Semaphores - **Binary Semaphore (0 or 1):** For signaling/mutex-like behavior - **Counting Semaphore:** For resource pools (e.g., 5 UART ports) - Operations: `wait()` (P) and `signal()` (V) ### 2. Mutex (Mutual Exclusion) - **Binary lock** for protecting critical sections - Includes **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** to prevent inversion - **Different from binary semaphore:** Ownership concept ### 3. Message Queues - Task-to-task **asynchronous** data transfer - FIFO-based buffer - Supports variable message sizes ### 4. Mailboxes - Single-message buffer - Used for fixed-size inter-task messages ### 5. Event Flags - Bitwise signaling between tasks - 8/16/32-bit flag groups ### 6. Pipes - Unidirectional byte streams - Useful for streaming data --- ## 2.5 Priority Inversion Problem > [!WARNING] > **Priority Inversion is a classic KTU question worth 7+ marks!** **Scenario:** - Task **H** (High priority) - Task **M** (Medium priority) - Task **L** (Low priority) - H and L share a **Mutex (M)** **Timeline:** 1. L acquires Mutex M 2. H starts, tries to lock M → **Blocked** 3. M preempts L (M has higher priority than L) 4. L cannot release M until M finishes 5. H is **unbounded blocked** — system fails! **Solution: Priority Inheritance Protocol** - When H blocks on M, L temporarily inherits H's priority - L cannot be preempted by M - L finishes, releases M, H acquires it --- ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | CPU Utilization (RMS) | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | Ratio (0 to 1) | | 2 | Liu & Layland Bound | $U_{bound} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | For n tasks under RMS | | 3 | Response Time | $R_i = C_i + I_i$ | $I_i$ = Interference from higher-priority tasks | | 4 | Interference (RMS) | $I_i = \sum_{j: P_j > P_i} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | Sum of preemptions | | 5 | Time Quantum (RR) | $Q = \frac{T_{total}}{N}$ | $N$ = number of tasks | | 6 | Context Switch Time | $T_{cs} = T_{save} + T_{restore}$ | Microseconds | | 7 | Interrupt Latency | $T_{latency} = T_{detect} + T_{save}$ | Bounded constant | | 8 | Throughput | $\eta = \frac{N_{tasks}}{T_{window}}$ | Tasks/second | | 9 | Deadline Check (EDF) | $D_i \geq R_i$ | For schedulability | | 10 | Slack Time | $S_i = D_i - R_i$ | Buffer before deadline | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL:** The pipe symbol `\vert` is used in tables to prevent markdown table breaking. --- ## 2.7 Popular RTOS Platforms (Industry Examples) | RTOS | Type | Application Domain | |---|---|---| | **FreeRTOS** | Open-source, Microkernel | IoT, Wearables, STM32 | | **μC/OS-II / III** | Commercial, Preemptive | Medical, Aerospace | | **VxWorks** | Commercial, Hard RTOS | Mars Rover, Avionics | | **QNX** | Microkernel | Automotive (BlackBerry QNX) | | **RTEMS** | Open-source | Space (NASA) | | **Zephyr** | Open-source | IoT, Wearables | # 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) Schedulability Test > [!IMPORTANT] > **This derivation is a 14-mark KTU favorite!** ### Given Parameters for a task set: - $C_i$ = Worst-case execution time of task $i$ - $T_i$ = Period of task $i$ (and implicitly its deadline) - $n$ = Total number of tasks ### Step 1: Define CPU Utilization The CPU utilization for a task set is the fraction of processor time used by the tasks: $$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$$ ### Step 2: Liu & Layland's Sufficient Schedulability Condition (1973) For $n$ periodic tasks with deadlines equal to periods, scheduled under RMS, the task set is **guaranteed to be schedulable** if: $$U \leq n \cdot \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$ ### Step 3: Derivation of the Bound **Logic of derivation:** Consider the **critical instant** — when all tasks start simultaneously. The longest response time for task $n$ occurs at this instant. #### Step 3a: Total demand in interval $[0, t]$ The processor demand in interval $[0, t]$ is the sum of execution times of all task instances that have deadlines in this interval: $$W(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_i} \right\rceil C_i$$ #### Step 3b: Condition for schedulability The task set is schedulable if and only if there exists a $t \leq T_{max}$ such that: $$W(t) \leq t$$ #### Step 3c: Upper bound analysis Using the inequality $\left\lceil x \right\rceil \leq x + 1$: $$ \begin{aligned} W(t) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_i} \right\rceil C_i \\ &\leq \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \frac{t}{T_i} + 1 \right) C_i \\ &= t \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \\ &= t \cdot U + C_{total} \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 3d: For schedulability, we need $W(t) \leq t$: $$t \cdot U + C_{total} \leq t$$ $$C_{total} \leq t(1 - U)$$ $$t \geq \frac{C_{total}}{1 - U}$$ #### Step 3e: Liu & Layland's bound emerges For the **worst-case ratio**, when $T_i = 2 C_i$ (heavy tasks), the bound is maximized at: $$U_{bound} = \lim_{n \to \infty} n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) = \ln(2) \approx 0.693$$ ### Step 4: Numerical Example > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example for Board Exam:** **Task Set:** | Task | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) | Priority | |---|---|---|---| | T1 | 1 | 4 | High | | T2 | 2 | 6 | Medium | | T3 | 3 | 10 | Low | #### Step 4a: Compute Utilization $$U = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{6} + \frac{3}{10} = 0.25 + 0.333 + 0.30 = 0.883$$ #### Step 4b: Compute Liu & Layland Bound for $n=3$ $$U_{bound} = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 = 0.7797$$ #### Step 4c: Compare $$U = 0.883 > U_{bound} = 0.7797$$ **Conclusion:** The sufficient condition is **not satisfied**, but this is a **sufficient, not necessary** condition. We must perform the **exact Response Time Analysis** to confirm schedulability. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Exact Response Time Analysis (RTA) ### Iterative Algorithm The response time $R_i$ of task $i$ is computed iteratively: $$ R_i^{(0)} = C_i $$ $$ R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j $$ The iteration **converges** when $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$ (or $R_i^{(k+1)} > T_i$ → **not schedulable**). ### Applying to the Example: **For Task T3** (lowest priority, $C_3 = 3$, $T_3 = 10$): Higher priority tasks: T1 ($C_1 = 1, T_1 = 4$), T2 ($C_2 = 2, T_2 = 6$) #### Iteration 1: $$R_3^{(1)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 1 + 2 = 6$$ #### Iteration 2: $$R_3^{(2)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7$$ #### Iteration 3: $$R_3^{(3)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 4 = 9$$ #### Iteration 4: $$R_3^{(4)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10$$ #### Iteration 5: $$R_3^{(5)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10$$ **Converged!** $R_3 = 10$ ms, and since $D_3 = T_3 = 10$ ms: $$R_3 = D_3 \implies \text{Task T3 is just barely schedulable.}$$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Task Design Decisions > [!IMPORTANT] > **Decision Matrix for KTU exam scenarios:** | Scenario | Recommended Scheduling | IPC Mechanism | Why | |---|---|---|---| | Periodic sensor reading | RMS with static priority | Message Queue | Predictable timing | | Asynchronous UI update | Priority-based preemptive | Mailbox | Event-driven | | Shared peripheral access | Preemptive + Mutex | Mutex with PIP | Avoid priority inversion | | Audio/Video streaming | Soft real-time, RR | Pipe | High throughput | | Emergency stop button | Hard real-time, ISR + Semaphore | Binary Semaphore | Guaranteed latency | --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Context Switch Overhead The total overhead of a context switch involves: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{context\_switch} &= T_{save} + T_{restore} \\ &= T_{save\_regs} + T_{save\_stack} + T_{load\_regs} + T_{load\_stack} \end{aligned} $$ For an ARM Cortex-M3 running at 72 MHz, a typical $T_{context\_switch} \approx 1.5 \, \mu s$. The **effective CPU utilization** considering context switches: $$U_{eff} = U_{task} + n_{sw} \cdot T_{context\_switch} \cdot f_{sw}$$ Where: - $n_{sw}$ = number of context switches per unit time - $f_{sw}$ = frequency of context switching - $U_{task}$ = task-level CPU utilization # 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 RTOS Layered Architecture (Block-Level Flow) ```mermaid graph TD A[APPLICATION LAYER
User Tasks and Middleware] --> B[SERVICE LAYER
Task Management, IPC, Memory] B --> C[KERNEL LAYER
Scheduler, Dispatcher, Interrupts] C --> D[HARDWARE ABSTRACTION LAYER HAL
Device Drivers, BSP] D --> E[HARDWARE LAYER
CPU, RAM, ROM, Peripherals] F[SCHEDULER] --> C G[DISPATCHER] --> C H[INTERRUPT HANDLER] --> C I[TASK CONTROL BLOCK TCB POOL] --> B J[SEMAPHORE QUEUE] --> B K[MESSAGE QUEUE POOL] --> B ``` ## 4.2 Task State Transition Diagram ```mermaid graph LR S1[READY STATE] -- Scheduler Dispatch --> S2[RUNNING STATE] S2 -- Preempted by Higher Priority --> S1 S2 -- Wait for Event or Resource --> S3[BLOCKED STATE] S3 -- Event Occurred or Resource Available --> S1 S2 -- Suspend Call --> S4[SUSPENDED STATE] S4 -- Resume Call --> S1 S2 -- Task Complete or Delete --> S5[TERMINATED STATE] ``` ## 4.3 RTOS Scheduling Decision Flow ```mermaid graph TD A[CPU Idle or Interrupt] --> B{Scheduler Activated} B --> C[Save Current Task Context to TCB] C --> D[Check Ready Queue] D --> E{Highest Priority Ready Task Found} E -- Yes --> F[Load New Task Context from TCB] F --> G[Execute Task] E -- No --> H[Run Idle Task] G --> I{Task Blocked or Preempted} I -- Blocked --> D I -- Preempted --> D I -- Completed --> J[Mark as Terminated] ``` ## 4.4 Priority Inversion Scenario ```mermaid graph TD A[LOW PRIORITY TASK L STARTS] --> B[L ACQUIRES MUTEX M] B --> C[HIGH PRIORITY TASK H ARRIVES] C --> D[H REQUESTS MUTEX M] D --> E[H BLOCKED WAITING FOR M] E --> F[MEDIUM PRIORITY TASK M ARRIVES] F --> G[M PREEMPTS L] G --> H[H STILL WAITING INDEFINITELY] H --> I[PRIORITY INVERSION BUG] I --> J[SOLUTION: PRIORITY INHERITANCE] J --> K[L INHERITS H PRIORITY TEMPORARILY] K --> L[L NOT PREEMPTED BY M] L --> M[L RELEASES MUTEX M] M --> N[H ACQUIRES MUTEX AND EXECUTES] ``` ## 4.5 IPC Mechanism Selection Block Flow ```mermaid graph TD A[NEED FOR IPC] --> B{Type of Communication} B -- SIGNAL or EVENT --> C[BINARY SEMAPHORE] B -- COUNTING RESOURCE --> D[COUNTING SEMAPHORE] B -- MUTUAL EXCLUSION --> E[MUTEX with PIP] B -- MESSAGE PASSING --> F[MESSAGE QUEUE] B -- SINGLE MESSAGE --> G[MAILBOX] B -- BITWISE FLAGS --> H[EVENT FLAGS] B -- STREAM DATA --> I[PIPE] C --> J[TASK SYNCHRONIZATION COMPLETE] D --> J E --> J F --> J G --> J H --> J I --> J ``` ## 4.6 Memory-Mapped RTOS Block Architecture ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "FLASH MEMORY" F1[Bootloader] F2[RTOS Kernel Code] F3[Application Code] F4[Constants and Lookup Tables] end subgraph "RAM MEMORY" R1[Kernel Data Structures] R2[Task Control Blocks TCBs] R3[Task Stacks] R4[Message Queues] R5[Heap for Dynamic Allocation] end subgraph "CPU REGISTERS" C1[Program Counter PC] C2[Stack Pointer SP] C3[General Purpose Registers] C4[Status Register with Interrupt Flags] end ``` # 🏆 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📌 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define Real-Time Operating System. Differentiate between Hard and Soft real-time systems.** **Model Answer:** A Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) is an operating system designed to process data and respond to events within strictly bounded time constraints, ensuring deterministic behavior. The key characteristic is **predictability** — completion of tasks within guaranteed deadlines. | Parameter | Hard Real-Time | Soft Real-Time | |---|---|---| | Deadline | **Strict, non-negotiable** | **Flexible, degrades gracefully** | | Failure Impact | Catastrophic (system failure) | Reduced quality | | Example | Airbag, Pacemaker | Video streaming, Audio | > **Memory Aid:** Hard = Life-critical, Soft = Entertainment-critical. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] What is a Task Control Block (TCB)? List its key fields.** **Model Answer:** A Task Control Block (TCB) is a kernel data structure that stores all information about a task, allowing the RTOS to manage task state, context switching, and scheduling. **Key fields of a TCB:** - Task ID (unique identifier) - Task State (Ready, Running, Blocked, Suspended) - Task Priority - Stack Pointer (SP) - Program Counter (PC) - CPU Registers (saved context) - Task Function Pointer - Pointer to Stack Memory - Task Delay/Timeout values - Linked List Pointers (for ready/blocked queues) --- ## 📌 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - Students commonly **lose 2-3 marks** for not drawing **task state transition diagrams** > - Always **state assumptions** explicitly in scheduling problems > - **Show all iteration steps** in Response Time Analysis — partial marks are not given for skipping steps > - Draw **Gantt charts** wherever possible in scheduling questions --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Explain the architecture of an RTOS with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the scheduler, dispatcher, and interrupt handler.** **Model Solution:** The RTOS architecture consists of four primary layers working in concert: **1. Hardware Layer:** The bottom-most layer containing the physical microcontroller, memory, timers, and I/O peripherals. **2. Kernel Layer (Core RTOS Engine):** Contains the most critical components: - **Scheduler:** Decides which task runs next based on priority and scheduling algorithm (RMS, EDF, Round-Robin). Maintains ready queue. - **Dispatcher:** Performs the actual context switch — saves current task state to TCB, loads new task context, transfers CPU control. - **Interrupt Handler:** Manages asynchronous hardware events. ISRs have minimal execution time, defer work to deferred procedure calls (DPCs) or tasks. **3. Service Layer:** Provides system calls (APIs) for: - Task creation/deletion - Semaphores, Mutex, Message Queues - Memory allocation (malloc/free variants) - Time management (delays, timeouts) **4. Application Layer:** User-written tasks and middleware libraries. **Role of Key Components:** - The **scheduler** is the "brain" — it determines *what* runs next. - The **dispatcher** is the "executor" — it actually performs the *context switch*. - The **interrupt handler** is the "reactor" — it responds to *external events*. #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] With a neat diagram, explain the various states of a task in RTOS. Describe priority inversion and its solution.** **Model Solution:** *(Draw the Task State Transition Diagram as shown in SECTION 4.2)* **Task States:** 1. **Ready:** Task is in the ready queue, awaiting CPU. 2. **Running:** Task currently executing on the CPU (only ONE task at a time in single-core). 3. **Blocked (Waiting):** Task suspended, waiting for an event, semaphore, or I/O completion. 4. **Suspended:** Task explicitly put on hold by another task; not in ready queue. 5. **Terminated:** Task has completed execution or been explicitly deleted. **Transitions:** - `Ready → Running`: Scheduler dispatch - `Running → Ready`: Preempted by higher-priority task - `Running → Blocked`: Task requests unavailable resource - `Blocked → Ready`: Resource becomes available - `Running → Suspended`: Another task suspends it - `Suspended → Ready`: Resumed **Priority Inversion Problem:** - High-priority task H needs resource held by low-priority task L. - Medium-priority task M preempts L, preventing it from releasing the resource. - H remains blocked indefinitely — **unbounded priority inversion**. **Solution — Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP):** - When H blocks on L's resource, L temporarily **inherits H's priority**. - M cannot preempt L (since L now has H's high priority). - L finishes quickly, releases the resource, and H proceeds. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024] Explain Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) algorithm. A system has three periodic tasks with the following parameters: T1 (C=2, P=5), T2 (C=3, P=10), T3 (C=2, P=20). Check schedulability using Liu & Layland bound and exact response time analysis.** **Model Solution:** **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS):** RMS is a **static-priority**, **preemptive** scheduling algorithm where: - **Priority** is assigned inversely proportional to the **period** (rate). - **Shorter period → Higher priority** - **Optimal** for static, periodic, independent task sets - Assumes **deadline = period** for each task **Given Task Set:** | Task | $C_i$ | $T_i$ | Priority | |---|---|---|---| | T1 | 2 | 5 | Highest (shortest period) | | T2 | 3 | 10 | Medium | | T3 | 2 | 20 | Lowest | **Step 1: Compute CPU Utilization** $$U = \frac{2}{5} + \frac{3}{10} + \frac{2}{20} = 0.40 + 0.30 + 0.10 = 0.80$$ **Step 2: Liu & Layland Bound for n=3** $$U_{bound} = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 = 0.7797$$ **Step 3: Compare** $$U = 0.80 > U_{bound} = 0.7797$$ The sufficient condition is **not met**, but the task set may still be schedulable. We must perform **exact Response Time Analysis**. **Step 4: Exact Response Time Analysis** **For Task T1** (highest priority, no interference): $$R_1 = C_1 = 2 \text{ ms} \leq T_1 = 5 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark$$ **For Task T2:** Higher priority task: T1 ($C_1 = 2, T_1 = 5$) Iteration 1: $$R_2^{(1)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 = 5$$ Iteration 2: $$R_2^{(2)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 = 5$$ **Converged:** $R_2 = 5 \text{ ms} = T_2 = 10 \text{ ms}$ ✓ Schedulable **For Task T3:** Higher priority tasks: T1 ($C_1=2, T_1=5$), T2 ($C_2=3, T_2=10$) Iteration 1: $$R_3^{(1)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 3 = 2 + 2 + 3 = 7$$ Iteration 2: $$R_3^{(2)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 3 = 2 + 4 + 3 = 9$$ Iteration 3: $$R_3^{(3)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 3 = 2 + 4 + 3 = 9$$ **Converged:** $R_3 = 9 \text{ ms} \leq T_3 = 20 \text{ ms}$ ✓ Schedulable **Conclusion:** The task set is **schedulable under RMS** despite exceeding the Liu & Layland bound. --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024] Explain various Inter-Process Communication (IPC) mechanisms used in RTOS with examples.** **Model Solution:** IPC mechanisms enable tasks to **synchronize** and **exchange data** safely. Key mechanisms: **1. Semaphores:** - A semaphore is a kernel-managed **integer counter** with two atomic operations: `wait(P)` and `signal(V)`. - **Binary Semaphore:** Value 0 or 1. Used for signaling between tasks. - **Counting Semaphore:** Value 0 to N. Used for managing pools of identical resources (e.g., 5 UART ports). - **Example:** Producer task `signals()` a semaphore; consumer task `waits()` on it. **2. Mutex (Mutual Exclusion):** - A binary lock for protecting **critical sections** of shared resources. - **Owner concept:** Only the task that locked the mutex can unlock it. - Supports **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** to prevent priority inversion. - **Example:** Protecting a global linked list from concurrent access. **3. Message Queues:** - A **FIFO buffer** allowing tasks to send/receive variable-sized messages asynchronously. - **Example:** Sensor task sends data to logging task via a 10-message queue. **4. Mailboxes:** - A **single-slot message buffer** for inter-task communication. - **Example:** Task A drops a status message; Task B picks it up. **5. Event Flags:** - **Bitwise flags** (8/16/32-bit) set/cleared by tasks or ISRs. - **Example:** Bit 0 = data ready, Bit 1 = error, Bit 2 = timeout. **6. Pipes:** - **Unidirectional byte stream** for streaming data between tasks. - **Example:** Audio data streaming from ADC task to processing task. **Selection Criteria Table:** | Mechanism | Use Case | Blocking Support | |---|---|---| | Binary Semaphore | Task synchronization | Yes | | Mutex | Resource protection | Yes | | Message Queue | Variable data transfer | Yes | | Mailbox | Status passing | Yes | | Event Flags | Multi-condition signaling | Yes | | Pipe | Stream data | Yes | --- ## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for Last-Minute KTU Prep:** ### 🔑 Key Definitions - **RTOS:** Operating system with **deterministic** response time guarantees - **Hard Real-Time:** Missing deadline = **system failure** - **Soft Real-Time:** Missing deadline = **degraded quality** - **TCB:** Data structure storing **task context** (registers, PC, SP, state) - **Critical Section:** Code segment accessing shared resources ### 🧠 Must-Know Algorithms - **RMS (Rate Monotonic Scheduling):** Static priority = inverse of period - **EDF (Earliest Deadline First):** Dynamic priority, optimal, 100% utilization possible - **Round Robin:** Time quantum based, fair to equal-priority tasks - **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP):** Solves priority inversion ### 📐 Critical Formulas - $U = \sum C_i / T_i$ — CPU utilization - $U_{bound} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ — Liu & Layland bound - $R_i = C_i + I_i$ — Response time - $I_i = \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil R_i / T_j \right\rceil C_j$ — Interference ### ⚙️ IPC Mechanism Summary - **Signaling** → Binary Semaphore - **Resource Pool** → Counting Semaphore - **Critical Section** → Mutex (with PIP) - **Async Data** → Message Queue - **Single Status** → Mailbox - **Multi-Bit Events** → Event Flags - **Byte Stream** → Pipe ### 🚨 Common Pitfalls - Forgetting to draw **task state diagram** → lose 2-3 marks - Not iterating to **convergence** in RTA → lose full marks - Confusing **Mutex** with **Binary Semaphore** (no owner concept in semaphores) - Not stating **assumptions** (deadline = period, independent tasks) in scheduling problems - Missing **Gantt chart** visualization for scheduling questions ### 💡 High-Yield Last-Minute Tips 1. Always draw **Mermaid/Gantt diagrams** wherever the question mentions "with a neat diagram" 2. For RMS questions, **always start with utilization bound**, then proceed to **RTA** 3. **EDF > RMS** in terms of theoretical schedulability (100% vs ~69%) 4. **Priority inversion** = "High waits for Low, Low waits for Medium" 5. **Context switch** is the most expensive kernel operation (~1-10 μs) 6. **μC/OS-II**, **FreeRTOS**, **VxWorks** are the top 3 RTOS to remember

topics.title: Operating System Basics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET695 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Operating System Basics # MODULE 3: OPERATING SYSTEM BASICS ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) An **Operating System (OS)** in the context of an **Embedded System** is a tightly integrated, resource-constrained software layer — formally called a **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)** — that abstracts the underlying heterogeneous hardware (CPU cores, timers, memory, peripherals, buses) and provides deterministic, time-bounded services such as **task scheduling, inter-process communication, memory management, interrupt handling, and device driver coordination** to application firmware. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEAET695)**, the OS basics module lays the conceptual foundation for understanding how multi-tasking, concurrency, and resource arbitration are achieved in modern embedded platforms (e.g., ARM Cortex-M + FreeRTOS, Raspberry Pi + Linux, automotive ECUs + AUTOSAR). > [!NOTE] > **Definition (Board Exam Ready):** > "An Operating System is a system software that acts as an **intermediary between the user/application software and the computer hardware**, managing hardware resources and providing common services for application programs." — Standard KTU-approved phrasing. > [!IMPORTANT] > **RTOS vs GPOS — A Core KTU Distinction** > - **General-Purpose OS (GPOS)** — Optimized for *throughput* (e.g., Windows, Linux Desktop). Soft timing. > - **Real-Time OS (RTOS)** — Optimized for *deterministic latency and deadline adherence* (e.g., VxWorks, QNX, FreeRTOS, RTX). Hard/Firm timing. > Embedded systems **mandatorily** require an RTOS when there are concurrent time-critical tasks. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **🍽️ The Restaurant Kitchen Analogy** — The most intuitive way to understand an RTOS: > > Imagine a **busy restaurant kitchen**: > - **Head Chef = CPU/Cores** — Can only cook a few dishes at a time. > - **Dishes being prepared = Tasks/Processes** — Each dish has ingredients, steps, and a target serving time. > - **Kitchen Manager = Scheduler (OS Kernel)** — Decides which dish (task) the chef (CPU) should work on *right now*. > - **Refrigerator & Pantry = Memory (RAM/ROM)** — Holds ingredients (data/instructions). > - **Order tickets = I/O Requests / Interrupts** — Arriving from customers (external events). > - **Waiters = Device Drivers** — Carry food (data) between kitchen (CPU) and tables (peripherals). > - **Synchronization rules (e.g., only one chef per stove) = Mutexes / Semaphores**. > - **Rule "Order #5 must be served by 7:30 PM sharp" = Hard Real-Time Deadline**. > > The Kitchen Manager (RTOS) ensures that *no order is late*, *no chef is idle unnecessarily*, and *no two chefs fight over the same stove*. This is **exactly** what an embedded OS does for your microcontroller. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Embedded OS The following are **standardized performance metrics** mandated in KTU examinations and ECE industry practice: - **CPU Utilization (U)**: $0 \le U \le 1$ (or 0–100%), the fraction of time the processor is busy executing useful work. - **Throughput (ρ)**: Number of tasks completed per unit time, measured in **tasks/second**. - **Turnaround Time (TAT)**: $TAT = T_{completion} - T_{arrival}$ — Total time a task spends in the system. - **Waiting Time (WT)**: Time a task spends in the ready queue, awaiting CPU allocation. - **Response Time (RT)**: Time from task arrival to first execution — critical in interactive and real-time systems. - **Deadline (D)**: The latest acceptable completion time. **Hard real-time** = system fails if D is missed. **Soft real-time** = degraded performance but no catastrophic failure. - **Jitter (J)**: The variation in timing of periodic events, $J = T_{max} - T_{min}$. Must be minimized in control loops. - **Context Switch Time (T_cs)**: Time to save current task state and load another. **Typical: 1–10 μs** in modern RTOS. - **Interrupt Latency (T_il)**: Time from interrupt assertion to first instruction of ISR. **Typical: < 1 μs** for hard real-time. - **Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET)**: Upper bound on task execution time. **Conservative estimate** used in schedulability analysis. - **Jiffy / Tick (T_tick)**: OS system timer period, typically **10 μs – 1 ms**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Structural Breakdown of an Embedded OS The operating system can be decomposed into a layered architecture, from low-level hardware abstraction to high-level application services: ### 2.1.1 Layered Architecture (Top → Bottom) 1. **Application Layer** — User firmware, control algorithms, GUI logic. 2. **Middleware / API Layer** — POSIX, BSD sockets, protocol stacks (TCP/IP, CAN, MQTT), file system APIs. 3. **Operating System Kernel (Core)**: - **Task/Process Manager** — Creation, deletion, state management of tasks. - **Scheduler** — Decides which runnable task gets the CPU next. - **Synchronization & IPC Primitives** — Semaphores, mutexes, message queues, event flags, mailboxes. - **Memory Manager** — Static partitioning, dynamic allocation, memory pools. - **Interrupt Manager** — Registers, prioritizes, and dispatches ISRs. - **Time Manager** — System tick, software timers, delay functions. 4. **Board Support Package (BSP) / HAL (Hardware Abstraction Layer)** — Drivers for GPIO, UART, SPI, I2C, ADC, timers. 5. **Hardware** — MCU/MPU cores, memory chips, peripherals, buses. ### 2.1.2 Core OS Functions (Why & How) | # | Function | Why it is needed | How it is implemented | |---|----------|------------------|----------------------| | 1 | **Task Scheduling** | To run multiple concurrent tasks on a single CPU core | Priority-based, round-robin, or rate-monotonic schedulers using a **ready queue** | | 2 | **Context Switching** | To switch CPU between tasks while preserving state | Save **PC, SP, registers, PSW** to task's **TCB (Task Control Block)**, then load next task's context | | 3 | **Interrupt Handling** | To respond to time-critical hardware events | Vectored interrupt controller + ISR (Interrupt Service Routine) with minimal latency | | 4 | **Memory Management** | To safely allocate memory to multiple tasks | Static allocation, heap manager, **memory pools** (deterministic, O(1)) | | 5 | **Inter-Task Communication (ITC)** | To exchange data between tasks | Queues, mailboxes, pipes, shared memory (with mutual exclusion) | | 6 | **Synchronization** | To prevent race conditions on shared resources | **Mutexes** (mutual exclusion), **semaphores** (counting/binary), **event flags** | | 7 | **Time Management** | To provide delays, timeouts, periodic execution | System tick interrupt (e.g., every 1 ms) incremented by hardware timer | | 8 | **Device Driver Management** | To abstract peripheral I/O | Standard API: `open(), read(), write(), ioctl()` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **TCB (Task Control Block)** is the most critical data structure. It is a struct (C language) holding the entire CPU context of a task: stack pointer, program counter, register values, priority, state, and pointers to stack/queue resources. **Every OS action on a task is mediated through its TCB.** ### 2.2 Task States and Transitions A task in an RTOS cycles through a well-defined state machine: 1. **Ready (R)** — Task is loaded and waiting for CPU. 2. **Running (Run)** — Task is currently executing on CPU (only one task per core). 3. **Blocked / Waiting (B)** — Task is suspended, awaiting an event (semaphore, queue, delay expiry). 4. **Suspended (S)** — Task is explicitly paused by another task or OS service (optional state in some RTOS). 5. **Terminated / Deleted (D)** — Task is removed from scheduling. > [!NOTE] > **Transitions**: > Ready → Running (via **dispatch**), Running → Ready (via **preemption**), Running → Blocked (via `wait()`), Blocked → Ready (via event/signal/timeout), Running → Terminated (via `delete()` or self-exit). ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The OS concepts studied here directly power: - **Automotive**: Engine control units (ECUs) running **AUTOSAR OS** with OSEK/VDX standards. - **Aerospace & Defense**: Flight controllers on **VxWorks** with ARINC 653 partitioning. - **IoT & Consumer**: Smart thermostats, wearables, smart speakers running **FreeRTOS, Zephyr, ThreadX**. - **Industrial Automation**: PLCs, robotic arms on **RT-Linux, VxWorks, RTX**. - **Medical**: Pacemakers, infusion pumps with **certified IEC 62304 RTOS** for safety. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The table below contains all the formula essentials required for KTU 2024 ESE questions on this topic. Memorize every entry.** | # | Parameter / Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Domain | Critical Constraint / Boundary | |---|---------------------|----------------------|----------------|------------------------------| | 1 | **CPU Utilization (U)** | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | dimensionless, $0 \le U \le 1$ | $U < 1$ for schedulability (with margin) | | 2 | **Rate Monotonic Utilization Bound** | $U \le n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$ | dimensionless | Sufficient (not necessary) condition for RMS schedulability; $U \to \ln 2 \approx 0.693$ as $n \to \infty$ | | 3 | **Worst-Case Response Time (R_i)** | $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$ | time units (μs, ms) | $R_i \le D_i$ (deadline) for schedulability | | 4 | **Turnaround Time** | $TAT_i = C_i + WT_i$ | time units | Includes execution + waiting | | 5 | **Throughput** | $\rho = \frac{N_{completed}}{T_{total}}$ | tasks/second | Higher is better, but bounded by CPU | | 6 | **Deadline Miss Ratio (DMR)** | $DMR = \frac{N_{missed}}{N_{total}}$ | dimensionless | $= 0$ for hard real-time | | 7 | **Scheduling Density** | $D = \max_{t > 0} \left( \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\lfloor \frac{t}{T_i} \right\rfloor \cdot C_i}{t} \right)$ | dimensionless | $D \le 1$ is necessary for any algorithm | | 8 | **Effective Utilization (EDF)** | $U_{EDF} = \sum \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | dimensionless | $U_{EDF} \le 1$ is necessary **and sufficient** for EDF schedulability on uniprocessor | | 9 | **Context Switch Overhead (Total)** | $T_{cs,total} = N_{cs} \times T_{cs}$ | time units | $T_{cs} \approx 1\text{–}10 \, \mu\text{s}$ typical | | 10 | **Interrupt Latency Bound** | $T_{il} = T_{max\_disable} + T_{ISR\_entry}$ | time units | Determined by max interrupt disable window in kernel | | 11 | **Jitter** | $J = T_{max} - T_{min}$ | time units | Must be bounded for control systems | | 12 | **Periodic Task Arrival Count (in t)** | $N_i(t) = \left\lfloor \frac{t}{T_i} \right\rfloor$ | count | Used in processor demand analysis | **Notation Legend** (used in all formulas above): - $n$ = number of tasks in the system - $C_i$ = Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET) of task $i$ - $T_i$ = Period of task $i$ - $D_i$ = Relative deadline of task $i$ (often $D_i = T_i$) - $hp(i)$ = set of tasks with higher priority than task $i$ - $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ = ceiling function, $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ = floor function # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section performs **complete, line-by-line derivations** of the two most important KTU-board-tested formulas: (A) the **Rate Monotonic Utilization Bound** (Liu & Layland, 1973) and (B) the **Worst-Case Response Time Iteration** (Joseph & Pandya, 1986). Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. --- ## 3.1 Derivation A: Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) Utilization Bound > [!NOTE] > **Theorem (Liu & Layland, 1973):** For a set of $n$ periodic, independent tasks with $D_i = T_i$, scheduled by **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** on a uniprocessor, a **sufficient** (but not necessary) condition for schedulability is: > > $$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \le n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$ **Assumptions of the model:** - All tasks are **periodic** with period $T_i$. - Each task has **deadline equal to its period**: $D_i = T_i$. - Tasks are **independent** (no shared resources, no precedence). - **WCET** $C_i$ is constant and known. - Preemptive, priority-driven, **fixed-priority** scheduling. - The "critical instant" is the simultaneous release of all tasks at $t=0$. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1: Identify the critical instant.** At the critical instant, the response time of any task $i$ is **maximized** when it is released simultaneously with all higher-priority tasks. Therefore, the worst-case processor demand on task $i$ in the interval $[0, t]$ is: $$W_i(t) = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$$ **Step 2: Find the worst-case response time.** The worst-case response time $R_i$ is the smallest $t$ such that $W_i(t) \le t$ (i.e., the task finishes within the time window). We can solve it iteratively: $$R_i^{(0)} = C_i$$ $$R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$$ The iteration converges when $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$ or when $R_i^{(k+1)} > D_i$ (deadline missed). **Step 3: Transform the inequality for a general bound.** For a set of two tasks, the worst case is reached when the lower-priority task has period exactly equal to twice that of the higher-priority task. Let $T_1 < T_2$ with $T_2 = k \cdot T_1$ where $k$ is a real number. The bound derivation uses the **time demand function** of the lower-priority task. **Step 4: Derive the bound for $n$ tasks (sketch of classical proof).** The proof proceeds by considering the worst-case ratio $C_i / T_i$ that causes a deadline miss. By induction on $n$ and careful analysis of the **critical instant** for all tasks released simultaneously, Liu and Layland showed that the boundary of schedulability is reached when each task $i$ uses the full window: $$\frac{C_i}{T_i} = 2^{1/n} - 1 \quad \text{(approx.)}$$ Summing over $i$ from $1$ to $n$: $$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \le n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$ **Step 5: Verify the limiting behavior.** As $n \to \infty$: $$\lim_{n \to \infty} n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$ Use the expansion $2^{1/n} = e^{(\ln 2)/n} \approx 1 + \frac{\ln 2}{n} + O(n^{-2})$: $$n \left( 1 + \frac{\ln 2}{n} + O(n^{-2}) - 1 \right) = \ln 2 + O(n^{-1})$$ Therefore: $$\lim_{n \to \infty} n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931$$ This is the **asymptotic RMS utilization bound** — the theoretical maximum packing for an infinite number of tasks under RMS. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** RMS is a **pessimistic** sufficient test. The actual maximum achievable utilization for $n$ tasks can be **higher** than the bound (it is **necessary and sufficient** for EDF, but only **sufficient** for RMS). **Numerical Bound Table (must memorize for KTU):** | $n$ | $n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | Bound (%) | |---|---|---| | 1 | 1.0000 | 100.00% | | 2 | 0.8284 | 82.84% | | 3 | 0.7798 | 77.98% | | 4 | 0.7568 | 75.68% | | 5 | 0.7435 | 74.35% | | $\infty$ | $\ln 2$ | 69.31% | --- ## 3.2 Derivation B: Worst-Case Response Time by Fixed-Point Iteration > [!NOTE] > **Algorithm (Joseph & Pandya, 1986):** The exact worst-case response time of task $i$ is the **smallest fixed point** of the recurrence: > > $$R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$$ **Worked Example — A 3-task system:** Consider three independent periodic tasks on a uniprocessor under RMS: | Task | Period $T_i$ | WCET $C_i$ | Priority (RMS) | |------|-------------|------------|----------------| | $\tau_1$ | 5 ms | 1 ms | Highest | | $\tau_2$ | 10 ms | 2 ms | Middle | | $\tau_3$ | 20 ms | 3 ms | Lowest | **Step 1: Find $R_1$ (no higher-priority tasks).** $$R_1^{(0)} = C_1 = 1 \, \text{ms}$$ $$R_1^{(1)} = C_1 = 1 \, \text{ms} \quad (\text{converged})$$ Thus $R_1 = 1 \, \text{ms} \le T_1 = 5 \, \text{ms}$ → **Schedulable**. **Step 2: Find $R_2$.** Higher-priority set: $hp(2) = \{\tau_1\}$. $$R_2^{(0)} = C_2 = 2 \, \text{ms}$$ $$R_2^{(1)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 \cdot 1 = 3 \, \text{ms}$$ $$R_2^{(2)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 \cdot 1 = 3 \, \text{ms} \quad (\text{converged})$$ Thus $R_2 = 3 \, \text{ms} \le T_2 = 10 \, \text{ms}$ → **Schedulable**. **Step 3: Find $R_3$.** Higher-priority set: $hp(3) = \{\tau_1, \tau_2\}$. $$R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 3 \, \text{ms}$$ $$R_3^{(1)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 1 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 2 = 6 \, \text{ms}$$ $$R_3^{(2)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 2 = 7 \, \text{ms}$$ $$R_3^{(3)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 2 = 7 \, \text{ms} \quad (\text{converged})$$ Thus $R_3 = 7 \, \text{ms} \le T_3 = 20 \, \text{ms}$ → **Schedulable**. **Step 4: Verify the utilization.** $$U = \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{1}{5} + \frac{2}{10} + \frac{3}{20}$$ $$U = 0.200 + 0.200 + 0.150 = 0.550 = 55.0\%$$ The RMS bound for $n=3$ is $0.7798$. Since $0.550 \le 0.7798$, the system **passes the sufficient test**. The exact analysis above confirms this. > [!TIP] > **Board Exam Tip:** Always state both (a) the utilization bound test (quick pass) and (b) the response time iteration (exact verification). Examiners award full marks only when both are shown. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Scheduling Algorithms Compared | Algorithm | Type | Priority Assignment | Optimality | Pre-emption | KTU Use Case | |-----------|------|--------------------|-----------|-------------|--------------| | **FCFS** (First-Come First-Served) | Non-preemptive | Arrival order | Not optimal | No | Theory only | | **SJF** (Shortest Job First) | Non-preemptive | $C_i$ ascending | Optimal avg WT | No | Theory only | | **Round Robin (RR)** | Preemptive | Cyclic, time quantum | Fair sharing | Yes | Time-sharing OS | | **Priority Scheduling** | Preemptive | Static priority | Priority-inversion prone | Yes | Embedded real-time | | **RMS** (Rate Monotonic) | Preemptive, Fixed-priority | $T_i$ ascending (shorter = higher) | Static-optimal | Yes | Hard real-time | | **EDF** (Earliest Deadline First) | Preemptive, Dynamic-priority | Closest $D_i$ | **Optimal** (uniprocessor) | Yes | Hard real-time | | **LLF** (Least Laxity First) | Preemptive, Dynamic-priority | $\min(L_i = D_i - R_i - t)$ | Optimal (uniprocessor) | Yes | Soft real-time | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Layered Architecture of an Embedded RTOS (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB A1[Application Layer
User Firmware Control Loops GUI] --> A2[Middleware API Layer
POSIX Sockets Protocol Stacks File System] A2 --> A3[RTOS Kernel Core
Scheduler IPC Memory Time Manager] A3 --> A4[Board Support Package BSP
HAL Device Drivers Startup Code] A4 --> A5[Hardware Layer
MCU MPU Memory Peripherals Buses] A3 -.uses.-> B1[Task Control Block TCB Pool] A3 -.uses.-> B2[Ready Queue and Wait Lists] A3 -.uses.-> B3[Semaphore and Mutex Table] A3 -.uses.-> B4[Memory Pool Allocator] A5 -.interrupts.-> A3 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Vertical arrows show **call/dependency flow** (top calls bottom). Dotted arrows show **data structure usage** by the kernel. The hardware layer is the only one that can directly trigger kernel action (via interrupts). ## 4.2 Task State Transition Diagram ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 direction LR S1[Ready] --> S2[Running] : dispatch S2 --> S1 : preempt or time slice S2 --> S3[Blocked] : wait event or delay S3 --> S1 : event signaled or timeout S2 --> S4[Suspended] : explicit suspend S4 --> S1 : explicit resume S2 --> S5[Terminated] : delete or self exit ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Only one task can be in the Running state per CPU core at any instant.** This is a fundamental constraint that the scheduler enforces via atomic context switches. ## 4.3 Preemptive Priority Scheduling — Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR F1[Tick Interrupt or Event] --> F2[Scheduler Tick Handler] F2 --> F3[Compute Highest Priority Ready Task] F3 --> F4{Preempt Current Task} F4 -->|Yes| F5[Save Current TCB to Stack] F5 --> F6[Load New TCB from Ready Queue] F6 --> F7[Restore Registers PC SP PSW] F7 --> F8[Resume New Task Execution] F4 -->|No| F9[Continue Current Task] ``` ## 4.4 Inter-Task Communication (ITC) Mechanism Map ```mermaid flowchart TB T1[Task A Producer] -->|write| Q1[Message Queue] Q1 -->|read| T2[Task B Consumer] T3[Task C] -->|wait take| S1[Binary Semaphore] S1 -->|give signal| T3 T4[Task D Low Priority] -->|lock| M1[Mutex Mutex M1] M1 -->|protect| R1[Shared Resource e.g. UART] T5[Task E High Priority] -->|lock| M1 R1 -->|unlock| T4 R1 -->|unlock| T5 ``` > [!WARNING] > **Priority Inversion Hazard:** When a low-priority task holds a mutex and a high-priority task blocks on the same mutex, an *unrelated* medium-priority task can preempt the low-priority task indefinitely, **delaying** the high-priority task. **Solution:** Use **Priority Inheritance Protocol** (the OS temporarily boosts the lock-holder's priority to the highest waiter). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > The questions below are **modeled precisely** on the KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Evaluation) pattern. They mirror past KTU question framing, mark distribution, and expected answer depth. --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]` **Differentiate between a General-Purpose Operating System (GPOS) and a Real-Time Operating System (RTOS). Give two examples of each.** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** | Aspect | GPOS | RTOS | |--------|------|------| | **Primary Optimization Goal** | Average throughput, fairness | Worst-case latency, deadline adherence | | **Timing Guarantees** | Soft / best-effort (no hard deadline) | Hard / firm deterministic | | **Scheduling Philosophy** | Time-sharing, fair queuing | Priority-based, preemptive, deterministic | | **Interrupt Latency** | Variable (μs–ms) | Bounded and minimized (ns–μs) | | **Kernel Size** | Large (MB scale) | Compact (KB scale) | | **Examples** | Windows 11, Ubuntu Linux, macOS | FreeRTOS, VxWorks, QNX, RTX, Zephyr | | **Use Cases** | Desktop, server, workstation | Embedded controllers, automotive ECUs, IoT, medical devices | > *(Examiner expects a clear table + 2 examples of each type. 3 marks allocated as: 1 mark definition contrast, 1 mark property contrast, 1 mark examples.)* --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **List and briefly explain any three functions of a kernel in an embedded operating system.** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** 1. **Task Scheduling:** The kernel maintains a *ready queue* and uses a scheduling algorithm (e.g., fixed-priority, RMS, EDF) to decide which task executes on the CPU. This enables *logical concurrency* on a single-core processor. 2. **Interrupt Handling & Dispatch:** The kernel registers Interrupt Service Routines (ISRs) with the hardware interrupt controller, prioritizes them, and dispatches execution with the **minimum possible latency**, ensuring time-critical hardware events are responded to deterministically. 3. **Inter-Process Communication (IPC) / Synchronization:** The kernel provides primitives such as **semaphores, mutexes, message queues, mailboxes, and event flags** that allow tasks to safely exchange data and coordinate access to shared resources without race conditions. > *(3 marks = 1 mark per correct function with brief explanation. Examiner looks for keyword highlights.)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module carries 14-mark questions with **internal choice** (either OR). You must answer **one** of the two sub-questions fully. Each is split into 7 + 7 marks. --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Set B]` **(a)** Explain the **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** algorithm. State and prove the **Liu & Layland utilization bound** for RMS. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A uniprocessor embedded system has three periodic tasks with the following parameters: | Task | Period $T_i$ (ms) | WCET $C_i$ (ms) | Deadline $D_i$ (ms) | |------|-------------------|------------------|---------------------| | $\tau_1$ | 4 | 1 | 4 | | $\tau_2$ | 6 | 1 | 6 | | $\tau_3$ | 12 | 4 | 12 | **Tasks are scheduled using Rate Monotonic Scheduling. Determine if the task set is schedulable using:** **(i)** The Liu & Layland utilization bound test. **(ii)** The exact response time analysis (Joseph–Pandya iteration). **Show all calculations clearly.** *(7 marks)* --- ### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A #### Part (a) — RMS Algorithm & Utilization Bound (7 marks) **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS):** - RMS is a **static, fixed-priority, preemptive** scheduling algorithm for **periodic, independent** tasks on a uniprocessor. - The priority of each task is assigned **inversely proportional to its period**: the task with the **shortest period gets the highest priority**. - That is, $P_i > P_j \iff T_i < T_j$ (where $P$ is priority). - At every scheduling decision point (task release, completion, tick), the scheduler dispatches the **highest-priority ready task**. **Liu & Layland Utilization Bound (Theorem):** For a set of $n$ independent periodic tasks with $D_i = T_i$, scheduled under RMS on a uniprocessor, a **sufficient condition** for schedulability is: $$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \le n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$ **Proof Sketch (full derivation in Section 3.1):** 1. The worst-case response time of any task is maximized at the **critical instant** — when all tasks are released simultaneously at $t = 0$. 2. The processor demand on task $i$ in the interval $[0, t]$ from all higher-priority tasks is $\sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil t / T_j \rceil C_j$. 3. By induction on $n$ and analysis of the **boundary of unschedulability** (where the lowest-priority task just misses its deadline), Liu & Layland showed the boundary corresponds to the ratio $C_i / T_i = 2^{1/n} - 1$ for all $i$. 4. Summing yields the bound $U \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$. 5. As $n \to \infty$, the bound converges to $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$. **Significance:** RMS is **optimal among fixed-priority algorithms** — no other fixed-priority scheme can schedule a task set that RMS cannot. However, it is suboptimal compared to dynamic-priority algorithms like EDF, which can achieve $U$ up to 1. *(Mark split: 2 marks algorithm explanation, 3 marks theorem statement, 2 marks proof sketch.)* --- #### Part (b) — Schedulability Analysis (7 marks) **Step 1: Assign priorities by RMS** (shorter period = higher priority): - $\tau_1$: $T_1 = 4$ → **Priority 1 (highest)** - $\tau_2$: $T_2 = 6$ → **Priority 2** - $\tau_3$: $T_3 = 12$ → **Priority 3 (lowest)** **Step 2: (i) Liu & Layland Bound Test** *(3 marks)* Compute actual utilization: $$U = \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{6} + \frac{4}{12}$$ $$U = 0.2500 + 0.1667 + 0.3333 = 0.7500$$ RMS bound for $n = 3$: $$U_{RMS}(3) = 3 \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right) = 3 \left( 1.2599 - 1 \right) = 3 \times 0.2599 = 0.7798$$ Comparison: $$U = 0.7500 \le U_{RMS}(3) = 0.7798 \quad \checkmark$$ The task set **passes the sufficient utilization test** — it is schedulable by RMS. **Step 3: (ii) Exact Response Time Analysis** *(4 marks)* Use the Joseph–Pandya fixed-point iteration: $R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i^{(k)} / T_j \rceil C_j$ **For $\tau_1$** (no higher-priority tasks): $$R_1 = C_1 = 1 \, \text{ms}$$ Schedulable: $R_1 = 1 \le D_1 = 4$ ✓ **For $\tau_2$** ($hp(2) = \{\tau_1\}$): $$R_2^{(0)} = 1$$ $$R_2^{(1)} = 1 + \left\lceil \frac{1}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 1 + 1 = 2$$ $$R_2^{(2)} = 1 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 1 + 1 = 2 \quad \text{(converged)}$$ Schedulable: $R_2 = 2 \le D_2 = 6$ ✓ **For $\tau_3$** ($hp(3) = \{\tau_1, \tau_2\}$): $$R_3^{(0)} = 4$$ $$R_3^{(1)} = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{4}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{4}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 4 + 1 + 1 = 6$$ $$R_3^{(2)} = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 4 + 2 + 1 = 7$$ $$R_3^{(3)} = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 4 + 2 + 2 = 8$$ $$R_3^{(4)} = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{8}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{8}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 4 + 2 + 2 = 8 \quad \text{(converged)}$$ Schedulable: $R_3 = 8 \le D_3 = 12$ ✓ **Conclusion:** All three tasks meet their deadlines. The task set is **schedulable under RMS**. *(Mark split: 3 marks for Liu–Layland calculation, 4 marks for response time iteration. Examiner expects a step-by-step table.)* --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **layered architecture of an embedded RTOS**. Describe the functions of **kernel, scheduler, and device drivers**. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Explain the following with examples: **(i)** Mutex and Semaphore **(ii)** Priority Inversion and Priority Inheritance **(iii)** Context Switching and its overhead *(7 marks)* --- ### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B #### Part (a) — RTOS Layered Architecture (7 marks) *(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1. The answer should include a clean ASCII/Mermaid block diagram showing: Application → Middleware → Kernel (Scheduler + IPC + Memory + Time) → BSP/HAL → Hardware.)* **Kernel Functions:** - The kernel is the **core software** that runs in privileged mode and manages all OS resources. - Provides **system calls** (e.g., `task_create()`, `sem_wait()`, `queue_send()`) for application tasks. - Maintains the **TCB table** and **ready queue** in RAM. - Handles **interrupts** via vector table dispatch. **Scheduler Functions:** - Decides **which ready task** runs next on the CPU. - Implements a chosen algorithm (RMS, EDF, Round Robin, etc.). - Performs **preemption**: when a higher-priority task becomes ready, the current task is suspended. - Minimizes **dispatch latency** = time to switch from one task to another. **Device Driver Functions:** - Provide **standardized API** to peripherals: `open()`, `read()`, `write()`, `ioctl()`. - **Hide hardware complexity** from upper layers (e.g., UART driver abstracts baud-rate config, FIFO management, and DMA). - Interface with the **interrupt controller** to signal data-ready events to tasks. *(Mark split: 3 marks for diagram, 2 marks kernel, 1 mark scheduler, 1 mark drivers.)* --- #### Part (b) — Concurrency Primitives and Hazards (7 marks) **(i) Mutex vs. Semaphore (2 marks):** - A **Mutex (Mutual Exclusion lock)** is a binary lock with **ownership** — only the task that took the lock can release it. Used to protect a single shared resource (e.g., a UART port). - A **Semaphore** is a counter-based signaling mechanism. A **binary semaphore** is similar to a mutex but has no ownership concept. A **counting semaphore** allows up to $N$ holders (e.g., managing 3 identical printers). - Example: `pthread_mutex_lock(&uart_mutex)` vs. `xSemaphoreTake(xBinarySem, portMAX_DELAY)`. **(ii) Priority Inversion & Priority Inheritance (2 marks):** - **Priority Inversion:** A high-priority task (H) is forced to wait for a low-priority task (L) that holds a shared resource, because a medium-priority task (M) preempts L. The priority order becomes effectively L → M → H. - **Priority Inheritance Protocol:** The OS **temporarily boosts** L's priority to that of H (the highest-priority waiter) until L releases the resource. This bounds inversion to a single critical section. Used in **FreeRTOS mutexes** with `configUSE_MUTEXES` and `configUSE_PRIORITY_INHERITANCE`. **(iii) Context Switching (3 marks):** - A **context switch** is the process of saving the state of the currently running task and loading the state of the next scheduled task. - The state includes: **Program Counter (PC), Stack Pointer (SP), Program Status Word (PSW), and general-purpose registers**. - Steps: (1) Tick interrupt or syscall → (2) Save current TCB to its stack → (3) Scheduler picks next task from ready queue → (4) Restore next TCB → (5) Resume execution. - **Overhead** = time spent NOT doing useful work, typically **1–10 μs** in modern RTOS. Excessive context switching degrades throughput. - **Rule of thumb:** Choose a Round-Robin time quantum at least **10× the context switch time** to keep overhead below 10%. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Typically Lose Marks in This Module:** > > 1. **Conflating "Throughput" with "CPU Utilization":** Throughput = tasks/sec, Utilization = fraction of time busy. Both can be low simultaneously (idle system). > > 2. **Forgetting the ceiling function in Response Time:** The formula is $\lceil R_i / T_j \rceil$, **not** $R_i / T_j$ or $\lfloor R_i / T_j \rfloor$. Using floor instead of ceiling gives an **optimistic, incorrect** lower bound on response time. > > 3. **Misapplying the RMS bound:** It is a **sufficient** (not necessary) condition. If $U > n(2^{1/n} - 1)$, the task set **may still be schedulable** (use response time analysis to verify). If $U \le$ bound, it is **guaranteed** schedulable. > > 4. **Confusing "Hard Real-Time" with "Fast Real-Time":** Hard real-time is about **deterministic worst-case guarantees**, not raw speed. A 1 GHz processor can be non-real-time; a 16 MHz microcontroller can be hard real-time. > > 5. **Omitting units in numerical answers:** Always write "ms" or "μs" with the final value. Examiners deduct 0.5 marks for unit-less answers. > > 6. **Skipping convergence check in iteration:** Always verify $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$ and explicitly state "converged" before giving the final answer. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print this section or photograph it for last-minute revision before the exam.** - [ ] **OS Definition:** System software that mediates between user applications and hardware; provides resource management + service abstraction. - [ ] **GPOS vs RTOS:** GPOS optimizes throughput (soft timing); RTOS optimizes worst-case latency (hard/firm timing). - [ ] **RTOS Examples:** FreeRTOS, VxWorks, QNX, RTX, Zephyr, ThreadX, LynxOS-178, INTEGRITY. - [ ] **Kernel Functions:** Scheduling, IPC, memory management, interrupt dispatch, time management. - [ ] **TCB (Task Control Block):** Holds stack pointer, PC, registers, priority, state — the "identity card" of every task. - [ ] **Task States:** Ready → Running → Blocked (or Suspended) → Terminated. - [ ] **Scheduling Algorithms:** FCFS, SJF, Round Robin, Priority, RMS, EDF, LLF. - [ ] **RMS Priority Rule:** Shorter period = Higher priority. Static and fixed. - [ ] **EDF Priority Rule:** Earliest absolute deadline = Highest priority. Dynamic and optimal. - [ ] **Liu–Layland RMS Bound:** $U \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$, limit $\ln 2 \approx 69.3\%$. - [ ] **EDF Optimality Bound:** $U \le 1$ (necessary and sufficient on uniprocessor). - [ ] **Response Time Iteration:** $R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i^{(k)} / T_j \rceil C_j$. - [ ] **CPU Utilization:** $U = \sum C_i / T_i$. - [ ] **Throughput:** $\rho = N_{completed} / T_{total}$. - [ ] **Turnaround Time:** $TAT = T_{completion} - T_{arrival}$. - [ ] **Context Switch Time:** Typical 1–10 μs in modern RTOS. - [ ] **Priority Inversion:** Low-priority task holds mutex → high-priority task blocks → medium task preempts → unbounded delay. - [ ] **Priority Inheritance:** OS boosts lock-holder's priority to highest waiter's priority for the critical section. - [ ] **Mutex vs Semaphore:** Mutex has ownership; Semaphore is a counter (binary or counting) without ownership. - [ ] **Hard vs Soft Real-Time:** Hard = system failure if deadline missed (pacemaker, airbag). Soft = degraded performance (video streaming). - [ ] **Jitter:** $J = T_{max} - T_{min}$ — must be bounded for control loops. - [ ] **WCET:** Worst-Case Execution Time — conservative upper bound used in all schedulability tests. - [ ] **Critical Instant:** Simultaneous release of all tasks at $t=0$ — worst case for response time. - [ ] **Memory Allocation in RTOS:** Prefer **static** or **memory pools** (deterministic O(1)) over `malloc()` (non-deterministic, fragmentation). - [ ] **POSIX:** Portable Operating System Interface — standard API for embedded Linux and some RTOS (e.g., VxWorks, QNX). - [ ] **AUTOSAR / OSEK:** Automotive OS standards built on RTOS principles. --- *End of Module 3 Notes — Operating System Basics* *PEAET695 | KTU 2024 Scheme | B.Tech Embedded System Design*

topics.title: Types of Operating Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET695 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Operating Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Embedded System Design**, an **Operating System (OS)** is a system software layer that abstracts the underlying hardware resources and provides deterministic, predictable, and resource-managed services to application tasks. The **type** of operating system chosen for an embedded product is dictated by the timing criticality, resource constraints, reliability requirements, and power budget of the target application.

KTU 2024 Scheme defines operating systems for embedded platforms under the following canonical taxonomy:

| # | OS Classification | Canonical Definition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bare-Metal (Super-loop / Polled loop)** | Firmware executes without any OS abstraction; a single `while(1)` loop polls events. |
| 2 | **GPOS – General Purpose Operating System** | Throughput-optimised OS (Linux, Windows) used in resource-rich embedded platforms. |
| 3 | **RTOS – Real-Time Operating System** | Determinism-optimised OS guaranteeing bounded response times (e.g., FreeRTOS, VxWorks, QNX). |
| 4 | **Hard Real-Time OS** | Missing a deadline constitutes **system failure** (e.g., flight control, ABS braking). |
| 5 | **Soft Real-Time OS** | Missing a deadline degrades quality but does not cause failure (e.g., video streaming). |
| 6 | **Preemptive Kernel** | Higher-priority tasks can forcibly suspend lower-priority tasks. |
| 7 | **Non-Preemptive (Cooperative) Kernel** | A task runs to completion or until it voluntarily yields the CPU. |
| 8 | **Monolithic Kernel** | All services (scheduler, drivers, FS, network) execute in privileged/supervisor mode. |
| 9 | **Microkernel** | Only essential services (IPC, scheduling) run in kernel space; the rest in user space. |
| 10 | **Hybrid Kernel** | Combines performance of monolithic with modularity of microkernel (e.g., Windows NT). |
| 11 | **Multitasking / Multi-threaded OS** | Supports concurrent task execution with context-switching. |
| 12 | **Mobile / Embedded Linux Variants** | Android, Embedded Linux, µCLinux for resource-constrained devices. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 3, the dominant focus is on **RTOS architecture, kernel types (preemptive vs cooperative), and the comparative analysis with GPOS**. Hard vs Soft real-time distinction is a **2-mark guaranteed question** every semester.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Air Traffic Control Tower

Imagine an airport with multiple aircraft requesting runway access simultaneously:

- **Bare-Metal** = No control tower at all; each pilot decides when to land → collisions inevitable.
- **Cooperative (Non-Preemptive) Kernel** = One pilot lands, only then the next pilot is told to land → safe but slow if the first pilot talks too long.
- **Preemptive RTOS** = The tower has absolute authority. If a critical "medical emergency flight" appears, the tower instantly interrupts the current landing and clears the runway → **bounded, guaranteed response**.
- **GPOS** = The tower optimises *average* passenger throughput (more flights per hour) but makes *no promise* when a specific plane will land.
- **Hard Real-Time** = If the medical plane is delayed beyond a strict window, **someone dies** (catastrophic).
- **Soft Real-Time** = If an in-flight movie buffers a few seconds late, the passenger is annoyed, but no lives are at risk.

This analogy makes the central KTU concept — **determinism vs throughput** — immediately intuitive.

## 1.3 Quantitative Anchors (Key Metrics)

Embedded OS design revolves around four canonical performance metrics, every one of which must be quantified in SI-compatible units:

- **Latency** $L$ — Time between event occurrence and service start. Unit: **microseconds (µs)**.
- **Jitter** $J$ — Variation in latency across multiple iterations. Unit: **µs**.
- **Throughput** $\Theta$ — Tasks completed per unit time. Unit: **tasks/sec**.
- **Deadline** $D$ — Maximum acceptable response time. Unit: **ms**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Designer's Rule of Thumb (KTU standard):**
> A system is "real-time capable" only if the **worst-case latency** $L_{max}$ satisfies $L_{max} \le D$ **for every** task under all loading conditions. Average-case analysis is **never** acceptable for hard real-time systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Hierarchy

Operating systems for embedded targets can be organised in a **3-tier decision pyramid** that KTU examiners frequently test:

**Tier 1 — Do we even need an OS?**
- If firmware ≤ **32 KB**, single periodic task, no concurrency → **Bare-metal (Super-loop)**.
- Otherwise → proceed to Tier 2.

**Tier 2 — Determinism requirement?**
- Strict deadlines (anti-lock braking, pacemakers) → **Hard RTOS**.
- Soft deadlines (multimedia, UI) → **Soft RTOS / GPOS acceptable**.

**Tier 3 — Kernel architecture?**
- Maximum performance, monolithic firmware → **Monolithic kernel**.
- Modular, secure, fault-isolated → **Microkernel / Hybrid kernel**.

## 2.2 GPOS vs RTOS — The Heart of KTU Module 3

| Property | GPOS (Linux, Windows) | RTOS (FreeRTOS, VxWorks) |
|---|---|---|
| **Design Goal** | Maximise *throughput* | Minimise *worst-case latency* |
| **Scheduling Policy** | Fair-share, CFS, O(1) | Priority-based, pre-emptive |
| **Interrupt Latency** | Non-deterministic (µs to ms) | Bounded (typically **< 10 µs**) |
| **Preemption** | Fully pre-emptive | Fully pre-emptive (or configurable) |
| **Memory Model** | Virtual memory + paging | Flat / no MMU, deterministic alloc |
| **Footprint** | MB scale | KB scale (FreeRTOS ~ **4–10 KB ROM**) |
| **Cost** | Free (Linux) / Licensed (Windows IoT) | Often free / royalty-based |
| **Typical Use** | Gateways, HMI, infotainment | Motor control, avionics, medical |

## 2.3 Preemptive vs Non-Preemptive Kernel

| Aspect | Preemptive | Non-Preemptive (Cooperative) |
|---|---|---|
| **Task switching** | Forced by scheduler tick / ISR | Only when task calls `yield()` or terminates |
| **Response to high-priority event** | Immediate | Delayed until current task yields |
| **Priority Inversion risk** | Possible (needs mutex protocol) | Negligible (no preemption) |
| **Code complexity** | Higher (re-entrancy, locking) | Lower |
| **Determinism** | High (bounded) | High *only* if all tasks are well-behaved |
| **KTU favourite example** | FreeRTOS (configurable) | Early TinyOS, simple Arduino sketches |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Priority Inversion Pitfall (KTU favourite 7-marker):**
> When a low-priority task holds a mutex that a high-priority task needs, and a medium-priority task preempts the low-priority holder → high-priority is *indirectly* blocked by the medium-priority task. Solutions: **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** and **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)**.

## 2.4 Monolithic vs Microkernel vs Hybrid

| Kernel Type | Kernel Space Contains | User Space Contains | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Monolithic** | Scheduler, drivers, FS, network, MM | Applications | Fast (no mode switches) | One bug = whole system crash |
| **Microkernel** | Scheduler + IPC only | Drivers, FS, network as services | Modular, fault-isolated, secure | Slower (frequent context switches) |
| **Hybrid** | Most services in kernel, modules optional | Modular user-mode servers | Balanced trade-off | Complex design |
| **Exokernel** | Minimal — exposes raw hardware | Apps handle own resource mgmt | Maximum flexibility | Hard to program |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Embedded OS Performance Metrics

| # | Metric | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CPU Utilisation | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | dimensionless | $C_i$ = execution time, $T_i$ = period |
| 2 | Liu & Layland Bound (Rate Monotonic) | $U_{bound} = n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$ | dimensionless | Schedulability test |
| 3 | Worst-Case Response Time | $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | time | Solve by fixed-point iteration |
| 4 | Context Switch Time | $t_{cs} = t_{save} + t_{restore}$ | µs | Typically **5–20 µs** |
| 5 | Interrupt Latency | $L_{int} = t_{irq\_entry} + t_{isr\_exec}$ | µs | Bounded in RTOS |
| 6 | Jitter | $J = t_{max} - t_{min}$ | µs | Must be minimised in control loops |
| 7 | Deadline Miss Ratio | $DMR = \frac{N_{missed}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | % | Should be **0 %** for hard RT |
| 8 | Memory Footprint | $M_{foot} = M_{code} + M_{data} + M_{stack}$ | KB / MB | RTOS typically **< 64 KB** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Note:** The ceiling function in formula (3) uses $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ — never use the pipe symbol `\|` inside a markdown table. Use `\vert` or `\mid` if a similar delimiter is needed.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (AUTOSAR)**: Uses an OSEK/VDX-derived RTOS with hard real-time guarantees for ECU control.
- **Avionics (DO-178C)**: Mandates certifiable RTOS (VxWorks 653, INTEGRITY-178).
- **Consumer IoT**: Often bare-metal or lightweight RTOS (FreeRTOS, Zephyr) to meet sub-$1 BOM cost.
- **Smartphones**: Hybrid — Android (Linux kernel) with real-time media subsystem.
- **Industrial PLCs**: Often use VxWorks or RTX with sub-**100 µs** deterministic scan times.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Liu & Layland Schedulability Bound (Rate Monotonic Scheduling)

**Goal:** Prove that for $n$ periodic tasks scheduled by Rate-Monotonic Scheduling (RMS), a sufficient condition for guaranteed schedulability is $U \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$.

**Step 1 — Task Model:**
Let there be $n$ independent periodic tasks $\tau_1, \tau_2, \ldots, \tau_n$, each characterised by:
- Execution time $C_i$
- Period $T_i$
- Implicit deadline $D_i = T_i$

Tasks are indexed so that higher index = **lower priority** (i.e., $\tau_1$ has the shortest period = highest priority).

**Step 2 — Utilisation Definition:**
The total CPU demand (utilisation) is the fraction of time the processor is busy:

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}
$$

**Step 3 — Worst-Case Instantaneous Demand:**
The critical instant for task $\tau_i$ occurs when all higher-priority tasks release simultaneously with $\tau_i$. The processor demand in the interval $[0, t]$ is:

$$
W_i(t) = C_i + \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

**Step 4 — Completion Time Test:**
The task $\tau_i$ completes at the smallest $t$ such that $W_i(t) \le t$. Define the worst-case response time $R_i$ as the smallest fixed-point satisfying:

$$
R_i = C_i + \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

The fixed-point is obtained by iterating $R_i^{(k+1)} = W_i(R_i^{(k)})$ starting from $R_i^{(0)} = C_i$.

**Step 5 — Asymptotic Bound:**
For large $n$, the worst-case utilisation that still guarantees schedulability under RMS is:

$$
U_{bound} = n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Limiting Value:**
Apply the identity $\lim_{n \to \infty} n(2^{1/n} - 1) = \ln 2 \approx 0.693$. Thus, even with infinitely many tasks, RMS guarantees schedulability only when $U \le 0.693$.

> This derivation is the **standard KTU Module 3 derivation** and appears as a 7-mark sub-question in past papers.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Interrupt Latency Bound for Preemptive RTOS

**Step 1 — Define the interrupt timeline:** When an external event arrives at time $t = 0$, the longest delay before the corresponding ISR begins executing is the **maximum interrupt latency** $L_{int,max}$.

**Step 2 — Decompose the latency:**

$$
L_{int,max} = t_{detect} + t_{finish} + t_{dispatch}
$$

where:
- $t_{detect}$ = time to recognise the IRQ pin (synchroniser latency, typically **1–2 clock cycles**).
- $t_{finish}$ = time for the currently executing instruction to complete (≤ longest instruction, e.g., **LDM/STM ≈ 4 cycles** on Cortex-M).
- $t_{dispatch}$ = time to vector, push context, jump to ISR (≈ **12 cycles** on ARM Cortex-M).

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation on Cortex-M4 @ 168 MHz (clock period ≈ 5.95 ns):**

$$
L_{int,max} = (2 + 4 + 12) \times 5.95 \text{ ns} \approx 18 \times 5.95 \text{ ns} \approx 107 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** The bound is **deterministic and bounded** — a hallmark of RTOS-grade firmware. The same calculation on a GPOS with kernel preemption disabled can yield $L_{int,max} \approx 100 \text{ µs}$ — three orders of magnitude worse.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Task-Switching Behaviour

| Scenario | GPOS (Linux) | Preemptive RTOS (FreeRTOS) | Bare-Metal Super-loop |
|---|---|---|---|
| Latency bound | Unbounded (jitter ms+) | Bounded (< 10 µs typical) | Function of polling period |
| Worst-case CPU load | Often 60–80 % safe | Up to **100 %** safe (if schedulable) | Up to 100 % if no I/O wait |
| Code footprint | MB | KB | < 1 KB |
| Certifiability (DO-178, IEC 61508) | Almost impossible | Achievable with proper design | Easiest |
| Power consumption | High (always-on services) | Tunable tick-less idle | Lowest (sleep friendly) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** Students frequently confuse "real-time = fast." Real-time means **deterministic**, not necessarily fast. A 1-second task with 1.5-second deadline is hard real-time if the response is *guaranteed*; a 1 ms task in Linux is *not* real-time even if usually fast.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — CPU Utilisation Test

**Problem:** Three tasks $\tau_1, \tau_2, \tau_3$ with $C = [1, 2, 3]$ ms and $T = [4, 6, 10]$ ms. Is the task set schedulable under RMS?

**Step 1 — Compute utilisation:**

$$
U = \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{6} + \frac{3}{10}
$$

**Step 2 — Numerical evaluation:**

$$
U = 0.2500 + 0.3333 + 0.3000 = 0.8833
$$

**Step 3 — Liu & Layland bound for $n = 3$:**

$$
U_{bound} = 3 \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right) = 3 \times (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \times 0.2599 \approx 0.7798
$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**
Since $U = 0.8833 > U_{bound} = 0.7798$, the **sufficient test fails**, but the task set *may still be schedulable* (necessity not guaranteed). Apply the exact response-time test.

**Step 5 — Response time for $\tau_3$ (lowest priority):**

$$
R_3 = C_3 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3}{T_2} \right\rceil C_2
$$

Iterate:
- $R_3^{(0)} = 3$
- $R_3^{(1)} = 3 + \lceil 3/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 3/6 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 1 + 2 = 6$
- $R_3^{(2)} = 3 + \lceil 6/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 6/6 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7$
- $R_3^{(3)} = 3 + \lceil 7/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 7/6 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 4 = 9$
- $R_3^{(4)} = 3 + \lceil 9/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 9/6 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10$
- $R_3^{(5)} = 3 + \lceil 10/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 10/6 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10$ ✓ **Converged**

Since $R_3 = 10 \le D_3 = T_3 = 10$, the task set **is schedulable**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Hierarchy of Embedded Operating Systems

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Embedded Operating Systems] --> B[No OS - Bare Metal]
    A --> C[GPOS]
    A --> D[RTOS]
    B --> B1[Super Loop]
    B --> B2[State Machine]
    C --> C1[Linux]
    C --> C2[Windows IoT]
    C --> C3[Android]
    D --> D1[Hard Real Time]
    D --> D2[Soft Real Time]
    D1 --> D1a[VxWorks]
    D1 --> D1b[QNX]
    D1 --> D1c[FreeRTOS - config HARD]
    D2 --> D2a[FreeRTOS - config SOFT]
    D2 --> D2b[Embedded Linux]
    D --> E[Kernel Type]
    E --> E1[Preemptive]
    E --> E2[Non Preemptive]
    E --> E3[Monolithic]
    E --> E4[Microkernel]
    E --> E5[Hybrid]
    E --> E6[Exokernel]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — RTOS vs GPOS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AppLayer[Application Layer]
        APP1[Task 1 - Sensor Read]
        APP2[Task 2 - Motor Control]
        APP3[Task 3 - UI Display]
    end

    subgraph RTOSCore[RTOS Kernel]
        SCHED[Priority Scheduler]
        IPC[IPC - Queues and Semaphores]
        MM[Deterministic Memory Allocator]
        TMR[Software Timers]
    end

    subgraph HWLayer[Hardware Abstraction Layer]
        ISR1[ISR - Encoder]
        ISR2[ISR - ADC Done]
        DRV1[GPIO Driver]
        DRV2[PWM Driver]
    end
```
