```mermaid
    APP1 --> SCHED
    APP2 --> SCHED
    APP3 --> SCHED
    SCHED --> IPC
    SCHED --> MM
    SCHED --> TMR
    SCHED --> ISR1
    SCHED --> ISR2
    ISR1 --> DRV1
    ISR2 --> DRV2
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Choosing the Right OS Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start - New Embedded Project] --> Q1{Task Count more than 1?}
    Q1 -- No --> BM[Use Bare Metal Super Loop]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Hard Real Time Deadline?}
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Need Rich UI or Networking?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> RT[Select Hard RTOS - VxWorks or FreeRTOS HARD]
    Q3 -- Yes --> GPL[Select GPOS - Embedded Linux or Android]
    Q3 -- No --> SRT[Select Soft RTOS - FreeRTOS or Zephyr]
    BM --> Done[Project OS Finalised]
    RT --> Done
    GPL --> Done
    SRT --> Done
```

## 4.4 Preemption Timeline Diagram (Textual Schematic)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant L as Low Priority Task
    participant M as Medium Priority Task
    participant H as High Priority Task
    participant S as Scheduler
    L->>S: Running - tick 0
    Note over L: Acquires Mutex M
    S->>M: Tick 5 - M preempts L
    M->>S: Running
    H->>S: Tick 7 - H wakes - blocks on Mutex M
    Note over H: Priority Inversion begins
    M->>S: Tick 8 - M voluntarily yields
    Note over L: L resumes - PIP boosts L to H priority
    L->>S: Tick 9 - L releases Mutex M
    S->>H: Tick 9 - H immediately preempts and runs
    H->>S: Tick 10 - H completes
    Note over L: L restored to original priority
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**
**Differentiate between Hard Real-Time Operating System and Soft Real-Time Operating System. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Hard RTOS | Soft RTOS |
|---|---|---|
| Deadline | Strict — missing it = system failure | Soft — missing degrades quality |
| Guarantee | Worst-case response is provably bounded | Only statistical / average-case guarantee |
| Example | VxWorks in flight control system | FreeRTOS in multimedia playback |
| Design focus | Determinism over throughput | Throughput with acceptable latency |
| Verification | Schedulability proofs, formal methods | Heuristic testing sufficient |
| Cost | Higher (certification overhead) | Lower |

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark table + 0.5 mark example + 0.5 mark application)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
**What is the difference between a monolithic kernel and a microkernel? List one advantage and one disadvantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **monolithic kernel** runs all OS services (scheduler, file system, drivers, network stack) inside a single privileged address space, leading to fast execution but a single fault crashing the whole system. *(1 mark)*

A **microkernel** keeps only essential services (scheduling, IPC) in kernel space and moves drivers, file systems, and protocol stacks to user-space servers, giving fault isolation and modularity at the cost of performance overhead due to frequent mode switches. *(1 mark)*

- Monolithic advantage: **High performance** (no context switches between kernel subsystems).
- Monolithic disadvantage: **Poor fault isolation** (driver crash = kernel crash).
- Microkernel advantage: **Robustness and security** (user-mode drivers cannot corrupt kernel).
- Microkernel disadvantage: **Performance overhead** (IPC and mode switches).

*(0.5 mark for each advantage/disadvantage pair)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **preemptive Real-Time Operating System**. Compare it with a **General Purpose Operating System** in terms of scheduling, latency, and memory model. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Three periodic tasks are scheduled under **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** with the following parameters:
- $\tau_1$: $C_1 = 2$ ms, $T_1 = 6$ ms
- $\tau_2$: $C_2 = 3$ ms, $T_2 = 12$ ms
- $\tau_3$: $C_3 = 4$ ms, $T_3 = 24$ ms

Using the **Liu & Layland bound**, determine whether the task set is schedulable. If the bound fails, verify using the **exact response-time test**. *(7 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Explain the **types of operating systems** used in embedded systems. Discuss the role of **bare-metal, GPOS, RTOS, and hybrid kernels** with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** What is **priority inversion**? Explain with a timing diagram. Describe how **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** solves the problem. Mention any one alternative protocol. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solutions for Question A

**Solution A(a):**

A **preemptive RTOS** architecture consists of the following layered components (from hardware upward): *(3 marks for diagram + 1 mark for labelling)*

1. **Hardware Layer** — CPU core (e.g., ARM Cortex-M), timers, peripherals, memory.
2. **Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)** — Vendor-supplied register-level drivers.
3. **RTOS Kernel Core** — Scheduler, dispatcher, synchronisation primitives (semaphores, mutexes, queues), memory manager, software timers, interrupt controller interface.
4. **System Services Layer** — File system (optional), network stack, command shell.
5. **Application Layer** — User tasks/threads with priorities assigned by the developer.

**Comparison with GPOS:** *(3 marks)*

| Feature | Preemptive RTOS | GPOS |
|---|---|---|
| Scheduling | Static priority, pre-emption by tick or event | Dynamic priority, fair-share, throughput-oriented |
| Latency | Bounded (< 10 µs typical) | Non-deterministic (100 µs – 10 ms) |
| Memory Model | Flat / no MMU, deterministic allocator | Virtual memory with paging, MMU mandatory |

*(1 mark for conclusion emphasising that RTOS trades throughput for guaranteed latency)*

---

**Solution A(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute total CPU utilisation:** *(2 marks)*

$$
U = \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{2}{6} + \frac{3}{12} + \frac{4}{24} = 0.3333 + 0.2500 + 0.1667 = 0.7500
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Liu & Layland bound for $n = 3$:** *(2 marks)*

$$
U_{bound} = 3 \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right) = 3 \times 0.2599 = 0.7798
$$

Since $U = 0.7500 < U_{bound} = 0.7798$, the **sufficient test passes**, and the task set is guaranteed schedulable under RMS.

**Step 3 — Verification by exact response-time test (optional credit):** *(3 marks)*

For $\tau_3$ (lowest priority):

$$
R_3 = C_3 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3}{T_2} \right\rceil C_2
$$

- $R_3^{(0)} = 4$
- $R_3^{(1)} = 4 + \lceil 4/6 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 4/12 \rceil \cdot 3 = 4 + 2 + 3 = 9$
- $R_3^{(2)} = 4 + \lceil 9/6 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 9/12 \rceil \cdot 3 = 4 + 4 + 3 = 11$
- $R_3^{(3)} = 4 + \lceil 11/6 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 11/12 \rceil \cdot 3 = 4 + 4 + 3 = 11$ ✓

Since $R_3 = 11 \le D_3 = 24$, task $\tau_3$ meets its deadline. **Task set is schedulable.**

---

### Model Solutions for Question B

**Solution B(a):**

Embedded systems employ five principal OS categories: *(7 marks distributed across five types)*

1. **Bare-Metal (No OS)** — A super-loop polls events sequentially. Used in **Arduino UNO**, **8051-based toys**, simple sensor nodes. Footprint < 1 KB.
2. **General Purpose OS (GPOS)** — Linux, Windows IoT, Android. Optimised for average throughput. Used in **infotainment systems**, **gateways**, **smart speakers**.
3. **Real-Time OS (RTOS)** — FreeRTOS, VxWorks, QNX, Zephyr. Guarantees bounded latency. Used in **automotive ECUs**, **pacemakers**, **drone flight controllers**.
4. **Hybrid Kernel** — Windows NT, XNU (macOS/iOS). Combines monolithic performance with microkernel modularity. Used in **automotive infotainment with safety-critical RTOS co-processor**.
5. **Multitasking / Multi-threaded Embedded OS** — Supports concurrent tasks with priority-based preemption. Forms the backbone of modern RTOS offerings.

A well-illustrated block diagram showing these OS layers along with typical kernel–user space boundaries earns full marks.

---

**Solution B(b):**

**Priority Inversion** is a scheduling anomaly in which a higher-priority task is **indirectly preempted** by a lower-priority task, with the medium-priority tasks acting as the actual preemption source. *(2 marks)*

**Timing diagram explanation:** *(2 marks)*

1. Task $L$ (low) acquires a shared mutex $M$.
2. Task $H$ (high) becomes ready, preempts $L$, and tries to lock $M$ — but $M$ is held by $L$. $H$ blocks.
3. Task $M$ (medium) becomes ready and **preempts $L$** (since $L$ is runnable).
4. $L$ cannot release $M$ because $M$ is running instead. Effectively, $H$ is blocked by $M$ even though $M$ has no relation to the lock.

**Solution — Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP):** *(2 marks)*

When a high-priority task $H$ blocks on a mutex held by a low-priority task $L$, the kernel **temporarily boosts $L$'s priority to that of $H$**. Now $L$ runs at $H$'s priority, preventing $M$ from preempting it. $L$ finishes its critical section, releases $M$, and its priority is restored. $H$ then runs immediately.

**Alternative Protocol:** *(1 mark)*

- **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP):** Each mutex is assigned a priority equal to the highest priority task that may lock it. Any task that locks the mutex is immediately raised to that ceiling priority, preventing deadlock among multiple mutexes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Conflating "real-time" with "fast"** — Real-time means *deterministic*, not *fast*. Examiners deduct 1 mark if this distinction is missing.
> 2. **Liu & Layland bound misuse** — The bound is **sufficient, not necessary**. Students often claim "task set unschedulable" when the bound fails, losing 2 marks. Always cross-check with the exact response-time test.
> 3. **Confusing preemption with context switch** — Context switch is the *mechanism*; preemption is the *trigger*. Using these interchangeably costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Missing the term "bounded latency"** — Every RTOS definition must include the phrase "bounded/worst-case latency." Omission = 0.5 mark penalty.
> 5. **Monolithic vs Microkernel confusion** — Many students incorrectly claim that Linux is a microkernel. Linux is **monolithic with dynamically loadable modules**. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this factual error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Operating System classification** for embedded platforms hinges on **determinism vs throughput** trade-off.
- **Bare-metal** = no OS abstraction, smallest footprint, simplest design; chosen when firmware ≤ 32 KB and concurrency ≤ 1.
- **GPOS** (Linux, Windows) optimises *average throughput*; latency is **non-deterministic** — unsuitable for hard real-time.
- **RTOS** optimises *worst-case latency*; guarantees bounded response — mandatory for hard real-time embedded.
- **Hard real-time**: missing deadline = system failure (e.g., ABS braking, pacemaker).
- **Soft real-time**: missing deadline degrades quality but does not cause failure (e.g., video streaming).
- **Preemptive kernel** allows forced context switches; essential for predictable high-priority response.
- **Cooperative (non-preemptive) kernel** requires tasks to voluntarily yield; lower complexity but no latency guarantee.
- **Monolithic kernel** = all services in privileged mode (fast, fragile). Example: Linux, traditional RTOS kernels.
- **Microkernel** = only scheduler + IPC in kernel mode (slow, robust). Example: QNX, MINIX 3.
- **Hybrid kernel** = compromise (Windows NT, XNU).
- **Priority inversion** is a classic RTOS pitfall solved by **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** or **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)**.
- **Liu & Layland bound** $U_{bound} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ is **sufficient but not necessary** for RMS schedulability; limit = **ln 2 ≈ 0.693**.
- **Exact response-time test** uses fixed-point iteration: $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i / T_j \rceil C_j$.
- **Interrupt latency** $L_{int,max} \le$ (sync + max instruction + dispatch) cycles — deterministic in RTOS.
- **Key sizes to memorise**: FreeRTOS ROM ≈ **4–10 KB**, typical RTOS RAM ≈ **< 64 KB**, GPOS footprint ≈ **MB scale**.
- **Real-time ≠ fast** — it is *deterministic*, and this distinction is the single most-tested KTU concept.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: Tasks — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS)** for embedded systems, a **Task** (often synonymous with a **Thread** of execution) is defined as the smallest sequential, schedulable unit of work that an RTOS kernel recognizes and can independently dispatch onto the CPU. Formally, a task is an independent, semi-autonomous computational entity that possesses its own dedicated execution context (program counter, CPU register set, and stack space), competes for processor time under a predefined scheduling policy, and executes a dedicated portion of the application logic.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, a task is formally characterized by the triple $\langle \text{TCB}, \text{Stack}, \text{Entry Point} \rangle$, where:
- **TCB** (Task Control Block) is the kernel data structure describing the task's state, priority, and context.
- **Stack** is the private memory region used to store local variables, return addresses, and saved registers.
- **Entry Point** is the address of the C function containing the task's infinite loop (`for(;;)` or `while(1)`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A task in an embedded RTOS is *not* a heavyweight process. It does not own a separate address space (no virtual memory). All tasks of an embedded application typically share a single linear physical memory map — this is precisely why embedded tasks are cheap to create and switch.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen during the dinner rush:

| Kitchen Element | Embedded RTOS Equivalent |
|---|---|
| **The Head Chef** | The **RTOS Scheduler** — decides who works next |
| **A Recipe in progress** | A **Task** — one self-contained piece of cooking |
| **The Chef's notepad** | **Task Control Block (TCB)** — tracks order, table, ingredients used |
| **Cutting board & station** | **Task Stack** — workspace for that specific recipe |
| **Passing the torch from chef A to chef B** | **Context Switch** — saving A's state, loading B's state |

Each **recipe (task)** is a complete, independent "to-do": e.g., *"make pasta"*, *"plate dessert"*. The head chef (scheduler) decides which recipe is being worked on *right now*. When the head chef shouts "switch!", the current chef's notepad (TCB) is filed away, the next chef's notepad is opened, and work continues. This is **multitasking** — the *illusion* of simultaneous execution on a single CPU.

## 1.3 Fundamental Distinctions (Critical for 2-Mark Definitions)

A **Task** is sometimes confused with a **Process** and a **Thread**. The KTU 2024 syllabus requires students to clearly differentiate:

> [!NOTE]
> - **Process:** A heavyweight, memory-isolated program (own virtual address space, own code/data/heap). Typical of desktop OS (Linux/Windows).
> - **Thread (Lightweight Process):** Shares address space with sibling threads, but has its own registers and stack. Typical of general-purpose computing.
> - **Embedded Task:** A single, self-contained execution flow in an RTOS with **its own stack**, **own TCB**, **static priority**, and **infinite loop body**. Address space is shared, and there is **no fork/exec** model.

## 1.4 Anatomy of a Task — The Three Components

A task in embedded RTOS consists of three tightly coupled components:

1. **Code (Task Body):** A `void task_function(void *arg)` C function containing an infinite loop. It must never return; otherwise, behavior is undefined (in FreeRTOS it must call `vTaskDelete(NULL)`).
2. **Stack:** A statically or dynamically allocated memory array (`StackType_t pxStack[STACK_SIZE]`) used for local variables, function-call frames, and interrupt-context saves. Typical size: **128 words to 1024 words** depending on call depth.
3. **TCB (Task Control Block):** A kernel-side `struct tcb` containing the task's name, priority, state, stack pointer, list pointers, etc.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of thumb:** If two tasks share a global variable and you don't protect it, you have a bug. Tasks in embedded RTOS are *cooperating* by default — synchronization is **not** automatic.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Task States — The Lifecycle Model

Every embedded RTOS task exists in one of a small, finite set of states. The canonical state model (used by FreeRTOS, µC/OS-II, VxWorks, etc.) recognizes **four fundamental states**:

| State | Symbol | Description | CPU Ownership |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Running** | $\mathbb{R}$ | Currently executing on the CPU | **Holds** the CPU |
| **Ready** | $\mathbb{R}_d$ | Eligible to run, but a higher/equal-priority task owns the CPU | Waits in Ready List |
| **Blocked** | $\mathbb{B}$ | Waiting for a temporal or synchronization event (delay, semaphore, queue, mutex) | Released |
| **Suspended** | $\mathbb{S}$ | Inactive by explicit API call (`vTaskSuspend()`); not in any scheduler list | Inert |

### 2.1.1 Transition Triggers

$$\text{Running} \xrightarrow{\text{scheduler preempts}} \text{Ready}$$

$$\text{Running} \xrightarrow{\text{delay / IPC wait}} \text{Blocked}$$

$$\text{Running} \xrightarrow{\text{vTaskSuspend()}} \text{Suspended}$$

$$\text{Ready} \xrightarrow{\text{scheduler dispatch}} \text{Running}$$

$$\text{Blocked} \xrightarrow{\text{event occurs (timeout/signal)}} \text{Ready}$$

$$\text{Suspended} \xrightarrow{\text{vTaskResume()}} \text{Ready}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight:** A task **never** goes directly from Blocked → Running. It must first transition to Ready, and then the scheduler must pick it. This indirection is what enables deterministic scheduling.

## 2.2 Task Control Block (TCB) — The Task's "Passport"

The TCB is a kernel-allocated `struct` that fully describes a task. Its classical layout (FreeRTOS-style) includes:

```
struct TCB {
    volatile StackType_t    *pxTopOfStack;    // Current stack pointer
    ListItem_t              xStateListItem;   // Linkage into Ready/Blocked list
    ListItem_t              xEventListItem;   // Linkage into event wait list
    UBaseType_t             uxPriority;       // Base priority
    UBaseType_t             uxBasePriority;   // For priority inheritance
    uint16_t                usStackDepth;     // Words
    char                    pcTaskName[configMAX_TASK_NAME_LEN];
    // ... optional: notifications, tags, etc.
};
```

### 2.2.1 Why the TCB Matters

The TCB is the **only** object the scheduler manipulates during a context switch. The TCB's `pxTopOfStack` field *is* the saved execution context — when the scheduler restores a task, it loads the CPU's SP register from this field. A lost or corrupted TCB silently kills the task and corrupts the kernel.

## 2.3 Task Priority and the Ready List

Tasks of identical priority are organized into a **Ready List** (often a doubly linked list, ring, or bitmap-indexed array). For $n$ priority levels, the scheduler's dispatch decision reduces to:

$$\text{Next Task} = \arg\max_{i \in \text{Ready}} \left\{ \text{priority}(i) \right\}$$

For a fixed-priority preemptive scheduler, this is an $O(1)$ decision if a **bitmap priority table** is used (e.g., FreeRTOS's "Ready Bits" with `uxTopReadyPriority`). The highest set bit $\Rightarrow$ highest non-empty priority queue.

## 2.4 Context Switching — The Atomic Handover

A **context switch** is the act of saving the outgoing task's CPU state into its TCB/stack and loading the incoming task's state from its TCB/stack. The classical sequence (pseudocode):

```
PendSV_Handler():
    save_caller_regs()              // push R4..R11 onto outgoing task stack
    outgoing_TCB.sp = current_sp    // snapshot pointer
    incoming_sp = incoming_TCB.sp  // load new pointer
    restore_callee_regs()           // pop R4..R11 from incoming task stack
    EXC_RETURN                      // atomically restore PSP and return
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Context switch time $T_{cs}$ is **not zero**. It is a real overhead, typically **1–50 µs** depending on MCU and compiler. For a 1 ms periodic task, a 10 µs context switch adds **1%** CPU load just to switching.

## 2.5 Task Creation and Termination

The lifecycle of a task in code (FreeRTOS/µC/OS-II style):

```
BaseType_t xTaskCreate(
    TaskFunction_t    pxTaskCode,   // entry point (function pointer)
    const char *const pcName,       // human-readable name
    configSTACK_DEPTH_TYPE usStackDepth,
    void *const pvParameters,       // argument to entry point
    UBaseType_t uxPriority,         // initial priority
    TaskHandle_t *pxCreatedTask     // out: handle for control APIs
);
```

To terminate a task:
- **Self-delete:** `vTaskDelete(NULL)` inside the task body.
- **Delete another:** `vTaskDelete(xHandle)`.
- **Idle task cleanup:** When a task self-deletes, its TCB and stack are released only by the **Idle Task** (the lowest-priority kernel task).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Deleting a task that is currently holding a **mutex** causes **priority inversion to become permanent**. Always release mutexes *before* deleting.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every quantitative relation you will need for KTU Module 3 questions on tasks.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula or Rule | Units / Typical Values |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CPU Utilization (single task)** | $U_i = \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le U_i \le 1$ |
| 2 | **Total CPU Utilization** | $U_{tot} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} U_i$ | Must be $\le 1$ (RMA theorem) |
| 3 | **Rate Monotonic Bound (Liu & Layland)** | $U_{bound} = n\left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$ | $n=1\!\to\!1$, $n=2\!\to\!0.83$, $n\!\to\!\infty\!\to\!\ln 2 \approx 0.693$ |
| 4 | **Context Switch Overhead Fraction** | $f_{cs} = \dfrac{T_{cs} \cdot N_{cs}}{T_{hyper}}$ | % of hyperperiod consumed by switching |
| 5 | **Hyperperiod (LCM of periods)** | $H = \text{lcm}(T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n)$ | Scheduling frame length |
| 6 | **Response Time of Task $i$** | $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \dfrac{R_i}{T_j}\right\rceil C_j$ | Iterative fixed-point equation |
| 7 | **Schedulability Test** | $\forall i: R_i \le D_i$ | $D_i$ = deadline |
| 8 | **Stack Size Rule of Thumb** | $\text{Stack} \ge \text{deepest call depth} \times \text{words/frame} + \text{ISR depth} + \text{safety margin}$ | In machine words |
| 9 | **Task State Count Invariant** | $\#\text{Running} + \#\text{Ready} + \#\text{Blocked} + \#\text{Suspended} = N$ | Always holds |
| 10 | **Priority Levels** | $0 \le P \le \text{MAX\_PRIO} - 1$ | Higher number = higher priority (FreeRTOS) |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Notation rule for KTU answers:** Use $C_i$ for computation time and $T_i$ for period, *not* $T$ and $C$ alone, otherwise the marker will not know which is which.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The task model is the bedrock of every commercial embedded product:
- **Automotive ECU (AUTOSAR):** Each Runnable is a task; tasks are statically configured with priority, period, and stack.
- **Drone flight controller (PX4, NuttX):** ~30–50 tasks — attitude control, GPS fusion, telemetry, logging — all cooperative real-time tasks.
- **IoT sensor node (FreeRTOS):** Two to five tasks — sample, encrypt, transmit, sleep — orchestrated by an RTOS scheduler.

Without tasks, you would be writing a **superloop** with `if(flag)` checks. With tasks, you get preemptive, priority-driven concurrency with deterministic timing — the difference between a hobbyist Arduino sketch and a certified medical device.

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Rate Monotonic Utilization Bound

The **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** algorithm assigns priorities *inversely* to periods: shorter period ⇒ higher priority. Liu & Layland (1973) proved that a set of $n$ periodic, independent, preemptable tasks is **guaranteed schedulable** if:

$$U_{tot} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \le n\left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$$

We now **derive this bound** for the critical worst-case $n = 2$ case to understand the underlying logic.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Let two tasks exist: $\tau_1$ (shorter period, higher priority) and $\tau_2$ (longer period, lower priority). By RMS, $T_1 < T_2$ and $P_1 > P_2$. Worst-case phasing aligns both task releases at $t = 0$.

### 3.1.2 Worst-Case Response Time of $\tau_2$

The lowest-priority task $\tau_2$ suffers maximum interference when $\tau_1$ preempts it as often as possible. In any interval of length $R_2$, the number of $\tau_1$ arrivals is $\left\lceil R_2 / T_1 \right\rceil$.

$$\begin{aligned}
R_2 &= C_2 + \left\lceil \frac{R_2}{T_1} \right\rceil \cdot C_1 \\
\end{aligned}$$

To find the **critical point** (smallest $R_2$ satisfying the equation), use the **fixed-point iteration**:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_2^{(0)} &= C_2 \\
R_2^{(k+1)} &= C_2 + \left\lceil \frac{R_2^{(k)}}{T_1} \right\rceil \cdot C_1 \\
\end{aligned}$$

The iteration terminates when $R_2^{(k+1)} = R_2^{(k)}$, or when $R_2^{(k)} > T_2$ (missed deadline).

### 3.1.3 Continuous-Time Upper Bound

To obtain a *closed-form sufficient* bound, replace the ceiling by continuous ceiling (worst case):

$$\begin{aligned}
R_2 &\le C_2 + \left( \frac{R_2}{T_1} + 1 \right) C_1 \\
R_2 - \frac{C_1}{T_1} R_2 &\le C_2 + C_1 \\
R_2 \left( 1 - \frac{C_1}{T_1} \right) &\le C_2 + C_1 \\
R_2 &\le \frac{C_1 + C_2}{1 - C_1/T_1} \\
\end{aligned}$$

Schedulability requires $R_2 \le T_2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{C_1 + C_2}{1 - C_1/T_1} &\le T_2 \\
C_1 + C_2 &\le T_2 \left( 1 - \frac{C_1}{T_1} \right) \\
\frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} &\le 1 - \frac{C_1}{T_1} \\
2 \cdot \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} &\le 1 \\
\end{aligned}$$

In the symmetric worst case where $U_1 = U_2 = U/2$ and $C_i = U T_i / 2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
U_1 + U_2 &\le 2 \cdot \frac{U}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{T_1}{T_1}\right) + \frac{U}{2} \cdot 1 \\
U_{tot} &\le 2 \left(2^{1/2} - 1\right) \approx 0.828 \\
\end{aligned}$$

which is the well-known **82.8% bound** for $n = 2$. Generalizing, taking the limit $n \to \infty$ yields $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Response-Time Analysis (RTA) Iteration

For arbitrary priority assignment, the **exact** schedulability test is given by the **response-time recurrence**. We derive it.

### 3.2.1 Worst-Case Interference

Task $\tau_i$ is released at the **critical instant** — a moment when all higher-priority tasks are released simultaneously. This is the phasing that maximizes interference on $\tau_i$.

During the time $\tau_i$ executes, it can be preempted by every higher-priority task $\tau_j \in hp(i)$ each time $\tau_j$ is released. The number of releases of $\tau_j$ in interval $[0, R_i]$ is $\left\lceil R_i / T_j \right\rceil$.

### 3.2.2 The Recurrence

$$\begin{aligned}
R_i^{(0)} &= C_i \\
R_i^{(k+1)} &= C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j \\
\end{aligned}$$

Iteration terminates when $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$ (fixed point found) **or** $R_i^{(k)} > D_i$ (deadline missed).

> [!NOTE]
> **Termination guarantee:** $R_i^{(k)}$ is a non-decreasing sequence bounded above by $D_i$. If no fixed point is found before exceeding $D_i$, the task is **not schedulable** under the given priority assignment.

### 3.2.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

Let three tasks have:
- $\tau_1$: $T_1 = 4$, $C_1 = 1$, $D_1 = 4$
- $\tau_2$: $T_2 = 5$, $C_2 = 2$, $D_2 = 5$  
- $\tau_3$: $T_3 = 20$, $C_3 = 4$, $D_3 = 20$

Priority by Rate Monotonic: $P_1 > P_2 > P_3$.

**Test $\tau_3$ (worst case, full RTA):**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(0)} &= 4 \\
R_3^{(1)} &= 4 + \left\lceil \frac{4}{4} \right\rceil (1) + \left\lceil \frac{4}{5} \right\rceil (2) \\
        &= 4 + 1 + 2 = 7 \\
R_3^{(2)} &= 4 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{4} \right\rceil (1) + \left\lceil \frac{7}{5} \right\rceil (2) \\
        &= 4 + 2 + 4 = 10 \\
R_3^{(3)} &= 4 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{4} \right\rceil (1) + \left\lceil \frac{10}{5} \right\rceil (2) \\
        &= 4 + 3 + 4 = 11 \\
R_3^{(4)} &= 4 + \left\lceil \frac{11}{4} \right\rceil (1) + \left\lceil \frac{11}{5} \right\rceil (2) \\
        &= 4 + 3 + 6 = 13 \\
R_3^{(5)} &= 4 + \left\lceil \frac{13}{4} \right\rceil (1) + \left\lceil \frac{13}{5} \right\rceil (2) \\
        &= 4 + 4 + 6 = 14 \\
R_3^{(6)} &= 4 + \left\lceil \frac{14}{4} \right\rceil (1) + \left\lceil \frac{14}{5} \right\rceil (2) \\
        &= 4 + 4 + 8 = 16 \\
R_3^{(7)} &= 4 + \left\lceil \frac{16}{4} \right\rceil (1) + \left\lceil \frac{16}{5} \right\rceil (2) \\
        &= 4 + 4 + 8 = 16 \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Fixed point reached:** $R_3 = 16 \le D_3 = 20$ ✓ **Schedulable.**

**Test $\tau_2$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_2^{(0)} &= 2 \\
R_2^{(1)} &= 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{4} \right\rceil (1) = 3 \\
R_2^{(2)} &= 2 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil (1) = 3 \\
\end{aligned}$$

Fixed point $R_2 = 3 \le 5$ ✓

**Test $\tau_1$:**

$$R_1 = C_1 = 1 \le 4 \checkmark$$

All three tasks meet deadlines. Total utilization:

$$U_{tot} = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{5} + \frac{4}{20} = 0.25 + 0.40 + 0.20 = 0.85$$

This exceeds $n(2^{1/n} - 1) = 3(2^{1/3} - 1) \approx 0.78$, yet **the system is still schedulable** — a perfect illustration of why exact RTA is stronger than the Liu–Layland sufficient bound.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Superloop (No RTOS) | Cooperative | Preemptive RTOS (Tasks) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Concurrency** | None — sequential | Logical only | True (kernel-enforced) |
| **Latency for low-prio work** | Unbounded | Unbounded (busy-wait) | Bounded |
| **Determinism** | Poor | Medium | High |
| **Code complexity** | Low | Medium | Higher |
| **RAM cost (per concurrency unit)** | ~0 bytes | ~stack only | Stack + TCB (≈ 60–100 B) |
| **CPU overhead** | 0% | 0% | 1–5% (scheduler tick) |
| **Failure mode** | Whole app hangs if loop blocks | One stuck task stalls all | Isolated by scheduler |

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Task State Machine

The following Mermaid block represents the **canonical task state transition diagram** for a preemptive RTOS kernel.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Ready : xTaskCreate()
    Ready --> Running : Scheduler dispatches
    Running --> Ready : Preempted by higher priority
    Running --> Blocked : Delay / IPC wait
    Running --> Suspended : vTaskSuspend()
    Blocked --> Ready : Timeout or signal received
    Suspended --> Ready : vTaskResume()
    Running --> [*] : vTaskDelete()
    Suspended --> [*] : vTaskDelete()
    Blocked --> [*] : vTaskDelete()
```

## 4.2 Context Switch Data Flow

This block diagram captures the architectural interactions that occur during a single context switch, from the perspective of the kernel's PendSV exception handler on an ARM Cortex-M core.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Tick ISR fires] --> B{Scheduler runs}
    B --> C[Compare priorities of Ready tasks]
    C --> D[Pick highest priority task]
    D --> E[Outgoing task: save R4 to R11 onto its stack]
    E --> F[Store updated SP into outgoing TCB]
    F --> G[Load new SP from incoming TCB]
    G --> H[Restore R4 to R11 from incoming stack]
    H --> I[EXC_RETURN restores PSP]
    I --> J[Incoming task resumes execution]
    B --> K[No switch needed if same task]
    K --> L[Return from interrupt]
```

## 4.3 Multi-Task System Architecture

This schematic depicts how the kernel, ready list, and individual task control blocks interact at runtime.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph KERNEL[RTOS Kernel]
        SCHED[Scheduler]
        READYLIST[Ready List]
        TICK[System Tick Timer]
    end
    subgraph TASKS[Application Tasks]
        T1[Task 1 TCB + Stack]
        T2[Task 2 TCB + Stack]
        T3[Task 3 TCB + Stack]
        TIDLE[Idle Task]
    end
    SCHED --> READYLIST
    READYLIST --> T1
    READYLIST --> T2
    READYLIST --> T3
    READYLIST --> TIDLE
    TICK --> SCHED
    SCHED -.context switch.-> T1
    SCHED -.context switch.-> T2
    SCHED -.context switch.-> T3
    SCHED -.context switch.-> TIDLE
```

## 4.4 TCB Internal Structure

A block-level view of the Task Control Block fields and their semantic grouping.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TCB[Task Control Block]
        A[pxTopOfStack pointer]
        B[uxPriority base]
        C[xStateListItem]
        D[xEventListItem]
        E[pcTaskName 16 chars]
        F[usStackDepth]
        G[uxBasePriority for inheritance]
        H[Critical nesting count]
    end
    A --> A1[Saved R0 to R15 + xPSR on stack]
    B --> B1[Used by scheduler comparator]
    C --> C1[Doubly linked list anchor for Ready or Blocked list]
    D --> D1[Sort key = wake-up tick value]
    E --> E1[Debug and trace identification]
    F --> F1[Stack overflow checking boundary]
    G --> G1[Original priority before mutex inheritance]
    H --> H1[Critical section depth guard]
```

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a task in an embedded RTOS. List any two differences between a task and a process.**

**Model Answer:**

A **task** is the basic unit of execution in an embedded RTOS. It is an independent, semi-autonomous thread of control characterized by its own stack, its own Task Control Block (TCB), and a static priority under a fixed-priority scheduler. A task typically runs an infinite loop and never returns; it terminates only when explicitly deleted or when the system resets.

| Aspect | Task (RTOS) | Process (Desktop OS) |
|---|---|---|
| Address space | Shared with all tasks | Private, isolated virtual memory |
| Creation cost | Lightweight (TCB + stack) | Heavyweight (page tables, file handles) |
| Typical use | Embedded firmware | Desktop / server applications |

**(3 marks)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of the Task Control Block (TCB). Mention any four fields stored in it.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Task Control Block (TCB)** is a kernel data structure that stores all runtime information about a task. It is the *only* object the scheduler manipulates when deciding which task to run next or when performing a context switch.

Four important fields:
1. **Stack Pointer (`pxTopOfStack`)** — points to the task's current top of stack, used during context save/restore.
2. **Priority (`uxPriority`)** — base priority used by the scheduler to compare readiness.
3. **State List Item (`xStateListItem`)** — links the TCB into the Ready or Blocked list.
4. **Task Name (`pcTaskName`)** — a short identifier for debugging and tracing.

**(3 marks)**

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

#### (a) Describe the various states of an RTOS task with a neat state transition diagram. Discuss the role of the scheduler in each transition. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

An RTOS task can exist in one of four canonical states:

1. **Running:** The task is currently executing on the CPU. Only one task per CPU core can be in this state at any instant.
2. **Ready:** The task is eligible to execute but is not currently on the CPU because an equal or higher-priority task is running. Ready tasks are kept in a priority-ordered Ready List.
3. **Blocked:** The task has voluntarily yielded the CPU because it is waiting on a temporal event (delay expiry), a synchronization object (semaphore, mutex, queue), or a notification. Blocked tasks are removed from the Ready List and placed in an event-wait list.
4. **Suspended:** The task is explicitly placed out of the scheduler's purview by an API call (`vTaskSuspend()`). It will not be scheduled until another task (or ISR) calls `vTaskResume()`.

**Role of the Scheduler in Each Transition:**

| Transition | Trigger | Scheduler's Action |
|---|---|---|
| Ready → Running | Highest-priority Ready task is picked | Loads TCB, restores context |
| Running → Ready | Higher-priority task became Ready (e.g., ISR unblocks it) | Preempts current task, saves context |
| Running → Blocked | Task calls delay, semaphore-wait, or queue-receive | Removes from Ready list, inserts into wait list |
| Blocked → Ready | Waited event occurs (timer tick matches wakeup time, or signal arrives) | Moves TCB back to Ready list |
| Suspended → Ready | `vTaskResume()` invoked | Reinserts into Ready list at base priority |

**State Transition Diagram:**

```
                    xTaskCreate()
                          |
                          v
   +----------+   dispatch   +-----------+   preempt   +---------+
   |  Ready   | --------->   |  Running  | ----------> |  Ready  |
   +----------+              +-----------+             +---------+
        ^                       |   |  \----vTaskSuspend()---->+-----------+
        |                       |   |                          | Suspended |
        | wakeup / event         |   |  vTaskDelete()             +-----------+
        |                       |   +---------> [* (dead)]
   +----------+                 |
   | Blocked  |<-----IPC wait --+
   +----------+
        | vTaskDelete()
        v
       [*]
```

The scheduler is **passive** for transitions *into* Blocked/Suspended (the task initiates them). The scheduler is **active** for transitions from Ready → Running and for the preemption path Running → Ready. **(7 marks)**

#### (b) What is a context switch? Explain its sequence of operations in an ARM Cortex-M based RTOS. Estimate the context switch time if 16 general-purpose registers must be saved, each save takes 2 cycles, and the CPU runs at 72 MHz. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **context switch** is the act of saving the state (CPU registers, program counter, stack pointer) of the currently executing task, and restoring the state of a different task so that it can resume execution as if it had never been interrupted.

**Sequence of Operations (ARM Cortex-M, FreeRTOS-style PendSV handler):**

1. **Hardware auto-saves** R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR onto the outgoing task's stack (8 registers, exception entry).
2. **PendSV handler pushes** R4–R11 onto the outgoing task's stack (8 more registers).
3. **Snapshot the SP:** Save the updated stack pointer into the outgoing task's TCB (`pxTopOfStack = SP`).
4. **Select next task:** Scheduler logic (often in `vTaskSwitchContext()`) picks the highest-priority Ready task.
5. **Load new SP:** Read incoming task's stack pointer from its TCB.
6. **Restore R4–R11** from the incoming stack (software pop).
7. **Exception return (`EXC_RETURN`):** Hardware auto-restores R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR; control transfers to the incoming task.

**Time Estimate:**

- Software save of 8 registers (R4–R11): $8 \times 2 = 16$ cycles.
- Software restore of 8 registers: $8 \times 2 = 16$ cycles.
- Scheduler logic (list traversal): ≈ 20 cycles (typical).
- Total ≈ $16 + 16 + 20 = 52$ cycles.

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{cs} &= \frac{52 \text{ cycles}}{72 \times 10^6 \text{ cycles/sec}} \\
       &\approx 0.722 \text{ µs} \\
\end{aligned}$$

This is the order of magnitude typical for ARM Cortex-M3/M4 context switches. **(7 marks)**
