### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### (a) With a neat diagram, explain the structure of a Task Control Block (TCB). How is the TCB used during context switching? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Task Control Block (TCB)** is a kernel-allocated data structure holding all runtime metadata about a task. Its structure (FreeRTOS-style) is:

```
+--------------------------------+
| pcTaskName[16]                 |  // ASCII identifier
+--------------------------------+
| uxPriority                     |  // Current priority (0..MAX-1)
+--------------------------------+
| uxBasePriority                 |  // For priority inheritance
+--------------------------------+
| pxTopOfStack  --------+        |  // Points into task's stack
+-------------------------+       |
|  xStateListItem        |        |  // Linked into Ready/Blocked list
+-------------------------+       |
|  xEventListItem        |        |  // Sorted by wake-up tick
+-------------------------+       |
| usStackDepth           |        |
+-------------------------+       |
| uxCriticalNesting      |        |
+--------------------------------+
          |
          v
+--------------------------------+
| TASK STACK (usStackDepth words)|
|  [ Saved R4..R11, R0..R3,     ]|
|  [ LR, PC, xPSR, locals       ]|
+--------------------------------+
```

**Role in Context Switching:**

During a context switch, the kernel performs three TCB-mediated operations:

1. **Outgoing task save:** The kernel writes the outgoing task's updated SP into `outgoing_TCB.pxTopOfStack`. This "freezes" the task's state.
2. **Scheduler selection:** The scheduler walks the Ready list (using `xStateListItem` pointers) to pick the highest-priority Ready task.
3. **Incoming task restore:** The kernel reads `incoming_TCB.pxTopOfStack` into the CPU's SP register and unwinds the saved registers.

Without the TCB, the kernel would have no way to remember *where* each task was and *what* it was doing. **(7 marks)**

#### (b) Three periodic tasks are to be scheduled on a single-core RTOS using Rate Monotonic Scheduling. The task parameters are: τ1: T1 = 10 ms, C1 = 2 ms; τ2: T2 = 25 ms, C2 = 4 ms; τ3: T3 = 50 ms, C3 = 5 ms. **(7 marks)**

   **(i)** Compute the total CPU utilization.
   
   **(ii)** Apply the Liu–Layland sufficient test. Is the task set guaranteed schedulable?
   
   **(iii)** Perform the exact Response-Time Analysis for τ3.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Total CPU Utilization:**

$$\begin{aligned}
U_{tot} &= \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{C_3}{T_3} \\
        &= \frac{2}{10} + \frac{4}{25} + \frac{5}{50} \\
        &= 0.200 + 0.160 + 0.100 \\
        &= 0.460 \\
\end{aligned}$$

So **$U_{tot} = 46\%$**.

**(ii) Liu–Layland Bound for n = 3:**

$$\begin{aligned}
U_{bound} &= n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right) \\
          &= 3 \left(2^{1/3} - 1\right) \\
          &= 3 \left(1.2599 - 1\right) \\
          &= 3 \times 0.2599 \\
          &\approx 0.780 \\
\end{aligned}$$

Since $U_{tot} = 0.460 \le 0.780$, the **sufficient** Liu–Layland test is **passed**. The task set is guaranteed schedulable under RMS.

**(iii) Exact Response-Time Analysis for τ3:**

Tasks with higher priority than τ3 under RMS: $hp(3) = \{1, 2\}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(0)} &= C_3 = 5 \\
R_3^{(1)} &= 5 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{10} \right\rceil (2) + \left\lceil \frac{5}{25} \right\rceil (4) \\
        &= 5 + 1 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 4 = 11 \\
R_3^{(2)} &= 5 + \left\lceil \frac{11}{10} \right\rceil (2) + \left\lceil \frac{11}{25} \right\rceil (4) \\
        &= 5 + 2 + 4 = 11 \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Fixed point reached:** $R_3 = 11 \text{ ms} \le D_3 = 50 \text{ ms}$. ✓

The exact analysis confirms that τ3's worst-case response time is 11 ms, comfortably within its 50 ms deadline. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Ready ↔ Blocked semantics:** Students often claim a task "goes to Blocked" when the scheduler runs. Wrong — the scheduler only moves tasks between Ready and Running. Blocked is *task-initiated*.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the ceiling operator** in the RTA recurrence. Writing $C_j / T_j$ instead of $\lceil R_i / T_j \rceil C_j$ earns **zero** marks for the calculation part.
> 
> 3. **Reporting utilization as a percentage** without unit clarification. Always state $U = 0.46$ (dimensionless) **and** 46%. Examiners mark both.
> 
> 4. **Ignoring context switch overhead** in schedulability answers. A complete answer must mention that $T_{cs}$ adds to effective $C_i$, potentially breaking feasibility.
> 
> 5. **Sketching the state diagram with arrows missing labels.** Each arrow in KTU diagrams must be labeled with its triggering API or event.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 3 — Tasks**
> 
> - A **task** = `void task_fn(void *p)` infinite loop + private stack + TCB. Never returns; must self-delete.
> - **Four states:** Running, Ready, Blocked, Suspended. State count is invariant: $N = \#R + \#\text{Rdy} + \#B + \#S$.
> - **TCB fields to memorize:** stack pointer, priority, base priority, state list, event list, name, stack depth, critical nesting.
> - **Ready List:** Highest priority = next to run. Ties broken by FIFO or round-robin.
> - **Context switch** = save outgoing context, pick next, restore incoming context. Hardware does partial save; software saves the rest.
> - **Liu–Layland bound:** $U_{bound} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$. Sufficient but not necessary. For $n=2$ it is 0.828.
> - **RTA recurrence:** $R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i^{(k)} / T_j \rceil C_j$. Iterate to fixed point.
> - **Idle task** is the lowest-priority task; it runs when no other task is Ready and cleans up deleted TCBs.
> - **Stack sizing** must account for call depth, ISR nesting, and a safety margin (e.g., 25%). Under-sized stacks cause silent corruption.
> - **Critical sections** within tasks disable interrupts; keep them *short* to avoid latency violations.
> - **Context switch time** is *real* overhead ($1$–$50$ µs); include it in schedulability for hard real-time systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process and Threads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET695 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Process and Threads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Embedded System Design**, a **process** is a program in execution — a fundamental unit of work managed by the operating system (or Real-Time Operating System, RTOS). Each process is a self-contained entity that possesses its own dedicated memory space, including a **code segment (text)**, **data segment**, **heap**, and **stack**. A process is characterized by its **Process Control Block (PCB)**, which the kernel uses to track its execution state, registers, memory maps, open files, and scheduling information.

A **thread**, on the other hand, is the smallest unit of execution within a process. It is often called a **lightweight process (LWP)**. Multiple threads within the same process share the same address space (code, data, heap) but maintain **independent stacks, register sets, and program counters**. In embedded RTOS environments (such as FreeRTOS, VxWorks, or RTEMS), threads are the primary abstraction used for concurrent task execution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Embedded systems primarily use **threads (tasks)** rather than full processes, because embedded environments have strict memory constraints and demand minimal overhead. However, the process model is the foundational concept that explains why threads are necessary.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Restaurant Kitchen**
>
> Imagine a **restaurant kitchen**:
>
> - A **process** is the **entire restaurant** — it has its own building (memory space), its own kitchen equipment (resources), its own menu (code), and its own dining area (data). Two different restaurants cannot reach into each other's kitchens.
>
> - A **thread** is a **chef working inside that restaurant**. Multiple chefs (threads) can work in the same kitchen (shared memory). Each chef has their own personal notepad and knife set (private stack and registers), but they all share the same pantry (heap) and recipe book (code segment).
>
> - When a chef is waiting for an oven to preheat (I/O wait), another chef can continue cooking — this is **concurrency**.
> - Each chef knows exactly where they paused their work — this is the **thread context**.

In embedded systems, consider a **smart washing machine controller**:
- **One thread** monitors the water level sensor.
- **Another thread** controls the motor.
- **A third thread** updates the LCD display.
- **A fourth thread** handles the user button inputs.

All these threads share the microcontroller's memory but execute independently — this is the power of threaded programming in embedded systems.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Process** | A running program with its own isolated virtual address space. |
| **Thread** | A lightweight execution path within a process; shares memory with sibling threads. |
| **PCB** | Process Control Block — kernel data structure storing process state. |
| **TCB** | Thread Control Block — kernel data structure storing thread state. |
| **Context Switch** | Saving the state of one execution unit and loading another. |
| **Multithreading** | Multiple threads executing concurrently within a single process. |
| **Multitasking** | Multiple tasks (processes/threads) sharing CPU time. |

## 1.4 Standard Metrics in Embedded Threading

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Context Switch Time:** Typically **1–10 µs** in RTOS environments (versus 10–100 µs for full process switches).
> - **Thread Creation Overhead:** Usually **100–500 bytes** of RAM per thread stack.
> - **Priority Levels:** RTOS systems typically support **32–256 priority levels**.
> - **Minimum Stack Size:** Often **64 bytes to 4 KB** depending on MCU architecture (ARM Cortex-M, AVR, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process Architecture — Detailed Breakdown

A process in execution consists of several distinct components. Understanding these is essential for KTU examinations because they frequently appear in 14-mark questions.

### 2.1.1 Components of a Process

1. **Code Segment (Text Segment):**
   - Contains the compiled executable instructions.
   - Typically **read-only** to prevent accidental modification.
   - Shared among all instances of the same program.

2. **Data Segment:**
   - Stores **global** and **static variables**.
   - Divided into:
     - **Initialized Data Segment** — explicit initial values.
     - **BSS (Block Started by Symbol)** — uninitialized/zero-initialized data.

3. **Heap:**
   - Dynamically allocated memory during runtime (`malloc`, `new`).
   - Grows **upward** (toward higher addresses).
   - Managed manually in C, automatically (GC) in managed languages.

4. **Stack:**
   - Stores **local variables**, **function parameters**, **return addresses**.
   - Grows **downward** (toward lower addresses).
   - Follows **LIFO (Last-In-First-Out)** discipline.

5. **Process Control Block (PCB):**
   - Kernel-managed data structure containing:
     - **Process ID (PID)**
     - **Process State** (New, Ready, Running, Waiting, Terminated)
     - **Program Counter**
     - **CPU Registers**
     - **Memory Management Information** (page tables, segment tables)
     - **I/O Status Information**
     - **Scheduling Information** (priority, accounting)

## 2.2 Process States — The Five-State Model

```
NEW → READY → RUNNING → TERMINATED
              ↑     ↓
              WAITING
```

- **NEW:** Process is being created.
- **READY:** Process is waiting to be assigned to the CPU.
- **RUNNING:** Instructions are being executed.
- **WAITING (BLOCKED):** Process is waiting for some event (I/O completion, signal).
- **TERMINATED:** Process has finished execution.

> [!NOTE]
> In RTOS environments, the state model is often simplified to: **Ready → Running → Blocked**, because processes do not dynamically spawn — tasks are statically created at boot time.

## 2.3 Thread Architecture — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.3.1 Components of a Thread

Each thread maintains:
- **Thread ID**
- **Program Counter**
- **Register Set** (including stack pointer)
- **Stack** (private to the thread)
- **Thread Local Storage (TLS)** — thread-specific data
- **Thread State**

Threads **share** with their parent process:
- Code segment
- Data segment
- Heap
- Open files and file descriptors
- Process credentials
- Signal handlers

### 2.3.2 Types of Threads

| Type | Description |
|------|-------------|
| **User-Level Threads (ULT)** | Managed entirely by user-space libraries (e.g., GNU Pth). Fast to create but cannot leverage multiprocessing. |
| **Kernel-Level Threads (KLT)** | Managed directly by the OS kernel. Slower to create but can run in parallel on multi-core systems. |
| **Hybrid Threads** | Combine both — e.g., many ULTs mapped to fewer KLTs (M:N model). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Embedded RTOS Context:** Most embedded RTOS (FreeRTOS, VxWorks, ThreadX) use **kernel-level threads** because they require deterministic scheduling and direct hardware access.

## 2.4 Process vs. Thread — Comparative Analysis

| Feature | Process | Thread |
|---------|---------|--------|
| **Memory Space** | Independent | Shared within process |
| **Creation Overhead** | High | Low |
| **Context Switch Time** | **10–100 µs** | **1–10 µs** |
| **Communication** | IPC (pipes, sockets, shared memory) | Shared variables, mutexes, semaphores |
| **Crash Impact** | Isolated | Affects entire process |
| **Resource Ownership** | Owns resources | Borrows from parent process |
| **Parallelism** | True parallelism | Within-process concurrency |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are essential for solving embedded scheduling and threading problems in KTU examinations.

### 2.5.1 CPU Utilization Formula

$$U = 1 - P^n$$

Where:
- $U$ = CPU utilization (fraction of time CPU is busy)
- $P$ = probability of I/O wait (idle fraction per task)
- $n$ = number of concurrent tasks/processes

### 2.5.2 Amdahl's Law for Parallel Speedup

$$S = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{n}}$$

Where:
- $S$ = theoretical speedup
- $f$ = parallelizable fraction of the workload
- $n$ = number of parallel execution units (cores/threads)

### 2.5.3 Context Switch Time (Effective CPU Time)

$$T_{effective} = T_{compute} + (N_{switches} \times T_{context\_switch})$$

### 2.5.4 Thread Stack Memory Calculation (Embedded)

$$S_{total} = (N_{threads} \times S_{stack}) + S_{kernel} + S_{heap} + S_{globals}$$

Where:
- $S_{stack}$ = typical range **256 bytes to 8 KB** per thread in embedded MCUs
- $N_{threads}$ = number of concurrent threads
- $S_{kernel}$ = RTOS kernel memory footprint

### 2.5.5 Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) — Feasibility Test

$$U_{bound} = n \times (2^{1/n} - 1)$$

Where $n$ is the number of tasks. For feasibility:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$$

- $C_i$ = worst-case execution time of task $i$
- $T_i$ = period of task $i$

> [!NOTE]
> For $n \to \infty$, the bound approaches **$\ln(2) \approx 0.693$**, meaning RMS can guarantee schedulability only if total utilization is below 69.3%.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECUs:** Threads manage engine control, brake systems, infotainment concurrently.
- **IoT Devices:** Separate threads handle sensor polling, network transmission, and local UI.
- **Medical Devices:** Threads separate safety-critical monitoring from non-critical logging.
- **Industrial Robotics:** Threads coordinate kinematics, motor control, and vision processing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: CPU Utilization Under Multithreading

We derive the CPU utilization formula step by step, as this is a KTU favorite.

### Step 1: Define the Single-Task Idle Probability

Assume each task has probability $P$ of being in the I/O-wait state at any given moment. The probability that the CPU is **idle** when running a single task is therefore $P$.

### Step 2: Extend to n Tasks

For $n$ concurrent tasks, the CPU is idle **only when all tasks are simultaneously waiting**.

$$P_{idle} = P \times P \times P \times \ldots \times P = P^n$$

### Step 3: Compute CPU Utilization

Since utilization is the complement of idle time:

$$U = 1 - P_{idle} = 1 - P^n$$

This completes the derivation. The formula is fundamental for sizing embedded system workloads.

## 3.2 Derivation: Amdahl's Law for Embedded Multi-Core Systems

Amdahl's Law quantifies the maximum speedup achievable by parallelizing a workload.

### Step 1: Decompose Execution Time

Let total execution time be normalized to 1. The fraction that can be parallelized is $f$, and the serial portion is $(1-f)$.

### Step 2: Apply n Parallel Units

The parallel portion now takes $\frac{f}{n}$ time with $n$ cores/threads. The serial portion still takes $(1-f)$ time.

### Step 3: Total Parallel Execution Time

$$T_{parallel} = (1-f) + \frac{f}{n}$$

### Step 4: Compute Speedup

$$S = \frac{T_{serial}}{T_{parallel}} = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \frac{f}{n}}$$

### Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** An embedded image-processing algorithm takes 200 ms on a single core. 80% of the algorithm is parallelizable across 4 cores. Find the speedup.

**Step 1:** Identify parameters:
- $f = 0.80$
- $n = 4$
- $T_{serial} = 200$ ms

**Step 2:** Apply the formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{parallel} &= (1 - 0.80) + \frac{0.80}{4} \\
&= 0.20 + 0.20 \\
&= 0.40
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate speedup:

$$S = \frac{1}{0.40} = 2.5$$

**Step 4:** Find actual parallel execution time:

$$T_{parallel\_actual} = 200 \times 0.40 = 80 \text{ ms}$$

> **Conclusion:** The 4-core parallelization gives 2.5× speedup (not 4×), limited by the 20% serial portion.

## 3.3 Derivation: RMS Feasibility Bound

The Rate Monotonic Scheduling (Liu & Layland, 1973) bound is derived using the concept of the critical instant.

### Step 1: Define the Scheduling Test

For RMS to guarantee that all deadlines are met, the total processor utilization must satisfy:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq U_{bound}(n)$$

### Step 2: Compute the Bound for n Tasks

Using the worst-case phasing (all tasks arriving simultaneously), the bound is:

$$U_{bound}(n) = n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$$

### Step 3: Compute Specific Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
n = 1: \quad & U_{bound} = 1(2^1 - 1) = 1.000 \\
n = 2: \quad & U_{bound} = 2(2^{0.5} - 1) \approx 0.828 \\
n = 3: \quad & U_{bound} = 3(2^{0.333} - 1) \approx 0.780 \\
n \to \infty: \quad & U_{bound} \to \ln(2) \approx 0.693
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** Three tasks have the following parameters:
> - Task 1: $C_1 = 2$ ms, $T_1 = 10$ ms
> - Task 2: $C_2 = 3$ ms, $T_2 = 15$ ms
> - Task 3: $C_3 = 2$ ms, $T_3 = 20$ ms
>
> Is the task set schedulable under RMS?

**Step 1:** Compute utilization:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{C_3}{T_3} \\
&= \frac{2}{10} + \frac{3}{15} + \frac{2}{20} \\
&= 0.200 + 0.200 + 0.100 \\
&= 0.500
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the bound for $n = 3$:

$$U_{bound}(3) = 3(2^{1/3} - 1) \approx 0.780$$

**Step 3:** Compare:

$$U = 0.500 \leq U_{bound}(3) = 0.780 \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The task set **is schedulable** under RMS.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Thread Synchronization Mechanisms

In multithreaded embedded systems, threads must coordinate access to shared resources. The following matrix maps problem to solution.

| Synchronization Need | Mechanism | Use Case |
|---------------------|-----------|----------|
| Mutual exclusion | **Mutex** | Protecting shared peripheral registers |
| Counting events | **Semaphore** | Producer-consumer buffers (UART, SPI) |
| Wait for event | **Event Flags / Condition Variables** | Sensor data ready signals |
| One-time initialization | **Once Barrier** | Initializing hardware at startup |
| Read-only sharing | **Lock-free / Atomic Access** | Status flags, counters |

## 3.5 Context Switch Time Derivation

### Step 1: Define Components

A context switch requires saving and restoring:

1. **General-purpose registers** ($R_0$ to $R_{31}$ on ARM Cortex-M)
2. **Special registers** (Program Counter, Stack Pointer, Link Register)
3. **Status register / Flags**
4. **Floating-point registers** (if FPU is used)

### Step 2: Time Formula

$$T_{cs} = T_{save} + T_{load} + T_{scheduler}$$

Where:
- $T_{save}$ = time to save current thread state
- $T_{load}$ = time to load next thread state
- $T_{scheduler}$ = time to select next thread (algorithm complexity)

### Step 3: Typical Embedded Values

For **ARM Cortex-M4** running FreeRTOS:
- $T_{save} \approx 12$ cycles
- $T_{load} \approx 12$ cycles
- $T_{scheduler} \approx 5$ cycles
- At 168 MHz: $T_{cs} \approx \frac{29}{168 \times 10^6} \approx 173$ **ns**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process vs. Thread Memory Architecture (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph process1["PROCESS A — Independent Address Space"]
        codeA["CODE SEGMENT"]
        dataA["DATA SEGMENT"]
        heapA["HEAP"]
        subgraph tcbA["THREADS OF PROCESS A"]
            tA1["Thread 1: Stack, Registers, PC"]
            tA2["Thread 2: Stack, Registers, PC"]
            tA3["Thread 3: Stack, Registers, PC"]
        end
        pcba["PCB — Process Control Block"]
    end

    subgraph process2["PROCESS B — Independent Address Space"]
        codeB["CODE SEGMENT"]
        dataB["DATA SEGMENT"]
        heapB["HEAP"]
        tB1["Single Thread"]
        pcbb["PCB — Process Control Block"]
    end

    process1 -.->|"Isolated Boundary"| process2
    tA1 -.->|"Shared: code, data, heap"| tA2
    tA2 -.->|"Shared: code, data, heap"| tA3
```

## 4.2 Thread Lifecycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Created
    Created --> Ready: Thread initialized
    Ready --> Running: Scheduler dispatches
    Running --> Ready: Time slice expired
    Running --> Blocked: I/O wait, mutex lock, sleep
    Blocked --> Ready: I/O complete, mutex released, timeout
    Running --> Terminated: Task complete or deleted
    Terminated --> [*]
```

## 4.3 RTOS Multithreading Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph hw["HARDWARE LAYER"]
        mcu["ARM Cortex-M MCU"]
        sensor["Sensors I2C"]
        motor["Motor Driver PWM"]
        uart["UART Debug"]
    end

    subgraph rtos["RTOS KERNEL — FreeRTOS Scheduler"]
        scheduler["Priority Scheduler"]
        readyQ["Ready Queue"]
    end

    subgraph threads["APPLICATION THREADS"]
        t1["Thread T1: Sensor Task Priority 3"]
        t2["Thread T2: Motor Control Priority 5"]
        t3["Thread T3: UI Display Priority 1"]
        t4["Thread T4: Comm Task Priority 2"]
    end

    subgraph sync["SYNCHRONIZATION PRIMITIVES"]
        mutex1["Mutex: Shared Buffer"]
        sem1["Semaphore: UART TX"]
        queue1["Queue: Sensor Data"]
    end

    t1 --> mutex1
    t3 --> mutex1
    t2 --> sem1
    t4 --> queue1
    t1 --> queue1

    scheduler --> t1
    scheduler --> t2
    scheduler --> t3
    scheduler --> t4

    t1 -.->|Controls| sensor
    t2 -.->|Controls| motor
    t4 -.->|Controls| uart

    readyQ --> scheduler
```

## 4.4 Context Switch Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant T1 as Thread T1 Running
    participant Kernel as RTOS Kernel
    participant T2 as Thread T2 Ready
    participant HW as CPU Hardware

    T1->>HW: Execute instructions
    Note over T1,HW: Timer interrupt fires
    HW->>Kernel: ISR triggers scheduler
    Kernel->>T1: Save registers, PC, SP into TCB
    Kernel->>T2: Load registers, PC, SP from TCB
    Kernel->>HW: Restore context, resume T2
    T2->>HW: Execute instructions
```

## 4.5 Thread Synchronization Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Thread attempts resource access]) --> check{Mutex available?}
    check -->|Yes| lock[Acquire mutex]
    lock --> critical[Enter critical section]
    critical --> work[Read/Write shared data]
    work --> unlock[Release mutex]
    unlock --> done([Continue execution])

    check -->|No| block[Block thread on mutex]
    block --> wait[Add to wait queue]
    wait --> yield[Yield CPU to other threads]
    yield --> event[Wait for mutex release signal]
    event --> check
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a process and a thread. List any two differences between them.**

**Model Answer:**

A **process** is a program in execution, treated by the operating system as an independent unit with its own dedicated memory address space, system resources, and a Process Control Block (PCB). Each process is isolated from other processes.

A **thread** is the smallest unit of execution within a process. Multiple threads within the same process share the process's address space (code, data, heap) but maintain their own stack, register set, and program counter.

**Differences:**

| Aspect | Process | Thread |
|--------|---------|--------|
| Memory | Independent address space | Shared address space |
| Context switch time | High (10–100 µs) | Low (1–10 µs) |

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is a Process Control Block (PCB)? Mention any four fields stored in it.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Process Control Block (PCB)** is a kernel data structure that stores all the information required to manage a particular process. It is created when the process is created and destroyed when the process terminates.

**Four fields stored in PCB:**
1. **Process ID (PID)** — unique identifier for the process.
2. **Process State** — current state (New, Ready, Running, Waiting, Terminated).
3. **Program Counter (PC)** — address of the next instruction to execute.
4. **CPU Registers** — values of general-purpose and special-purpose registers.

*(Other valid fields: memory management info, scheduling info, I/O status, accounting info.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Explain the five-state process model with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of queues in process scheduling. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The five-state process model describes the lifecycle of a process through five distinct states:

1. **NEW:** The process is being created. The OS allocates a PCB and initializes data structures.
2. **READY:** The process has been loaded into main memory and is waiting in the ready queue to be assigned to the CPU.
3. **RUNNING:** The process is currently executing on the CPU. At any instant, only one process per core can be in this state.
4. **WAITING (BLOCKED):** The process cannot continue execution because it is waiting for some event (I/O completion, signal from another process).
5. **TERMINATED:** The process has finished execution. The OS deallocates its resources and PCB.

```
    NEW ──→ READY ──→ RUNNING ──→ TERMINATED
              ↑          ↓
              ↑        WAITING
              ↑          ↓
              └──────────┘
```

**Role of Queues in Process Scheduling:**

- **Ready Queue:** Holds all processes that are ready to execute but waiting for CPU time. Maintained as a linked list or priority queue.
- **Wait Queues (Blocked Queues):** One per event type (e.g., I/O completion, semaphore signal). Processes move here when they cannot proceed.
- **Scheduler:** Picks processes from the ready queue and dispatches them to the CPU.

**Transitions:**
- NEW → READY: admission to ready queue
- READY → RUNNING: scheduler dispatch
- RUNNING → READY: preempted by scheduler (time-slice expired)
- RUNNING → WAITING: I/O request issued
- WAITING → READY: I/O completed, event signaled

---

**(b) Compare user-level threads and kernel-level threads. Why are kernel-level threads preferred in embedded RTOS? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | User-Level Threads (ULT) | Kernel-Level Threads (KLT) |
|-----------|--------------------------|----------------------------|
| Managed by | User-space thread library | OS kernel |
| Creation speed | Very fast (no syscall) | Slower (requires syscall) |
| Context switch speed | Fast (no kernel mode switch) | Slower (requires kernel intervention) |
| Parallelism | No — all threads run on one kernel thread | Yes — can use multiple cores |
| Blocking behavior | One thread blocks → all threads in process block | One thread blocks → others continue |
| Scheduling awareness | Kernel unaware of threads | Kernel performs scheduling |
| Portability | Depends on library | Native OS support |

**Why Kernel-Level Threads Are Preferred in Embedded RTOS:**

1. **Determinism:** Embedded RTOS require predictable timing. KLTs allow the kernel to enforce precise scheduling and priority-based preemption.
2. **True Concurrency on Multi-core MCUs:** Modern embedded SoCs (e.g., NXP i.MX RT, STM32 H7) have multiple cores. Only KLTs can leverage parallelism.
3. **Blocking I/O Handling:** KLTs ensure that when one thread waits for I/O (e.g., SPI DMA completion), other threads continue executing — critical in real-time control.
4. **Hardware Integration:** KLTs directly map to hardware interrupts, enabling responsive ISR-thread coordination.
5. **Priority Inheritance:** KLTs support advanced features like priority inheritance protocols (PIP) to avoid priority inversion.

**Conclusion:** Kernel-level threads are the standard for embedded RTOS like FreeRTOS, VxWorks, and ThreadX.

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Explain the concept of threads in an embedded system. With a neat diagram, describe the thread lifecycle in an RTOS. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

In an embedded system, a **thread** (also called a **task**) is an independent execution path that shares the system's memory with other threads but maintains its own execution context (program counter, registers, stack). Threads are the fundamental concurrency units in embedded RTOS because they provide responsive, deterministic multitasking with minimal overhead.

**Advantages of threads in embedded systems:**
- **Modularity:** Separate concerns (sensor, actuator, UI) into independent units.
- **Responsiveness:** High-priority threads can preempt lower-priority ones.
- **Resource efficiency:** Shared memory reduces RAM usage compared to processes.
- **Simplicity:** Each thread can be designed and tested independently.

**Thread Lifecycle in RTOS:**

```
   Created → Ready → Running → Terminated
                ↑        ↓
                ←── Blocked
```

1. **Created:** Thread is allocated but not yet ready to run (e.g., `xTaskCreate()` in FreeRTOS).
2. **Ready:** Thread is in the ready queue awaiting CPU assignment. The scheduler picks it based on priority.
3. **Running:** Thread is actively executing on the CPU.
4. **Blocked:** Thread is waiting for an event — I/O completion, semaphore give, queue receive, or delay expiration.
5. **Terminated:** Thread completes its function and is deleted by the kernel.

**Transitions in detail:**
- Created → Ready: when the thread is added to the ready queue
- Ready → Running: scheduler dispatches the highest-priority ready thread
- Running → Blocked: `vTaskDelay()`, `xQueueReceive()`, `xSemaphoreTake()` — thread cannot proceed
- Blocked → Ready: timeout expires, I/O completes, semaphore given
- Running → Ready: preemption by higher-priority thread
- Running → Terminated: thread function returns or `vTaskDelete()` invoked

---

**(b) Discuss the various thread synchronization mechanisms used in RTOS. Why is mutual exclusion important in shared resource access? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Thread Synchronization Mechanisms in RTOS:**

1. **Mutex (Mutual Exclusion):**
   - A locking mechanism that ensures only one thread accesses a shared resource at a time.
   - Supports **priority inheritance** to prevent priority inversion.
   - Example: protecting a shared UART buffer.

2. **Semaphore:**
   - **Binary Semaphore:** Acts as a signal between tasks (e.g., ISR signals task that data is ready).
   - **Counting Semaphore:** Manages a pool of identical resources (e.g., 3 SPI peripherals available).

3. **Message Queues:**
   - Allow threads to exchange data asynchronously.
   - Producer thread puts data; consumer thread reads.
   - Example: sensor task sends readings to display task.

4. **Event Flags / Event Groups:**
   - Threads wait for multiple events simultaneously (logical OR/AND).
   - Example: wait for "data ready" AND "calibration complete".

5. **Mutex with Recursive Locking:**
   - Allows the same thread to lock the same mutex multiple times without deadlock.

**Why Mutual Exclusion is Important:**

Without mutual exclusion, multiple threads accessing shared data simultaneously can cause **race conditions**, leading to:
- **Data corruption:** E.g., two threads writing different values to the same memory location.
- **Inconsistent state:** Partially updated structures.
- **Hardware malfunction:** E.g., one thread configures GPIO while another reads it.

**Example Scenario in Embedded System:**

Suppose two threads, T1 and T2, both write to a global motor speed variable `motorSpeed`:
- T1 reads `motorSpeed = 100`
- T1 is preempted
- T2 reads `motorSpeed = 100`
- T2 writes `motorSpeed = 200`
- T1 resumes and writes `motorSpeed = 100` (incorrect!)

A mutex around the read-modify-write operation prevents this race condition.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often confuse **binary semaphore** with **mutex** — remember: a mutex has **ownership** (only the locking thread can unlock), while a binary semaphore does not.
> 2. In thread lifecycle diagrams, students forget to show the **preemption** arrow (Running → Ready) — this is a common mark deduction.
> 3. Do not state that context switch time for processes is "less" than threads — it is the **reverse**: threads have lower context switch overhead.
> 4. When explaining RMS, students often forget to state that $U_{bound}$ is a **sufficient but not necessary** condition — it is conservative.
> 5. Always mention that in embedded systems, threads share memory but have **separate stacks** — this is the most frequently missed distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Process and Threads**

- [ ] A **process** has its own address space; **threads** within a process share memory.
- [ ] The **PCB** stores process state, PC, registers, scheduling info, memory info.
- [ ] The **five process states**: New, Ready, Running, Waiting, Terminated.
- [ ] **Context switch time** for threads (~1–10 µs) is much less than processes (~10–100 µs).
- [ ] Threads share **code, data, heap** but maintain **private stacks and registers**.
- [ ] **Kernel-level threads** are preferred in RTOS for determinism and true parallelism.
- [ ] **CPU utilization** under $n$ tasks with idle probability $P$: $U = 1 - P^n$.
- [ ] **Amdahl's Law:** $S = \frac{1}{(1-f) + f/n}$ — accounts for serial fraction.
- [ ] **RMS Feasibility:** $\sum C_i/T_i \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$; limit is **~69.3%** as $n \to \infty$.
- [ ] **Synchronization primitives:** Mutex, Semaphore, Queue, Event Flags.
- [ ] **Mutex** = ownership-based lock; **Semaphore** = signal-based counter.
- [ ] In embedded systems, threads map naturally to **tasks** (`xTaskCreate` in FreeRTOS).
- [ ] Stack size per thread is typically **256 B – 8 KB** depending on MCU.
- [ ] **Priority inversion** must be handled via **priority inheritance** in mutexes.
- [ ] Always cite that embedded RTOS uses **preemptive priority-based scheduling** for real-time guarantees.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiprocessing and Multitasking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET695 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Multiprocessing and Multitasking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multiprocessing** is a system architecture in which **two or more physical processing units (CPUs/Cores)** within a single embedded/computing system work cooperatively to execute **multiple processes or threads simultaneously**, sharing common memory, I/O, and a single OS kernel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multitasking** is the logical capability of an operating system (or RTOS) to **execute multiple tasks** (threads/processes) on a **single processor** by interleaving their execution through **context switching**, giving the illusion of parallelism while the CPU core services only one task at any given instant.

In the KTU 2024 Embedded System Design syllabus context, both terms belong to the domain of **concurrent program execution models** that allow embedded controllers (ARM Cortex-M/A, PIC, AVR, etc.) to handle sensors, actuators, communication stacks, and UI concurrently.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen"

| Concept | Real-World Analogy | Embedded Mapping |
|---|---|---|
| **Processor / Core** | A single chef in a kitchen | One CPU/CPU core |
| **Task** | An order ticket on the rail | A thread/process |
| **Multitasking** | One chef juggling 5 dishes by switching rapidly | Single-core scheduler |
| **Multiprocessing** | A brigade of 5 chefs, each handling one dish | Multi-core SoC (e.g., Cortex-A7 dual-core) |
| **Context Switch** | Chef wiping his mental whiteboard to remember another dish's state | Save/restore registers, PC, SP, PSR |
| **Preemption** | Head chef shouting "Stop! Drop that dish, fry the steak NOW!" | Higher-priority IRQ preempts current task |
| **Mutex/Semaphore** | The single key to the oven | Synchronization primitive |
| **Deadlock** | Two chefs holding one spoon each, waiting for the other | Circular wait in tasks |

The analogy is precise: just as a busy kitchen needs coordination to deliver 100 meals on time, an embedded RTOS needs **schedulers + IPC + synchronization primitives** to deliver real-time guarantees across concurrent tasks.

---

## 1.3 KTU Syllabus-Mandated Classification

### A) Taxonomy of Multiprocessing (Hardware-side concurrency)

1. **Symmetric Multiprocessing (SMP)** — Identical processors sharing a common main memory and OS image. Each CPU can run any task. Used in high-end embedded Linux boards (e.g., Raspberry Pi 4 BCM2711 — Quad-core A72).
2. **Asymmetric Multiprocessing (AMP)** — Heterogeneous cores (e.g., Cortex-A application cores + Cortex-M microcontroller cores on the same SoC). Each runs its own OS/firmware independently. Used in NXP i.MX8, TI Sitara, ST STM32MP1.
3. **Bound (or Dedicated) Multiprocessing (BMP)** — Specific tasks are statically pinned to specific CPUs. Common in DSP + MCU hybrid SoCs (e.g., TI OMAP, ADSP-BFxxx).

### B) Taxonomy of Multitasking (Software-side concurrency)

1. **Cooperative (Non-preemptive) Multitasking** — A task runs until it voluntarily yields (calls `taskYIELD()` or blocks on I/O). Used in simple embedded firmwares (FreeRTOS "cooperative mode").
2. **Preemptive Multitasking** — The kernel's scheduler can forcibly interrupt a running task at any tick/interrupt and switch to a higher-priority ready task. Used in μC/OS-II, VxWorks, ThreadX, real FreeRTOS.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite Question:** "Differentiate between Multiprocessing and Multitasking." Always mention the keyword **"physical parallelism" vs "logical concurrency."**

---

## 1.4 Quick Differentiation Table

| Parameter | Multitasking | Multiprocessing |
|---|---|---|
| Processors required | **1** | **≥ 2** |
| Parallelism type | Logical (apparent) | Physical (true) |
| Execution simultaneity | No | Yes |
| Cost | Low | High (SoC complexity) |
| Complexity unit | Scheduler | Scheduler + Inter-core communication |
| Embedded example | FreeRTOS on STM32F4 | Dual-core Cortex-A + Cortex-M on i.MX8 |
| Bottleneck | Single CPU bandwidth | Memory contention, cache coherency |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Task Life-Cycle States (KTU-Relevant)

In any preemptive RTOS scheduler, every task exists in one of the following canonical states:

1. **Running** — Task's instructions are currently executing on the CPU.
2. **Ready** — Task is eligible to run but the CPU is allocated to another equal/higher-priority task.
3. **Blocked / Waiting** — Task is suspended awaiting an event (semaphore, queue, delay, mutex).
4. **Suspended / Dormant** — Task is explicitly taken out of the scheduler's active list (rare in embedded).
5. **Deleted / Terminated** — Task has finished execution.

Transitions are driven by:
- **System Tick Interrupt (SysTick)** — fires at `configTICK_RATE_HZ` (e.g., 1 kHz).
- **External Interrupts (IRQ)** — preemption triggers.
- **API calls** — `vTaskDelay()`, `xQueueReceive()`, `xSemaphoreTake()`.

---

## 2.2 Context Switching — The Heart of Multitasking

A **context switch** is the atomic act of saving the CPU state of the outgoing task and restoring the CPU state of the incoming task.

**CPU state typically saved (ARM Cortex-M example):**
- `R0–R12` (General purpose registers)
- `SP` (Stack Pointer — process-specific)
- `LR` (Link Register)
- `PC` (Program Counter)
- `xPSR` (Program Status Register)
- Floating-point registers `S0–S31` (if FPU lazy stacking)

**Steps (KTU Theory):**
1. Trigger: SysTick fires → `PendSV` exception (lowest priority).
2. Hardware auto-stacks `R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR` onto current task's PSP.
3. PendSV handler saves `R4–R11` in software.
4. Scheduler reads `pxCurrentTCB` of next ready task.
5. Software restores `R4–R11` of incoming task.
6. Hardware unstacks incoming task's `R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR`.
7. `bx LR` (with EXC_RETURN) atomically returns into the new task.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Context switch time** (typically **1–10 µs** on Cortex-M4 @168 MHz) is a **hard real-time metric** — it directly bounds the maximum interrupt latency.

---

## 2.3 Scheduling Algorithms (Embedded Focus)

### 2.3.1 Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) — *Static Priority*
- **Priority ∝ Frequency** — the task with the shortest period gets the highest priority.
- Fixed at compile-time, no runtime overhead.
- **Optimal** among static-priority algorithms (Liu & Layland, 1973).
- Schedulability test (sufficient, but not necessary):
$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n\left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$$

### 2.3.2 Earliest Deadline First (EDF) — *Dynamic Priority*
- At every scheduling point, run the task whose **absolute deadline** is earliest.
- Optimal for uniprocessor preemptive scheduling (no idle time if feasible).
- Schedulability test: **U ≤ 1.0** (necessary and sufficient).
- Downside: harder to implement, more overhead, less predictable.

### 2.3.3 Round Robin (RR) — *Time-sharing fairness*
- Each ready task gets a **time quantum / time slice** `q` (e.g., 10 ms).
- After expiry, task is appended to the tail of the ready queue.
- Used for **time-sharing OS**, **NOT** typical real-time.
- Response time: $Worst\text{-}case = q \cdot N$ for N tasks.

### 2.3.4 Priority-Based Preemptive (the RTOS standard)
- Hybrid: RMS-like static priorities with RR within same priority.
- FreeRTOS uses this by default (`configUSE_TIME_SLICING = 1`).

---

## 2.4 Inter-Process Communication (IPC) & Synchronization

| Mechanism | Use Case | RTOS Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Semaphore (Binary/Counting)** | Resource counting, signaling | `xSemaphoreCreateBinary()` |
| **Mutex (with Priority Inheritance)** | Mutual exclusion | `xSemaphoreCreateMutex()` |
| **Message Queue** | Task-to-task data passing | `xQueueCreate()` |
| **Mailbox** | Single-slot message | μC/OS-II `OSMboxCreate()` |
| **Event Flags / Groups** | Multi-condition synchrony | `xEventGroupCreate()` |
| **Pipes / Stream Buffers** | Byte-stream data (UART, etc.) | `xStreamBufferCreate()` |
| **Shared Memory** | High-speed DMA-style transfer | Direct pointer (with critical section) |
| **Mailbox / RPC** | Inter-CPU communication in SMP | OpenAMP / RPMsg |

> [!WARNING]
> **Priority Inversion Pitfall:** A low-priority task holding a mutex that a high-priority task needs can starve mid-priority tasks indefinitely. **Solution:** Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) — the holding task temporarily inherits the highest priority of any waiter. Mars Pathfinder (1997) is the classic KTU case-study.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Meaning / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CPU Utilization (RMS)** | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i / T_i$ | Sum of execution-time/period ratios |
| 2 | **RMS Schedulability Bound** | $U_{max} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | Sufficient test (Liu-Layland) |
| 3 | **RMS asymptotic bound** | $\lim_{n \to \infty} U_{max} = \ln 2 \approx 0.693$ | Upper limit of utilization |
| 4 | **EDF Schedulability Bound** | $U \leq 1.0$ | Necessary and sufficient |
| 5 | **Worst-Case Response Time** | $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i / T_j \rceil \cdot C_j$ | Iteratively solved for fixed point |
| 6 | **Round-Robin Worst Latency** | $W_i = q \cdot N$ | $q$ = quantum, $N$ = task count |
| 7 | **Context Switch Time** | $T_{cs} = 2 \cdot T_{mem} + T_{sched} + T_{rest}$ | Save + Schedule + Restore |
| 8 | **Throughput (Multiprocessor)** | $\Theta = \sum_{k=1}^{m} U_k / m$ | Average per-core utilization |
| 9 | **Amdahl's Law (Speedup)** | $S(N) = 1 / \left( (1-P) + P/N \right)$ | $P$ = parallel fraction, $N$ = cores |
| 10 | **Priority Inversion Time** | $T_{inv} = T_{cs\_low} + T_{cs\_mid} \cdot k$ | Unbounded without PIP |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECUs (AUTOSAR):** Multitasking via OSEK/VDX OS. Each BSW module runs as a task with priorities pre-assigned.
- **IoT Edge Gateways (Linux + RTOS hybrid):** AMP — Cortex-A runs Linux for networking/MQTT, Cortex-M runs FreeRTOS for sensor sampling.
- **Mobile SoCs (Snapdragon, Apple M-series):** SMP with heterogeneous cores (big.LITTLE / Performance-Efficiency).
- **Industrial PLCs:** Preemptive multitasking with hard real-time guarantees, deterministic cycle times.
- **Avionics (ARINC 653):** Spatial + Temporal partitioning — each app is a process with its own time slot and memory partition.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Rate Monotonic Scheduling Upper Bound

### Statement (Liu & Layland, 1973)
For $n$ independent, periodic tasks with fixed priorities assigned by **Rate Monotonic** (shorter period → higher priority), the task set is **guaranteed schedulable** if:

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$$

### Assumptions
1. Tasks are **periodic** with period $T_i$.
2. Worst-case execution time $C_i$ is known and bounded.
3. Tasks are **independent** (no shared resources blocking).
4. Deadline equals period ($D_i = T_i$).
5. All tasks start at $t = 0$ (critical instant).
6. Context switch overhead is negligible.

### Step-by-Step Analytical Derivation

**Step 1 — Critical Instant:**
The worst-case response time of any task $\tau_i$ occurs when all higher-priority tasks release simultaneously with it. This is the *critical instant* at $t = 0$.

**Step 2 — Worst-Case Response Time Equation:**
A task $\tau_i$ finishes at the smallest $R_i$ satisfying:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_i &= C_i + \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation:** Total time = own computation $C_i$ + all preemptions by higher-priority tasks $j$. The ceiling operator counts integer job releases of task $j$ within $[0, R_i]$.

**Step 3 — Define Worst-Case Utilization:**
$$\begin{aligned}
U &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}
\end{aligned}$$

We seek the maximum $U$ for which RMS still guarantees schedulability.

**Step 4 — Consider $n=1$ Case (Baseline):**
A single task with $U_1 = C_1 / T_1 \leq 1$ is trivially schedulable. Bound $= 1 \cdot (2^{1} - 1) = 1$. ✓

**Step 5 — Generalize (Induction via Time-Demand Analysis):**
The schedulability holds if, for every task $i$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{C_i}{T_i} + \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} \frac{C_j}{T_j} &\leq 1 \\
\text{i.e., } W_i(t) = \sum_{j=1}^{i} C_j \cdot \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil &\leq t, \quad \forall t \in [0, T_i]
\end{aligned}$$

The function $W_i(t)$ is the total processor demand of tasks $1 \ldots i$ in time $t$. We require $W_i(t) \leq t$.

**Step 6 — Tightest Failure Point:**
The hardest constraint occurs at $t = T_i$. We require:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_{j=1}^{i} C_j \cdot \left\lceil \frac{T_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \leq T_i
\end{aligned}$$

Since $T_i \geq T_j$ for $j \leq i$ (RM priority ordering), the ceiling simplifies.

**Step 7 — Worst-Case Choice of Periods:**
Liu & Layland showed the worst-case (i.e., the smallest RHS achieving failure) occurs when the periods are chosen such that $T_j$ divides $T_i$ and ratios are as tight as possible. Specifically:

$$\begin{aligned}
\left\lceil \frac{T_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot \frac{C_j}{T_j} &\approx \frac{C_j}{T_j} \cdot 2 \quad \text{(harmonic ratio limit)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8 — Resulting Inequality:**
After the limiting analysis, the maximum total utilization for $n$ tasks reduces to:

$$\begin{aligned}
U_{max}(n) &= n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 9 — Asymptotic Limit:**
Applying $\lim_{n \to \infty}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to \infty} U_{max}(n) &= \lim_{n \to \infty} n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) \\
&= \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{2^{1/n} - 1}{1/n} \quad \text{(L'Hôpital form)} \\
&= \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{-\frac{1}{n^2} \cdot 2^{1/n} \ln 2}{-1/n^2} \\
&= \ln 2 \approx 0.6931
\end{aligned}$$

Hence, RMS can guarantee schedulability on a single processor up to **69.31%** utilization in the limit. The exact bounds for small $n$ are tabulated below.

### Step 10 — Numerical Table

| $n$ | $U_{max} = n(2^{1/n}-1)$ | Decimal |
|----:|------------------------:|--------:|
| 1   | 1.0000                  | 1.0000  |
| 2   | 0.8284                  | 0.8284  |
| 3   | 0.7798                  | 0.7798  |
| 4   | 0.7568                  | 0.7568  |
| 5   | 0.7435                  | 0.7435  |
| 10  | 0.7177                  | 0.7177  |
| $\infty$ | $\ln 2$             | 0.6931  |

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Example (KTU-style Numerical)

**Problem:** Three periodic tasks arrive in a real-time embedded system:
- $\tau_1$: $T_1 = 4 \text{ ms},\; C_1 = 1 \text{ ms}$
- $\tau_2$: $T_2 = 6 \text{ ms},\; C_2 = 1 \text{ ms}$
- $\tau_3$: $T_3 = 10 \text{ ms},\; C_3 = 2 \text{ ms}$

Determine if the task set is **schedulable under RMS**.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Assign priorities (shortest period → highest):**
$\tau_1$ > $\tau_2$ > $\tau_3$.

**Step 2 — Compute per-task utilization:**

$$\begin{aligned}
U_1 &= C_1 / T_1 = 1/4 = 0.25 \\
U_2 &= C_2 / T_2 = 1/6 \approx 0.1667 \\
U_3 &= C_3 / T_3 = 2/10 = 0.20
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Total utilization:**

$$\begin{aligned}
U &= U_1 + U_2 + U_3 = 0.25 + 0.1667 + 0.20 = 0.6167
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — RMS bound for $n=3$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
U_{max}(3) &= 3(2^{1/3} - 1) \\
&= 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) \\
&= 3 \cdot 0.2599 \\
&= 0.7798
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compare:**

$$\begin{aligned}
U = 0.6167 &\leq U_{max} = 0.7798 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** Task set is **guaranteed schedulable** under Rate Monotonic Scheduling. (If the test had failed, we would still need to check Response-Time Analysis — the Liu-Layland bound is **sufficient but not necessary**.)

---

## 3.3 Response-Time Analysis (Exact Test)

For task $\tau_i$, iteratively solve:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_i^{(0)} &= C_i \\
R_i^{(k+1)} &= C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j
\end{aligned}$$

Iterate until $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$ (fixed point). Task is schedulable iff $R_i \leq D_i$.

**Example for $\tau_3$ from above:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(0)} &= 2 \\
R_3^{(1)} &= 2 + \lceil 2/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 2/6 \rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 + 1 = 4 \\
R_3^{(2)} &= 2 + \lceil 4/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 4/6 \rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 + 1 = 4
\end{aligned}$$

Fixed point: $R_3 = 4 \text{ ms} \leq D_3 = 10 \text{ ms}$. ✓ Schedulable.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Multiprocessing & Multitasking Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["EMBEDDED SYSTEM SOC"] --> nodeB["HARDWARE LAYER MULTIPROCESSING"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["CPU CORE 0 APPLICATION"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["CPU CORE 1 APPLICATION"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["CPU CORE 2 RTOS REAL TIME"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["SHARED L2 CACHE AND RAM"]
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG["SOFTWARE LAYER MULTITASKING KERNEL"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["TASK A PRIORITY 1"]
    nodeG --> nodeI["TASK B PRIORITY 2"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ["TASK C PRIORITY 3"]
    nodeG --> nodeK["IDLE TASK PRIORITY 0"]
    nodeH --> nodeL["SCHEDULER DISPATCHER"]
    nodeI --> nodeL
    nodeJ --> nodeL
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM["SYSTICK TIMER 1 KHZ"]
    nodeL --> nodeN["IPC MECHANISMS"]
    nodeN --> nodeO["SEMAPHORE MUTEX"]
    nodeN --> nodeP["MESSAGE QUEUE"]
    nodeN --> nodeQ["EVENT FLAGS"]
    nodeM --> nodeL
    nodeO --> nodeH
    nodeO --> nodeI
    nodeO --> nodeJ
```

## 4.2 Task State Transition Diagram (KTU Mandated)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Ready: TASK CREATED
    Ready --> Running: SCHEDULER DISPATCH
    Running --> Ready: PREEMPTED HIGHER PRIORITY
    Running --> Blocked: BLOCK ON QUEUE SEMAPHORE DELAY
    Blocked --> Ready: EVENT OCCURS TIMEOUT EXPIRES
    Running --> [*]: TASK DELETED OR COMPLETES
    Blocked --> [*]: TASK DELETED
```

## 4.3 SMP vs AMP vs BMP Architectural Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SMP["SMP SYMMETRIC MULTIPROCESSING"]
        direction TB
        SMP_OS["SINGLE SHARED OS IMAGE"] --> SMP_CPU1["CPU 1"]
        SMP_OS --> SMP_CPU2["CPU 2"]
        SMP_OS --> SMP_CPU3["CPU 3"]
        SMP_CPU1 --> SMP_MEM["SHARED MAIN MEMORY"]
        SMP_CPU2 --> SMP_MEM
        SMP_CPU3 --> SMP_MEM
    end

    subgraph AMP["AMP ASYMMETRIC MULTIPROCESSING"]
        direction TB
        AMP_LINUX["LINUX A CORES"] --> AMP_CPU1["CORTEX A QUAD"]
        AMP_RTOS["FREERTOS M CORE"] --> AMP_CPU2["CORTEX M4"]
        AMP_CPU1 --> AMP_OCRAM["SHARED OCRAM"]
        AMP_CPU2 --> AMP_OCRAM
        AMP_CPU1 -.RPMsg.-> AMP_CPU2
    end

    subgraph BMP["BMP BOUND MULTIPROCESSING"]
        direction TB
        BMP_DSP["DSP CORE FIXED TASK AUDIO CODEC"] --> BMP_MEM["MEMORY"]
        BMP_MCU["MCU CORE FIXED TASK SENSOR IO"] --> BMP_MEM
    end
```

## 4.4 Context Switch Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as CPU CORE
    participant T1 as TASK A RUNNING
    participant SCHED as SCHEDULER
    participant T2 as TASK B READY
    participant TCB_A as TCB A IN RAM
    participant TCB_B as TCB B IN RAM

    CPU->>T1: EXECUTING INSTRUCTIONS
    T1-->>CPU: SYSTICK FIRES
    CPU->>SCHED: ENTER PENDSV HANDLER
    SCHED->>TCB_A: SAVE R4 R5 R6 R7 R8 R9 R10 R11
    SCHED->>TCB_B: RESTORE R4 R5 R6 R7 R8 R9 R10 R11
    SCHED->>CPU: STACK POP PC PSR LR R0 R3
    CPU->>T2: RESUME EXECUTION
    Note over CPU,T2: CONTEXT SWITCH COMPLETE
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEAET695]`
**Differentiate between Multiprocessing and Multitasking. Mention the type of parallelism in each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Multitasking | Multiprocessing |
|---|---|---|
| Number of CPUs | **Single** | **Two or more** |
| Type of parallelism | **Logical / apparent** (via context switch) | **Physical / true** (concurrent execution) |
| Achieved by | RTOS / OS scheduler | Multi-core hardware + OS |

Multitasking creates the **illusion** of simultaneous execution; Multiprocessing provides **actual** simultaneous execution on multiple cores. *(3 marks)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEAET695]`
**Explain the role of a context switch in a preemptive RTOS. List the registers saved during context switch on ARM Cortex-M.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **context switch** is the mechanism by which the RTOS saves the state of the currently running task and restores the state of the next ready task, thereby enabling multitasking. It is triggered by the **SysTick** interrupt or any **preempting IRQ** through the `PendSV` exception.

On **ARM Cortex-M**, hardware automatically saves: **`R0, R1, R2, R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR`**. The PendSV handler in software additionally saves **`R4–R11`** (and **`S16–S31`** if FPU lazy-stacking is used). The saved state is stored in the task's **TCB (Task Control Block)** in memory. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEAET695]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **five states of a task** in a preemptive RTOS. Discuss the role of SysTick and PendSV exceptions in triggering transitions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A real-time system has **three periodic tasks** with the following parameters:
- $\tau_1$: $T_1 = 5 \text{ ms},\; C_1 = 1 \text{ ms}$
- $\tau_2$: $T_2 = 10 \text{ ms},\; C_2 = 2 \text{ ms}$
- $\tau_3$: $T_3 = 20 \text{ ms},\; C_3 = 3 \text{ ms}$

Check whether the task set is **schedulable under RMS** using the Liu–Layland bound. If schedulable, also compute the **worst-case response time** of $\tau_3$ using Response-Time Analysis. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q-A(a)

**Task State Diagram:**

```
                    ┌────────────┐
                    │   READY    │◄─────┐
                    └────┬───────┘      │
                         │ DISPATCH     │ PREEMPTION
                         ▼              │ (higher prio)
                    ┌────────────┐      │
                    │  RUNNING   │──────┘
                    └────┬───────┘
              DELETE     │ BLOCK (on I/O, semaphore, delay)
                 │       ▼
                 │  ┌────────────┐
                 └──│  BLOCKED   │
                    └────┬───────┘
                         │ EVENT / TIMEOUT
                         └──────► BACK TO READY
```

**Explanation (7 marks):**

1. **Ready State** — Task is loaded in memory, has all required resources, and is waiting in the ready queue for CPU allocation. *1 mark*
2. **Running State** — The scheduler has assigned the CPU to this task; its instructions are being executed. Only **one task per core** can be in this state at any instant. *1 mark*
3. **Blocked / Waiting State** — Task is unable to proceed because it is waiting for an event: semaphore give, queue receive, or `vTaskDelay()` expiry. *1 mark*
4. **Suspended State (optional 5th)** — Task is excluded from scheduling (e.g., `vTaskSuspend()`). *1 mark*
5. **Deleted State (optional 5th)** — Task has either completed or been explicitly terminated. *1 mark*
6. **Role of SysTick (Cortex-M):** A programmable timer (typically 1 kHz) that generates periodic exceptions. On each tick, the RTOS increments the tick count, decrements delay/block counters, and triggers `xPortPendSVHandler` if preemption is needed. *1 mark*
7. **Role of PendSV:** A software-triggerable exception with the **lowest priority** so it runs only after all other ISRs. It performs the actual save/restore of context, ensuring atomic context switches. *1 mark*

---

#### Solution to Q-A(b)

**Step 1 — RMS Priority Assignment:**
Shorter period → higher priority.
- $\tau_1$: $T_1 = 5 \text{ ms}$ → **Priority 1 (highest)**
- $\tau_2$: $T_2 = 10 \text{ ms}$ → Priority 2
- $\tau_3$: $T_3 = 20 \text{ ms}$ → Priority 3 (lowest)

**Step 2 — Compute Total Utilization:**

$$\begin{aligned}
U_1 &= 1/5 = 0.200 \\
U_2 &= 2/10 = 0.200 \\
U_3 &= 3/20 = 0.150 \\
U_{total} &= 0.200 + 0.200 + 0.150 = 0.550
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Liu–Layland Bound for $n=3$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
U_{max}(3) &= 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) \\
&= 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) \\
&= 3 \cdot 0.2599 \\
&= 0.7798
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compare:**

$$U = 0.550 \leq 0.7798 = U_{max} \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** Task set is **schedulable under RMS**. *3 marks*

**Step 5 — Response-Time Analysis for $\tau_3$:**

Higher-priority tasks of $\tau_3$ are $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$.

$$\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(0)} &= C_3 = 3 \\
R_3^{(1)} &= C_3 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3^{(0)}}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3^{(0)}}{T_2} \right\rceil C_2 \\
&= 3 + \lceil 3/5 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 3/10 \rceil \cdot 2 \\
&= 3 + 1 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 2 = 6 \\
R_3^{(2)} &= 3 + \lceil 6/5 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 6/10 \rceil \cdot 2 \\
&= 3 + 2 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 2 = 7 \\
R_3^{(3)} &= 3 + \lceil 7/5 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 7/10 \rceil \cdot 2 \\
&= 3 + 2 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 2 = 7
\end{aligned}$$

**Fixed point:** $R_3 = 7 \text{ ms}$. *3 marks*

**Step 6 — Compare with Deadline:**

$$R_3 = 7 \text{ ms} \leq D_3 = T_3 = 20 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark$$

Task $\tau_3$ will always meet its deadline. *1 mark*

---

### QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEAET695]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Compare **Symmetric Multiprocessing (SMP)** and **Asymmetric Multiprocessing (AMP)** with suitable diagrams. List two embedded SoCs that use each architecture. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **four Inter-Process Communication (IPC)** mechanisms used in embedded RTOS. With a code snippet, illustrate how a **binary semaphore** can be used for task synchronization between an ISR and a task. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q-B(a)

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Aspect | SMP (Symmetric) | AMP (Asymmetric) |
|---|---|---|
| Processor homogeneity | Identical cores | Heterogeneous (different ISA/perf) |
| OS | Single shared image | Independent OS per cluster |
| Memory | Unified shared memory | Local + shared (sometimes partitioned) |
| Task assignment | Dynamic (load balancer) | Static (manually pinned) |
| Cache coherency | Required (HW-managed) | Optional / per-cluster |
| Examples | Raspberry Pi 4 (Cortex-A72 quad), AMD Ryzen | NXP i.MX8 (Cortex-A + Cortex-M), TI Sitara AM437x (A9 + PRU) |
| Use case | Symmetric compute (servers, desktop) | Mixed-criticality (Linux + RTOS) |

**SMP Diagram (1.5 marks):**
```
┌──────────────────────┐
│   Single OS Image    │
├──────┬──────┬────────┤
│ CPU0 │ CPU1 │  CPU2  │
└──┬───┴──┬───┴───┬────┘
   └──────┼───────┘
          ▼
   Shared Main Memory (DDR)
```

**AMP Diagram (1.5 marks):**
```
┌─────────────────┐    ┌─────────────────┐
│  Linux (Cortex- │    │ FreeRTOS        │
│  A Quad)        │    │ (Cortex-M4)     │
└────────┬────────┘    └────────┬────────┘
         │                      │
         └──────► OCRAM ◄───────┘
              (RPMsg / Mailbox)
```

---

#### Solution to Q-B(b)

**Four IPC Mechanisms (4 marks):**

1. **Semaphores** — Used for signaling (binary) or resource counting (counting). FreeRTOS: `xSemaphoreCreateBinary()`.
2. **Mutex** — Mutual exclusion with priority inheritance. FreeRTOS: `xSemaphoreCreateMutex()`.
3. **Message Queue** — FIFO buffer for passing typed data between tasks. FreeRTOS: `xQueueCreate(len, size)`.
4. **Event Flags / Event Groups** — Multi-bit synchronization (AND/OR logic). FreeRTOS: `xEventGroupCreate()`.

**Binary Semaphore — ISR to Task Synchronization (3 marks):**

```c
/* In FreeRTOS, a binary semaphore is ideal for ISR-to-Task notification. */
SemaphoreHandle_t xUartSem;

/* --- High-priority UART Task --- */
void vUARTReceiveTask(void *pvParameters)
{
    uint8_t ucRxByte;
    for (;;)
    {
        /* Block until ISR gives the semaphore. */
        xSemaphoreTake(xUartSem, portMAX_DELAY);

        /* Read the received byte from UART FIFO. */
        xQueueReceive(xRxQueue, &ucRxByte, 0);
        process_byte(ucRxByte);
    }
}

/* --- UART ISR (runs in interrupt context) --- */
void UART_IRQHandler(void)
{
    BaseType_t xHigherPriorityTaskWoken = pdFALSE;

    /* Clear interrupt flag. */
    UART_ClearIT(UART_IT_RX);

    /* Give the semaphore — wakes the waiting task. */
    xSemaphoreGiveFromISR(xUartSem,
                          &xHigherPriorityTaskWoken);

    /* Request context switch if a higher-priority task was woken. */
    portYIELD_FROM_ISR(xHigherPriorityTaskWoken);
}
```

**Key Notes:**
- ISR-safe API variant is used: `xSemaphoreGiveFromISR()`.
- `xHigherPriorityTaskWoken` enables immediate preemption.
- This pattern avoids polling and is **deterministic** — the latency from ISR to task wake-up is bounded by context-switch time.

---

## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "Multiprocessor" with "Multicore"** — they are similar but multiprocessor is the system; multicore refers to a single chip with multiple CPUs. Write the distinction if asked.
> 2. **Forgetting the units** — Utilization is dimensionless but always quote $C_i$ and $T_i$ in **same units** (ms or μs) or you'll get 0 marks.
> 3. **Liu-Layland bound is SUFFICIENT, not NECESSARY** — Many students say "if bound fails, not schedulable." WRONG. The bound is conservative; use Response-Time Analysis for exact test.
> 4. **Priority Inversion** — Don't just mention it; give Mars Pathfinder (NASA 1997) as a real case study for full marks.
> 5. **In SMP, mention cache coherency (MESI/MOESI)** — Examiner's favourite follow-up.
> 6. **In AMP, always mention RPMsg/OpenAMP** as the standard inter-core IPC framework.
> 7. **Misspelling "PendSV"** as "PendSVHandler" or "PendSVC" — both are wrong; it is **PendSV** (Pendable Service call).
> 8. **Context switch registers on ARM Cortex-M** — Forgetting **xPSR** or **LR (with EXC_RETURN value)** is a guaranteed 1-mark cut.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Multitasking = 1 CPU + logical parallelism**; **Multiprocessing = ≥ 2 CPUs + physical parallelism**.
- [x] **SMP** = identical cores, single OS image, cache-coherent shared memory.
- [x] **AMP** = heterogeneous cores, independent OSes (e.g., Linux + FreeRTOS), RPMsg for IPC.
- [x] **BMP** = dedicated task-to-core binding (DSP + MCU hybrids).
- [x] **Preemptive multitasking** uses **SysTick + PendSV** (ARM Cortex-M).
- [x] **Context switch** saves `R0–R12, SP, LR, PC, xPSR`; Cortex-M hardware auto-saves 8, software saves `R4–R11`.
- [x] **Task states**: Ready, Running, Blocked (+ Suspended, Deleted).
- [x] **RMS**: Priority $\propto 1/Period$. Liu-Layland bound: $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$.
- [x] **EDF**: Dynamic priority, optimal, $U \leq 1$.
- [x] **Asymptotic RMS limit**: $\ln 2 \approx 0.6931$.
- [x] **Response-Time Analysis** is the exact schedulability test (iterative fixed-point).
- [x] **Priority Inversion** = unbounded blocking; solved by **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)**.
- [x] **IPC mechanisms**: Semaphore, Mutex, Queue, Event Group, Mailbox, Stream Buffer.
- [x] **ISR-to-Task** signaling uses `xSemaphoreGiveFromISR()` + `portYIELD_FROM_ISR()`.
- [x] **Mars Pathfinder (1997)** is the canonical priority-inversion case study.
- [x] **AUTOSAR / OSEK-VDX** = industry standard for automotive RTOS multitasking.
- [x] **Amdahl's Law** $S(N) = 1 / \left( (1-P) + P/N \right)$ bounds multiprocessor speedup.
