topics.title: Task Scheduling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET695 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Task Scheduling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Task Scheduling** is the mechanism employed by the Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) kernel to select, from a pool of ready tasks, the specific task that must be allotted CPU execution time at any given instant. It is a deterministic decision-making process that determines the *order* and *duration* of task execution while respecting temporal (timing) constraints.

In the context of KTU 2024 Embedded System Design (Module 3), task scheduling is treated as the **core kernel activity** of an RTOS, working in tandem with the **Scheduler**, the **Dispatcher**, and the **Task Control Block (TCB)**.

### Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Aligned)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Task / Thread** | Independent, sequential program unit with its own stack and context |
| **Scheduler** | The kernel component that decides *which* task runs next |
| **Dispatcher** | The module that performs the actual context switch |
| **Context Switch** | Saving state of current task and loading state of next task |
| **Jitter** | The deviation in expected execution timing |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Air Traffic Controller" Model

Imagine a busy airport with multiple aircraft (tasks) waiting to land. Each aircraft has a **fuel level** (deadline), a **priority** (emergency/cargo/commercial), and a **runway** (CPU). The **Air Traffic Controller (Scheduler)** stands at the tower and decides:

- Which plane gets the runway *right now*?
- Which plane must be *held in the pattern* (waiting queue)?
- Which plane gets *preempted* (asked to circle again)?

Just as a controller uses **rules** (priority, time-of-arrival, urgency) to prevent collisions, the **RTOS scheduler** uses **algorithms** (Rate Monotonic, EDF, Round Robin) to prevent deadline misses and ensure predictability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Scheduling Critical in Embedded Systems?**
> Unlike desktop computing (where "fast enough" is acceptable), embedded real-time systems demand **determinism** — the system must guarantee a known response within a bounded time. A scheduler is the *heart of this guarantee*.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Liu & Layland Upper Bound for Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS):** **U ≤ n(2^(1/n) - 1)**
- **Theoretical Maximum Utilization (n → ∞):** **ln(2) ≈ 0.693** or **69.3%**
- **Typical Context Switch Time:** **1 – 10 µs** in modern MCUs
- **Standard Tick Rate (RTOS):** **1 ms** to **10 ms**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Task States and Transitions

A task in an RTOS is never static — it transitions between well-defined states. Understanding these states is fundamental to analyzing scheduling behavior.

### The Five Canonical Task States

1. **Ready** — Task is loaded in memory and waiting for the CPU.
2. **Running** — Task is currently being executed by the CPU (only ONE task in this state per core).
3. **Blocked / Waiting** — Task is waiting for an event (I/O completion, semaphore, delay).
4. **Suspended** — Task is intentionally put to sleep by the application.
5. **Terminated / Deleted** — Task has finished its job and is no longer in the scheduler's view.

### The "Why" Behind State Transitions

- **Ready → Running:** Scheduler *dispatches* the highest-priority ready task.
- **Running → Ready:** A higher-priority task became ready, causing *preemption*.
- **Running → Blocked:** Task issues a system call (e.g., `vTaskDelay()`, semaphore wait).
- **Blocked → Ready:** The awaited event has occurred (ISR signals completion).
- **Running → Terminated:** Task calls `vTaskDelete()` on itself or its function returns.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A *non-preemptive* scheduler only allows transitions to Ready/Blocked via task cooperation. A *preemptive* scheduler can forcibly move a task from Running → Ready on every tick.

## 2.2 Classification of Scheduling Algorithms

### A. Based on Preemption

| Type | Behavior | Pros | Cons |
|------|----------|------|------|
| **Non-Pemptive (Cooperative)** | Task runs to completion or voluntary yield | Simple, no race conditions | Poor response time; one bad task freezes the system |
| **Preemptive** | Scheduler can forcibly remove CPU from running task | Predictable, real-time capable | Higher context-switch overhead |

### B. Based on Static vs Dynamic Priority

| Type | Examples | When Priority is Assigned |
|------|----------|--------------------------|
| **Static Priority** | Rate Monotonic (RMS), Deadline Monotonic | At design/compile time |
| **Dynamic Priority** | Earliest Deadline First (EDF), Least Laxity First (LLF) | At runtime, per scheduling decision |

### C. Classic Scheduling Algorithms

#### 1. **First-Come, First-Served (FCFS) / FIFO**
- Tasks are queued in arrival order.
- Non-preemptive.
- *KTU Note:* Used in simple main-loop super-loops; **NOT suitable for hard real-time**.

#### 2. **Round-Robin (RR)**
- Each task gets an equal *time quantum* (e.g., 10 ms).
- After quantum expiry, task is preempted and goes to back of queue.
- *KTU Use Case:* Time-sharing systems, fair multi-tasking.

#### 3. **Priority Scheduling**
- Each task has a numerical priority.
- Highest-priority ready task always runs.
- *Risk:* **Priority Inversion** (handled by Priority Inheritance Protocol).

#### 4. **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** — *Static Priority*
- **Rule:** Task with *shorter period* gets *higher priority*.
- Optimal among static-priority algorithms.
- **Liu & Layland Test:** Schedulable if total utilization ≤ n(2^(1/n) - 1).

#### 5. **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** — *Dynamic Priority*
- **Rule:** Task with *closest absolute deadline* runs first.
- **Optimal** for uniprocessor systems — can achieve 100% utilization theoretically.
- *Drawback:* Overhead of dynamic priority reassignment at every scheduling point.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Description | Units / Range |
|---|------------------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $U_i = \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}$ | Utilization of a single task | $0 < U_i \leq 1$ |
| 2 | $U_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}$ | Total CPU utilization | $0 \leq U_{total} \leq 1$ |
| 3 | $U_{bound} = n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$ | Liu & Layland bound for RMS schedulability | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $U_{max} = \ln(2) \approx 0.693$ | Asymptotic RMS utilization (n → ∞) | ~69.3% |
| 5 | $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \dfrac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | Worst-case response time (RTA) | Time units |
| 6 | $WCET$ | Worst-Case Execution Time | µs / ms |
| 7 | $T$ (Period) | Time between successive activations | ms |
| 8 | $D$ (Deadline) | Time by which task must complete | ms |
| 9 | $L = D - R$ | Laxity (Slack time) | Time units |
| 10 | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{N_{completed}}{T_{observation}}$ | Tasks completed per unit time | tasks/sec |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Never use the pipe symbol `|` inside a table cell. Always use $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ or $\mid \cdot \mid$ in LaTeX.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECUs (Engine Control):** OSEK/VDX OS uses fixed-priority RMS for sub-millisecond control loops.
- **Aerospace (Flight Control):** ARINC 653 uses partitioned scheduling with strict time windows.
- **IoT Edge Devices:** FreeRTOS scheduler balances sensor polling vs. cloud communication tasks.
- **Medical Devices (Pacemakers):** Hard real-time RMS scheduling ensures life-critical timing.
- **Industrial PLCs:** Cyclic executive scheduling with deterministic scan times.

## 2.5 Rate Monotonic Scheduling — Deep Dive

### Assumptions (Liu & Layland Model — 1973)

1. All tasks are **periodic**.
2. Deadlines equal periods: $D_i = T_i$.
3. Tasks are **independent** (no shared resources or precedence).
4. WCET $C_i$ is known and constant.
5. All tasks are **ready at time t = 0**.

### Priority Assignment Rule
$$P_i > P_j \iff T_i < T_j$$

(Shorter period → Higher priority)

### Why RMS is Optimal Among Static-Priority Schemes
Liu & Layland proved that **no other static-priority algorithm can schedule a task set that RMS fails to schedule**, *provided* all the model assumptions hold. This makes RMS the gold standard for fixed-priority real-time systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Liu & Layland Utilization Bound

This is a classic KTU examination derivation. We derive the upper-bound utilization for n tasks under RMS.

### Setup
- $n$ periodic tasks, each with period $T_i$ and WCET $C_i$.
- The critical instant (worst-case scenario) occurs when **all tasks are released simultaneously at $t = 0$**.
- The system is schedulable if **every task meets its deadline** at the critical instant.

### Derivation for n = 2 Tasks (Building Block)

Consider two tasks: Task 1 (priority 1, higher) and Task 2 (priority 2, lower). Periods: $T_1 < T_2$.

**Worst-Case Response Time of Task 2:**
The processor demand of Task 1 in any time interval of length $t$ is:

$$\text{Demand}_1(t) = \left\lceil \dfrac{t}{T_1} \right\rceil \cdot C_1$$

The total demand of both tasks in $[0, t]$:

$$W(t) = C_2 + \left\lceil \dfrac{t}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1$$

The schedulability condition is:

$$\exists\, t \leq T_2 : W(t) \leq t$$

### Boundary Condition (Worst-Case Tight Bound)

For the tightest utilization bound, we consider the *just-schedulable* case where Task 2's deadline is *just* met. This occurs at the scheduling point:

$$t = T_2 \quad \text{and} \quad \left\lceil \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} \right\rceil = 2 \text{ (just before completion of Task 1's second job)}$$

This implies:

$$T_2 = 2 T_1 - \epsilon \quad \text{where } \epsilon \to 0^+$$

Substituting into the demand equation:

$$C_1 \cdot 2 + C_2 = 2T_1 - \epsilon$$

The utilization of this tight configuration is:

$$U = \dfrac{C_1}{T_1} + \dfrac{C_2}{T_2} = \dfrac{2C_1}{2T_1} + \dfrac{C_2}{2T_1 - \epsilon}$$

As $\epsilon \to 0$ and $C_2$ saturates (worst case $C_2 = T_2$ is impossible; rather we use the *just-saturated* case $C_2 = T_1$ for the derivation limit):

$$U = \dfrac{C_1}{T_1} + \dfrac{C_1}{2T_1} = \dfrac{3C_1}{2T_1} = 2\left(\dfrac{C_1}{T_1}\right) \cdot \dfrac{3}{4}$$

Generalizing, for $n$ tasks the worst-case bound converges to:

$$\boxed{U_{bound} = n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)}$$

### Verification: n = 1, n = 2, n → ∞

- **n = 1:** $U = 1 \cdot (2^1 - 1) = 1$ → 100% (obvious — one task can use the full CPU).
- **n = 2:** $U = 2 \cdot (2^{0.5} - 1) = 2 \cdot (1.4142 - 1) = 0.8284$ → 82.84%.
- **n = 3:** $U = 3 \cdot (2^{0.333} - 1) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 0.7798$ → 77.98%.
- **n = 5:** $U = 5 \cdot (2^{0.2} - 1) = 5 \cdot 0.1487 = 0.7435$ → 74.35%.
- **n → ∞:** $U_{\infty} = \lim_{n\to\infty} n(2^{1/n} - 1) = \ln(2) \approx 0.6931$ → 69.31%.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** The bound $n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ is a **sufficient but not necessary** condition. A task set with $U > U_{bound}$ may still be schedulable — it is just not *guaranteed* to be by the Liu & Layland test. For a definitive answer, use the **Response Time Analysis (RTA)** iterative equation.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Example: KTU-Style Problem

> **[KTU University Exam Style]** *Three tasks are scheduled using RMS. Given (T1=4, C1=1), (T2=5, C2=2), (T3=20, C3=3). Check if the task set is schedulable using Liu & Layland test. Then verify with Response Time Analysis.*

### Step 1: Assign Priorities (Inverse of Period)
- Task 1: T = 4 → **Highest priority**
- Task 2: T = 5 → **Middle priority**
- Task 3: T = 20 → **Lowest priority**

### Step 2: Compute Total Utilization

$$U_{total} = \dfrac{1}{4} + \dfrac{2}{5} + \dfrac{3}{20}$$

$$U_{total} = \dfrac{5}{20} + \dfrac{8}{20} + \dfrac{3}{20} = \dfrac{16}{20} = 0.80$$

### Step 3: Compute Liu & Layland Bound (n = 3)

$$U_{bound} = 3 \cdot \left(2^{1/3} - 1\right) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 0.7798$$

### Step 4: Compare

$$U_{total} = 0.80 > U_{bound} = 0.7798$$

The Liu & Layland test **FAILS** — but this does not mean the task set is unschedulable, only that the test is inconclusive. We must proceed to RTA.

### Step 5: Response Time Analysis (Iterative)

**Task 1 (highest priority):**

$$R_1^{(0)} = C_1 = 1$$

$$R_1 = 1 \leq T_1 = 4 \quad ✓ \text{ Schedulable}$$

**Task 2:**

$$R_2^{(0)} = C_2 = 2$$

$$R_2^{(1)} = C_2 + \left\lceil \dfrac{R_2^{(0)}}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 = 2 + \left\lceil \dfrac{2}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3$$

$$R_2^{(2)} = 2 + \left\lceil \dfrac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3$$

Converged: $R_2 = 3 \leq T_2 = 5$ ✓ **Schedulable**

**Task 3:**

$$R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 3$$

$$R_3^{(1)} = C_3 + \left\lceil \dfrac{R_3^{(0)}}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 + \left\lceil \dfrac{R_3^{(0)}}{T_2} \right\rceil C_2$$

$$= 3 + \left\lceil \dfrac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \dfrac{3}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 1 + 2 = 6$$

$$R_3^{(2)} = 3 + \left\lceil \dfrac{6}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \dfrac{6}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 4 = 9$$

$$R_3^{(3)} = 3 + \left\lceil \dfrac{9}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \dfrac{9}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10$$

$$R_3^{(4)} = 3 + \left\lceil \dfrac{10}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \dfrac{10}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10$$

Converged: $R_3 = 10 \leq T_3 = 20$ ✓ **Schedulable**

### Step 6: Final Conclusion

Although the Liu & Layland bound is *exceeded* (0.80 > 0.78), the **Response Time Analysis proves the task set IS schedulable** under RMS, with worst-case response time of Task 3 being 10 ms (well within its 20 ms period).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** RTA gives a definitive schedulability verdict and reveals *how much slack* exists. The Liu & Layland test is a quick design-time estimate, while RTA is the rigorous verification.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Scheduling Algorithms

| Property | FCFS | Round Robin | RMS (Static) | EDF (Dynamic) |
|----------|------|-------------|--------------|---------------|
| **Optimality** | No | Fairness-based | Optimal (static) | Optimal (dynamic) |
| **Preemption** | No | Yes (quantum) | Yes | Yes |
| **Runtime Overhead** | Very Low | Low | Low | High |
| **Schedulability Test** | None | None | Liu-Layland / RTA | Processor Demand |
| **Max Achievable Util.** | Variable | Variable | ~69.3% (worst case) | 100% (theoretical) |
| **Implementation** | Trivial | Easy | Moderate | Complex |
| **Use Case** | Simple loops | Time-sharing | Hard real-time | Soft real-time / dynamic |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Task State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Created
    Created --> Ready : Task Created or Resumed
    Ready --> Running : Scheduler Dispatch
    Running --> Ready : Preempted by Higher Priority
    Running --> Blocked : Wait Event or Delay
    Running --> Suspended : vTaskSuspend Call
    Blocked --> Ready : Event Occurred
    Suspended --> Ready : vTaskResume Call
    Running --> Terminated : Task Function Returns
    Terminated --> [*] : Cleanup
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Solid arrows show legal transitions. A task can only enter the **Running** state from **Ready** — this enforces the single-CPU execution model.

## 4.2 RMS Scheduling Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Application Layer] --> B[Task Creation API]
    B --> C[Task Control Block Pool]
    C --> D[Ready Queue]
    D --> E{RMS Scheduler}
    E --> F[Priority by Period T sub i]
    F --> G[Scheduler Tick ISR]
    G --> H[Dispatcher]
    H --> I[CPU Core]
    I --> J[Running Task]
    J --> K{Task Event}
    K -->|Yields| D
    K -->|Blocks| L[Blocked Queue]
    L --> M[Event Signaling]
    M --> D
    K -->|Terminates| N[Cleanup]
```

## 4.3 Scheduling Decision Flow — RMS vs EDF

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Task Activated] --> B{Scheduling Policy}
    B -->|Static Priority RMS| C[Assign Priority by Period]
    B -->|Dynamic Priority EDF| D[Compare Absolute Deadlines]
    C --> E[Fixed Priority Table Lookup]
    D --> F[Heap or Sorted List]
    E --> G[Highest Priority Ready Task]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Dispatcher Performs Context Switch]
    H --> I[CPU Executes Task]
```

## 4.4 Context Switch Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as CPU
    participant Sched as Scheduler
    participant Disp as Dispatcher
    participant T1 as Task1
    participant T2 as Task2

    T1->>CPU: Executing
    Sched->>CPU: Tick Interrupt
    Sched->>Sched: Evaluate Ready Queue
    Sched->>Disp: Choose Task2
    Disp->>T1: Save TCB1 context
    Disp->>T2: Load TCB2 context
    Disp->>CPU: Restore Program Counter
    CPU->>T2: Begin Execution
```

## 4.5 Priority Inversion Problem Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Task H High Priority] -->|Blocked on Mutex| B[Task M Medium Priority]
    B -->|Preempts| C[Task L Low Priority Holds Mutex]
    C -->|Cannot Release Mutex| A
    A -->|Waits Indefinitely| D[DEADLOCK-LIKE BEHAVIOR]
    A -.->|Solution: Priority Inheritance| E[Task L Temporarily Boosted to High Priority]
    E -->|Releases Mutex Quickly| A
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between preemptive and non-preemptive scheduling. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Non-Preemptive | Preemptive |
|--------|---------------|------------|
| **CPU Release** | Task releases CPU voluntarily | Scheduler can forcibly preempt |
| **Response Time** | Unpredictable for low-priority tasks | Predictable, priority-respecting |
| **Context Switch** | Less frequent → lower overhead | Frequent → higher overhead |
| **Use Case** | Simple embedded loops | Hard real-time RTOS |

- **Advantage of Non-Preemptive:** Lower overhead; no race conditions during kernel critical sections.
- **Advantage of Preemptive:** Guarantees bounded response time for high-priority tasks; suitable for hard real-time systems.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Define the term "context switch" and list its typical steps.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Context Switch** is the kernel procedure of saving the execution state (registers, program counter, stack pointer) of the currently running task and loading the state of the next scheduled task.

**Steps:**
1. **Save Context:** Push CPU registers of current task onto its stack; copy stack pointer into TCB.
2. **Update TCB State:** Mark current task as Ready/Blocked.
3. **Scheduler Invocation:** Choose next task from ready queue.
4. **Load Context:** Copy new task's stack pointer from TCB; pop registers.
5. **Restore Execution:** Jump to saved Program Counter of new task.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Q: (a) Explain the Rate Monotonic Scheduling algorithm with its assumptions. Derive the Liu & Layland utilization bound for $n$ tasks.** **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Definition:** RMS is a *static-priority*, *preemptive* scheduling algorithm where the priority of a periodic task is assigned inversely proportional to its period. Task with the **shortest period receives the highest priority**.

**Assumptions of the Liu & Layland Model:**
1. All tasks are **periodic** with period $T_i$.
2. The deadline of each task equals its period: $D_i = T_i$.
3. Tasks are **independent** — no shared resources or inter-task communication.
4. The Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET) $C_i$ is known, bounded, and constant.
5. All tasks are released **simultaneously at $t = 0$** (critical instant).
6. Scheduling overhead (context switch, interrupt) is **negligible**.
7. The system is a **single-processor** uniprocessor system.

**Derivation of Liu & Layland Bound:**

The critical instant for a task $\tau_i$ is when all higher-priority tasks are released at the same time, causing maximum preemption. For the system to be schedulable, every task's response time must satisfy $R_i \leq T_i$.

Consider $n$ tasks under RMS. The worst-case processor demand over $[0, t]$ is:

$$W(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\lceil \dfrac{t}{T_i} \right\rceil C_i$$

For schedulability:

$$W(t) \leq t \quad \forall\, t \leq T_n$$

By analyzing the boundary case (worst-case packing) and using induction over $n$, Liu & Layland proved that a sufficient condition for schedulability is:

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n\left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$$

The bound is **tightest** (smallest) for large $n$:

$$U_{\infty} = \lim_{n\to\infty} n\left(2^{1/n} - 1\right) = \ln(2) \approx 0.6931$$

This means RMS can *guarantee* scheduling only when total CPU utilization stays below **~69.3%** in the worst case.

---

**Q: (b) Three periodic tasks are scheduled under RMS. Given data: τ₁ (T=10, C=2), τ₂ (T=15, C=4), τ₃ (T=30, C=5). Check schedulability using (i) Liu & Layland bound and (ii) Response Time Analysis.** **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1: Priority Assignment (Inverse of Period)**
- τ₁: T=10 → **Highest priority (P1)**
- τ₂: T=15 → **Middle priority (P2)**
- τ₃: T=30 → **Lowest priority (P3)**

**Step 2: Liu & Layland Bound Test**

$$U_{total} = \dfrac{2}{10} + \dfrac{4}{15} + \dfrac{5}{30} = 0.2000 + 0.2667 + 0.1667 = 0.6333$$

$$U_{bound} = 3 \cdot \left(2^{1/3} - 1\right) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 = 0.7798$$

Since $0.6333 \leq 0.7798$, the task set **PASSES the Liu & Layland test** and is *guaranteed schedulable*.

**Step 3: Response Time Analysis**

**For τ₁:**
$R_1 = C_1 = 2$ → $R_1 = 2 \leq T_1 = 10$ ✓

**For τ₂:**
$R_2^{(0)} = C_2 = 4$
$R_2^{(1)} = 4 + \left\lceil \dfrac{4}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 4 + 2 = 6$
$R_2^{(2)} = 4 + \left\lceil \dfrac{6}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 4 + 2 = 6$
Converged: $R_2 = 6 \leq T_2 = 15$ ✓

**For τ₃:**
$R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 5$
$R_3^{(1)} = 5 + \left\lceil \dfrac{5}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \dfrac{5}{15} \right\rceil \cdot 4 = 5 + 2 + 4 = 11$
$R_3^{(2)} = 5 + \left\lceil \dfrac{11}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \dfrac{11}{15} \right\rceil \cdot 4 = 5 + 4 + 4 = 13$
$R_3^{(3)} = 5 + \left\lceil \dfrac{13}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \dfrac{13}{15} \right\rceil \cdot 4 = 5 + 4 + 4 = 13$
Converged: $R_3 = 13 \leq T_3 = 30$ ✓

**Conclusion:** All three tasks meet their deadlines. The system is **fully schedulable** with a worst-case response time for τ₃ of 13 ms (slack of 17 ms).

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Q: (a) Explain the Earliest Deadline First (EDF) scheduling algorithm. Compare it with Rate Monotonic Scheduling.** **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Earliest Deadline First (EDF):**
EDF is a *dynamic-priority*, *preemptive* scheduling algorithm. At every scheduling point (e.g., task arrival, task completion, or tick), the scheduler selects the task with the **earliest absolute deadline** $d_i$ to execute next.

**Properties of EDF:**
- **Optimal:** For uniprocessor systems, EDF can schedule *any* task set whose total utilization does not exceed 1.0 ($U \leq 1$).
- **Dynamic Priority:** Task priorities are recomputed on-the-fly.
- **No Idle Time:** As long as any task is ready, the CPU runs the one closest to its deadline.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | RMS | EDF |
|--------|-----|-----|
| **Priority** | Static (fixed) | Dynamic (recomputed) |
| **Optimality** | Optimal among static-priority | Optimal in general |
| **Max Utilization** | ~69.3% (worst case) | 100% (theoretical) |
| **Implementation** | Simple fixed table | Needs priority queue (heap) |
| **Overhead** | Low | Higher (deadline tracking) |
| **Predictability** | High (deterministic) | Lower (dynamic analysis hard) |
| **Transient Overload** | Predictable failure | Unpredictable (cascading misses) |
| **Used In** | FreeRTOS, VxWorks, OSEK | Some research RTOS, multimedia |

**When to Choose Which:**
- **RMS:** Hard real-time, safety-critical (automotive, avionics, medical).
- **EDF:** Soft real-time, high-utilization systems, mixed-criticality workloads.

---

**Q: (b) What is priority inversion? Explain with a real-time example. How is it solved using the Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)?** **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Definition:**
**Priority Inversion** is an undesirable scheduling anomaly where a higher-priority task is *indirectly* preempted by a lower-priority task, effectively "inverting" the relative priorities. This happens when tasks share resources protected by mutexes.

**Classic Example — Mars Pathfinder Bug (1997):**
The Mars Pathfinder spacecraft experienced system resets due to priority inversion in its real-time bus management task. A low-priority meteorological task held a shared bus semaphore. A medium-priority communication task would preempt the low-priority task. Meanwhile, a high-priority bus management task would block on the same semaphore. The system task (TCBS) eventually saw the high-priority task not running and reset the system.

**Sequence of Events:**
1. **Task H** (high priority) wants the bus → **BLOCKS** (Task L holds it).
2. **Task M** (medium priority) becomes runnable → **preempts Task L**.
3. **Task L** can never finish releasing the bus (because M is hogging CPU).
4. **Task H** waits indefinitely → **deadline missed**.

**Solution: Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP):**

In PIP, when a high-priority task blocks on a resource held by a lower-priority task, the **lower-priority task's priority is temporarily boosted** to that of the highest-priority waiter.

**How PIP Works:**
- Task L holds semaphore → Task H blocks.
- Task L's priority is **immediately raised to H's priority**.
- Task M cannot preempt Task L anymore (since L now has H's priority).
- Task L finishes its critical section quickly, releases the semaphore.
- Task L's priority reverts to original.
- Task H acquires the semaphore and runs.

**Benefits:**
- **Bounded blocking time:** H is blocked only for the duration of L's critical section.
- **No unbounded priority inversion.**
- **Transitive inheritance** is also supported in advanced variants (e.g., Priority Ceiling Protocol).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "preemption" with "context switch":** Preemption is the *decision* to remove CPU; context switch is the *mechanism*. Both are required together.
> 2. **Forgetting to list the Liu & Layland assumptions:** A full-mark answer must state all 7 model assumptions before applying the formula.
> 3. **Misapplying RTA:** Many students forget to iterate until convergence. A single iteration is *insufficient*.
> 4. **Using pipes `|` in LaTeX inside markdown tables** — this breaks the table rendering. Always use $\lvert \cdot \rvert$.
> 5. **Confusing "Laxity" with "Slack" terminology** — they are the same; both mean $D - R$.
> 6. **In priority-inversion answers, failing to mention the Mars Pathfinder case study** — examiners often award bonus marks for this real-world example.
> 7. **Forgetting to verify $R_i \leq T_i$** in RTA — the converged value must be explicitly compared with the period.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Task Scheduling (Module 3):**

- **Task States:** Ready, Running, Blocked, Suspended, Terminated — know transitions.
- **Preemptive vs Non-Preemptive:** Preemptive = better response; Non-preemptive = lower overhead.
- **Static Priority (RMS):** Priority $\propto 1/Period$. Optimal for fixed-priority.
- **Dynamic Priority (EDF):** Priority $\propto$ Earliest Deadline. Optimal overall.
- **Liu & Layland Bound:** $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$. Sufficient, not necessary.
- **Asymptotic Limit:** $U_{\infty} = \ln(2) \approx 0.6931$ (~69.3%).
- **Response Time Analysis (RTA):** Iterative equation $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$. Continue until $R_i$ converges.
- **Priority Inversion:** Solved by **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** or **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)**.
- **Context Switch:** Save context → Schedule → Load context. Time: 1–10 µs typical.
- **Key RTOS Examples:** FreeRTOS, VxWorks, QNX, OSEK/VDX, RTEMS.
- **Classic Case Study:** **Mars Pathfinder Bug (1997)** — textbook example of priority inversion failure.
- **Hard vs Soft Real-Time:** Hard = deadline miss = system failure. Soft = degraded performance acceptable.
- **Jitter:** Variation in expected timing. RMS minimizes release jitter for high-priority tasks.
- **Common Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $U_i = C_i / T_i$
  - $U_{total} = \sum C_i/T_i$
  - $U_{bound} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$
  - RTA iterative form
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Task Communication: Shared Memory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET695 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Task Communication: Shared Memory ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of an RTOS-based **Embedded System Design**, **Shared Memory** is a task communication mechanism in which two or more concurrent tasks exchange data by accessing a common region of the Random Access Memory (RAM). Instead of the kernel actively copying a message from one task's private stack to another's (as in message passing), both tasks simply read from and write to the same physical memory addresses, which are typically declared as global variables, static buffers, or mailbox/mail queue structures. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 3 Highlight:** Shared Memory is the **fastest** form of inter-task communication because it eliminates the overhead of kernel-mediated data copying. However, it introduces the classic problem of **Race Conditions**, which must be solved using Mutual Exclusion primitives (Mutex, Binary Semaphore, or Interrupt Disabling). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **shared whiteboard** mounted on the wall of a co-working office: - **The Whiteboard** = the shared memory region (a global buffer in RAM). - **The Office Workers (Tasks)** = independent threads/tasks running in the RTOS. - Any worker can walk up to the whiteboard, **read** the latest project status (read operation), or **write** new information (write operation). - The whiteboard itself does **not** coordinate who can use it; the office manager (the kernel / programmer) must set up **rules** (Mutex/Semaphores) so that two workers do not try to erase/write on it at the exact same moment — otherwise, the information becomes garbled (a **race condition**). In a single-chip microcontroller like the ARM Cortex-M4 (used in STM32), shared memory usually means **global C variables** accessible by all tasks, or a **mailbox** managed by the RTOS kernel (e.g., FreeRTOS, µC/OS-II). ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants | Metric | Standard Value / Notation | |---|---| | Access Latency | A few CPU clock cycles (≈ **1–10 ns** at 100 MHz) | | Throughput | Limited only by bus width (**32-bit** on ARM Cortex-M) | | Criticality | **High** — requires synchronization | | Synchronization Primitive | **Mutex / Binary Semaphore / Interrupt Disable** | > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway:** Shared Memory trades **safety complexity** for **speed**. It is the preferred IPC mechanism in hard real-time systems (engine control units, motor controllers) where deterministic, low-latency communication is mandatory. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Shared Memory Communication The mechanism operates in a strict logical sequence: - **Step 1 — Memory Allocation:** The programmer declares a global variable, a static array, or a struct in the application's data section. This memory resides in **RAM** and is visible to all tasks. - **Step 2 — Producer Task Writes:** The *Producer* task computes a result and writes it into the shared variable. In ARM Cortex-M assembly, this is a simple `STR` (Store Register) instruction to a fixed address. - **Step 3 — Synchronization Signal:** A flag, semaphore, or mutex is given/taken to signal the *Consumer* task that new data is available. This prevents the consumer from reading stale data. - **Step 4 — Consumer Task Reads:** The *Consumer* task reads the variable (an `LDR` instruction) and processes it. - **Step 5 — Critical Section Protection:** Steps 2 and 4 must be wrapped in a **Critical Section** — a region where preemption and interrupts are temporarily disabled — to guarantee atomicity. ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step - **Why global variables?** Because each task has its own private stack in RAM, but the global data section is mapped to the same physical address for all tasks. The Memory Protection Unit (MPU), if enabled, must explicitly grant access to both tasks. - **Why synchronization?** Without it, the RTOS scheduler can preempt the producer *mid-write*, causing the consumer to read a partially updated value (e.g., 16 bits new + 16 bits old of a 32-bit integer) — this is called a **Torn Read**. - **Why critical sections?** To prevent **Task Interleaving** at the instruction level. The minimum time a variable is exposed to inconsistency is the *worst-case execution time* (WCET) of the unprotected write sequence. ### 2.3 The Race Condition Problem A **Race Condition** occurs when the output of a system depends on the non-deterministic order in which concurrent tasks access the shared resource. The classic KTU textbook example: Two tasks increment a shared 32-bit counter `cnt`. $$ cnt = cnt + 1 $$ This single C line compiles to **three** ARM instructions: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{LDR } R0, [\&cnt] \quad \text{(Read cnt into R0)} \\ &\text{ADD } R0, R0, \#1 \quad \text{(Increment R0)} \\ &\text{STR } R0, [\&cnt] \quad \text{(Store R0 back to cnt)} \end{aligned} $$ If the scheduler preempts Task A *between* the `LDR` and the `STR`, Task B will load the **old** value, increment it, store it back, and then Task A will overwrite it with its own (now-stale) increment. The counter effectively increases by 1 instead of 2 — **lost update bug**. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes | |---|---|---| | Race Condition Probability | $P_{\text{race}} \approx \dfrac{T_{\text{critical}}}{T_{\text{sched}}}$ | Dimensionless ratio; $T_{\text{critical}}$ = critical section duration, $T_{\text{sched}}$ = scheduling period | | Atomicity Guarantee | Requires $\le 1$ bus cycle for write | Use **bit-band aliasing** on Cortex-M3/M4 for single-bit atomic access | | Shared Memory Latency | $t_{\text{access}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{bytes}}}{f_{\text{bus}} \times W_{\text{bus}}}$ | seconds; $N$ = bytes, $f$ = bus freq (Hz), $W$ = bus width (bits) | | Priority Inversion Bound (with PIP) | $t_{\text{inv}} \le T_{\text{cs, max}} \times N_{\text{tasks}}$ | seconds; bounded by N critical sections | | Memory Visibility Barrier | $\text{DMB (Data Memory Barrier)}$ | ARM instruction; enforces memory ordering across cores/tasks | | Shared Buffer Size Rule | $N_{\text{slots}} \ge P_{\text{producer}} \times t_{\text{latency, max}}$ | slots; prevents producer overflow | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Shared memory is the backbone of **Automotive ECU communication** (CAN bus gateways), **IoT sensor fusion** (IMU + GPS shared data), and **real-time control loops** where the control task must read the sensor task's output within a hard deadline (e.g., 1 ms PID loop in a quadcopter flight controller). ### 2.5 Memory Visibility and the MPU In modern ARM Cortex-M processors, the **Memory Protection Unit (MPU)** can be configured to: - Mark the shared region as **Non-Privileged, Read/Write accessible**. - Mark the producer-only or consumer-only buffers as **Read-Only or Write-Only** for the other task, providing hardware-level access control. - Use the **bit-band alias** region (address `0x22000000` on Cortex-M3/M4) to make individual bit writes atomic — eliminating the need for a read-modify-write sequence in interrupt service routines. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Worst-Case Shared Memory Latency for an RTOS Consider an RTOS with **n** periodic tasks scheduled by Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS). The shared memory access time for the *k*-th task must fit within its deadline $D_k$. **Step 1 — Define the scheduling period.** The scheduler dispatches tasks every minor cycle $T_{\text{sched}}$. For RMS: $$ T_{\text{sched}} = \text{GCD}(T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n) $$ **Step 2 — Define the critical section execution time.** Let $C_{\text{cs},k}$ be the WCET of task *k*'s critical section that touches shared memory. **Step 3 — Define blocking time.** Under the **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)**, the worst-case blocking time for task *k* is: $$ B_k = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \max(\text{usage of } R_j \text{ by } \tau_k) $$ For a single shared resource, this simplifies to: $$ B_k = \sum_{j \in \text{lower priority}} C_{\text{cs}, j} $$ **Step 4 — Apply the RMS schedulability test with blocking.** A task set is schedulable under RMS + PIP if: $$ \forall k: \quad \sum_{i=1}^{k} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i} + \dfrac{B_k}{T_k} \le i (2^{1/i} - 1) $$ where the right-hand side is the Liu & Layland utilization bound for *i* tasks. **Step 5 — Solve for the maximum allowed critical section length.** Rearranging for $C_{\text{cs},k}$: $$ C_{\text{cs},k} \le T_k \left[ k(2^{1/k} - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{k} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i} \right] $$ This is the **maximum time** a task is allowed to hold the shared memory lock before the system becomes unschedulable. ### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example **Problem:** Three tasks share a global buffer. Their parameters are: | Task | Period $T_i$ (ms) | WCET $C_i$ (ms) | Priority | |---|---|---|---| | $\tau_1$ | 10 | 2 | Highest | | $\tau_2$ | 20 | 4 | Medium | | $\tau_3$ | 40 | 8 | Lowest | Find the maximum allowable critical section length for $\tau_1$. **Step 1 — Compute the utilization bound for $k=2$ (tasks 1 and 2):** $$ U_{\text{bound}}(2) = 2(2^{1/2} - 1) = 2(1.4142 - 1) = 0.8284 $$ **Step 2 — Compute the actual utilization of tasks 1 and 2:** $$ U_{1,2} = \dfrac{C_1}{T_1} + \dfrac{C_2}{T_2} = \dfrac{2}{10} + \dfrac{4}{20} = 0.20 + 0.20 = 0.40 $$ **Step 3 — Solve for $C_{\text{cs},1}$:** $$ C_{\text{cs},1} \le T_1 \left[ U_{\text{bound}}(2) - U_{1,2} \right] $$ Substituting: $$ C_{\text{cs},1} \le 10 \text{ ms} \times (0.8284 - 0.40) = 10 \times 0.4284 = 4.284 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 4 — Interpret the result.** Task $\tau_1$ can hold the shared memory lock for at most **4.284 ms** per period. Since this is well below its 10 ms period, the system is **schedulable** with shared memory access. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Shared Memory vs. Alternatives | Parameter | Shared Memory | Message Queue | Message Mailbox | Pipes | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Speed** | ★★★★★ (Fastest) | ★★★☆☆ | ★★★★☆ | ★★★☆☆ | | **Kernel Overhead** | Zero copy | 1–2 copies | 1 copy | 2 copies | | **Synchronization Needed** | **Yes (Mutex/Sem)** | Built-in (block on empty/full) | Built-in | Built-in | | **Data Size Limit** | Unlimited (RAM) | Fixed slot size | Fixed slot size | Stream | | **Race Condition Risk** | **High** | Low | Low | Low | | **Determinism** | Best (with PIP) | Good | Good | Good | | **Typical KTU Use** | ISR ↔ Task, Fast IPC | Multi-rate data | Command passing | Log streams | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Examiner Insight:** Whenever you see a question comparing "which IPC is fastest" or "which has the lowest overhead," the answer is **always Shared Memory** — provided the synchronization overhead is properly accounted for. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Shared Memory with Mutex Protection The following Mermaid diagram maps the *flow of control and data* between the producer, the shared buffer, the mutex, and the consumer. It is rendered as a functional block architecture because physical hardware drawings of RAM cells are not meaningful at the RTOS level. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Task Producer tauP
Priority High] --> B{Acquire Mutex M
OSMutexPend} B -->|Lock Granted| C[Write Data to
Shared Buffer Addr 0x20004000] C --> D[Release Mutex M
OSMutexPost] D --> E[Signal Consumer
via Binary Semaphore S] B -->|Lock Busy| F[Block Task
Place in Pend List] F -->|Mutex Available| B E --> G[Task Consumer tauC
Priority Low] G --> H{Acquire Mutex M
OSMutexPend} H -->|Lock Granted| I[Read Data from
Shared Buffer Addr 0x20004000] I --> J[Process Data
Apply Control Algorithm] J --> K[Release Mutex M
OSMutexPost] H -->|Lock Busy| L[Block Task
Wait for S] L -->|Semaphore Signaled| H K --> M[RAM Region
0x20004000 - 0x20004FFF
Shared Global Buffer] I --> M C --> M N[MPU Configuration
Region 7: RW Access
Non-Privileged] -.-> M O[ARM DMB Instruction
Memory Barrier] -.-> C O -.-> I ``` ### 4.2 Sequence Diagram: Race Condition and Mutex Resolution This sequence diagram illustrates the **timeline** of a typical shared memory access with and without a mutex, highlighting where the race condition would occur. ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Sched as RTOS Scheduler participant Prod as Producer Task participant Mut as Mutex M participant Buf as Shared Buffer participant Cons as Consumer Task participant ISR as Interrupt Service Routine Note over Prod,Cons: === WITHOUT MUTEX (RACE CONDITION) === Sched->>Prod: Dispatch tauP Prod->>Buf: LDR R0, [buf] (load old value) Sched->>ISR: Interrupt occurs ISR->>Buf: LDR R1, [buf] (load same old value) ISR->>Buf: ADD R1, R1, #1 ISR->>Buf: STR R1, [buf] (store new value) Sched->>Prod: Resume tauP Prod->>Buf: ADD R0, R0, #1 (uses stale R0) Prod->>Buf: STR R0, [buf] (overwrites ISR's update) Note over Buf: LOST UPDATE! Counter = 1, not 2 Note over Prod,Cons: === WITH MUTEX (CORRECT) === Sched->>Prod: Dispatch tauP Prod->>Mut: Acquire (lock) Mut-->>Prod: Granted Prod->>Buf: LDR R0, [buf] Prod->>Buf: ADD R0, R0, #1 Prod->>Buf: STR R0, [buf] Prod->>Mut: Release (unlock) Sched->>ISR: Interrupt occurs ISR->>Mut: Acquire (lock) Mut-->>ISR: Granted ISR->>Buf: LDR R1, [buf] (gets NEW value) ISR->>Buf: ADD R1, R1, #1 ISR->>Buf: STR R1, [buf] ISR->>Mut: Release (unlock) Note over Buf: CORRECT! Counter = 2 ``` ### 4.3 State Machine: Mutex Ownership Transitions The mutex protecting the shared memory transitions through a deterministic set of states. This is the **core finite state machine** that KTU expects students to reproduce. ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Unlocked Unlocked --> Locked: Task A calls
OSMutexPend
and is granted Locked --> Unlocked: Task A calls
OSMutexPost
releases lock Locked --> Blocked: Task B calls
OSMutexPend
while lock is held Blocked --> Ready: Task A releases
OSMutexPost
wakes Task B Ready --> Locked: Scheduler dispatches
Task B
lock granted Locked --> Inherited: Higher priority
Task C blocks
on lock Inherited --> Locked: Task A releases
priority restored Unlocked --> [*] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When drawing this in an exam, always label the **priority inheritance** transition explicitly. Examiners award bonus marks for showing the PIP mechanism that prevents unbounded priority inversion (the famous "Mars Pathfinder bug" scenario). ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEAET695]** **Q1. Define shared memory communication in RTOS. Mention one major problem associated with it.** **Model Answer:** Shared memory is an inter-task communication mechanism in which two or more tasks exchange data by accessing a common region of RAM, typically declared as global variables or static buffers. It is the fastest IPC method as it avoids kernel-mediated data copying. The major problem is the **race condition** — when multiple tasks access the shared data concurrently without synchronization, leading to data corruption or lost updates. Mutual exclusion primitives (mutex, semaphore) are required to serialize access. > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEAET695]** **Q2. List any three synchronization mechanisms used to protect shared memory in an RTOS.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Mutex (Mutual Exclusion Lock)** — allows only one task to enter the critical section at a time; supports priority inheritance. 2. **Binary Semaphore** — provides lock/unlock signaling between tasks; no ownership concept. 3. **Interrupt Disabling** (`OS_ENTER_CRITICAL()` / `OS_EXIT_CRITICAL()`) — temporarily masks interrupts to make a short critical section atomic. *(Other valid answers: Counting semaphore, spinlock, test-and-set instruction.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, PEAET695, Module 3, Question Choice A]** ### **Question A (14 Marks):** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **shared memory communication model** in an RTOS. Show how a Mutex protects the shared region. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A shared 32-bit counter is updated by two tasks. The counter is incremented using the statement `cnt = cnt + 1;`. Show, with a sample execution trace, how a **race condition** can occur if the counter is unprotected. Suggest **two** synchronization techniques to prevent it. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Question A:** **(a) Shared Memory Communication Model with Mutex (7 Marks):** **Diagram (to be drawn in exam):** ``` +----------------+ +----------------+ | Task 1 | acquire(M) | Task 2 | | (Producer) |------------->[MUTEX M]<------| (Consumer) | | | release(M) | | +-------+--------+ +--------+-------+ | | | WRITE | READ v v +---------------------------------------------------------------+ | SHARED MEMORY REGION (Global Buffer in RAM) | | Address: 0x20004000 - 0x20004FFF | +---------------------------------------------------------------+ ``` **Explanation (write in 5–6 bullet points):** 1. Both tasks have access to a **common RAM region** declared as a global variable/array. The compiler places this in the `.data` or `.bss` section of memory. 2. Task 1 (Producer) **acquires Mutex M** before writing. If the mutex is already held, it is placed in the **pending list** and the scheduler picks the next ready task. 3. Once the lock is granted, the producer performs an atomic write (`STR` instruction on ARM) to the shared address. 4. The producer **releases Mutex M** and optionally signals the consumer via a semaphore to indicate "data ready." 5. The scheduler preempts the producer and dispatches Task 2 (Consumer). It acquires the same mutex, **reads** the data using `LDR`, processes it, and releases the lock. 6. The MPU (if configured) restricts the region to read-only or write-only access for each task, providing **hardware-level isolation** in addition to software-level mutex protection. **(b) Race Condition on 32-bit Counter (7 Marks):** **Compilation of `cnt = cnt + 1` into 3 ARM instructions:** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{LDR R0, [\&cnt]} \quad \text{; Load cnt into R0 from RAM} \\ &\text{ADD R0, R0, \#1} \quad \text{; Increment R0} \\ &\text{STR R0, [\&cnt]} \quad \text{; Store R0 back to RAM} \end{aligned} $$ **Sample Execution Trace (assume initial cnt = 5):** | Step | Task A (Producer) | Task B (Consumer) | cnt in RAM | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | `LDR R0, [cnt]` → R0 = 5 | — | 5 | | 2 | **PREEMPTED by scheduler** | — | 5 | | 3 | — | `LDR R1, [cnt]` → R1 = 5 | 5 | | 4 | — | `ADD R1, R1, #1` → R1 = 6 | 5 | | 5 | — | `STR R1, [cnt]` → cnt = 6 | 6 | | 6 | **RESUMED** | — | 6 | | 7 | `ADD R0, R0, #1` → R0 = 6 (stale!) | — | 6 | | 8 | `STR R0, [cnt]` → cnt = 6 (no change) | — | 6 | **Expected counter value after both tasks = 7. Actual = 6. Lost update occurred.** **Two Synchronization Techniques:** 1. **Mutex Lock:** Wrap the read-modify-write sequence with `OSMutexPend(M)` and `OSMutexPost(M)`. The RTOS ensures atomicity by checking the lock state and blocking the second task until the first releases it. 2. **Disable Interrupts / OS_ENTER_CRITICAL():** For a 3-instruction critical section on a single-core Cortex-M, briefly disable context-switch interrupts. This prevents preemption exactly between the LDR and STR, making the sequence atomic from the scheduler's perspective. *(Acceptable third option: Use the Cortex-M3/M4 **bit-band alias** region `0x22000000` to perform single-instruction atomic bit writes, eliminating the need for a read-modify-write entirely.)* --- > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, PEAET695, Module 3, Question Choice B]** ### **Question B (14 Marks):** **(a)** Compare **shared memory** communication with **message passing** communication in RTOS. Tabulate at least **five** parameters. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)**. How does it prevent **unbounded priority inversion** in shared memory access? Illustrate with a suitable example involving three tasks (H, M, L). **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Question B:** **(a) Shared Memory vs. Message Passing (7 Marks):** | Parameter | Shared Memory | Message Passing | |---|---|---| | **Data Transfer Mechanism** | Direct read/write to common RAM | Kernel copies message from sender's buffer to receiver's queue | | **Speed / Latency** | **Fastest** (≈ 1–10 CPU cycles) | Slower (involves memcpy, context switches) | | **Kernel Overhead** | Minimal (lock/unlock only) | High (system call + copy) | | **Synchronization** | **Must be provided externally** (mutex, semaphore) | **Built-in** (blocking send/receive) | | **Data Size** | Limited only by available RAM | Limited by queue slot size / message pool | | **Race Condition Risk** | **High** — must be managed by programmer | Low — kernel handles atomicity | | **Determinism** | Best when PIP is applied | Good but queue full/empty can cause blocking | | **Suitable Use Case** | High-frequency ISR ↔ Task data | Inter-process command passing, log messages | **Conclusion (write 2–3 lines):** Shared memory is preferred for **time-critical, high-frequency** data exchange (sensor data, control loops), while message passing is preferred for **structured, command-based** communication (task control blocks, configuration updates). **(b) Priority Inheritance Protocol (7 Marks):** **Definition:** PIP is a synchronization protocol in which, when a high-priority task blocks on a mutex currently held by a lower-priority task, the kernel **temporarily raises** the lower-priority task's priority to that of the highest-priority waiter. The priority is **restored** to its original value when the lock is released. **Three-Task Scenario (Mars Pathfinder-style inversion):** - **Task H** (Priority = 3, High) — needs shared data buffer. - **Task M** (Priority = 2, Medium) — does not use the buffer, just CPU-bound. - **Task L** (Priority = 1, Low) — currently holds the mutex on the shared buffer. **Timeline WITHOUT PIP (Unbounded Priority Inversion):** | Time | Event | Running Task | |---|---|---| | t0 | L acquires mutex on shared buffer | L | | t1 | H becomes ready, preempts L, tries to lock → **BLOCKS** | L (resumes) | | t2 | M becomes ready, preempts L (H is still blocked!) | M | | t3 | M runs to completion | M | | t4 | L resumes, finishes critical section, releases mutex | L | | t5 | H finally acquires the lock | H | **Problem:** H (highest priority) is blocked not just by L, but by **M as well** — a medium-priority task that has nothing to do with the shared resource. This is **unbounded** because M can be arbitrarily long. **Timeline WITH PIP (Bounded Priority Inversion):** | Time | Event | Running Task | |---|---|---| | t0 | L acquires mutex on shared buffer | L (priority = 1) | | t1 | H becomes ready, preempts L, tries to lock → **BLOCKS** | — | | t2 | Kernel **inherits H's priority** to L → L now runs at priority 3 | L (priority = 3) | | t3 | M becomes ready, but cannot preempt L (3 > 2) | L continues | | t4 | L finishes critical section, releases mutex, priority restored to 1 | L | | t5 | H immediately acquires the lock | H | **Result:** H is blocked **only** for the duration of L's critical section, regardless of M's execution time. The inversion is now **bounded** by the critical section length. **KTU 2024 Insight:** The famous **Mars Pathfinder** spacecraft (1997) suffered system resets due to this exact bug. The fix was to enable PIP in the VxWorks RTOS from Earth — a classic real-world case study examiners love. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Do NOT** claim shared memory has "no race condition" because it's "fast" — speed is unrelated to atomicity. > 2. **Do NOT** confuse binary semaphores with mutexes in your PIP explanation; mutexes have **ownership** and **priority inheritance**, binary semaphores do not. > 3. **Do NOT** forget the **DMB (Data Memory Barrier)** instruction when describing ARM Cortex-M shared memory — without it, the compiler/CPU can reorder memory accesses and break your synchronization. > 4. In the race condition trace, examiners will **strictly deduct 1 mark** if you do not show the 3-instruction breakdown of `cnt = cnt + 1`. > 5. When drawing the block diagram, **always label the mutex state** (locked/unlocked) and the **RAM address** of the shared buffer — generic boxes fetch only partial marks. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** Shared Memory is the fastest IPC mechanism in RTOS where tasks communicate by accessing a common RAM region (global variable, static buffer, or mailbox). - **Key Advantage:** Zero kernel copy overhead — access time is just 1–10 CPU cycles. - **Key Disadvantage:** Requires explicit synchronization to prevent **race conditions**, **torn reads**, and **lost updates**. - **Synchronization Primitives:** Mutex (with Priority Inheritance), Binary Semaphore, Counting Semaphore, Interrupt Disable, Spinlock, Test-and-Set. - **The Classic Race Condition:** The statement `cnt = cnt + 1` is **NOT atomic** — it compiles to 3 ARM instructions (LDR, ADD, STR), any preemption between which causes data corruption. - **PIP (Priority Inheritance Protocol):** When a high-priority task blocks on a mutex held by a lower-priority task, the lock-holder's priority is **temporarily boosted** to that of the highest waiter. This **bounds** priority inversion to a single critical section. - **ARM Cortex-M Specifics:** Use the **bit-band alias** (`0x22000000`) for atomic single-bit access; use the **`DMB`** instruction to enforce memory ordering after every shared write. - **MPU Configuration:** The Memory Protection Unit can mark the shared region as **read-only for one task** and **write-only for the other**, adding hardware-level safety to software mutex protection. - **Schedulability Impact:** Adding a critical section of length $C_{\text{cs}}$ to a task consumes a portion of its slack time. The Liu & Layland bound must be re-verified with the blocking term $B_k / T_k$. - **Real-World Case Study:** The 1997 **Mars Pathfinder** bug is the textbook example of unbounded priority inversion; it was fixed remotely by enabling PIP via a VxWorks patch. - **KTU Exam Frequency:** Shared Memory + Race Condition + PIP is a **guaranteed** 14-mark question in every KTU end-semester paper for PEAET695 under the 2024 scheme.

topics.title: Message Passing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET695 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Message Passing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Message Passing** is an **Inter-Process Communication (IPC)** mechanism in which cooperating tasks/tasks exchange data and synchronization signals by transmitting discrete **messages** through a kernel-managed communication channel, *instead* of directly accessing a shared memory region. The Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) kernel is the *trusted intermediary* that serializes, buffers, and routes messages between sender and receiver tasks.

In a hard real-time embedded system, message passing is preferred over shared memory because:

* It avoids **race conditions**, **deadlocks**, and **data corruption** caused by unsynchronized memory access.
* It enforces **information hiding** (the sender need not know the receiver's internal data layout).
* It maps cleanly onto **distributed multiprocessor** and **networked embedded** architectures (CAN, Ethernet, RS-485, Wireless Sensor Networks).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Postal Service"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Intuitive Understanding):**
> Imagine two office workers, **Alice** and **Bob**, sitting in different rooms. They cannot walk into each other's rooms to read/write on the same whiteboard (that would be *shared memory*). Instead, they send **sealed letters** through the **company post office** (the *RTOS kernel*).
>
> 1. Alice writes a letter (the **message**).
> 2. She drops it in the **outgoing mail slot** (`POST`/`SEND` system call).
> 3. The post office sorts it, queues it, and delivers it to Bob's **incoming mail slot** (`PEND`/`RECEIVE` system call).
> 4. Bob picks it up at his convenience.
>
> The post office guarantees: **no letter is lost**, **letters arrive in order**, and **Alice never sees Bob's private desk**. This is *exactly* what message passing guarantees for embedded tasks.

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of Message Passing in RTOS

| Pillar | Purpose | RTOS Primitive |
|---|---|---|
| **Mailbox** | Single-message handoff (1-slot buffer) | `OSMboxPost`, `OSMboxPend` |
| **Message Queue** | Multi-message FIFO buffer | `OSQPost`, `OSQPend` |
| **Pipe** | Byte-stream channel | POSIX `pipe()`, `read`, `write` |

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Bold

* **Message = pointer to a memory block** (μC/OS-II convention, *standard* in commercial RTOS).
* **Typical mailbox depth = 1 message** (fixed).
* **Typical message queue depth = N messages** (configurable, e.g., 4, 8, 16, 32).
* **Context-switch time** for `POST` / `PEND` ≈ **1–10 µs** (cortex-M3 @ 72 MHz).
* **Blocking timeout** parameter is of type **`INT16U`** or **`INT32U`** ticks in μC/OS-II (0 = non-blocking, `OS_FOREVER` = wait indefinitely).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Board Exam Tip:** Always state the *unit* of the timeout (clock ticks vs milliseconds vs microseconds). Examiners explicitly check this.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Message Passing (Hierarchical Breakdown)

#### A. By Communication Channel
* **Direct Message Passing** — sender names the receiver explicitly: `send(P, msg)` and `receive(Q, msg)`.
* **Indirect Message Passing** — messages routed via a **port / mailbox / queue**: `send(mailbox, msg)` and `receive(mailbox, msg)`.

#### B. By Synchronization Semantics
* **Synchronous (Rendezvous)** — sender blocks until receiver accepts the message. No buffering required. Highest coupling.
* **Asynchronous (Buffered)** — sender deposits message in kernel buffer and continues. Receiver collects later. Decouples timing.
* **Asynchronous with Non-Blocking Receive** — receiver polls; if no message present, returns immediately with an error code (e.g., `OS_ERR_NONE` vs `OS_ERR_TIMEOUT`).

#### C. By Buffering Discipline
* **Zero-capacity (rendezvous)** — no buffering; sender must wait.
* **Bounded-capacity** — finite FIFO queue of N messages (most common in RTOS).
* **Unbounded-capacity** — theoretically infinite buffer; never used in hard real-time (unpredictable).

### 2.2 Core RTOS Primitives (Detailed Operations)

#### 2.2.1 Mailbox

A **mailbox** is a kernel object containing **one** pointer (32-bit address on 32-bit MCU). The pointed-to memory is the *message payload*, typically a user-allocated structure.

**Operational Logic (Why & How):**

1. **Why a pointer, not the message itself?** — Mailbox must be **size-agnostic**. A pointer lets the sender pass *any* data structure (struct, array, buffer) without copying, achieving **zero-copy IPC**.
2. **How POST works:**
   * If mailbox is **empty** → deposit pointer, mark as full, wake highest-priority waiting task.
   * If mailbox is **full** → task either blocks (default) or returns error (with `OS_OPT_POST_NON_BLOCKING`).
3. **How PEND works:**
   * If mailbox is **full** → atomically read pointer, mark as empty, return success.
   * If mailbox is **empty** → task is placed in a **Waiting List** sorted by priority; runs only when POST or timeout fires.
4. **Overflow behavior:** Posting to a full mailbox is *lost* or *blocks* — depends on `OS_OPT_POST_*` flag.

#### 2.2.2 Message Queue

A **message queue** is a kernel object holding an **array of message pointers** in **FIFO** order with depth **N**.

**Operational Logic:**

1. **Internal data structure:** Circular buffer of size `N` with `in`, `out` indices.
2. **POST:** Write pointer at `in`, advance `in ← (in + 1) mod N`. If full → block or fail.
3. **PEND:** Read pointer at `out`, advance `out ← (out + 1) mod N`. If empty → block or timeout.
4. **Wake-up policy:** When POST unblocks a task, the **highest-priority** waiting task is made ready (preemptive priority scheduling).
5. **Use case:** Decoupling high-frequency ISR from low-frequency processing task; e.g., UART ISR deposits received bytes into a queue, application task drains the queue at its own rate.

#### 2.2.3 Pipe (POSIX)

A **pipe** is a kernel-managed **byte-stream FIFO** with two file descriptors: `read end` and `write end`. Used heavily in Linux/RT-POSIX embedded systems (e.g., embedded Linux on Raspberry Pi, industrial gateways).

* **Anonymous pipe** — parent-child only (single-host).
* **Named pipe (FIFO)** — accessible by name on filesystem, supports unrelated processes.

#### 2.2.4 Signals

A **signal** is a *software interrupt* delivered to a task. It is the lightest, lowest-bandwidth message — typically carries only a **signal number** (1–31 in POSIX), no payload.

| Signal Property | Detail |
|---|---|
| Asynchronous | Can arrive at any instruction boundary |
| Predefined handlers | `SIGTERM`, `SIGKILL`, `SIGINT`, `SIGALRM` |
| Custom handlers | `signal(SIGUSR1, handler)` |
| Real-time signals | `SIGRTMIN` to `SIGRTMAX` carry an integer/pointer payload |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` (not `\vert) for absolute value to preserve markdown table syntax. Likewise, all set/magnitude delimiters are rendered as $\vert \cdot \vert$.

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables / Boundary Conditions | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mailbox state (binary) | $M \in \{ 0, 1 \}$ | $M = 0$ empty, $M = 1$ full | dimensionless |
| 2 | Message queue depth | $N$ | $1 \le N \le 2^{16}$ typical | messages |
| 3 | Occupancy at time $t$ | $Q(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \mathbf{1}[\text{slot}_k \neq \emptyset]$ | $0 \le Q(t) \le N$ | messages |
| 4 | Circular buffer index update | $in_{next} = (in + 1) \bmod N$ | wrap-around at $N$ | integer index |
| 5 | Producer rate condition (no overflow) | $\lambda_{in} \le \mu_{out} + \frac{N}{\Delta t}$ | $\lambda_{in}$ POSTs/sec, $\mu_{out}$ PENDs/sec | Hz |
| 6 | Mean waiting time (M/M/1 approximation) | $W_q = \dfrac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda}$ | $\rho = \lambda / \mu < 1$ | seconds |
| 7 | Blocking timeout value | $\tau \in \{0,\; \text{OS\_FOREVER},\; [1,\, 2^{32}-1]\}$ | $0$ = non-blocking, $\infty$ = forever | clock ticks |
| 8 | Priority wakeup guarantee | $T_{wait} \le T_{max} = \sum_{i \in \text{tasks}} C_i$ | if preemption disabled | ticks |
| 9 | Message size (μC/OS-II) | pointer = address = $A$ | $A \in [0,\; 2^{32}-1]$ on 32-bit MCU | bytes |
| 10 | Total message memory | $M_{total} = N \times S_{ptr} + M_{struct}$ | $S_{ptr} = 4$ bytes (32-bit) | bytes |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Automotive ECUs (AUTOSAR):** Inter-Ecu communication over CAN uses COM-based message passing with PDU (Protocol Data Unit) containers — direct industry application.
* **Industrial IoT Gateways:** MQTT pub-sub on top of TCP sockets = distributed message passing.
* **Medical Devices (FDA Class III):** Mailboxes used to hand off ADC samples from interrupt context to a safety-critical processing task, guaranteeing deterministic latency.
* **Aerospace (DO-178C):** DO-178C Level A software *requires* bounded buffering and worst-case execution time (WCET) analysis — message queues are analyzable; shared memory often is not.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Analysis: Mailbox `POST–PEND` State Machine

We will derive the **state-transition function** for a single-mailbox system, which is a fundamental analytical model tested in KTU 14-mark problems.

#### 3.1.1 System Definition

Let:
* $M$ = mailbox state, $M \in \{0, 1\}$ where $0$ = empty, $1$ = full.
* $S$ = set of tasks waiting on the mailbox (priority queue).
* $p$ = pointer to the message payload, $p \in \mathcal{P}$ where $\mathcal{P}$ is the set of all valid memory addresses.
* $\tau$ = timeout value in ticks, $\tau \in \mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0} \cup \{\infty\}$.

#### 3.1.2 State-Transition Derivation for `OSMboxPost(mbox, p)`

$$
\text{POST}(M, S, p, opt) \;\longrightarrow\; (M', S', r)
$$

$$
M' = \begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } M = 0 \text{ and } S = \emptyset \\[4pt]
1 & \text{if } M = 0 \text{ and } S \neq \emptyset \text{ (msg handed to waiter)} \\[4pt]
1 & \text{if } M = 1 \text{ and } opt = \text{BLOCK} \\[4pt]
1 & \text{if } M = 1 \text{ and } opt = \text{NON\_BLOCK}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step-by-step explanation (each line):**

* **Line 1:** Mailbox is empty, no one waiting → simply store the pointer. Success return code $r = \text{OS\_ERR\_NONE}$.
* **Line 2:** Mailbox is empty, but a task `T_i` is already blocked on `PEND`. The kernel **atomically** delivers `p` to the highest-priority waiter, wakes `T_i`, and marks the mailbox full (the waiter will read it in its next execution slice). This avoids a needless state change to empty.
* **Line 3:** Mailbox is full and caller chose blocking semantics → caller task joins the *sender* wait list (or fails with timeout).
* **Line 4:** Mailbox is full and caller chose non-blocking → return `OS_ERR_MBOX_FULL`.

#### 3.1.3 State-Transition Derivation for `OSMboxPend(mbox, τ, err)`

$$
\text{PEND}(M, S, p, \tau) \;\longrightarrow\; (M', S', p', r)
$$

$$
(M', p') = \begin{cases}
(0,\; p_{mailbox}) & \text{if } M = 1 \text{ at entry} \\[4pt]
(0,\; p_{posted}) & \text{if } M = 0,\; \tau = \infty,\; \text{then POST wakes it} \\[4pt]
(0,\; \text{NULL}) & \text{if } M = 0,\; \tau = 0,\; \text{non-blocking timeout} \\[4pt]
(0,\; \text{NULL}) & \text{if } M = 0,\; 0 < \tau < \infty,\; \text{timeout fires before POST}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step-by-step explanation:**

* **Case 1 (instant success):** Mailbox already holds a pointer; atomically read it and clear the mailbox.
* **Case 2 (blocked wake-up):** Caller is suspended; when a future POST deposits a pointer, the kernel wakes the highest-priority waiter and hands it the pointer.
* **Case 3 (polling):** Mailbox empty, caller does not want to wait → return immediately with `OS_ERR_PEND_WOULD_BLOCK` or `OS_ERR_TIMEOUT`.
* **Case 4 (timeout):** The kernel's tick ISR decrements a per-task timer; when it hits zero, the task is moved from waiting list to ready list with `OS_ERR_TIMEOUT`.

#### 3.1.4 Critical-Section Guarantee

The **read–modify–write** of $M$ (the state flag) and $p$ (the pointer) must be **atomic**. On ARM Cortex-M, this is achieved by disabling interrupts (`CPU_CRITICAL_ENTER()`) for the few cycles of the update, or by using the dedicated hardware semaphore if present.

$$
\text{ISR-disabled window: } T_{crit} = T_{load} + T_{store} + T_{branch} \approx 5 \text{ to } 20 \text{ cycles}
$$

$$
T_{crit}^{max} = 20 \text{ cycles} \times T_{clk} = \frac{20}{72 \times 10^{6}} \approx 277.8 \text{ ns}
$$

This is the **upper bound on interrupt latency** introduced by message-passing primitives — a key KTU board question on RTOS design.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Choice of Primitive

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is **directly mapped** to KTU 2024 Course Outcomes CO3 (design RTOS-based applications) and CO4 (analyze synchronization trade-offs).

| Decision Criterion | **Mailbox** | **Message Queue** | **Pipe** | **Signal** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Payload size | Pointer (any size) | Pointer per slot | Byte stream | Signal number only |
| Buffering depth | 1 message | N messages | N bytes (e.g., 4096) | 1 pending signal type |
| ISR → Task | **Excellent** | **Excellent** | Poor | Good |
| Task ↔ Task | Good | **Excellent** | Good | Limited |
| Multi-producer | Risky (data race) | **Safe (FIFO)** | Safe (atomic write) | Safe |
| Determinism | **High** | **High** | Medium | Medium |
| Memory overhead | **Lowest** | Medium | High | Lowest |
| KTU typical marks | 3–4 | 7–14 | 3–4 | 3 |
| μC/OS-II API | `OSMbox*` | `OSQ*` | N/A | N/A |
| FreeRTOS API | (use queue) | `xQueue*` | `xStreamBuffer*` | `xTaskNotify` |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A temperature sensor task produces readings at **100 Hz**. A logger task consumes them at a variable rate with mean **80 Hz** but worst-case **120 Hz** for 5 ms every 1 second. Design a message queue depth such that **no message is ever lost**.

**Given:**
* $\lambda_{in} = 100$ messages/sec
* $\mu_{out} = 80$ messages/sec average
* Burst: 120 Hz for 5 ms every 1 s

**Step 1 — Compute burst duration message count:**

$$
N_{burst} = 120 \times 0.005 = 0.6 \text{ messages in 5 ms}
$$

Since message count must be integer and the burst may straddle a tick boundary, conservatively round up to **1 message per burst event**, but accumulated bursts during steady-state deficit matter more.

**Step 2 — Compute backlog during steady-state deficit:**

$$
\Delta = \lambda_{in} - \mu_{out} = 100 - 80 = 20 \text{ messages/sec (excess)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute maximum transient buildup:**

Over a 1-second window:
$$
N_{build} = 20 \times 1 = 20 \text{ messages}
$$

**Step 4 — Add safety margin (KTU examiners reward this):**

$$
N_{design} = N_{build} \times 1.5 = 20 \times 1.5 = 30 \text{ messages}
$$

**Answer:** Configure message queue with depth $N = 32$ (next power of 2 for efficient modulo arithmetic on most RTOS ports).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often forget to specify the **unit of timeout** (ticks vs ms) or the **overflow policy** (blocking vs overwrite). Always state both.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Message Passing Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P["Producer Domain (Sensor / ISR Context)"]
        A1[Sensor Task T1] -->|OSMboxPost ptr| B1
        A2[UART ISR] -->|OSQPost byte ptr| B1
        A3[CAN RX ISR] -->|OSQPost frame ptr| B1
    end

    subgraph K["RTOS Kernel (Trusted Intermediary)"]
        B1[Mailbox Pool\nmb1 mb2 mb3] --> C1{Queue Manager}
        C1 --> D1[Message Queue Q1\ndepth N=32]
        C1 --> D2[Message Queue Q2\ndepth N=8]
        C1 --> D3[Mailbox M1\ndepth 1]
    end

    subgraph R["Consumer Domain (Processing Tasks)"]
        D1 --> E1[Control Task T2]
        D1 --> E2[Logger Task T3]
        D2 --> E3[Display Task T4]
        D3 --> E4[Safety Watchdog T5]
    end

    classDef kernel fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc8800,stroke-width:2px;
    class B1,C1,D1,D2,D3 kernel;
```

### 4.2 Mailbox State Machine (POST / PEND Transitions)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Empty
    Empty --> Full: POST p\nerr = NONE
    Full --> Empty: PEND\nreturns p
    Empty --> Empty: PEND tau=0\nerr = TIMEOUT
    Empty --> Waiting: PEND tau greater than 0\nTask in wait list
    Waiting --> Empty: POST p\nWakes highest-prio waiter\nReturns p to waiter
    Waiting --> Empty: Tick ISR timeout\nerr = TIMEOUT
    Full --> Full: POST tau=0\nerr = FULL
    Full --> Full: PEND tau=0\nerr = TIMEOUT
```

### 4.3 Message Queue Internal Buffer (Circular FIFO)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Q["Message Queue Q  depth N = 8"]
        S0[Slot 0]:::empty
        S1[Slot 1 ptr A]:::full
        S2[Slot 2 ptr B]:::full
        S3[Slot 3 ptr C]:::full
        S4[Slot 4]:::empty
        S5[Slot 5]:::empty
        S6[Slot 6]:::empty
        S7[Slot 7]:::empty
        IN[IN index = 3]:::ptr
        OUT[OUT index = 1]:::ptr
        S0 --- S1 --- S2 --- S3 --- S4 --- S5 --- S6 --- S7 --- S0
    end

    classDef full fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1px;
    classDef empty fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#999,stroke-dasharray:3 3;
    classDef ptr fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc5500,stroke-width:2px;
    class S1,S2,S3 full;
    class S0,S4,S5,S6,S7 empty;
    class IN,OUT ptr;
```

### 4.4 Producer–Consumer Sequence Diagram (Mailbox handoff)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant ISR as UART ISR
    participant K as RTOS Kernel
    participant M as Mailbox mbUART
    participant T as Logger Task T_Log
    Note over ISR: RX byte arrives
    ISR->>ISR: Build message struct at buf
    ISR->>K: OSMboxPost mbUART ptr=buf
    alt Mailbox empty
        K->>M: Store pointer
        K-->>ISR: Return OS_ERR_NONE
    else Mailbox full or task waiting
        K->>T: Wake highest priority waiter
        K-->>T: Deliver pointer to T_Log
    end
    Note over T: Task is scheduled
    T->>K: OSMboxPend mbUART tau=OS_FOREVER
    K->>T: Return pointer buf
    T->>T: Process buffer
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**
*Define message passing. List any two RTOS primitives used for message passing.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Message Passing** is an inter-task communication mechanism in which cooperating tasks exchange data by sending and receiving *messages* through kernel-managed channels (mailboxes, queues, pipes), rather than sharing a common memory region.
>
> *Two RTOS primitives:*
> 1. **Mailbox** — single-slot pointer handoff.
> 2. **Message Queue** — multi-slot FIFO of pointers.
>
> *(Acceptable alternatives: Pipe, Signal, Event Flag.)*

**Mark Distribution (as per KTU 2024 valuation key):**
* Definition — **1.5 marks**
* Any two primitives with one-line description — **1.5 marks**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*Distinguish between a mailbox and a message queue in μC/OS-II.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Mailbox | Message Queue |
|---|---|---|
| Buffer depth | 1 message | N messages |
| Internal storage | Single pointer | Array of pointers (FIFO) |
| Typical API | `OSMboxPost`, `OSMboxPend` | `OSQPost`, `OSQPend` |
| Use case | Simple ISR → task signaling | Burst data handoff |

*(Any three valid distinctions — full marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> Each long-answer question has **two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks**. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q3 (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **mailbox** mechanism in detail with reference to μC/OS-II. Discuss the `OSMboxPost()` and `OSMboxPend()` functions, including blocking and non-blocking semantics.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
A mailbox in μC/OS-II is a kernel object that holds **one** pointer to a message. The pointer can address any data structure in memory (zero-copy IPC).

**OSMboxPost() — POST Operation (2.5 marks):**
* Prototype: `void OSMboxPost(OS_EVENT *pevent, void *pmsg);`
* If mailbox is **empty** → pointer is stored, mailbox state → full; if a task is waiting, the highest-priority waiter is woken immediately.
* If mailbox is **full** → default behavior returns `OS_ERR_MBOX_FULL`. With `OS_OPT_POST_BLOCKING` (newer ports) or custom code, sender can block on a *sender wait list* until the mailbox is emptied.
* Returns: `OS_ERR_NONE`, `OS_ERR_MBOX_FULL`, `OS_ERR_EVENT_TYPE`, `OS_ERR_PEND_ABORT`.

**OSMboxPend() — PEND Operation (2 marks):**
* Prototype: `void *OSMboxPend(OS_EVENT *pevent, INT16U timeout, INT8U *perr);`
* If mailbox is **full** → atomically returns the pointer, sets mailbox to empty.
* If mailbox is **empty** → current task is suspended in mailbox's wait list (sorted by priority); resumes when POST delivers a message or `timeout` ticks elapse.
* Returns: pointer on success, `NULL` on timeout/abort.

**Blocking vs Non-Blocking (1 mark):**
* `timeout = 0` → non-blocking (poll mode).
* `timeout = OS_FOREVER` → infinite block.
* `timeout = N` → block up to N ticks; converted to ms via `OS_TICKS_PER_SEC`.

---

**Q3 (b) [7 Marks]** Compare **message passing** with **shared memory** as IPC mechanisms. State the conditions under which each is preferred in an RTOS.

**Model Solution:**

| Criterion | Shared Memory | Message Passing |
|---|---|---|
| Speed | Faster (no kernel copy) | Slightly slower (kernel involvement) |
| Synchronization burden | Programmer must use mutex/semaphore | Kernel handles serialization |
| Race conditions | Possible if not protected | Eliminated by design |
| Code complexity | Higher | Lower |
| Determinism | Lower (locks introduce jitter) | Higher (kernel provides WCET bounds) |
| Distributed support | Not natural | Natural (CAN, TCP/IP, MQTT) |
| Memory cost | One buffer | Mailbox + wait lists |
| Analyzability | Hard (lock ordering, priority inversion) | Easier (bounded queues) |

**Preference Rules (3 marks for this section):**

* **Use shared memory** when:
  - Single-processor, single-address-space.
  - Extremely high throughput required (e.g., pixel pipeline of a camera).
  - All cooperating tasks are written and verified by the same team.
  - Latency budget is in the sub-microsecond range.

* **Use message passing** when:
  - Tasks may run on different cores / processors / ECUs.
  - System must be **safety-critical** (ISO 26262, DO-178C, IEC 62304).
  - Designer wants to enforce **modularity and information hiding**.
  - Communication must traverse a network (CAN, Ethernet, Wireless).
  - Bounded worst-case latency is required (formal schedulability analysis).

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q4 (a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a **message queue** in an RTOS. Show how multiple producers and multiple consumers are handled, and discuss the role of priority inversion avoidance.

**Model Solution:**

**Diagram (2 marks):**
```
  Producer 1 ─┐                ┌─► Consumer 1
  Producer 2 ─┼─► QUEUE (FIFO)─┼─► Consumer 2
  Producer 3 ─┘                └─► Consumer 3
```

**Internal Structure (2 marks):**
* Circular buffer of `N` slots, each holding a pointer.
* `in` index advances on `OSQPost`, `out` index advances on `OSQPend`, both modulo `N`.
* Wait list per queue, sorted by task priority.

**Multi-Producer / Multi-Consumer Handling (2 marks):**
* `in` index update is atomic (protected via `CPU_CRITICAL_ENTER()`).
* Multiple producers POSTing concurrently cannot corrupt `in` because the critical section is short.
* Similarly for `out` on the consumer side.
* If the queue is full, late POSTers are either blocked (sender wait list) or rejected.

**Priority Inversion Avoidance (1 mark):**
