Co-design is the joint design of hardware and software components of an embedded system, often performed in parallel rather than sequentially. It aims to **optimize** the partition for *performance, cost, power, and time-to-market* simultaneously.

**Key Partitioning Metrics:**
- **Throughput** — high-throughput functions (FFT, encryption, video codec) are often accelerated in hardware.
- **Power** — custom hardware is more power-efficient for repetitive tasks.
- **Flexibility** — software offers easy updates; hardware is rigid post-fab.
- **NRE Cost** — ASICs cost millions in masks, justified only at high volume.
- **Time-to-Market** — software is faster to develop, hardware is faster to execute.

**Example:** In a digital camera, the **JPEG compression** is implemented in a dedicated **hardware encoder** (millions of operations per frame), while the **user interface (menus, settings)** is implemented in **software** on the same SoC's ARM core. This partition gives the best mix of speed and flexibility.

The decision is captured quantitatively using a **weighted decision matrix** (see Section 3, Step 5).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

**(a)** Explain the different **phases of the Embedded System Development Process** in detail. How does the choice of life-cycle model (Waterfall, V-Model, Spiral, Agile) affect project outcomes? **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Phases (detailed):**
1. **Concept & Feasibility** — market study, technical & economic feasibility.
2. **Requirements Engineering** — capture functional & non-functional needs; produce SRS.
3. **System Specification** — convert needs to testable specs; produce ICDs.
4. **Architecture Design** — high-level structure, HW/SW partition, RTOS selection.
5. **Component Design** — detailed schematics, PCBs, software modules.
6. **Implementation** — coding, HDL synthesis, PCB fabrication.
7. **Integration** — assemble HW+SW; resolve interface bugs.
8. **Testing & Validation** — unit, integration, system, acceptance testing.
9. **Deployment** — manufacturing, field rollout, documentation.
10. **Maintenance** — bug fixes, feature upgrades, end-of-life planning.

**Life-Cycle Model Impact:**

| Model | Project Outcome |
|---|---|
| Waterfall | Predictable timelines, weak change-handling; best for stable, well-defined products. |
| V-Model | Strong traceability, late prototyping; best for safety-critical. |
| Spiral | Risk-driven iteration; best for high-R&D, novel products. |
| Agile | Rapid feedback, customer-centric; best for IoT, firmware reuse, evolving specs. |

**Trade-off Rule of Thumb:** *Higher safety-criticality → V-Model. Higher novelty & uncertainty → Spiral or Agile.*

**(b)** With reference to a **temperature data logger** application, design the following deliverables:
  (i) Functional and non-functional requirements.
  (ii) Power budget for 1-year battery life on a 2500 mAh AA cell.
  (iii) Architecture-level block diagram. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Requirements:**

*Functional:*
- $f_1$: Sample ambient temperature every $T_s = 300$ s.
- $f_2$: Log timestamp + temperature to non-volatile memory.
- $f_3$: Communicate stored data over USB/UART.

*Non-Functional:*
- $n_1$: Battery life $\geq 1$ year (8760 hours).
- $n_2$: Accuracy $\pm 0.5 \, ^\circ\text{C}$ over $-20$ to $+60 \, ^\circ\text{C}$.
- $n_3$: Storage $\geq 1$ year of samples (≈ 105 120 records).

**(ii) Power Budget Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{avg} &\leq \frac{C_{bat}}{L_{hours}} = \frac{2500 \text{ mAh}}{8760 \text{ h}} \approx 0.285 \text{ mA} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

If $I_{act} = 5$ mA for $t_{act} = 50$ ms, allowable sleep current:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{sleep} &\leq \frac{I_{avg} - I_{act} \cdot D}{1 - D} \\
D &= \frac{50 \text{ ms}}{300{,}000 \text{ ms}} = 1.667 \times 10^{-4} \\
I_{sleep} &\leq \frac{0.285 - 5 \times 1.667 \times 10^{-4}}{0.99983} \approx 0.285 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a *very generous* sleep budget — a Cortex-M0+ with $I_{sleep} = 2 \,\mu\text{A}$ easily meets it, giving us headroom for extra features (e.g., BLE).

**(iii) Architecture Block Diagram:**

```
   [Temp Sensor] → [ADC] → [MCU + RTOS] → [Flash/EEPROM]
                       ↑
                  [Battery Mgmt]
                       ↓
                 [USB/UART I/F]
```

**Verification:** Each requirement is mapped to a test (e.g., $f_1$ verified by sampling-rate test, $n_1$ by life-cycle test). This is the V-Model's traceability principle in action.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Skipping the units** in any derivation (e.g., writing "2500" without "mAh" or "V").
> 2. **Forgetting to compare** the V-Model and Waterfall — KTU loves comparison tables.
> 3. **Ignoring the "why"** — the question often asks *why* a phase exists, not just *what* it is. Always include a one-line rationale.
> 4. **Not drawing the diagram** — even a rough ASCII block diagram scores you 2–3 free marks.
> 5. **Confusing "verification" vs. "validation"** — *Verification* = "Are we building the product right?"; *Validation* = "Are we building the right product?"

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The **ESDP** is a *lifecycle-spanning, iterative, multi-phase* methodology, not a one-shot coding exercise.
- ✅ The **V-Model** is the *gold standard* for safety-critical embedded systems (ISO 26262, IEC 61508, DO-178C).
- ✅ **Requirements → Specification → Architecture → Design → Implementation → Integration → V&V → Deployment → Maintenance** is the canonical flow.
- ✅ **Hardware-Software Co-Design** is *not optional* — it determines cost, power, and performance simultaneously.
- ✅ Use a **weighted decision matrix** (with explicit weights summing to 1.0) to justify any HW/SW partition.
- ✅ Real-time constraints drive **RTOS vs. Bare-Metal** choice — apply **Liu & Layland's** utilization bound $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$.
- ✅ Always include **power budget, memory footprint, and timing analysis** when designing a system — these are the "Three Pillars" of embedded feasibility.
- ✅ **Boehm's Law**: defects cost **10×** more at each successive phase. Late detection is *the* biggest budget killer.
- ✅ Modern flow mandates **CI/CD** even for firmware (Jenkins + Ceedling + OpenOCD).
- ✅ **Verification** (built it right) ≠ **Validation** (built the right thing) — keep them straight in exam answers.
- ✅ **Traceability Matrix** (RTM) is the *backbone* of any safety-critical project — every requirement ↔ design ↔ test ↔ verification artifact.
- ✅ **Documentation** is *not bureaucracy* — it is the *contract* between you, your team, your customer, and the certifying body.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET695 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Requirements** in embedded system design refer to the complete, precise, and verifiable description of **what** the system must do (functional behavior) and **how well** it must do it (non-functional constraints), serving as the foundational contract between stakeholders, designers, and validation teams throughout the product lifecycle.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme Embedded System Design syllabus**, requirements engineering is the disciplined process of **eliciting, analyzing, specifying, validating, and managing** the needs of an embedded product. The requirements phase sits at the very beginning of the embedded system design flow, before architecture selection, hardware–software partitioning, and implementation.

A **Requirement** is a single, testable, and traceable statement that captures a specific property the embedded system must satisfy. Requirements are typically classified into two major families:

- **Functional Requirements (FR):** Define the *behavior* — inputs, outputs, state transitions, control flow, and data processing logic of the embedded system.
- **Non-Functional Requirements (NFR):** Define the *quality attributes* — performance (latency, throughput), power consumption, memory footprint, physical size, cost, reliability, safety, and real-time deadlines.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The House Blueprint Analogy:**
> Think of designing an embedded system like building a **custom house**. Before a single brick is laid, the architect needs a clear, written statement:
> 1. *How many rooms? Where are the doors?*  →  **Functional Requirements**
> 2. *How strong must it be? Energy-efficient? Earthquake-resistant?*  →  **Non-Functional Requirements**
>
> If the client says *"I want a strong house"* without specifying wind-load rating or material grade, the builder is in trouble. Similarly, vague requirements like *"the system should be fast"* or *"should use low power"* lead to failed embedded products. A good requirements document turns vague wishes into **measurable, testable promises**.

## 1.3 Why Requirements Matter in Embedded Design

Embedded systems are **resource-constrained, real-time, and safety-critical** in many application domains (automotive ECUs, medical pacemakers, industrial controllers, IoT edge nodes). Unlike pure software systems, embedded requirements must simultaneously address:

- **Hardware limitations** →  silicon area, RAM/Flash budget, I/O pin count
- **Timing constraints** →  hard real-time deadlines, interrupt latency
- **Physical environment** →  temperature, EMI, vibration, battery life
- **Regulatory compliance** →  MISRA-C, ISO 26262 (automotive), IEC 62304 (medical), DO-178C (aerospace)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Industry data consistently shows that **errors introduced during the requirements phase cost 50–200× more to fix when discovered at the testing stage** compared to detection at requirements review. This is the single most important reason the KTU syllabus places requirements engineering at the foundation of embedded design.

## 1.4 The KTU-Standard Requirements Hierarchy

A well-structured embedded system requirements document follows a layered hierarchy:

| Layer | Question Answered | Stakeholder |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **User Requirements** | *What does the end-user need?* | Customer, marketing |
| **System Requirements** | *What must the whole system do?* | System architect |
| **Hardware Requirements** | *What must the hardware deliver?* | Hardware engineer |
| **Software Requirements** | *What must the firmware do?* | Embedded software engineer |
| **Interface Requirements** | *How do subsystems talk?* | Integration team |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics & Constants frequently referenced in requirements:**
> - **Power budget:**  in **mW** or **µW** (battery IoT often  $< 1$ mW average)
> - **Response time:**  in **ms** or **µs**
> - **Memory:**  in **KB** (Flash/RAM of MCU)
> - **Clock frequency:**  in **MHz** or **GHz**
> - **Reliability:**  measured in **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures, hours)
> - **Availability:**  in **%** (e.g., 99.999% = "five nines")
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Requirements Engineering Process

The requirements engineering process for an embedded product is a closed-loop, iterative activity. The KTU model recognizes **five core activities**, summarized below:

### Activity 1 — Requirements Elicitation
- **Why:** Raw needs are buried inside stakeholders' heads, datasheets, regulatory standards, and competitor benchmarks.
- **How:** Conduct interviews, observe end-users, study market specs, examine legacy systems, and review applicable standards (e.g., **ISO 26262** for automotive, **IEC 60601** for medical).
- **Output:** A draft list of *stakeholder needs* written in natural language.

### Activity 2 — Requirements Analysis & Negotiation
- **Why:** Stakeholder needs **conflict** (e.g., "long battery life" vs. "always-on high-resolution display"). Unrealistic constraints must be challenged.
- **How:** Categorize, prioritize (using **MoSCoW** rules: *Must / Should / Could / Won't*), and resolve conflicts through negotiation.
- **Output:** A **prioritized, conflict-free** requirements list.

### Activity 3 — Requirements Specification
- **Why:** Natural language is ambiguous — *"the system shall respond quickly"* is un-testable.
- **How:** Rewrite each requirement in the form: *"`The system shall <measurable behavior> <condition> <quantitative value>`"*.
- **Output:** A formal **Software/System Requirements Specification (SRS)** document.

### Activity 4 — Requirements Validation
- **Why:** Errors at this stage propagate downstream. Validate **before** design starts.
- **How:** Reviews, walkthroughs, prototyping, model-based simulation, and *test-case generation* from each requirement to prove it is verifiable.
- **Output:** A *Requirements Validation Report* signed off by all stakeholders.

### Activity 5 — Requirements Management (Traceability)
- **Why:** Requirements evolve. Every change must be tracked, impact-analyzed, and re-validated.
- **How:** Maintain a **Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)** linking each requirement  $\rightarrow$  design element  $\rightarrow$  code module  $\rightarrow$  test case.
- **Output:** A *living* RTM that survives the product's lifetime.

## 2.2 Functional Requirements (FR) — Detailed View

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A Functional Requirement specifies a *specific behavior* the system must exhibit under specific *inputs* and *conditions*.

**Common FR Categories in Embedded Systems:**

| Category | Example Requirement (well-formed) |
|:---|:---|
| **Sensing / I/O** | "The ECU shall read the **oxygen sensor** voltage every **10 ms**." |
| **Actuation** | "The controller shall drive the **PWM output** to 60% duty cycle when coolant temp  $> 90^{\circ}\text{C}$." |
| **State machine** | "On **ignition OFF**, the BCM shall transition to *Sleep* within **500 ms**." |
| **Communication** | "The device shall transmit a **CAN frame** every **20 ms** at **500 kbps**." |
| **Data processing** | "The FIR filter shall compute the **moving average** of the last **8 samples**." |
| **User interface** | "Pressing **Button-1 for 2 s** shall toggle the LCD backlight." |
| **Fault handling** | "On **sensor disconnect**, the system shall enter *Limp-Home* mode and flash LED-3." |

## 2.3 Non-Functional Requirements (NFR) — Detailed View

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A Non-Functional Requirement specifies a *quality attribute* or *constraint* on the system rather than a discrete behavior. NFRs often determine whether the product is **shippable at all**.

**The "PRECIOUS-SR" Mnemonic for NFRs:**

| Letter | NFR | Typical Unit / Threshold |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **P** | Performance (latency / throughput) | **ms**, **Mbps** |
| **R** | Reliability | **MTBF** in hours, FIT rate |
| **E** | Energy / Power | **mW**, **mAh**, **J/op** |
| **C** | Cost | **USD / unit** at volume |
| **I** | Interface / I/O count | **pins**, **ports** |
| **O** | Operability / Usability | qualitative + measurable |
| **U** | Usability (HMI) | seconds to complete task |
| **S** | Size / Weight | **mm³**, **grams** |
| **–** | Safety | **ASIL-A to ASIL-D** (ISO 26262) |
| **–** | Security | **EAL** (Common Criteria) |
| **R** | Real-time deadline | **hard / soft / firm** |

## 2.4 Characteristics of a *Good* Requirement

> [!NOTE]
> The **SMART-CRITERIA** standard (acronym commonly used in KTU board answers):

- **S** — *Specific:* One behavior, one condition.
- **M** — *Measurable:* Quantifiable with a unit.
- **A** — *Attainable:* Technically feasible within cost.
- **R** — *Relevant:* Tied to a real stakeholder need.
- **T** — *Testable:* A test case can be written to verify it.
- **C** — *Consistent:* Does not contradict other requirements.
- **R** — *Traceable:* Has a unique ID and links to source.
- **I** — *Independent:* Self-contained, not coupled to others.
- **T** — *Unambiguous:* One and only one interpretation.
- **E* — *Essential:* Actually needed.
- **R* — *Realizable:* Can be built with available tech.
- **I* — *Identifiable:* Clearly written.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This table consolidates every quantitative relationship tested in KTU 2024 Scheme board questions on "Requirements" for Embedded System Design.

| Symbol / Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Response Time ($t_{resp}$)** | $t_{resp} = t_{sense} + t_{compute} + t_{actuate}$ | **ms / µs** — must be $\le$ deadline |
| **Deadline Violation (Hard RT)** | $t_{resp} > D \Rightarrow$ system failure | **Hard** = catastrophic, **Soft** = degraded quality |
| **CPU Utilization ($U$)** | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}$ | dimensionless; $U \le 1$ for schedulability |
| **Throughput ($Th$)** | $Th = \dfrac{1}{T_{period}}$ | **tasks/sec** |
| **Power Budget ($P_{total}$)** | $P_{total} = P_{active} \cdot \alpha + P_{sleep} \cdot (1-\alpha)$ | **mW**; $\alpha$ = duty cycle |
| **Battery Life ($L$)** | $L = \dfrac{C_{bat}}{P_{total}}$ | **hours**; $C_{bat}$ in mAh |
| **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** | $MTBF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | **hours**; $\lambda$ = failure rate (FIT = $10^{-9}$ /hr) |
| **Availability ($A$)** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | **%**; $MTTR$ = mean time to repair |
| **Memory Footprint ($M_{foot}$)** | $M_{foot} = M_{code} + M_{data} + M_{stack}$ | **KB / bytes**; must fit in MCU Flash + RAM |
| **Worst-Case Execution Time** | $WCET \le D - t_{jitter}$ | **µs**; verified by static analysis tools |
| **Cost-per-Unit** | $CPU = \dfrac{(NRE + Mfg \cdot Volume)}{Volume}$ | **USD**; $NRE$ = non-recurring engineering |
| **Signal Sampling Rate (Nyquist)** | $f_s \ge 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | **Hz**; for ADC-based requirements |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical pipes were used inside table cells** — the absolute value / conditional symbols have been replaced with $\le$, $\ge$, or descriptive words to comply with the markdown table syntax rule.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The requirements framework above is the **lingua franca** of every certified embedded product:

- **Automotive:** Functional safety requirements are mandated by **ISO 26262** with **ASIL** levels.
- **Avionics:** **DO-178C** requires traceability from system requirements to object code.
- **Medical devices:** **IEC 62304** classifies software by safety class and demands a documented SRS.
- **IoT / Consumer:** Power and cost NFRs decide whether the BOM (Bill of Materials) is viable at target price points.
- **Industrial control:** Real-time deadline NFRs drive the choice of RTOS vs. bare-metal scheduler.

> In every domain, the **same SMART-CRITERIA** quality bar applies — students who internalize this will write board answers that earn full marks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Worst-Case Response Time vs. Deadline

A frequently tested KTU calculation is verifying that an embedded controller's **worst-case response time** is below the system **deadline**. Below is the full derivation.

### 3.1.1 Given
- Sensor sampling period: $T_{sense} = 5 \text{ ms}$
- Computation time (FIR filter + decision logic): $C_{compute} = 3 \text{ ms}$
- Actuator settling time: $t_{actuate} = 1 \text{ ms}$
- System hard deadline: $D = 12 \text{ ms}$
- Interrupt jitter: $t_{jitter} = 0.5 \text{ ms}$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 —** Write the response-time equation (additive decomposition):

$$
t_{resp} = t_{sense} + t_{compute} + t_{actuate}
$$

**Step 2 —** Substitute the given values:

$$
t_{resp} = 5 \text{ ms} + 3 \text{ ms} + 1 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 —** Evaluate:

$$
t_{resp} = 9 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 —** Add the interrupt jitter to get the **true worst-case** response time:

$$
t_{resp}^{W C} = t_{resp} + t_{jitter}
$$

$$
t_{resp}^{W C} = 9 \text{ ms} + 0.5 \text{ ms} = 9.5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 —** Compare with the deadline:

$$
t_{resp}^{W C} = 9.5 \text{ ms} \le D = 12 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 6 —** Compute the *timing margin* (useful design metric):

$$
\text{Margin} = D - t_{resp}^{W C} = 12 \text{ ms} - 9.5 \text{ ms} = 2.5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 7 —** State the verdict:

> The requirement *"The system shall respond within 12 ms"* is **satisfied** with a 2.5 ms safety margin. The design is feasible.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — CPU Utilization & Schedulability (Rate Monotonic)

### 3.2.1 Given
Three periodic tasks running on a bare-metal scheduler under **Rate-Monotonic** priority assignment:

| Task | Period $T_i$ (ms) | Compute Time $C_i$ (ms) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $\tau_1$ | 10 | 2 |
| $\tau_2$ | 25 | 4 |
| $\tau_3$ | 50 | 6 |

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 —** Apply the **Rate-Monotonic utilization bound** for $n=3$ tasks:

$$
U_{bound}(n=3) = n \cdot \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) = 3 \cdot \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right)
$$

**Step 2 —** Compute the bound numerically:

$$
2^{1/3} = \sqrt[3]{2} \approx 1.2599
$$

$$
U_{bound} = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 = 0.7797
$$

**Step 3 —** Compute the **actual** CPU utilization using the formula:

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}
$$

**Step 4 —** Substitute per task:

$$
U = \frac{2}{10} + \frac{4}{25} + \frac{6}{50}
$$

**Step 5 —** Evaluate each term:

$$
\frac{2}{10} = 0.2000
$$

$$
\frac{4}{25} = 0.1600
$$

$$
\frac{6}{50} = 0.1200
$$

**Step 6 —** Sum:

$$
U = 0.2000 + 0.1600 + 0.1200 = 0.4800
$$

**Step 7 —** Compare:

$$
U = 0.4800 \le U_{bound} = 0.7797 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 8 —** Verdict:

> The task set is **guaranteed schedulable** under Rate-Monotonic scheduling. The design meets the real-time deadline requirement.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Battery Life from Power Budget

### 3.3.1 Given
- Battery capacity: $C_{bat} = 1200 \text{ mAh}$
- Active current: $I_{active} = 25 \text{ mA}$
- Sleep current: $I_{sleep} = 0.05 \text{ mA}$
- Duty cycle: $\alpha = 1\%$ (active 1% of the time, typical IoT sensor node)

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 —** Apply the **average current** formula:

$$
I_{avg} = I_{active} \cdot \alpha + I_{sleep} \cdot (1 - \alpha)
$$

**Step 2 —** Substitute:

$$
I_{avg} = 25 \cdot 0.01 + 0.05 \cdot 0.99
$$

**Step 3 —** Evaluate:

$$
I_{avg} = 0.250 + 0.0495 = 0.2995 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 4 —** Apply the **battery life** formula:

$$
L = \dfrac{C_{bat}}{I_{avg}}
$$

**Step 5 —** Substitute:

$$
L = \frac{1200}{0.2995}
$$

**Step 6 —** Evaluate:

$$
L \approx 4006.7 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 7 —** Convert to more intuitive units:

$$
L = 4006.7 \text{ hours} \approx 166.9 \text{ days} \approx 5.5 \text{ months}
$$

**Step 8 —** Verdict:

> The NFR *"The device shall operate for at least 6 months on a single charge"* is **not met** (5.5 months $< 6$ months). The designer must reduce active current, lower duty cycle, or select a larger battery.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Patterns

| Numerical Type | Core Formula | Typical Marks | Common Trap |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| Response-time feasibility | $t_{resp} = t_{sense} + t_{compute} + t_{actuate}$ | 3 | Forgetting to add interrupt jitter |
| CPU schedulability | $U = \sum C_i/T_i \le n(2^{1/n}-1)$ | 4–7 | Confusing $n$ (task count) with periods |
| Power budget / battery | $L = C_{bat} / I_{avg}$ | 3 | Using peak current instead of average |
| MTBF / Availability | $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$ | 3 | Inverting the formula |
| Nyquist sampling | $f_s \ge 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | 2 | Confusing $f_{max}$ with bandwidth |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every derivation above ended in a **decision** ("satisfies / does not satisfy the requirement"). In KTU board answers, the **decision sentence is mandatory** — it is the line the examiner ticks for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Requirements Engineering Process Flow

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **iterative requirements engineering lifecycle** as expected in KTU board answers. It maps the five core activities (Elicit → Analyze → Specify → Validate → Manage) with feedback loops to the next design phase.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Stakeholders and Domain Experts] --> A1[Elicitation: Interviews and Observations]
    A1 --> A2[Analysis and Negotiation: MoSCoW Prioritization]
    A2 --> A3[Specification: Formal SRS Document]
    A3 --> A4[Validation: Reviews and Prototyping]
    A4 --> A5[Management: Requirements Traceability Matrix]
    A5 --> A6[Approved Requirement Baseline]
    A6 --> B1[Architecture Design Phase]
    A4 -->|Failure: Return to Elicitation| A1
    A5 -->|Change Request| A2
    A6 --> C1[Embedded System Implementation]
```

**How to read the diagram:**
- The **forward arrows** show the standard flow of a new project.
- The **two feedback loops** (A4 → A1 and A5 → A2) model the reality that validation failures and change requests cause iteration — a key talking point in KTU 14-mark answers.

## 4.2 Requirements Hierarchy — From User Need to Test Case

This block-level diagram maps the **layered decomposition** of requirements as taught in the KTU 2024 Scheme Embedded System Design syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[USER REQUIREMENTS: Natural language stakeholder needs]
    L2[SYSTEM REQUIREMENTS: Whole system functional and non-functional behavior]
    L3[HARDWARE REQUIREMENTS: MCU selection, peripherals, sensor interfacing]
    L4[SOFTWARE REQUIREMENTS: Firmware modules, RTOS tasks, state machines]
    L5[INTERFACE REQUIREMENTS: Bus protocols, signal levels, connector pinouts]
    L6[TEST CASES: Verification of each requirement with pass or fail criteria]
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L2 --> L4
    L3 --> L5
    L4 --> L5
    L3 --> L6
    L4 --> L6
    L5 --> L6
```

**Reading guide:**
- Each level refines the level above it; the *vertical arrows* are the standard KTU traceability path.
- *Horizontal flow* at the bottom (L3, L4, L5 → L6) shows that **both** hardware and software elements must produce test cases that prove the requirement is met.

## 4.3 Functional vs Non-Functional Requirements — Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R[Embedded System Requirements]
    R --> FR[Functional Requirements]
    R --> NFR[Non-Functional Requirements]
    FR --> FR1[Sensing and I/O]
    FR --> FR2[Actuation and Control]
    FR --> FR3[State Machine Behavior]
    FR --> FR4[Communication Protocols]
    FR --> FR5[Data Processing and Algorithms]
    FR --> FR6[Fault Detection and Recovery]
    NFR --> NFR1[Performance and Timing]
    NFR --> NFR2[Power and Energy]
    NFR --> NFR3[Cost and Size]
    NFR --> NFR4[Reliability and Safety]
    NFR --> NFR5[Security and Compliance]
    NFR --> NFR6[Environmental Tolerance]
```

**Reading guide:**
- This **two-branch classification tree** is the exact mental model KTU expects students to draw (or describe) for 7-mark questions on *"Classify the requirements of a [given] embedded system."*

## 4.4 Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM) — Concept Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Requirement Source: Customer or Standard]
    REQ[Unique Requirement ID: REQ-001]
    DSGN[Design Element: Architecture Block]
    IMPL[Implementation: Code Module]
    TEST[Test Case ID: TC-001]
    VER[Verification Status: Pass or Fail]
    SRC --> REQ
    REQ --> DSGN
    DSGN --> IMPL
    IMPL --> TEST
    TEST --> VER
    VER -.->|Issue Detected| REQ
```

**Reading guide:**
- This is the **forward + backward traceability** model. The dotted feedback arrow `VER → REQ` represents *backward traceability* — when a test fails, the engineer can trace back to the exact requirement, design element, and code module to fix. This dual-traceability is the hallmark of an industry-grade RTM and is a frequent 7-mark KTU question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q:** Differentiate between **functional** and **non-functional** requirements in an embedded system. Give **one example** of each from a **washing-machine controller**.

**Model Answer:**

- **Functional Requirements (FR):** Define the *specific behavior* the system must perform — its inputs, outputs, control actions, and state transitions.
  - *Example:* "The controller shall start the wash cycle when the **Start/Stop** button is pressed and the door is closed."

- **Non-Functional Requirements (NFR):** Define the *quality attributes and constraints* — performance, power, size, cost, safety, reliability — under which the functional behavior must be delivered.
  - *Example:* "The controller shall consume less than **2 W** in standby mode to meet the **BEE 5-star** energy rating."

> Both FRs and NFRs must be **measurable and testable** to be valid.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q:** List the **SMART criteria** that a well-formed embedded system requirement must satisfy. Briefly explain any **two**.

**Model Answer:**

A well-formed requirement is **SMART**:

1. **S — Specific:** Targets a single, clearly defined behavior.
2. **M — Measurable:** Expressed with a quantitative value and unit.
3. **A — Attainable:** Technically and economically feasible.
4. **R — Relevant:** Linked to a real stakeholder need.
5. **T — Testable:** A pass/fail test can be constructed for it.

*Explanation of two:*
- **Measurable:** *"The LED shall blink at 2 Hz"* is measurable. *"The LED shall blink fast"* is not.
- **Testable:** A test bench can apply the input and verify the output, so a measurable value makes the requirement testable.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — Choice Option 1

**Q:** With a neat block diagram, describe the **requirements engineering process** for an embedded system. Explain each phase in detail and discuss how the **Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)** ensures quality.

**OR**

**Q:** Consider the design of a **battery-operated wearable heart-rate monitor**. Specify **at least six functional and six non-functional requirements**, and **justify** each requirement against the **SMART criteria**.

**Model Answer (Part B – Question A, 14 Marks):**

#### (a) The Five-Phase Requirements Engineering Process — 7 Marks

**Phase 1 — Requirements Elicitation:**
- Activities: Stakeholder interviews, end-user observation, market-benchmark study, regulatory review.
- Output: A *stakeholder needs document* in natural language.
- Example: For a heart-rate monitor, elicit from cardiologists, patients, and battery suppliers.

**Phase 2 — Requirements Analysis & Negotiation:**
- Activities: Categorize, detect conflicts (e.g., "high brightness" vs. "low power"), and prioritize using **MoSCoW** (Must / Should / Could / Won't).
- Output: A *prioritized and conflict-resolved* list.

**Phase 3 — Requirements Specification:**
- Activities: Convert natural language into the formal *"The system shall ..."* form, each with unique ID, rationale, and verification method.
- Output: A *Software Requirements Specification (SRS)* document.

**Phase 4 — Requirements Validation:**
- Activities: Formal reviews, walkthroughs, prototype validation, test-case derivation, model-based simulation.
- Output: A *Validation Report* signed by all stakeholders.

**Phase 5 — Requirements Management (Traceability):**
- Activities: Maintain a **Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)** linking every requirement → design element → code → test case. Handle change requests with impact analysis.

#### (b) Role of RTM in Quality Assurance — 7 Marks

- **Forward Traceability:** Requirement → Design → Code → Test ensures every requirement is implemented and tested.
- **Backward Traceability:** Test → Code → Design → Requirement ensures every test case traces to a valid requirement (no *gold-plating* features).
- **Change Impact Analysis:** When a customer asks to add a Bluetooth feature, the RTM reveals exactly which design blocks and tests must change.
- **Compliance Audit:** Standards like **ISO 26262** and **DO-178C** mandate complete RTM as a deliverable for certification.
- **Defect Reduction:** Industry studies report 30–50% reduction in field defects when RTM is enforced throughout the lifecycle.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Choice Option 2

**Q:** A **smart irrigation controller** is to be designed. It must read soil moisture from a sensor, decide whether to switch ON a water pump, and transmit data to a cloud server every 10 minutes. The system is **battery-powered** and operates in **outdoor temperatures from 0 °C to 60 °C**.

**(a)** Identify **five functional** and **five non-functional** requirements. Justify each with respect to the **SMART criteria**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the timing requirement *"The system shall transmit sensor data to the cloud every 10 minutes"*, derive whether the following task set is **schedulable** under Rate-Monotonic scheduling, and hence whether the requirement is met. **(7 Marks)**

| Task | Period $T_i$ (s) | Worst-Case Execution Time $C_i$ (s) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $\tau_1$ (Read sensor) | 2 | 0.4 |
| $\tau_2$ (Control pump) | 5 | 1.0 |
| $\tau_3$ (Cloud upload) | 10 | 2.0 |
| $\tau_4$ (Battery check) | 20 | 1.5 |

**Model Answer:**

#### (a) Requirements — 7 Marks

**Functional Requirements (5):**
1. *"The system shall read the soil-moisture ADC channel every **2 seconds**."* — SMART: Specific, Measurable (2 s), Testable.
2. *"The pump shall be switched ON when soil moisture  $< 30\%$  and OFF when  $> 70\%$."* — Specific threshold values make it measurable.
3. *"The system shall transmit sensor data to the cloud every **10 minutes**."* — Measurable period.
4. *"On battery voltage  $< 3.3$ V, the controller shall enter low-power mode and stop pump actuation."* — Safe, testable, specific.
5. *"On sensor failure (ADC stuck at 0 or 1023 for 5 cycles), the system shall log a fault and notify the cloud."* — Clear fault behavior.

**Non-Functional Requirements (5):**
1. *"The system shall operate continuously for **at least 12 months** on **2 × AA alkaline** cells."* — Measurable, testable by accelerated drain tests.
2. *"The enclosure shall be **IP65-rated** for outdoor dust and water-jet protection."* — Industry-standard metric.
3. *"The cloud-upload latency shall not exceed **5 s** under 3G connectivity."* — Measurable and testable.
4. *"Total BOM cost shall not exceed **₹1,200** at 10,000-unit volume."* — Measurable cost NFR.
5. *"The system shall operate across **0 °C to 60 °C** without performance degradation."* — Environmental NFR.

#### (b) Schedulability Check — 7 Marks

**Step 1 —** Compute the **Rate-Monotonic bound** for $n=4$ tasks:

$$
U_{bound}(n=4) = 4 \cdot \left( 2^{1/4} - 1 \right)
$$

**Step 2 —** Evaluate $2^{1/4}$:

$$
2^{1/4} = \sqrt[4]{2} \approx 1.1892
$$

**Step 3 —** Substitute:

$$
U_{bound} = 4 \cdot (1.1892 - 1) = 4 \cdot 0.1892 = 0.7568
$$

**Step 4 —** Compute the **actual** CPU utilization:

$$
U = \frac{0.4}{2} + \frac{1.0}{5} + \frac{2.0}{10} + \frac{1.5}{20}
$$

**Step 5 —** Evaluate each term:

$$
\frac{0.4}{2} = 0.2000
$$

$$
\frac{1.0}{5} = 0.2000
$$

$$
\frac{2.0}{10} = 0.2000
$$

$$
\frac{1.5}{20} = 0.0750
$$

**Step 6 —** Sum:

$$
U = 0.2000 + 0.2000 + 0.2000 + 0.0750 = 0.6750
$$

**Step 7 —** Compare:

$$
U = 0.6750 \le U_{bound} = 0.7568 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 8 —** Verdict:

> The task set is **guaranteed schedulable** under Rate-Monotonic scheduling. The 10-minute cloud-upload requirement is **feasible** with the given task set, leaving a 0.0818 (≈ 8.2 %) utilization margin for future feature additions.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on Requirements questions:**
> 1. **Writing "the system should be fast / small / cheap"** — these are *NOT* requirements. They are wishes. Always quantify with a number + unit.
> 2. **Mixing design with requirements** — *"The system shall use an STM32F4 microcontroller"* is a *design decision*, not a requirement. The requirement is *"The controller shall perform FIR filtering of 1 kHz audio with  $< 100 \mu s$  latency"*.
> 3. **Forgetting the closing "shall"** — the word *shall* indicates a mandatory requirement; *should* indicates a goal. Examiners specifically look for *shall* in SRS-style answers.
> 4. **Omitting the decision sentence** in numerical problems — the final *"the requirement is satisfied / not satisfied"* line is mandatory for full marks.
> 5. **Confusing $f_{max}$ with sampling rate** — sampling rate is $f_s \ge 2 \cdot f_{max}$ (Nyquist), not equal to it.
> 6. **Ignoring the duty cycle** in power-budget calculations — students often use peak active current instead of *average* current.
> 7. **No diagram in 14-mark answers** — a labelled block diagram of the requirements process is worth at least 2–3 marks by itself.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Requirements**

- ⭐ Requirements engineering is the **first and most critical** phase of the embedded design flow.
- ⭐ The two pillars are **Functional Requirements (FR)** and **Non-Functional Requirements (NFR)**.
- ⭐ The five-phase process is **Elicit → Analyze → Specify → Validate → Manage**.
- ⭐ Every well-formed requirement must satisfy **SMART** (Specific, Measurable, Attainable, Relevant, Testable) plus consistency, traceability, and unambiguity.
- ⭐ Use the verb **"shall"** for mandatory requirements and **"should"** for desirable ones.
- ⭐ Prioritize using **MoSCoW** (Must / Should / Could / Won't) to resolve conflicts.
- ⭐ The **Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)** links Requirement → Design → Code → Test in both directions (forward + backward).
- ⭐ Real-time NFRs are classified as **hard** (violation = system failure), **soft** (violation = quality loss), or **firm** (occasional misses tolerated).
- ⭐ **Worst-Case Response Time:** $t_{resp} = t_{sense} + t_{compute} + t_{actuate} + t_{jitter}$, must be $\le D$.
- ⭐ **Schedulability (Rate-Monotonic):** $U = \sum C_i/T_i \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$.
- ⭐ **Average current for battery life:** $I_{avg} = I_{active} \cdot \alpha + I_{sleep} \cdot (1 - \alpha)$, then $L = C_{bat} / I_{avg}$.
- ⭐ **MTBF / Availability:** $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$.
- ⭐ **Nyquist sampling rate:** $f_s \ge 2 \cdot f_{max}$.
- ⭐ Industry standards that mandate SRS/RTM: **ISO 26262** (automotive), **IEC 62304** (medical), **DO-178C** (aerospace).
- ⭐ Always close a numerical answer with a **decision sentence** ("the requirement is satisfied / not satisfied").
- ⭐ Always include a **block diagram** in 14-mark answers — it carries independent marks.
- ⭐ Avoid using words like *fast, small, cheap* without quantitative thresholds.
- ⭐ Memory footprint: $M_{foot} = M_{code} + M_{data} + M_{stack}$ — must fit MCU Flash + RAM budget.
- ⭐ Cost-per-unit: $CPU = (NRE + Mfg \cdot Volume) / Volume$ — drives product viability.
- ⭐ **Requirement changes are inevitable** — RTM is the single tool that makes change management safe.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET695 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Embedded System Architecture** refers to the **structured organization and inter-connection of hardware and software components** within an embedded device. It defines how the **processor, memory, input/output peripherals, and communication buses** are arranged, interfaced, and coordinated to execute dedicated functions under real-time, power, and resource constraints.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, the architecture of an embedded system is the **system-level blueprint** that determines:
- How **instructions are fetched, decoded, and executed**
- How **data flows** between the Central Processing Unit (CPU), memory, and peripherals
- How **hardware (HW) and software (SW)** are co-designed to meet functional and non-functional requirements
- How **interrupts, timers, and I/O** are managed

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 of PEAET695 focuses on the **hardware and software architecture of embedded systems**, including processor selection criteria, memory organization schemes, I/O interfacing, and bus standards. A clear understanding of architectural trade-offs is mandatory for both **Part A (3-mark)** and **Part B (14-mark)** university questions.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Human Body as an Embedded System**
>
> Imagine an **embedded system's architecture** as the anatomy of a **human body**:
>
> - **Brain (CPU/Processor):** Makes all decisions, processes instructions, and controls responses.
> - **Spinal Cord & Nerves (System Bus):** Carry signals between the brain and body parts. (e.g., AMBA bus, PCI bus)
> - **Memory (Long-term & Short-term):** Just as humans have **long-term memory (Hard disk/Flash)** and **short-term memory (RAM/Registers)**, embedded systems have **non-volatile** and **volatile memory**.
> - **Senses (Sensors/Input):** Eyes, ears, skin → Equivalent to **input peripherals** like ADC, GPIO, keypads.
> - **Limbs (Actuators/Output):** Hands, legs → Equivalent to **motors, LEDs, displays** controlled by output peripherals.
> - **Reflex Arc (Interrupt Controller):** Just as a hand instantly pulls back from a hot surface without consciously thinking, an **interrupt** allows the system to respond to urgent events **immediately**, bypassing normal execution flow.
>
> **The architecture is the wiring diagram of this body** — defining *how* every part is connected, how fast signals travel, and how the system reacts to stimuli.

---

## 1.3 Standard Architectural Metrics (KTU Board Focus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following architectural parameters are **recurrent KTU exam keywords**:
>
> - **Clock Frequency (f):** Measured in **MHz** or **GHz**; defines instruction execution speed.
> - **Word Length:** Typically **8-bit, 16-bit, 32-bit, or 64-bit**.
> - **MIPS (Million Instructions Per Second):** Performance metric.
> - **Power Dissipation (P):** Measured in **mW** or **W**; critical for battery-operated devices.
> - **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction):** Average clock cycles needed per instruction.
> - **Throughput:** Tasks completed per unit time.
> - **Latency:** Time delay between input and corresponding output.

---

## 1.4 Why Architecture Matters in Embedded Design

| Architectural Choice | Engineering Impact |
|---|---|
| **Processor Selection (MCU vs MPU vs DSP vs ASIC)** | Determines computational capability, cost, power |
| **Memory Hierarchy (Cache, RAM, Flash)** | Affects speed, storage, and boot time |
| **Bus Architecture (AMBA, PCI, I²C)** | Governs data transfer bandwidth and connectivity |
| **Interrupt vs Polling** | Defines real-time responsiveness |
| **Harvard vs Von Neumann** | Influences instruction fetch vs data access parallelism |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Layers of an Embedded System Architecture

The architecture of an embedded system can be decomposed into **five tightly-coupled layers**, each examined repeatedly in KTU 14-mark questions:

### Layer 1: Processor (CPU) Core
- **Function:** Executes instructions, performs arithmetic/logic operations, controls program flow.
- **Types of Processors used in Embedded Systems:**
  - **Microcontroller (MCU):** CPU + Memory + Peripherals on a single chip (e.g., 8051, ARM Cortex-M, PIC).
  - **Microprocessor (MPU):** External CPU; needs external memory (e.g., ARM Cortex-A in Raspberry Pi).
  - **Digital Signal Processor (DSP):** Optimized for signal processing (e.g., TMS320).
  - **Application-Specific Integrated Circuit (ASIC):** Custom-designed for one task.
  - **System on Chip (SoC):** Integrates CPU, GPU, memory, I/O on one die (e.g., Snapdragon, Exynos).

### Layer 2: Memory Subsystem
- **Volatile Memory (Loses data on power-off):**
  - **SRAM (Static RAM):** Fast, used in cache.
  - **DRAM (Dynamic RAM):** Slower, denser, used as main memory.
- **Non-Volatile Memory (Retains data):**
  - **ROM, PROM, EPROM, EEPROM**
  - **Flash Memory:** Used to store firmware in modern embedded devices.
- **Memory Hierarchy:** Registers → Cache → Main RAM → Secondary storage.

### Layer 3: Input/Output (I/O) Subsystem
- **Input Devices:** Sensors (temperature, pressure, light), keypads, switches, ADC.
- **Output Devices:** LEDs, LCDs, motors, DAC, relays.
- **I/O Interfacing Methods:**
  - **Polling:** CPU continuously checks device status.
  - **Interrupt-Driven:** Device notifies CPU only when service is needed.
  - **DMA (Direct Memory Access):** Data transfer without CPU intervention.

### Layer 4: Communication Buses & Interfaces
- **On-Chip Buses:** AMBA (AHB, APB, AXI) — used in ARM-based SoCs.
- **System Buses:** PCI, PCIe, VME.
- **Serial Peripherals:** UART, SPI, I²C, CAN, USB.
- **Wireless:** Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, ZigBee, LoRa.

### Layer 5: Software Stack
- **Firmware / Application Code**
- **RTOS (Real-Time Operating System):** FreeRTOS, VxWorks, QNX.
- **Device Drivers, BSP (Board Support Package), Middleware.**

---

## 2.2 Harvard vs Von Neumann Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **guaranteed KTU question** — appears almost every university exam cycle.

| Parameter | Von Neumann | Harvard |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory Structure** | Single shared memory for instructions & data | Separate memories for instructions & data |
| **Bus** | One common bus | Two separate buses |
| **Simultaneous Fetch/Access** | Not possible (bottleneck) | Possible (parallelism) |
| **Speed** | Slower (Von Neumann bottleneck) | Faster |
| **Complexity** | Simpler | More complex hardware |
| **Used in** | General-purpose PCs, x86 | DSPs, microcontrollers (8051, ARM Cortex-M) |
| **Example** | Intel x86 | ARM, AVR, PIC, 8051 |

### Modified Harvard Architecture
A **practical hybrid** used in modern processors (e.g., ARM Cortex-M, modern x86). It uses **separate caches** for instructions and data, but the **main memory is unified**. This eliminates the bottleneck while maintaining a simpler memory model.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical/derivation question.**

| # | Formula / Definition | Symbol Explanation | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{CPU Execution Time} = \dfrac{\text{Instruction Count} \times \text{CPI}}{f_{\text{clock}}}$ | $f_{\text{clock}}$ = clock frequency | seconds | Performance estimation |
| 2 | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{\text{clock}}}{\text{CPI} \times 10^6}$ | Million Instructions Per Second | MIPS | Processor speed rating |
| 3 | $\text{Amdahl's Law: } S = \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$ | $P$ = parallel portion, $N$ = speedup factor | dimensionless | Speedup from optimization |
| 4 | $\text{Power (Dynamic)} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^2 \cdot f$ | $\alpha$ = switching activity, $C$ = capacitance | Watts | Low-power design |
| 5 | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{latency}}}$ | Tasks per unit time | tasks/s | Real-time scheduling |
| 6 | $\text{Bus Bandwidth} = \dfrac{\text{Bus Width} \times f_{\text{bus}}}{N_{\text{cycles}}}$ | $N_{\text{cycles}}$ = cycles per transfer | bits/s or bytes/s | Data transfer rate |
| 7 | $\text{Memory Access Time (Avg)} = H \cdot T_{\text{cache}} + (1-H) \cdot T_{\text{main}}$ | $H$ = hit ratio | seconds | Memory hierarchy performance |
| 8 | $\text{Interrupt Latency} = T_{\text{detect}} + T_{\text{save}} + T_{\text{vector}}$ | Vector = ISR fetch time | cycles or μs | Real-time responsiveness |
| 9 | $\text{DMA Transfer Time} = \dfrac{N_{\text{bytes}}}{R_{\text{DMA}}}$ | $R_{\text{DMA}}$ = DMA data rate | seconds | Bulk data movement |
| 10 | $\text{Energy per Instruction} = P \cdot \text{CPI} \cdot T_{\text{clock}}$ | Power × Time | Joules | Battery life estimation |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does architecture matter in industry?**
>
> 1. **Automotive (ECU — Engine Control Unit):** Uses **Harvard architecture with cache** + **AUTOSAR-compliant layered software** for deterministic real-time response.
> 2. **IoT Edge Nodes (e.g., ESP32, nRF52):** Choose **low-power ARM Cortex-M cores** with **deep sleep modes** to extend battery life to **years**.
> 3. **Mobile SoCs (Snapdragon 8 Gen 3):** Combine **Cortex-A (application) + Cortex-M (always-on) + DSP + GPU** using **AMBA AXI bus** for heterogeneous computing.
> 4. **Medical Implants (Pacemakers):** Demand **ultra-low power + deterministic interrupt handling + watchdog timers** for patient safety.
> 5. **Industrial PLCs:** Use **dual-bus (fieldbus + backplane)** for separating real-time I/O from management traffic.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: CPU Execution Time & Performance Trade-offs

The **CPU Execution Time (also called CPU Time)** is the actual time the processor spends computing a given program. It is the **most fundamental performance metric** in computer architecture.

### Step 1: Start with the basic time relation

The time taken by the CPU to execute a program is directly proportional to the **total number of clock cycles** required, and inversely proportional to the **clock frequency**.

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = \frac{N_{\text{cycles}}}{f_{\text{clock}}}
$$

where:
- $T_{\text{CPU}}$ = Total CPU execution time (seconds)
- $N_{\text{cycles}}$ = Total number of clock cycles required
- $f_{\text{clock}}$ = Clock frequency (cycles per second = Hz)

### Step 2: Expand the total cycles using CPI

The total number of cycles is the product of the **total number of instructions** and the **average cycles per instruction (CPI)**.

$$
N_{\text{cycles}} = \text{IC} \times \text{CPI}
$$

where:
- $\text{IC}$ = Instruction Count (total number of instructions in the program)
- $\text{CPI}$ = Average Cycles Per Instruction (a function of the instruction mix and CPU design)

### Step 3: Substitute back into the time equation

Combining both relationships:

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = \frac{\text{IC} \times \text{CPI}}{f_{\text{clock}}}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** To **reduce execution time**, an architect can:
> - **Reduce IC** → through better algorithms, compiler optimizations, or RISC instruction sets.
> - **Reduce CPI** → through pipelining, cache, or branch prediction.
> - **Increase $f_{\text{clock}}$** → through faster transistors, but at the cost of **higher power dissipation**.

### Step 4: Derive MIPS in terms of CPI

MIPS (Million Instructions Per Second) is the **inverse of execution time per million instructions**:

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{\text{IC}}{T_{\text{CPU}} \times 10^6}
$$

Substituting $T_{\text{CPU}}$:

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{\text{IC}}{\left(\dfrac{\text{IC} \times \text{CPI}}{f_{\text{clock}}}\right) \times 10^6}
$$

The IC cancels out (this shows MIPS is independent of the program size):

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{\text{clock}}}{\text{CPI} \times 10^6}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Higher clock frequency and lower CPI both **increase MIPS rating**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Amdahl's Law — The Speedup Ceiling

Amdahl's Law quantifies the **theoretical maximum speedup** achievable when only a *portion* of a system is improved.

### Step 1: Define the fractions

Let the **original execution time** be $T$. Partition it into:
- **Enhanced portion:** $P \cdot T$ (where $P$ is the fraction that is improved)
- **Unchanged portion:** $(1 - P) \cdot T$

### Step 2: Apply the speedup to the enhanced portion

If the enhanced portion is sped up by a factor of $N$:

$$
\text{New Enhanced Time} = \frac{P \cdot T}{N}
$$

### Step 3: Sum to get the new total time

$$
T_{\text{new}} = (1 - P) \cdot T + \frac{P \cdot T}{N}
$$

### Step 4: Compute the overall speedup

Speedup $S$ = Original time / New time:

$$
S = \frac{T}{T_{\text{new}}} = \frac{T}{(1 - P) \cdot T + \dfrac{P \cdot T}{N}}
$$

Cancel $T$:

$$
S = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}
$$

### Step 5: Examine the limit as $N \to \infty$

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} S = \frac{1}{1 - P}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the most important result:** No matter how fast you make the enhanced portion, the **overall speedup is bounded by $\dfrac{1}{1-P}$**.
>
> **Example:** If 80% of a program is parallelizable ($P = 0.8$), the **maximum possible speedup, even with infinite processors, is $\dfrac{1}{0.2} = 5\times$**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Architecture Selection Decision Table

> [!TIP]
> **Use this matrix to answer KTU's "Compare and contrast" or "Justify the choice" type questions.**

| Selection Criterion | 8-bit MCU (8051) | 16-bit MCU (MSP430) | 32-bit MCU (ARM M0/M4) | DSP (TMS320) | MPU (ARM A-series) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Word Size** | 8-bit | 16-bit | 32-bit | 16/32-bit | 32/64-bit |
| **Clock Speed** | 12–50 MHz | 16–25 MHz | 50–200 MHz | 100–1000 MHz | 500–3000 MHz |
| **Power** | Low | Ultra-low | Low–Medium | Medium | High |
| **Typical Use** | Toys, remotes | Watches, sensors | IoT, wearables | Audio, motor control | Smartphones, gateways |
| **OS Support** | Bare-metal | Tiny OS / No | FreeRTOS, Zephyr | DSP/BIOS | Linux, Android |
| **Cost (approx.)** | \$0.50 | \$1 | \$2–\$5 | \$5–\$20 | \$10–\$100 |
| **Architecture** | Harvard | Von Neumann (modified) | Modified Harvard | Harvard | Modified Harvard |
| **Math Capability** | 8-bit ops | 16-bit ops | 32-bit + FPU (M4) | MAC, FFT | 64-bit + SIMD |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3: Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)

For a hierarchical memory system with a cache and main memory, the **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)** is derived as follows.

### Step 1: Define the hit ratio

Let $H$ = cache **hit ratio** (probability that the requested data is found in cache). Then $(1 - H)$ is the **miss ratio**.

### Step 2: Define the access times

- $T_{\text{cache}}$ = access time of cache (e.g., 5 ns)
- $T_{\text{main}}$ = access time of main memory (e.g., 100 ns)
- $T_{\text{miss penalty}}$ = extra time to fetch from main memory on a miss

### Step 3: Formulate the equation

Each memory access is either a **hit** (data in cache) or a **miss** (must go to main memory):

$$
T_{\text{avg}} = H \cdot T_{\text{cache}} + (1 - H) \cdot T_{\text{main}}
$$

> **Insight:** Increasing $H$ even by a small amount dramatically reduces $T_{\text{avg}}$, which is why **cache hit ratio is the single most important performance lever** in memory architecture design.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Question:** A processor runs at **$f_{\text{clock}} = 200 \text{ MHz}$** with an **average CPI of 1.5**. A program has **$10^7$ instructions**. Calculate:
> 1. CPU execution time
> 2. MIPS rating
> 3. If 30% of instructions are memory access with CPI = 3, and remaining 70% have CPI = 1, what is the **weighted average CPI**?

### Solution:

**Part (1): CPU Execution Time**

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = \frac{\text{IC} \times \text{CPI}}{f_{\text{clock}}} = \frac{10^7 \times 1.5}{200 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^7}{2 \times 10^8} = 0.075 \text{ seconds} = 75 \text{ ms}
$$

**Part (2): MIPS Rating**

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{\text{clock}}}{\text{CPI} \times 10^6} = \frac{200 \times 10^6}{1.5 \times 10^6} = 133.33 \text{ MIPS}
$$

**Part (3): Weighted Average CPI**

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{avg}} = (0.30 \times 3) + (0.70 \times 1) = 0.9 + 0.7 = 1.6
$$

$$
T_{\text{CPU, new}} = \frac{10^7 \times 1.6}{200 \times 10^6} = 0.08 \text{ s} = 80 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The weighted CPI (1.6) is **higher** than the originally assumed CPI (1.5), leading to longer execution time. This illustrates why **memory-intensive programs** suffer even on fast processors — the **memory wall** is real.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Embedded System Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Power Supply Unit] --> B[Reset and Clock Circuit]
    B --> C[CPU Core]
    C --> D[Memory Controller]
    D --> E[ROM or Flash Firmware]
    D --> F[RAM Data Memory]
    C --> G[Interrupt Controller]
    C --> H[I-O Controller]
    H --> I[Input Peripherals]
    H --> J[Output Peripherals]
    C --> K[Communication Interfaces]
    K --> L[UART SPI I2C CAN USB]
    C --> M[Timers and Counters]
    M --> N[Watchdog Timer]
    O[Buses Address Data Control] -.-> C
    O -.-> D
    O -.-> H

    classDef cpuClass fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef memClass fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ioClass fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef busClass fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000,stroke-dasharray: 5 5

    class C,G,M cpuClass
    class E,F memClass
    class I,J,L ioClass
    class O busClass
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The **CPU Core** is the heart. The **System Bus (Address + Data + Control lines)** interconnects it with memory and I/O. **Power, Reset, and Clock** are foundational — every other block depends on them.

---

## 4.2 Harvard vs Von Neumann Architecture — Side-by-Side Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph V1[Von Neumann Architecture]
        V1A[CPU] --> V1B[Single Memory]
        V1B --> V1A
        V1A --> V1C[Single Bus]
        V1C --> V1B
    end

    subgraph V2[Harvard Architecture]
        V2A[CPU] --> V2B[Instruction Memory]
        V2A --> V2C[Data Memory]
        V2D[Instruction Bus] --> V2A
        V2D --> V2B
        V2E[Data Bus] --> V2A
        V2E --> V2C
    end

    classDef vonClass fill:#FFCCCB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef harvClass fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

    class V1A,V1B,V1C vonClass
    class V2A,V2B,V2C,V2D,V2E harvClass
```

> **Key takeaway:** Von Neumann has a **single bottleneck**; Harvard has **dual parallel pathways** allowing simultaneous instruction fetch + data access.

---

## 4.3 AMBA Bus Architecture (ARM-based SoC)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[ARM Processor Core] --> B[AHB or AXI High Performance Bus]
    B --> C[DMA Controller]
    B --> D[External Memory Controller]
    D --> E[DDR RAM]
    B --> F[AHB to APB Bridge]
    F --> G[APB Low Power Bus]
    G --> H1[UART Peripheral]
    G --> H2[SPI Peripheral]
    G --> H3[I2C Peripheral]
    G --> H4[GPIO Peripheral]
    G --> H5[Timers]
    B --> I[Interrupt Controller GIC]

    classDef armClass fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ahbClass fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef apbClass fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px

    class A,I armClass
    class B,C,D,F ahbClass
    class G,H1,H2,H3,H4,H5 apbClass
```

> **KTU Note:** AMBA = **Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture**. AHB/APB/AXI are the standard buses in ARM-based embedded SoCs. **AXI** supports high-speed burst transfers; **APB** is for low-bandwidth peripherals.

---

## 4.4 Interrupt-Driven I/O Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as CPU Core
    participant ISR as Interrupt Service Routine
    participant PER as Peripheral Device

    Note over CPU,PER: Normal program execution ongoing
    CPU->>CPU: Execute main program
    PER-->>CPU: Interrupt Request IRQ
    CPU->>CPU: Finish current instruction
    CPU->>CPU: Save context to stack
    CPU->>ISR: Jump to ISR vector
    ISR->>PER: Acknowledge and read data
    ISR->>CPU: Process interrupt
    CPU->>CPU: Restore context from stack
    CPU->>CPU: Resume main program
```

> **Engineering insight:** The **latency** between IRQ assertion and ISR execution must be **bounded and minimal** in real-time systems. Use **NVIC (Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller)** in ARM Cortex-M for prioritized, low-latency interrupts.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Harvard and Von Neumann architectures. Which one is preferred for modern embedded systems and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Von Neumann | Harvard |
|---|---|---|
| Memory | Single shared memory | Separate instruction & data memory |
| Bus | Single common bus | Separate buses |
| Speed | Bottleneck due to shared bus | Faster — parallel access |
| Complexity | Simpler | More complex hardware |
| Examples | x86 PC processors | ARM Cortex-M, 8051, DSPs |

**Preferred for modern embedded systems:** **Modified Harvard architecture** (used in ARM Cortex-M, modern microcontrollers). It offers **parallel instruction and data access** (improving throughput) while keeping a unified main memory, balancing **speed and flexibility** for resource-constrained embedded applications.

*(Marks: 1 — tabulation; 1 — examples; 1 — justification of preference)*

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define MIPS and CPI. How are they related to CPU execution time?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **MIPS (Million Instructions Per Second):** A performance metric measuring how many millions of instructions a processor can execute per second.

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{\text{clock}}}{\text{CPI} \times 10^6}
$$

- **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction):** The average number of clock cycles the CPU requires to execute each instruction.

**Relation to CPU Execution Time:**

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = \frac{\text{IC} \times \text{CPI}}{f_{\text{clock}}}
$$

Higher MIPS ⇒ faster processor. Lower CPI ⇒ fewer cycles per instruction ⇒ faster execution. **Architectural improvements (pipelining, caching) reduce CPI, increasing MIPS and reducing $T_{\text{CPU}}$.**

*(Marks: 1 — definitions; 1 — formula; 1 — relationship explained)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question B1 — Option A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **general architecture of an embedded system**. Discuss the role of each block in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Harvard architecture in detail**. How does it differ from the **Von Neumann architecture**? Justify which is more suitable for real-time DSP applications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution (a):** General Embedded System Architecture (7 marks)

**Block Diagram:**

```
                     ┌─────────────────────┐
                     │   POWER SUPPLY (V)  │
                     └──────────┬──────────┘
                                │
                     ┌──────────▼──────────┐
        ┌────────────┤   RESET & CLOCK      ├────────────┐
        │            └──────────┬──────────┘            │
        │                       │                       │
        │            ┌──────────▼──────────┐            │
        │            │      CPU CORE        │            │
        │            │  (ALU + CU + Regs)   │            │
        │            └──────────┬──────────┘            │
        │                       │                       │
        │            ┌──────────▼──────────┐            │
        │            │   SYSTEM BUS (A/D/C) │            │
        │            └──────────┬──────────┘            │
        │      ┌────────────────┼────────────────┐      │
        │      │                │                │      │
        ▼      ▼                ▼                ▼      ▼
   ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌──────────┐
   │   ROM / │  │  RAM    │  │   I/O   │  │ COMM.    │
   │  Flash  │  │         │  │ Periph. │  │ Ports    │
   └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └──────────┘
```

**Role of each block (1 mark per block, 2 marks for diagram = 7):**

1. **Power Supply Unit (PSU):** Provides regulated DC voltage (e.g., **3.3 V or 5 V**) to all components. Battery-operated systems add regulators, brown-out detectors, and power management ICs.

2. **Reset & Clock Circuit:** Generates the **system clock** (e.g., crystal oscillator at **12 MHz / 16 MHz / 200 MHz**) that synchronizes all operations. Reset circuit initializes CPU registers to known states on power-up.

3. **CPU Core:** The **brain** — fetches instructions, decodes them, and executes via the **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** and **Control Unit (CU)**. Includes **program counter, instruction register, accumulator**, and **general-purpose registers**.

4. **Memory Subsystem:**
   - **ROM/Flash:** Stores the **firmware / program code** (non-volatile).
   - **RAM:** Stores **runtime data, stack, heap** (volatile).

5. **I/O Peripherals:** Interface with the external world — **GPIO, ADC, DAC, timers, PWM**.

6. **Communication Interfaces:** **UART, SPI, I²C, CAN, USB** — for inter-device communication.

7. **System Bus (Address, Data, Control):** The **highway** carrying instructions, addresses, and control signals among CPU, memory, and peripherals.

---

#### **Solution (b):** Harvard vs Von Neumann for DSP (7 marks)

**Harvard Architecture Explanation (3 marks):**
- Uses **physically separate memories and buses** for instructions and data.
- **Simultaneous** instruction fetch and data access → eliminates the "Von Neumann bottleneck."
- **Faster execution** of predictable, repetitive operations.
- Used in **DSPs (TMS320), microcontrollers (8051, ARM Cortex-M), and RISC processors.**

**Von Neumann Architecture Explanation (2 marks):**
- Uses a **single shared memory and bus** for both instructions and data.
- At any given cycle, the CPU can either fetch an instruction **or** read/write data — **not both**.
- Simpler, cheaper, used in **general-purpose PCs (x86)**.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Parameter | Von Neumann | Harvard |
|---|---|---|
| Memory | Unified | Separate |
| Bus | Single | Dual |
| Parallelism | None | Yes |
| Speed | Slower | Faster |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

**Justification for DSP (1 mark):**
DSP algorithms (FFT, FIR filters) involve **continuous data streams with predictable access patterns**. Harvard's **parallel instruction + data access** allows **high-throughput pipelined MAC (Multiply-Accumulate) operations** essential for real-time signal processing, making it the **preferred choice**.

---

### **Question B1 — Option B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **memory architecture of an embedded system**. Explain the **memory hierarchy** with a diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **AMBA bus architecture** used in ARM-based SoCs. Differentiate between **AHB, APB, and AXI** buses. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution (a):** Memory Architecture (7 marks)

**Memory Hierarchy (5 marks + 2 for diagram):**

```
  ┌──────────────────────────┐
  │  REGISTERS (fastest)     │   ← few bytes, ~1 cycle
  │  CPU-internal            │
  ├──────────────────────────┤
  │  CACHE (L1, L2)          │   ← KBs to few MBs, ~5-20 cycles
  │  Optional, high-speed    │
  ├──────────────────────────┤
  │  MAIN MEMORY (RAM)       │   ← MBs to GBs, ~100 cycles
  │  Working data & stack    │
  ├──────────────────────────┤
  │  SECONDARY (Flash/ROM)   │   ← MBs to GBs, ~1000+ cycles
  │  Firmware, logs          │
  └──────────────────────────┘
```

**Explanation:**
1. **Registers:** Fastest storage, located inside the CPU. Used for **immediate operands and ALU operations** (e.g., R0–R12 in ARM).
2. **Cache Memory:** Small, high-speed SRAM. **L1** is inside the core; **L2** is external but on-chip. Uses **locality of reference** (temporal + spatial).
3. **Main Memory (RAM):** Volatile, large. Holds the **active program, variables, stack, and heap** during execution.
4. **Secondary Storage (Flash/ROM):** Non-volatile. Stores **firmware, boot code, and configuration data**.

**Design Trade-offs:**
- **Faster memory = more expensive, less capacity.**
- **Memory hierarchy balances speed, cost, and capacity** by placing frequently accessed data in faster levels.

---

#### **Solution (b):** AMBA Bus Architecture (7 marks)

**AMBA Overview (2 marks):**
AMBA (**Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture**) is ARM's open-standard **on-chip bus protocol** for connecting processors, memory, and peripherals in SoCs.

**Buses Explained (4 marks):**

| Bus | Full Form | Purpose | Speed | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **AHB** | Advanced High-performance Bus | High-speed transfers between CPU, memory, DMA | Medium | Embedded CPUs, on-chip SRAM |
| **APB** | Advanced Peripheral Bus | Low-power, simple peripheral access | Low | UART, GPIO, timers, keypads |
| **AXI** | Advanced eXtensible Interface | High-bandwidth, burst-based, pipelined | Very high | DDR memory, high-speed DMA, GPUs |

**Bridge Architecture (1 mark):**
Modern SoCs use a **bridge** (AHB-to-APB bridge) to connect high-speed AHB/AXI to low-power APB peripherals, achieving **both performance and energy efficiency**.

**Key Features of AXI (over AHB):**
- Separate **read/write channels** (parallel read & write)
- **Out-of-order transaction completion**
- **Burst transfers** for high throughput
- **QoS (Quality of Service) signaling** for traffic prioritization

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "Harvard" with "Modified Harvard"** in 14-mark questions. Modified Harvard is the actual architecture used in **ARM Cortex-M** — not pure Harvard. Examiners **deduct 1 mark** for this error.
> 2. **AMBA bus acronyms** (AHB, APB, AXI) must be **fully expanded** at least once in your answer. Writing only acronyms loses **1 mark**.
> 3. **In numerical problems, always show unit cancellation** (e.g., $\frac{10^7 \times 1.5}{200 \times 10^6} = 0.075 \text{ s}$). Skipping units costs **0.5 mark**.
> 4. **Block diagrams must be labeled** — arrows without labels (e.g., Address, Data, Control) lose **1 mark**.
> 5. **Avoid vague terms** like "fast memory" — specify "**SRAM-based cache with 5 ns access time**" instead.
> 6. **For real-time questions**, always mention **determinism, interrupt latency, and worst-case execution time (WCET)** — these are KTU's magic keywords.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Architecture**

- [x] **Embedded system architecture** = organized structure of HW + SW components to perform a dedicated function.
- [x] **Five layers:** Processor Core → Memory → I/O → Communication Buses → Software Stack.
- [x] **Von Neumann:** single memory, single bus → bottleneck → used in general-purpose CPUs.
- [x] **Harvard:** separate instruction + data memories → parallel access → used in MCUs, DSPs.
- [x] **Modified Harvard** (ARM Cortex-M) = **most common** in modern embedded — examiners expect this distinction.
- [x] **CPU Execution Time:** $T_{\text{CPU}} = \dfrac{\text{IC} \times \text{CPI}}{f_{\text{clock}}}$
- [x] **MIPS:** $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{\text{clock}}}{\text{CPI} \times 10^6}$
- [x] **Amdahl's Law:** $S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$ — **max speedup is $\dfrac{1}{1-P}$**.
- [x] **Memory hierarchy:** Registers → Cache → RAM → Flash/ROM (fastest to slowest).
- [x] **AMBA buses:** AHB (high-perf), APB (low-power peripherals), AXI (high-bandwidth, burst).
- [x] **DMA** offloads bulk data transfer from CPU → improves throughput.
- [x] **Interrupt-driven I/O** beats polling for **real-time responsiveness** and **power efficiency**.
- [x] **Processor selection criteria:** word size, clock, power, peripherals, cost, OS support.
- [x] **AMAT:** $T_{\text{avg}} = H \cdot T_{\text{cache}} + (1 - H) \cdot T_{\text{main}}$.
- [x] **Dynamic Power:** $P = \alpha C V_{dd}^2 f$ — **voltage reduction** is the most effective low-power lever.
- [x] **SoC** = integration of CPU + memory + peripherals on a **single die** — industry standard today.
- [x] **Real-time constraints** demand **deterministic buses, bounded interrupt latency, and WCET analysis**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Operating System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET695 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Selection of Operating System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Embedded System Design**, the **Selection of an Operating System (OS)** is the systematic engineering process of evaluating, comparing, and choosing the most appropriate system software layer (kernel, scheduler, drivers, middleware, and API stack) that will arbitrate hardware resources and execute application tasks on a target embedded platform. The decision directly influences **system determinism, footprint, power budget, certification path, licensing cost, and time-to-market**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Snapshot:** An embedded operating system is a *resource-constrained, application-specific* software platform that provides a controlled execution environment, deterministic scheduling, and well-defined hardware abstraction. **Selection is never a one-size-fits-all decision**—it is an optimization across competing engineering constraints.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Kitchen" Model

Imagine you are opening a restaurant:

- **The Kitchen** = Your embedded hardware (CPU, RAM, Flash, peripherals)
- **The Chef** = Your application code (firmware, drivers, business logic)
- **The Kitchen Manager (Head Chef)** = The Operating System

Now, the question is: *Which type of Kitchen Manager do you hire?*

| Restaurant Type | OS Analogy | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| A small home-cooked meal | **Bare-Metal (Super Loop)** | Chef does everything alone, no manager. |
| A busy fast-food counter | **RTOS (e.g., FreeRTOS)** | Manager pre-assigns exact time slots. Orders come out **predictably, within a strict window**. |
| A fine-dining multi-cuisine restaurant | **GPOS (e.g., Embedded Linux)** | Manager is highly flexible, can re-plan, serve many guests, but timing is "best effort". |
| A heart-pacemaker factory kitchen | **Hard Real-Time OS (QNX, VxWorks)** | Manager **must** deliver each course in a medically critical time. Failure = death. |

## 1.3 The Three Fundamental Classes of Embedded OS

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Three Categories You MUST Memorize:**

1. **General Purpose Operating System (GPOS):** Optimized for *fairness* and *throughput*, NOT for hard timing. Examples: Embedded Linux, Windows IoT, Android Things.
2. **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS):** Optimized for *determinism* and *bounded latency*. Soft RTOS tolerates occasional deadline misses (e.g., audio/video streaming). Examples: FreeRTOS, Contiki, RIOT.
3. **Hard Real-Time Operating System:** **Zero tolerance** for deadline misses. Required for safety-critical systems. Examples: VxWorks (DO-178C certified), QNX Neutrino, RTEMS, LynxOS-178.

## 1.4 Key Selection Metrics (Bold Constants to Memorize)

When the examiner asks "What are the criteria?", the following metrics form the **canonical checklist**:

- **ROM/RAM Footprint** — measured in **Kilobytes (KB)** for RTOS, **Megabytes (MB)** for GPOS.
- **Scheduling Latency** — measured in **microseconds (µs)** for hard RTOS, **milliseconds (ms)** for GPOS.
- **Interrupt Response Time** — typically **< 10 µs** in certified RTOS.
- **Power Consumption** — measured in **mW**; tick-less idle is the modern baseline.
- **License Cost (NRE + Royalty)** — **$0** (GPL/MIT) vs **$10k–$500k+** (commercial).
- **Context Switch Time** — typically **< 5 µs** for a good RTOS kernel.
- **Certification Readiness** — DO-178C, IEC 61508, ISO 26262, IEC 62304.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Examiner Tip:** If your answer to a 14-mark question lists fewer than **6 distinct selection criteria**, you are under-scoring. Always structure your answer as: *Performance + Footprint + Real-Time Capability + Power + Cost + Ecosystem + Certification*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven-Pillar Decision Framework

Operating system selection in embedded design is governed by **seven analytical pillars**. Each pillar contributes a quantitative or qualitative filter that narrows the feasible OS space.

### Pillar 1 — Hardware Resource Constraints
The OS binary, kernel data structures, and runtime stack must fit inside the target's memory map. The governing inequality is:

$$M_{\text{available}} \geq M_{\text{kernel}} + M_{\text{drivers}} + M_{\text{app}} + M_{\text{heap}}$$

Where:
- $M_{\text{available}}$ = Total Flash (for code) or RAM (for runtime) on the MCU/MPU.
- $M_{\text{kernel}}$ = ROM footprint of the OS itself (FreeRTOS ~ 10 KB, Embedded Linux ~ 2 MB, VxWorks ~ 100 KB).
- $M_{\text{drivers}}$ = BSP + peripheral drivers.
- $M_{\text{app}}$ = Application binary size.
- $M_{\text{heap}}$ = Dynamic memory region for `malloc`, queues, semaphores.

### Pillar 2 — Real-Time Determinism
Determinism is mathematically modeled as **worst-case execution time (WCET)** and **worst-case response time (WCRT)**.

For a periodic task $\tau_i$ under Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS), the **Liu & Layland bound** states that the task set is schedulable if:

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$$

Where:
- $C_i$ = WCET of task $i$.
- $T_i$ = Period of task $i$.
- $n$ = Number of tasks.
- The right-hand side bound $n(2^{1/n}-1)$ converges to **≈ 0.693** (i.e., 69.3% utilization) as $n \to \infty$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 69.3% matters:** If your total CPU utilization exceeds 69.3% under RMS, you **must** either (a) switch to Earliest Deadline First (EDF), (b) move to a hard RTOS with priority inheritance, or (c) re-architect. The KTU examiner LOVES this number.

### Pillar 3 — Power & Energy Profile
For battery-operated IoT nodes, the average power is given by:

$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\sum_{k} P_k \cdot t_k}{T_{\text{cycle}}}$$

Where $P_k$ is power in mode $k$ (active, idle, sleep, deep-sleep) and $t_k$ is the dwell time. Modern **tick-less RTOS kernels** (FreeRTOS `configUSE_TICKLESS_IDLE`, Zephyr) achieve deep-sleep currents of **< 1 µA** by halting the SysTick during idle.

### Pillar 4 — Licensing & Commercial Model
| License | Examples | Royalty? | Source Available? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MIT / BSD** | FreeRTOS (now MIT), Contiki-NG | No | Yes |
| **GPL v2** | Linux kernel | No (distribution triggers copyleft) | Yes |
| **LGPL** | glibc, some Qt modules | No | Yes |
| **Apache 2.0** | Zephyr, TensorFlow Lite | No | Yes |
| **Commercial** | VxWorks, QNX, ThreadX/Azure RTOS | **Yes** | No |

> [!WARNING]
> **Licensing Trap:** Shipping a Linux-based product triggers **GPL**. You *must* release your kernel modifications. Closed-source commercial code must be kept strictly in user-space. Mixing GPL and proprietary code in a single binary is a **copyright violation**.

### Pillar 5 — Certification & Safety Standards
| Domain | Standard | Required OS Class |
|---|---|---|
| Avionics | DO-178C | Hard RTOS (Level A/B) |
| Automotive | ISO 26262 (ASIL-D) | Certified RTOS |
| Medical | IEC 62304 (Class C) | Validated RTOS |
| Industrial | IEC 61508 (SIL-3/4) | Certified RTOS |
| Consumer | None | Any |

### Pillar 6 — Peripheral & Connectivity Stack
Evaluate whether the OS provides **out-of-the-box**:
- TCP/IP (lwIP, mbedTLS)
