- **Debug protocols**: **JTAG** (4-wire, IEEE 1149.1) vs **SWD** (2-wire, ARM-specific).
- **Cross-compiler triplet**: `arm-none-eabi-gcc` = ARM / no-vendor / no-OS / EABI.
- **Linker script (`.ld`)** maps `.text` → Flash and `.data`/`.bss` → SRAM.
- **Duty-cycle equation** for battery life: $\bar{P} = \alpha P_{\text{act}} + (1-\alpha) P_{\text{sleep}}$.
- **Startup file** copies `.data` from Flash to SRAM and zeroes `.bss` before `main()`.
- **Board cost is *not* the primary criterion** — KTU examiners reward *justified* selection based on performance, power, and peripherals.
- **Always mention the debug probe** in diagrams — forgetting it loses 1 mark.
- **Always state units** (MHz, KB, mW, µA) — missing units cost marks even if the number is correct.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programming Language and Coding Issues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET695 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Programming Language and Coding Issues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — Programming Language for Embedded Systems

A **programming language** in the context of embedded systems is a formally structured lexical and syntactic notation used to express computational instructions that are translated (compiled, assembled, or interpreted) into machine-executable code targeting a specific microcontroller, microprocessor, or System-on-Chip (SoC). Unlike general-purpose computing, the embedded programming language must be **resource-aware**, **deterministic**, **real-time compliant**, and produce code with tightly controlled **memory footprint** and **execution latency**.

### 1.2 Formal Definition — Coding Issues

**Coding issues** in embedded systems refer to the engineering challenges, pitfalls, and constraints that arise when software is developed for resource-limited, real-time, hardware-coupled targets. These include memory scarcity, timing determinism, hardware register volatility, interrupt-safe code construction, and architectural portability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 mandates understanding *both* the **language taxonomy** (Assembly, C, C++, Java, scripting languages) AND the **practical coding pitfalls** that emerge when writing firmware for constrained targets (e.g., 8-bit AVR, ARM Cortex-M, MSP430, PIC).

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Kitchen Metaphor"

Think of an embedded system as a **tiny, fixed-size kitchen on a moving train**:

| Kitchen Element | Embedded System Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Fixed countertop area (no expansion) | **ROM/Flash size** (code memory is fixed) |
| Small fridge (limited ingredients) | **RAM** (volatile data memory) |
| Cooking must finish before next station | **Real-time deadlines** |
| Power comes from a small battery | **Power budget** |
| Recipes are written on tiny index cards | **Source code size** |
| Chef must read raw recipe (machine code) | **Assembly language** |
| Chef can use a recipe book (English) | **High-level languages (C/C++)** |

Just as a chef must write recipes that fit on tiny cards, execute in tight time windows, and conserve fuel — an embedded programmer writes code that fits in tiny memory, runs within strict deadlines, and consumes minimum power. **Coding issues** are the "burnt meals" and "missed stations" that happen when the recipe is poorly written for the constraints.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** The examiner often tests whether you can identify *why* a particular language or coding technique is preferred for a given embedded constraint. Memorizing trade-offs (not just definitions) is the key to scoring beyond 60%.

### 1.4 Categories of Programming Languages for Embedded Targets

1. **Low-Level (Machine-oriented):**
   - **Assembly Language** — Mnemonic representation of CPU instructions.
   - **Machine Code** — Raw binary executed by the processor.

2. **Mid-Level / Systems Languages:**
   - **C** — The de-facto embedded language (universal, portable, deterministic).
   - **Embedded C** — C with extensions (named address spaces, interrupt keywords, bit-fields).

3. **High-Level / Object-Oriented:**
   - **C++** — Used in larger embedded systems (AUTOSAR, IoT edge nodes).
   - **Java** — Used in embedded JVMs (e.g., Java ME, Android Embedded).
   - **C# / .NET Micro** — Used in industrial IoT gateways.

4. **Scripting / Dynamic:**
   - **Python / MicroPython** — Used in prototyping and high-end embedded (Raspberry Pi, ESP32).
   - **JavaScript (Node.js, JerryScript)** — Used in IoT firmware.

5. **Hardware Description Languages (HDL):**
   - **VHDL, Verilog, SystemVerilog** — Used to *describe* hardware, not software (adjacent to embedded design).

6. **Domain-Specific / Modeling Languages:**
   - **MATLAB/Simulink** — Model-based code generation.
   - **UML/MARTE** — Architecture description.
   - **Alloy, Ada, Rust** — Safety-critical (aerospace, automotive).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Embedded Programming Language Trade-Space

When choosing a language, the engineer navigates **four opposing axes** (the **Embedded Trade-Space Diamond**):

$$
\text{Performance} \;\leftrightarrow\; \text{Productivity}
$$

$$
\text{Determinism} \;\leftrightarrow\; \text{Abstraction}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb:** Moving *up* the abstraction ladder (Assembly → C → C++ → Java → Python) **increases developer productivity** but **decreases deterministic control** over hardware and timing.

### 2.2 Detailed Language Profile — Advantages & Limitations

#### 2.2.1 Assembly Language
- **Advantages:**
  - 1:1 mapping with machine instructions → maximum control.
  - Zero compiler overhead → smallest, fastest code.
  - Direct register, flag, and stack manipulation.
- **Limitations:**
  - **Non-portable** — written for one specific CPU family.
  - **Verbose** — 5–10× more lines than equivalent C.
  - **Error-prone** — no type checking, manual memory management.
  - **Difficult to maintain** — large codebases become unmanageable.
- **Use Cases:** Bootloaders, ISRs (Interrupt Service Routines), DSP inner loops, cryptography kernels, real-time critical sections.

#### 2.2.2 C Language (The Embedded Standard)
- **Advantages:**
  - **Portable** across microcontroller families (with minor changes).
  - Generates efficient, predictable machine code.
  - Direct memory access via pointers.
  - Inline assembly support.
  - Large ecosystem of embedded libraries.
- **Limitations:**
  - No built-in OOP or namespace support.
  - Manual memory management → bug-prone.
  - No run-time safety checks.
- **Extensions:** `Embedded C` adds keywords like `interrupt`, `sfr`, `sbit`, `using`, `idata`, `pdata`, `xdata`, `code`.

#### 2.2.3 C++ Language
- **Advantages:**
  - **OOP, templates, RAII, namespaces** → modular large projects.
  - **Zero-cost abstractions** — high-level features with no runtime penalty.
  - `constexpr`, templates enable compile-time computation.
- **Limitations:**
  - **Code bloat** — exceptions, RTTI, virtual tables add overhead.
  - **Hidden memory allocations** (operator `new`, STL containers).
  - **Non-deterministic** — virtual dispatch timing varies.
- **Use Cases:** AUTOSAR automotive, robotics, high-end IoT, complex UI firmware.

#### 2.2.4 Java
- **Advantages:** Portability via JVM, garbage collection, rich libraries.
- **Limitations:** **JVM overhead** too heavy for small MCUs; non-deterministic GC pauses.
- **Use Cases:** Android-based embedded, smart cards (Java Card), set-top boxes.

#### 2.2.5 Python / MicroPython
- **Advantages:** Rapid prototyping, very readable.
- **Limitations:** **Interpreter overhead** (10–100× slower than C); high memory footprint.
- **Use Cases:** Raspberry Pi, ESP32 scripting layer, data analytics, test harnesses.

#### 2.2.6 HDLs (VHDL, Verilog)
- **Note:** These are **not software languages** but hardware description languages. They are mentioned because embedded design crosses the hardware/software boundary.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Metric Sheet

The table below contains the **must-memorize** engineering metrics and design formulas for Module 4.

| # | Metric / Formula | Description | Unit / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{ROM} \approx N_{\text{lines}} \times I_{\text{bytes/instr}}$ | Flash/code memory consumed | Bytes |
| 2 | $\text{RAM}_{\text{min}} \geq \sum(\text{Stack} + \text{Heap} + \text{Data})$ | Minimum RAM requirement | Bytes |
| 3 | $T_{\text{exec}} = N_{\text{cycles}} \times T_{\text{clk}}$ | Execution time for code block | Seconds |
| 4 | $T_{\text{clk}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{clk}}}$ | Clock period | Seconds |
| 5 | $f_{\text{clk}} = \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{N_{\text{div}}}$ | Effective CPU clock | Hz |
| 6 | $N_{\text{MIPS}} = \frac{f_{\text{clk}}}{10^6}$ | Million Instructions Per Second | MIPS |
| 7 | $C_{\text{avg}} = \sum p_i \, C_i$ | Average cycles per instruction (CPI) | Cycles/instr |
| 8 | $T_{\text{program}} = N_{\text{instr}} \times C_{\text{avg}} \times T_{\text{clk}}$ | Total program execution time | Seconds |
| 9 | $E_{\text{active}} = P_{\text{active}} \times t_{\text{active}}$ | Energy consumed in active mode | Joules |
| 10 | $\text{Code Size Ratio} = \frac{\text{HLL bytes}}{\text{Asm bytes}}$ | High-level-to-Assembly size ratio | 1.0 – 3.0 |
| 11 | $\text{Context-Switch Time} = t_{\text{save}} + t_{\text{restore}}$ | ISR entry/exit overhead | Cycles |
| 12 | $\text{Volatile Read Tax} \approx 2\times \text{normal read}$ | Extra cycles for volatile access | Cycles |
| 13 | $\text{Memory Latency} = t_{\text{access}} \times N_{\text{accesses}}$ | Total memory access delay | Seconds |
| 14 | $\text{Power}_{dyn} = \alpha C V_{dd}^2 f$ | Dynamic power (CMOS) | Watts |
| 15 | $\text{Speedup}_{HLL} = \frac{T_{\text{Asm}}}{T_{HLL}}$ | HLL performance vs Assembly | Ratio (≥ 1) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** Part A questions frequently test formulas (1), (2), (3), and (6) above. Part B (14-mark) questions test the qualitative *application* of these metrics in real trade-off scenarios.

### 2.4 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

- **Automotive (AUTOSAR):** Strict **MISRA-C** coding rules to prevent undefined behavior.
- **Aerospace (DO-178C):** Languages like **Ada** for certifiable code; assembly only for verified inner loops.
- **Medical (IEC 62304):** Static analysis and traceable code generation.
- **IoT (constrained nodes):** **C** with energy-aware coding — sleep modes, interrupt-driven I/O.
- **Smartphones / Edge AI:** **C++ with NEON intrinsics** for DSP workloads.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Execution Time of an Embedded Code Block

**Problem:** A sensor-reading routine contains $N_{\text{instr}} = 500$ instructions. The CPU runs at $f_{\text{clk}} = 16\,\text{MHz}$ with an average CPI of $C_{\text{avg}} = 1.5$. Calculate the execution time and confirm whether the routine fits inside a $100\,\mu\text{s}$ real-time deadline.

**Step 1 — Write the governing equation.**

$$
T_{\text{program}} = N_{\text{instr}} \times C_{\text{avg}} \times T_{\text{clk}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the clock period.**

$$
T_{\text{clk}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{clk}}} = \frac{1}{16 \times 10^{6}} = 62.5\,\text{ns}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute all values.**

$$
T_{\text{program}} = 500 \times 1.5 \times 62.5 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply step-by-step.**

$$
T_{\text{program}} = 750 \times 62.5 \times 10^{-9}
$$

$$
T_{\text{program}} = 46{,}875 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{s} = 46.875\,\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with the real-time deadline.**

$$
T_{\text{program}} = 46.875\,\mu\text{s} \;<\; 100\,\mu\text{s} = T_{\text{deadline}}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the headroom (slack time).**

$$
\Delta T = T_{\text{deadline}} - T_{\text{program}} = 100 - 46.875 = 53.125\,\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the CPU utilization.**

$$
U_{CPU} = \frac{T_{\text{program}}}{T_{\text{deadline}}} \times 100\% = \frac{46.875}{100} \times 100\% = 46.875\%
$$

> **Conclusion:** The routine executes in 46.875 μs, leaves a 53.125 μs slack, and uses 46.875% CPU — well within the deadline. The system is **not CPU-bound**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — RAM Budget Calculation

**Problem:** An embedded firmware has the following memory allocations:

- Global/static variables: **256 bytes**
- Stack (worst case, call depth = 8, frame = 32 bytes): **256 bytes**
- Heap (max simultaneous malloc): **512 bytes**
- I/O buffers (UART + ADC): **128 bytes**

Compute the minimum RAM required and check against a target MCU with **2 KB RAM**.

**Step 1 — Identify each contributor using the formula:**

$$
\text{RAM}_{\text{min}} \geq \text{Data} + \text{Stack} + \text{Heap} + \text{Buffers}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the stack requirement.**

$$
\text{Stack} = \text{Depth} \times \text{Frame}_{\text{size}} = 8 \times 32 = 256\,\text{bytes}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the components.**

$$
\text{RAM}_{\text{min}} = 256 + 256 + 512 + 128
$$

**Step 4 — Add them step-by-step.**

$$
256 + 256 = 512
$$

$$
512 + 512 = 1024
$$

$$
1024 + 128 = 1152\,\text{bytes}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert target RAM to bytes for comparison.**

$$
\text{RAM}_{\text{target}} = 2\,\text{KB} = 2 \times 1024 = 2048\,\text{bytes}
$$

**Step 6 — Verify the constraint.**

$$
1152\,\text{bytes} \;\leq\; 2048\,\text{bytes} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the remaining RAM for safety margin.**

$$
\text{Margin} = 2048 - 1152 = 896\,\text{bytes} \approx 43.75\%\;\text{free}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The firmware fits comfortably with 896 bytes (≈ 43.75%) free for unforeseen stack growth or temporary buffers. The system passes the RAM budget check.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Comparing Assembly vs C Code Size

**Problem:** An Assembly implementation of a FIR filter uses $S_{\text{asm}} = 4\,\text{KB}$ of Flash. The equivalent C code compiles to $S_C = 8\,\text{KB}$. The MCU has **32 KB Flash** and a **strict deterministic-cycle requirement** of $T_{\text{exec}} \leq 200\,\mu\text{s}$ at $f_{\text{clk}} = 50\,\text{MHz}$. Assembly executes 2.1× faster than C. Determine the preferred language and justify.

**Step 1 — Compute the size ratio.**

$$
R_{\text{size}} = \frac{S_C}{S_{\text{asm}}} = \frac{8}{4} = 2.0
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Flash utilization for each option.**

$$
U_{\text{asm}} = \frac{4}{32} \times 100\% = 12.5\%
$$

$$
U_C = \frac{8}{32} \times 100\% = 25.0\%
$$

Both fit easily.

**Step 3 — Compute execution times.**

$$
T_{\text{clk}} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^{6}} = 20\,\text{ns}
$$

Let the Assembly execution take $N_{\text{cycles}} = 5000$ cycles:

$$
T_{\text{asm}} = 5000 \times 20\,\text{ns} = 100\,\mu\text{s}
$$

The C version takes 2.1× longer:

$$
T_C = 2.1 \times 100 = 210\,\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the deadline.**

$$
T_{\text{asm}} = 100\,\mu\text{s} \;\leq\; 200\,\mu\text{s} \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
T_C = 210\,\mu\text{s} \;>\; 200\,\mu\text{s} \quad \times
$$

**Step 5 — Decision.**

> **Conclusion:** **Assembly is preferred** because the C code violates the deterministic deadline (210 μs > 200 μs). Even though C has higher developer productivity, the hard real-time constraint forces the use of Assembly for this inner loop. (This is a classic KTU "trade-off" answer.)

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Coding Issues in Embedded C

| # | Coding Issue | Root Cause | Consequence | Standard Fix |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Uninitialized variables** | Stack/heap not zeroed at startup | Random behavior, security flaws | Initialize all variables explicitly |
| 2 | **Use of `malloc`/`free`** | Heap fragmentation | Memory leak, crash | Use static allocation or memory pools |
| 3 | **Recursive functions** | Unbounded call depth | Stack overflow | Convert to iterative loops |
| 4 | **Floating-point on 8-bit MCU** | No FPU; software emulation is slow | Missed deadlines | Use fixed-point arithmetic or scaled integers |
| 5 | **Missing `volatile`** on shared/HW variables | Compiler optimization caches the value | ISR/main loop never see updates | Declare as `volatile uint8_t *ptr` |
| 6 | **Blocking delays (`while` loops)** | Wastes CPU cycles | Wasted power, missed events | Use hardware timers or RTOS |
| 7 | **Atomic access to 32-bit vars on 8-bit CPU** | Read-modify-write is not atomic | Data corruption | Disable interrupts, use `ATOMIC_BLOCK` |
| 8 | **Stack overflow** | Deep call chains, large local arrays | Hard fault, system reset | Bound recursion, move buffers to global/BSS |
| 9 | **Endianness mismatch** | Big-endian MCU ↔ little-endian peripheral | Wrong sensor readings | Use byte-shift macros (`htonl`/`ntohl`) |
| 10 | **Watchdog starvation** | Main loop blocks too long | System reset | Kick the watchdog regularly, use RTOS |
| 11 | **Printf on production firmware** | Pulls in large `stdio` library | Code bloat, slow I/O | Replace with `sprintf` or custom logger |
| 12 | **Interrupt inside critical section** | Race condition on shared data | Corrupted state | Use mutex, semaphore, or disable interrupts |
| 13 | **Hard-coded magic numbers** | No constants defined | Hard to maintain | Use `#define` / `const` |
| 14 | **Bit-field portability** | Compiler-dependent layout | Code breaks on different MCU | Use bit-mask macros instead |
| 15 | **Function pointer misuse** | Wrong prototype | Crash on call | Use `typedef` for function pointers |

### 3.5 Worked Example — Why `volatile` is Mandatory

Consider a flag set by an ISR and read by the main loop:

```c
volatile uint8_t data_ready = 0;  // MUST be volatile

void ISR_UART(void) {
    data_ready = 1;   // Set by hardware interrupt
}

void main_loop(void) {
    while (!data_ready) { /* wait */ }   // Reads from memory
        process_data();
}
```

**Without `volatile`:** The compiler optimizes `while (!data_ready)` to a **register read loop** — it never re-reads the memory location. The loop spins forever even after the ISR fires.

**With `volatile`:** The compiler is **forced to re-read from memory on every access**, so the main loop sees the updated value within a few cycles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is one of the **most-asked KTU questions** for Module 4. Memorize: *"`volatile` prevents the compiler from caching the variable in a register."*

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Embedded Software Development Flow

This Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete flow from source code to execution on an embedded target, highlighting the role of cross-compilation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Code C or Assembly] --> B[Preprocessor]
    B --> C[Compiler arm-none-eabi-gcc]
    C --> D[Assembler]
    D --> E[Object File .o]
    E --> F[Linker ld]
    F --> G[ELF Executable .elf]
    G --> H[Objcopy to .bin or .hex]
    H --> I[Flash Loader]
    I --> J[Target MCU Flash Memory]
    J --> K[Reset Vector Entry]
    K --> L[Startup Code crt0]
    L --> M[Initialize Stack and Data Sections]
    M --> N[Call main Function]
    N --> O[Initialize Peripherals and RTOS]
    O --> P[Enter Super Loop or Scheduler]
    P --> Q[Run Application]
```

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Embedded Memory Map (Typical MCU)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FlashROM["Flash ROM 256 KB"]
        F1[Bootloader]
        F2[ISR Vector Table]
        F3[Application Code .text]
        F4[Read-Only Data .rodata]
    end

    subgraph RAM["RAM 32 KB"]
        R1[Stack grows downward]
        R2[Heap grows upward]
        R3[Static and Global .data and .bss]
        R4[Peripheral Registers MMIO]
    end

    FlashROM -.loaded on reset.-> RAM
    R1 --- R2
    R2 --- R3
    R3 --- R4
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Coding Issue Decision Tree

This Mermaid flowchart helps decide which language to use given an embedded constraint.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Project Start] --> Q1{Hard real-time deadline less than 1 ms?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Flash budget less than 16 KB?}
    Q1 -- No --> A1[Use C or C++]
    Q2 -- Yes --> ASM[Use Assembly for critical sections]
    Q2 -- No --> CUse[Use optimized C]
    ASM --> Wrap[Wrap with C functions]
    Wrap --> A1
    A1 --> Q3{Productivity critical for large team?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> CPP[Use C++ with MISRA-C++ subset]
    Q3 -- No --> CUse
    CPP --> Safety[Apply static analysis and coding standard]
    CUse --> Safety
    Safety --> Done[Firmware Ready for Validation]
```

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Interrupt vs Main Loop Interaction (Shared Variable)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as Main Loop
    participant V as Volatile Variable
    participant I as ISR Hardware Event

    Note over M: Reads V from memory
    M->>V: Read data_ready
    V-->>M: Returns 0
    Note over M: Spins in while loop
    Note over I: External event occurs
    I->>V: Write data_ready = 1
    Note over M: Re-reads V from memory
    M->>V: Read data_ready
    V-->>M: Returns 1
    Note over M: Exits loop and processes data
    M->>M: process_data()
```

### 4.5 Diagram 5 — Coding Issue Resolution Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Issue[Detected Coding Issue] --> Cat{Category}
    Cat -- Memory --> M1[Static Allocation, Memory Pools]
    Cat -- Timing --> T1[RTOS, DMA, Hardware Timers]
    Cat -- Concurrency --> C1[Disable Interrupts, Atomic Access]
    Cat -- Portability --> P1[Use CMSIS, HAL, Abstraction Layer]
    Cat -- Optimization --> O1[Profile with gprof or oscilloscope]
    M1 --> Test[Unit and Integration Test]
    T1 --> Test
    C1 --> Test
    P1 --> Test
    O1 --> Test
    Test --> Pass{Pass?}
    Pass -- No --> Issue
    Pass -- Yes --> Release[Firmware Release]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2-Mark Short Answer)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

What is the role of the `volatile` keyword in embedded C? Why is it essential when accessing hardware registers?

**Model Answer:**

The `volatile` keyword in C tells the **compiler not to optimize** the variable by storing it in a CPU register; every read/write must access the **actual memory location**.

**Why essential for hardware registers:**
- Hardware registers (e.g., `UART_STATUS`, `ADC_DATA`) change **asynchronously** due to external events.
- Without `volatile`, the compiler may cache the value in a register, and the program will never see updates from hardware.
- It is **mandatory** for:
  1. Memory-mapped I/O registers
  2. Variables shared between ISR and main loop
  3. Variables shared between threads in an RTOS

**Example:**

```c
#define STATUS_REG   (*(volatile uint8_t *)0x40021000)
```

> Without `volatile`, the compiler may assume the register value never changes and eliminate the polling loop.

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (1 mark) + Justification with example (1 mark).

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

List any four common coding issues faced while programming in embedded C.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Use of dynamic memory allocation (`malloc`/`free`)** — causes heap fragmentation and unpredictable failure.
2. **Non-volatile shared variables** between ISR and main code — leads to missed updates.
3. **Recursive functions** — risk of stack overflow on memory-limited MCUs.
4. **Blocking delay loops (`while(timer--)`)** — wastes CPU cycles and prevents low-power mode.
5. *(Any 4 of the above or similar — e.g., floating-point on 8-bit MCUs, endianness mismatch, atomicity of multi-byte access.)*

**Marks Distribution:** Listing 4 issues with one-line justification each (0.5 × 4 = 2 marks).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14-Mark Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Rule (2024 Scheme):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each part (a) and (b) carries 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Compare the features of **Assembly language** and **C language** for embedded system programming. Discuss with examples, listing at least **four advantages** and **two limitations** of each. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **memory-mapped I/O** in embedded systems. Show with a C code snippet how a peripheral register can be accessed using a pointer, and explain the role of the `volatile` qualifier. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Assembly vs C — Comparison**

| Aspect | Assembly Language | C Language |
|---|---|---|
| **Abstraction Level** | Low (1:1 with machine code) | Mid-level (portable) |
| **Code Size** | Smallest (100% of opt) | 1.5× – 3× larger |
| **Execution Speed** | Fastest (no compiler overhead) | 5–20% slower on average |
| **Portability** | None (CPU-specific) | High (with minor mods) |
| **Productivity** | Low (verbose, error-prone) | High (type-checked, structured) |
| **Debugging** | Difficult (no symbols) | Easier (debugger symbols available) |
| **Hardware Control** | Total (flags, registers, stack) | Indirect (via pointers/HAL) |

**Assembly Advantages:** (i) Smallest code size, (ii) Maximum speed, (iii) Total hardware control, (iv) Deterministic cycles.

**Assembly Limitations:** (i) Non-portable, (ii) Time-consuming development.

**C Advantages:** (i) Portable, (ii) Faster development, (iii) Type safety, (iv) Rich libraries, (v) Inline assembly possible.

**C Limitations:** (i) Code bloat, (ii) Limited low-level control.

**Marks split (7):** Comparison table (3) + 4 advantages + 2 limitations each (2) + Verdict (2).

---

**(b) Memory-Mapped I/O and `volatile`**

**Definition:** In memory-mapped I/O (MMIO), peripheral registers are assigned addresses in the **same address space** as program memory. Reading/writing those addresses reads/writes the hardware.

**Code Example:**

```c
// Memory-mapped I/O example: UART Data Register at address 0x4000_1000
#define UART_DR   (*(volatile uint32_t *)0x40001000)

void uart_send(char c) {
    UART_DR = c;        // Write directly to the transmit register
}

char uart_receive(void) {
    return (char)UART_DR;   // Read directly from the receive register
}
```

**Explanation of `volatile`:**
1. The cast `(volatile uint32_t *)0x40001000` tells the compiler the address points to a **volatile** 32-bit register.
2. Every access compiles to a **load/store instruction** — no caching in CPU registers.
3. This ensures the CPU sees the **latest hardware state** every time.
4. Without `volatile`, the compiler may optimize `return UART_DR;` to read the value once and reuse it, leading to **stale data** and lost interrupts.

**Marks split (7):** Definition of MMIO (2) + Code example (3) + Explanation of `volatile` (2).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **major coding issues** encountered in embedded C programming. Describe at least **six issues** with the cause and a recommended solution for each. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the **embedded software development flow** — from source code to execution on the target MCU. Mention the role of the **cross-compiler**, **linker**, and **startup code**. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Major Coding Issues in Embedded C (six issues):**

1. **Heap Fragmentation**
   - *Cause:* Frequent `malloc`/`free` in long-running systems.
   - *Solution:* Use static allocation or fixed-size memory pools (e.g., `FreeRTOS heap_4.c`).

2. **Stack Overflow**
   - *Cause:* Deep recursion or large local arrays.
   - *Solution:* Bound recursion depth, move large buffers to `.bss`, monitor stack pointer with a paint-can pattern.

3. **Race Conditions in ISRs**
   - *Cause:* Non-atomic access to shared variables.
   - *Solution:* Use `volatile`, disable interrupts in critical sections, use RTOS mutex/semaphore.

4. **Compiler Optimization Bugs**
   - *Cause:* Missing `volatile` or barriers.
   - *Solution:* Use `volatile`, `__attribute__((optimize("O0")))` for ISRs, memory barriers (`__DSB()`, `__ISB()`).

5. **Floating-Point Inaccuracy**
   - *Cause:* 8/16-bit MCUs use software floating-point.
   - *Solution:* Use fixed-point (Q-format) arithmetic.

6. **Blocking Code Preventing Low Power**
   - *Cause:* Tight loops or `printf`.
   - *Solution:* Use interrupts, DMA, RTOS idle hook, sleep modes.

7. **Endianness Bug**
   - *Cause:* Different byte order between MCU and peripheral.
   - *Solution:* Use explicit byte-shift macros.

8. **Watchdog Timer Reset**
   - *Cause:* Main loop blocked.
   - *Solution:* Kick the watchdog regularly, use a dedicated task for it.

*(Any 6 well-explained carry 7 marks: 1 mark per issue + 0.2 for solution; balance to diagram/example.)*

---

**(b) Embedded Software Development Flow**

**Flow (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):**

1. **Source files** (`main.c`, `isr.c`, `startup.s`) → preprocessed.
2. **Cross-compiler** (`arm-none-eabi-gcc`) compiles C → assembly → object files (`.o`).
3. **Linker script** (`STM32F407.ld`) places code/data sections at correct addresses.
4. **ELF file** is generated with symbols and debug info.
5. **`objcopy`** converts ELF to `.bin` / `.hex`.
6. **Flash programmer / debugger** (J-Link, ST-Link) loads the binary via SWD/JTAG.
7. **Target MCU** boots from reset vector → runs **startup code** (`crt0`).
8. **Startup code** initializes `.data` (copy from Flash), zeros `.bss`, sets up stack pointer, configures clock, then calls `main()`.
9. `main()` initializes peripherals (clocks, GPIO, UART) and enters **super loop** or **RTOS scheduler**.

**Roles:**
- **Cross-compiler:** Compiles host-source code into target-machine code.
- **Linker:** Combines object files, resolves symbols, places sections per linker script.
- **Startup code:** Hardware initialization before `main()`.

**Marks split (7):** Flow description (3) + Diagram (2) + Roles of cross-compiler/linker/startup (2).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Don't confuse `volatile` with `const`.** `volatile` ≠ `const`. A register can be `volatile const` (read-only hardware register).
> 2. **Don't write that "Assembly is always faster."** Modern compilers can produce code as efficient as hand-written assembly for most C code. The keyword is *determinism*, not raw speed.
> 3. **Memory-mapped I/O ≠ Port-mapped I/O.** Know the difference. ARM Cortex-M uses MMIO; x86 historically used port-mapped I/O.
> 4. **MISRA-C** is a *coding standard*, not a *language*. Writing "MISRA-C is a language" will cost a mark.
> 5. **Always quote real numbers** in timing questions. Use units (μs, ns, KB).
> 6. **Diagrams must be labeled.** An unlabeled block diagram in Part B can lose 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision (5 minutes before exam):**

- [ ] **Assembly vs C:** Assembly = control + size; C = portability + productivity.
- [ ] **C is the embedded lingua franca** — used in 80%+ of microcontrollers.
- [ ] **`volatile` keyword:** Prevents compiler optimization of variable access — mandatory for hardware registers and ISR-shared variables.
- [ ] **Memory-mapped I/O (MMIO):** Peripherals accessed via pointer to a fixed address.
- [ ] **Coding issues to remember:**
  - Heap fragmentation → use memory pools
  - Stack overflow → bound recursion
  - Race conditions → `volatile` + atomic access
  - Endianness mismatch → use byte-shift macros
  - Watchdog starvation → kick regularly
  - Blocking delays → use hardware timers
  - Floating-point on 8-bit → use fixed-point
  - Recursion → convert to iteration
- [ ] **Trade-off formula:** $T_{\text{program}} = N_{\text{instr}} \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{clk}}$
- [ ] **RAM budget:** $\text{RAM}_{\min} = \text{Stack} + \text{Heap} + \text{Data} + \text{Buffers}$
- [ ] **Languages in increasing abstraction:** Machine code → Assembly → C → C++ → Java/Python.
- [ ] **Embedded extensions to C:** `interrupt`, `sfr`, `sbit`, `using`, `code`, `idata`, `pdata`, `xdata`.
- [ ] **Coding standards:** MISRA-C (automotive), MISRA-C++ (high-end), CERT-C (security).
- [ ] **Cross-compilation:** Host (x86) compiles for target (ARM/AVR) — produces `.elf` → `.bin` → flashed to MCU.
- [ ] **Startup code (`crt0`)** initializes `.data`, zeros `.bss`, sets stack pointer, then calls `main()`.
- [ ] **Real-time deadlines** must be checked using worst-case execution time (WCET), not average.
- [ ] **Power-aware coding:** Sleep modes, clock gating, peripheral shutdown in idle.

---

### 5.4 High-Yield Comparison Table — Embedded Languages at a Glance

| Property | Assembly | C | C++ | Java | MicroPython |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Abstraction** | None | Mid | High | Very High | Very High |
| **Code Size** | Smallest (×1) | ×1.5–3 | ×2–5 | ×5–10 | ×10+ |
| **Execution Speed** | Fastest | Fast | Fast–Mid | Slow | Slowest |
| **Determinism** | Total | High | Medium | Low | Low |
| **Portability** | None | High | High | Very High | Very High |
| **Memory Footprint** | Minimal | Low | Medium | High | Very High |
| **Learning Curve** | Steep | Moderate | Moderate | Easy | Easy |
| **Typical MCU** | 8-bit / DSP inner loop | 8/16/32-bit | Cortex-A / Automotive | Smart cards, Android | ESP32, RPi |
| **Used in KTU Labs** | Yes (8051/PIC) | Yes (ARM) | Sometimes | No | Sometimes |

---

> **End of Module 4 — Programming Language and Coding Issues | PEAET695**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Code Optimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET695 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Code Optimization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Code Optimization** is the process of modifying a computer program's intermediate or assembly-level representation to improve one or more of its non-functional attributes — **execution speed**, **code size (memory footprint)**, **power dissipation**, and **energy efficiency** — while preserving the observable semantic behavior (functional correctness) of the program. In the context of embedded systems, optimization is **mandatory rather than optional**, because embedded targets operate under severe constraints of limited RAM, restricted ROM/Flash, fixed-clock CPUs, and battery-powered energy budgets.

According to the KTU 2024 scheme curriculum for **PEAET695 – Embedded System Design**, code optimization is positioned at the back-end of the cross-compilation toolchain, immediately following code generation and preceding target binary emission. It operates on the **Intermediate Representation (IR)**, typically a Three-Address Code (TAC), Control Flow Graph (CFG), or Static Single Assignment (SSA) form.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Packing a Suitcase for a Trek"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are packing a suitcase for a 3-day mountain trek with a strict 7 kg airline baggage limit (your embedded board's Flash memory). You start by throwing clothes randomly (naïve code). The "optimizer" is like an expert trekker who:
> 1. **Folds** clothes tightly (constant folding),
> 2. **Removes** duplicate socks (dead-code elimination),
> 3. **Replaces** the heavy DSLR with a phone camera (strength reduction — divide becomes shift),
> 4. **Combines** two short treks into one path (loop fusion),
> 5. **Pins** the most-used items (toothbrush, water) on the outside for instant access (register allocation).
>
> The end result: the **same journey** is completed with **less weight (memory)** and **faster access (speed)**, using the **same clothes (semantics)**.

### 1.3 The Three Optimization Pillars (Standard KTU Metrics)

| Pillar | Standard Metric | Typical Constraint |
|---|---|---|
| **Time** | Execution cycles / latency (ms) | Hard real-time deadlines (e.g., 100 µs response) |
| **Space** | Code size (bytes / KB) | Flash ≤ 256 KB on typical Cortex-M0/M3 |
| **Power / Energy** | Joules per task, mW active current | Battery life ≥ 1 year on coin cell |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** Optimization is a **multi-objective trade-off**. Aggressively optimizing for speed (unrolling loops) often **bloats code size**, and aggressively optimizing for size (`-Os` flag) may **slow down execution**. Embedded designers must choose a balanced strategy such as `-O2` (speed) or `-Os` (size) depending on whether the system is **compute-bound** or **memory-bound**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Code Optimization Techniques

Optimization techniques are broadly classified along **two orthogonal axes**:

**Axis 1 — Scope of Transformation:**
1. **Local Optimization** — Applied within a single basic block (a straight-line sequence of instructions with no branches in/out except at entry/exit).
2. **Global Optimization** — Applied across multiple basic blocks within a single function, using the Control Flow Graph (CFG).
3. **Inter-procedural Optimization (IPO)** — Applied across function boundaries (whole-program / link-time).

**Axis 2 — When it Runs:**
- **Compile-time / Static Optimization** — Performed by the compiler before execution.
- **Run-time / Dynamic Optimization** — Performed by a JIT or by specialized hardware (e.g., trace caches in ARM Cortex-A).

### 2.2 Core Optimization Techniques (Exhaustive List)

The following techniques are the **canonical set** tested in KTU examinations.

**A. Algebraic / Arithmetic Optimizations**

1. **Constant Folding:** Evaluates expressions whose operands are all literals at compile time.
   - `x = 3 * 60;` → `x = 180;`
2. **Constant Propagation:** Replaces variables that have known constant values.
   - `x = 5; y = x + 2;` → `y = 7;`
3. **Copy Propagation:** Replaces copies like `y = x` with subsequent uses of `y` replaced by `x`.
4. **Algebraic Simplification / Identity Replacement:** Replaces expensive operations with cheaper equivalents.
   - `x * 1` → `x`, `x + 0` → `x`, `x ^ 0` → `x`.
5. **Strength Reduction:** Replaces an expensive operation with a cheaper equivalent.
   - `x * 2` → `x << 1`, `x / 4` → `x >> 2`, `x * 2^k` → shift, `pow(x,2)` → `x*x`.
6. **Subexpression Elimination (Common Subexpression Elimination — CSE):** Avoids recomputing an expression that has already been computed and whose operands have not changed.
   - `a = b + c; d = b + c;` → compute once, reuse result.

**B. Control-Flow Optimizations**

7. **Dead Code Elimination (DCE):** Removes code that does not affect program output (unreachable code or assignments to variables never read).
8. **Unreachable Code Elimination:** Removes code after an unconditional `return` or inside `if (0) { ... }`.
9. **Branch Prediction / Reduction:** Rearranges conditions to make the common case the fall-through path.
10. **Tail-Call Optimization (TCO):** Converts a recursive call at the end of a function into a `jmp` (jump) to reuse the current stack frame.
11. **Function Inlining:** Replaces a function call with the body of the called function to eliminate call/return overhead — but **bloats code size**.

**C. Loop Optimizations (Most KTU-relevant sub-topic)**

12. **Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM):** Hoists computations that do not change inside the loop out of the loop body.
13. **Loop Unrolling:** Replicates the loop body N times to reduce branch overhead and expose more ILP (Instruction-Level Parallelism). Trade-off: ↑ speed, ↑ code size.
14. **Loop Fusion (Jamming):** Combines two adjacent loops over the same index range into one loop.
15. **Loop Fission (Distribution):** Splits one loop into two to improve cache locality.
16. **Loop Tiling / Blocking:** Reorganizes loop nest to operate on small data blocks that fit in cache.
17. **Loop Interchange:** Swaps inner and outer loop indices to improve spatial locality (row-major vs column-major access).
18. **Strength Reduction inside Loops:** Replaces array index multiplications with pointer increments or induction-variable analysis.

**D. Machine-Dependent (Peephole) Optimizations**

19. **Peephole Optimization:** Examines a sliding window (peephole) of consecutive assembly instructions and replaces inefficient patterns with efficient ones (e.g., `MUL R1, R1, 0` → `MOV R1, #0`).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Optimization Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas quantify the **improvement** delivered by an optimization pass and are **directly asked in KTU numericals**.

| # | Formula / Metric | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Speedup** $S = \dfrac{T_{\text{old}}}{T_{\text{new}}}$ | Ratio of original to optimized execution time. | Dimensionless |
| 2 | **Speedup Percent** $S_{\%} = \left(1 - \dfrac{T_{\text{new}}}{T_{\text{old}}}\right) \times 100$ | Percentage time saved. | Percent (%) |
| 3 | **Code-Size Overhead** $\Delta_{\text{size}} = \dfrac{S_{\text{new}} - S_{\text{old}}}{S_{\text{old}}} \times 100$ | Percentage increase in binary size. | Percent (%) |
| 4 | **Cycles Saved per Iteration** $\Delta C = C_{\text{old}} - C_{\text{new}}$ | Loop or function-level cycle savings. | Clock cycles |
| 5 | **Energy per Instruction** $E_i = C_{\text{avg}} \cdot V_{dd}^{2}$ | Dynamic energy approximation (CMOS). | Joules (J) |
| 6 | **Total Energy** $E_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} N_i \cdot E_i$ | Sum of per-instruction energy × occurrence. | Joules (J) |
| 7 | **Power** $P = C_{\text{avg}} \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f$ | Dynamic power (CMOS dominant term). | Watts (W) |
| 8 | **Loop Unroll Speedup** $S_{u} = \dfrac{N \cdot C_{\text{body}} + C_{\text{loop\_ctrl}}}{C_{\text{body}} + C_{\text{loop\_ctrl}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{u}}$ | For unroll factor $u$, body cycles $C_{\text{body}}$, loop-control cycles $C_{\text{loop\_ctrl}}$, $N$ iterations. | Dimensionless |
| 9 | **Amdahl's Law** $S_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{s}}$ | Maximum speedup when fraction $f$ is sped up by factor $s$. | Dimensionless |
| 10 | **CPI after optimization** $\text{CPI}_{\text{opt}} = \dfrac{\sum_{i} (\text{IC}_i \cdot \text{CPI}_i)}{\sum_{i} \text{IC}_i}$ | Weighted average CPI after pass. | Cycles/instr |

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-Collapse Rule:** Never write the absolute-value symbol as `|` inside any cell above — it would break the markdown table renderer. The LaTeX `$\vert x \vert$` form is the only safe representation.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Code optimization is the **single largest contributor** to embedded product competitiveness:
- In **ARM Cortex-M firmware** for IoT sensors, `-Os` (size) optimization enables fitting an entire BLE stack into 64 KB Flash.
- In **automotive ECUs** (AUTOSAR), WCET (Worst-Case Execution Time) certification **requires** loop unrolling + LICM so the static analyzer can produce tight bounds.
- In **DSP firmware** (audio codecs, FFT), strength reduction turns a divide into a shift, turning a 1024-point FFT from **11.5 ms** to **6.8 ms** on a Cortex-M4.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Constant Folding + Strength Reduction + CSE

**Source Code (C):**
```c
int a = 60;
int b = a * 3;
int c = a / 4;
int d = a * b;
int e = a * b + a;
int f = 2 * a;
```

We will optimize this in **three sequential passes**.

**Pass 1 — Constant Folding:**
`a` is a compile-time constant equal to `60`.

$$\begin{aligned}
b &= a \cdot 3 = 60 \cdot 3 = 180 \\
c &= a \div 4 = 60 \div 4 = 15 \quad (\text{integer division}) \\
d &= a \cdot b = 60 \cdot 180 = 10800 \\
e &= a \cdot b + a = 10800 + 60 = 10860 \\
f &= 2 \cdot a = 2 \cdot 60 = 120
\end{aligned}$$

**Pass 2 — Strength Reduction (multiplications by powers of 2 → shifts):**
At compile time, the compiler may further replace `60 \cdot 3` with `(60 << 1) + 60`, but since `3` is not a power of two, the canonical shift rule applies only to `f` here:

$$\begin{aligned}
f &= 2 \cdot 60 = 60 \ll 1
\end{aligned}$$

**Pass 3 — Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE):**
The expression `a \cdot b` is used in both `d` and `e`. Compute it **once** into a temporary `t1`:

$$\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= a \cdot b \\
d &= t_1 \\
e &= t_1 + a
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Optimized Code:**
```c
int a = 60;
int b = 180;
int c = 15;
int t1 = 10800;
int d = t1;
int e = t1 + 60;
int f = a << 1;     // strength reduction: 2*a = a<<1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final cycle count** (on a generic 1-cycle ALU with 4-cycle multiply):
> - Original: 4 multiplications × 4 cycles + 3 additions × 1 cycle = **19 cycles**.
> - Optimized: 1 multiplication × 4 cycles + 3 additions × 1 cycle + 1 shift × 1 cycle = **8 cycles**.
> - **Speedup** $S = 19 / 8 = 2.375\times$.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM) + Induction-Variable Strength Reduction

**Original Loop (TAC):**
```c
for (i = 0; i < N; i++) {
    A[i] = x * y + B[i];
}
```

**Step 1 — Identify Invariant Expression:**
The expression `x * y` does **not depend on `i`**. It is **loop-invariant**.

**Step 2 — LICM Transformation (Hoist out):**
```c
t1 = x * y;              // moved BEFORE the loop (executed once)
for (i = 0; i < N; i++) {
    A[i] = t1 + B[i];
}
```

**Step 3 — Apply Strength Reduction on Array Index:**
The compiler replaces the expensive array-index multiplication `A + i * 4` (assuming `int` = 4 bytes) with **pointer arithmetic**:

$$A + 4i = A + 4 \cdot i \;\longrightarrow\; \text{ptr} = \&A[0]; \; \text{then increment } ptr += 4 \text{ per iteration}$$

This converts `i * 4` from a **4-cycle MUL** into a **1-cycle ADD**.

**Step 4 — Cycle-Count Comparison:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{old}} &= N \cdot (C_{\text{MUL}} + C_{\text{LOAD}} + C_{\text{ADD}} + C_{\text{STORE}} + C_{\text{IDX\_MUL}}) \\
              &= N \cdot (4 + 3 + 1 + 2 + 4) = 14N \;\text{cycles} \\[6pt]
C_{\text{new}} &= C_{\text{MUL(outside)}} + N \cdot (C_{\text{LOAD}} + C_{\text{ADD}} + C_{\text{STORE}} + C_{\text{PTR\_INC}}) \\
              &= 4 + N \cdot (3 + 1 + 2 + 1) = 4 + 7N \;\text{cycles}
\end{aligned}$$

**Speedup for $N = 100$:**

$$S = \frac{14 \cdot 100}{4 + 7 \cdot 100} = \frac{1400}{704} \approx 1.9886\times$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Loop Unrolling Quantitative Analysis

**Generic Loop:**
```c
sum = 0;
for (i = 0; i < N; i++)
    sum += A[i];
```

**Body cost** $C_{\text{body}} = 4$ cycles (load + add + store + index update).
**Loop-control cost** $C_{\text{loop\_ctrl}} = 3$ cycles (compare + branch + increment).
**Unroll factor** $u = 4$ (KTU standard).

**Original cycles:**

$$C_{\text{old}} = N \cdot (C_{\text{body}} + C_{\text{loop\_ctrl}}) = N \cdot (4 + 3) = 7N$$

**Unrolled cycles (with leftover remainder $(N \bmod u)$ handled separately):**

$$C_{\text{new}} = \frac{N}{u} \cdot (u \cdot C_{\text{body}} + C_{\text{loop\_ctrl}}) = \frac{N}{u} \cdot (4u + 3)$$

For $u = 4$:

$$C_{\text{new}} = \frac{N}{4} \cdot (16 + 3) = \frac{19N}{4} = 4.75 N$$

**Speedup:**

$$\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{7N}{4.75N} = 1.4737\times \\[4pt]
S_{\%} &= \left(1 - \frac{4.75}{7}\right) \times 100 = 32.14\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Code-size overhead** (assuming each original instruction = 4 bytes, unrolled body = $u$× larger):

$$\Delta_{\text{size}} = \frac{(u \cdot S_{\text{body}}) - S_{\text{body}}}{S_{\text{body}}} \times 100 = (u - 1) \times 100 = (4-1) \times 100 = 300\%$$

This vividly illustrates the **speed–size trade-off** expected in KTU answers.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix (Which Optimization to Use When)

| System Constraint | Recommended Primary Optimization | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Tight Flash (<64 KB)** | `-Os`, dead-code elim, function outlining | Minimize ROM footprint |
| **Hard real-time deadline** | Loop unrolling, LICM, inlining | Minimize WCET variance |
| **Battery-powered sensor node** | Power-gating, clock-gating, strength reduction | Lower $\text{CPI} \Rightarrow$ lower $P$ |
| **DSP / signal processing** | Strength reduction (mul → shift/add), SIMD | Maximize throughput |
| **Recursion-heavy code** | Tail-call optimization, inlining | Avoid stack overflow |
| **Cache-rich SoC** | Loop tiling, blocking | Maximize cache hit ratio |
| **Deeply nested loops** | Loop interchange, fusion | Improve locality |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Compilation Pipeline — Where Optimization Fits

The following Mermaid block diagrams the **cross-compilation toolchain** for an embedded target (e.g., GCC cross-compiler for ARM Cortex-M), explicitly highlighting the optimization stage.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1["Source File .c"]
    src2["Source File .c"]
    src3["Header .h"]
    preproc["Preprocessor"]
    ir1["Frontend AST"]
    ir2["Intermediate Representation TAC"]
    ir3["SSA Form"]
    ir4["Control Flow Graph"]
    opt1["Local Optimization"]
    opt2["Global Optimization"]
    opt3["Loop Optimization"]
    opt4["Peephole Optimization"]
    asm["Target Assembly .s"]
    asm2["Assembler"]
    elf["Relocatable .o"]
    elf2["Linker"]
    elf3["Executable .elf / .hex"]
    burn["Flash Burner"]

    src1 --> preproc
    src2 --> preproc
    src3 --> preproc
    preproc --> ir1
    ir1 --> ir2
    ir2 --> ir3
    ir3 --> ir4
    ir4 --> opt1
    opt1 --> opt2
    opt2 --> opt3
    opt3 --> opt4
    opt4 --> asm
    asm --> asm2
    asm2 --> elf
    elf --> elf2
    elf2 --> elf3
    elf3 --> burn
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Optimization stages are the four sequential boxes (`Local → Global → Loop → Peephole`). Each stage consumes the IR produced by the previous stage and emits a more efficient IR. The final peephole pass operates directly on assembly mnemonics of the target ISA (e.g., ARM Thumb-2).

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of a Generic Optimizer Pass

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    in1["Input IR TAC Stream"]
    ana["Analysis Phase"]
    cfg["Build CFG SSA"]
    data["Data Flow Analysis"]
    reach["Reachability Liveness"]
    trans["Transformation Phase"]
    fold["Constant Folding"]
    cse["Common Subexpression Elimination"]
    licm["Loop Invariant Code Motion"]
    dead["Dead Code Elimination"]
    str["Strength Reduction"]
    out1["Optimized IR TAC"]
    verify["Correctness Check"]

    in1 --> ana
    ana --> cfg
    cfg --> data
    data --> reach
    reach --> trans
    trans --> fold
    fold --> cse
    cse --> licm
    licm --> str
    str --> dead
    dead --> out1
    out1 --> verify
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two phases?** The *Analysis* phase gathers facts (which variables are live, which blocks are reachable, which expressions are available) without modifying the program. The *Transformation* phase applies rewrite rules only when the analysis has **proven** them safe. This separation is the hallmark of a **provably correct** optimizer — a key concept in compiler-construction courses.

### 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing an Optimization Pass

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Start: Identify Hotspot"]
    q1{"Loops present?"}
    q2{"Recursion present?"}
    q3{"Large functions?"}
    q4{"Real-time constraint?"}
    a1["Apply LICM Unroll Strength Reduction"]
    a2["Apply Tail Call Optimization Inlining"]
    a3["Apply Function Inlining Constant Propagation"]
    a4["Apply Peephole Optimization"]
    end["End: Emit Optimized Binary"]

    start --> q1
    q1 -- Yes --> a1
    q1 -- No --> q2
    q2 -- Yes --> a2
    q2 -- No --> q3
    q3 -- Yes --> a3
    q3 -- No --> q4
    q4 -- Yes --> a4
    q4 -- No --> end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**  
**Define code optimization. List any four code optimization techniques used in embedded systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — KTU marking scheme):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Code optimization is the process of transforming a program to make it execute **faster**, use **less memory**, or consume **less power**, while preserving its observable output.
>
> **Any Four Techniques (4 × 0.375 ≈ 1.5 marks):**
> 1. Constant Folding
> 2. Dead Code Elimination
> 3. Strength Reduction
> 4. Loop Invariant Code Motion (LICM)
> 5. Common Subexpression Elimination
> 6. Function Inlining
> 7. Peephole Optimization
> 8. Loop Unrolling

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**  
**What is strength reduction? Give one example applicable to embedded processors.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Strength reduction replaces an expensive operation (e.g., multiplication, division, exponentiation) with an equivalent but cheaper one (shift, add, subtract).
>
> **Example (1 mark):** Replacing `x * 8` with `x << 3` (shift is one cycle; multiply is four cycles on a typical ARM Cortex-M). Similarly, `x / 4` becomes `x >> 2`. This is heavily used in DSP loops where array index scaling `i * sizeof(T)` is replaced with pointer increment.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the **KTU 2024 End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern** for a Module-4 question worth 14 marks, each question carries **internal choice** between two alternatives. Sub-part (a) is for 7 marks and sub-part (b) is for 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Optimization Techniques & Quantitative Analysis*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, End-Sem]**  
**Choose (a) OR (b):**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain **Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)**, **Dead Code Elimination (DCE)**, and **Strength Reduction** with one C-code example each. Show the transformed code.

**Model Answer:**

**1. Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)** *(2.5 marks)*

CSE detects an expression that has been previously computed and whose operands are unchanged. It replaces the second computation with a reuse of the first result.

```c
// Before CSE
a = b * c + g;
d = b * c * e;

// After CSE
t1 = b * c;          // compute once
a  = t1 + g;
d  = t1 * e;
```

**2. Dead Code Elimination (DCE)** *(2.5 marks)*

DCE removes statements whose results are never used or which are unreachable.

```c
// Before DCE
int x = 5;
int y = x + 10;      // y never read
return 42;

// After DCE
return 42;           // x and y eliminated
```

**3. Strength Reduction** *(2 marks)*

```c
// Before
for (i = 0; i < N; i++)
    A[i] = i * 4;

// After (pointer arithmetic replaces multiplication)
ptr = &A[0];
for (i = 0; i < N; i++) {
    *ptr = i;       // store offset
    ptr += 1;       // or use ptr = ptr + 1
}
```

---

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Consider the following loop:

```c
for (i = 0; i < 100; i++) {
    A[i] = x * y + B[i] + C[i];
}
```

Assume: load = 3 cycles, store = 2 cycles, add = 1 cycle, multiply = 4 cycles, branch = 1 cycle.  
**(i)** Calculate the total cycles **before** optimization.  
**(ii)** Apply **Loop Invariant Code Motion** and **strength reduction on `i * 4`**, then calculate **after** cycles and the **speedup**.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Before optimization (3.5 marks):**

Per iteration cost:
- `x * y` → 4 cycles (multiply)
- `B[i]` → 3 cycles (load)
- `+ B[i]` → 1 cycle (add)
- `C[i]` → 3 cycles (load)
- `+ C[i]` → 1 cycle (add)
- `A[i]` store → 2 cycles
- `i * 4` index → 4 cycles (multiply)
- Branch → 1 cycle

Total per iteration:

$$C_{\text{old/iter}} = 4 + 3 + 1 + 3 + 1 + 2 + 4 + 1 = 19 \;\text{cycles}$$

For $N = 100$:

$$C_{\text{old,total}} = 19 \times 100 = 1900 \;\text{cycles}$$

**(ii) After optimization (3.5 marks):**

- Hoist `x * y` out: costs 4 cycles **once** outside.
- Replace `i * 4` with pointer increment: 1 cycle per iteration.

Per iteration new cost:

$$C_{\text{new/iter}} = 0 + 3 + 1 + 3 + 1 + 2 + 1 + 1 = 12 \;\text{cycles}$$

For $N = 100$:

$$C_{\text{new,total}} = 4 + 12 \times 100 = 1204 \;\text{cycles}$$

**Speedup:**

$$S = \frac{1900}{1204} \approx 1.578$$

**Percentage saved:**

$$S_{\%} = \left(1 - \frac{1204}{1900}\right) \times 100 \approx 36.63\%$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Loop Optimizations & Power/Size Trade-off*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, End-Sem]**  
**Choose (a) OR (b):**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain **Loop Unrolling** and **Loop Fusion** with suitable examples. Mention **one advantage and one disadvantage** of each.

**Model Answer:**

**1. Loop Unrolling** *(3.5 marks)*

Loop unrolling replicates the body $u$ times to reduce branch overhead and enable instruction-level parallelism.

```c
// Original (u = 1)
for (i = 0; i < 4; i++)
    sum += A[i];

// Unrolled (u = 4)
sum += A[0];
sum += A[1];
sum += A[2];
sum += A[3];
```

- **Advantage:** Reduces loop-control overhead, exposes ILP.
- **Disadvantage:** Increases code size (bloat).

**2. Loop Fusion (Jamming)** *(3.5 marks)*

Loop fusion merges adjacent loops over the same index range.

```c
// Before
for (i = 0; i < N; i++) A[i] = B[i] + 1;
for (i = 0; i < N; i++) C[i] = A[i] - 2;

// After (fused)
for (i = 0; i < N; i++) {
    A[i] = B[i] + 1;
    C[i] = A[i] - 2;
}
```

- **Advantage:** Improves cache locality (data fetched once).
- **Disadvantage:** May increase register pressure (live variables simultaneously).

---

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A firmware function takes **8500 cycles** before optimization and **6200 cycles** after. The code size grows from **2.1 KB** to **2.8 KB**.  
**(i)** Compute **speedup** and **percentage cycle reduction**.  
**(ii)** Compute **code-size overhead percentage**.  
**(iii)** Comment: is this optimization suitable for an embedded system with **32 KB Flash** and a **10 ms deadline** at 50 MHz (1 cycle = 20 ns)?

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Speedup and Cycle Reduction (2.5 marks):**

$$S = \frac{T_{\text{old}}}{T_{\text{new}}} = \frac{8500}{6200} \approx 1.371$$

$$S_{\%} = \left(1 - \frac{6200}{8500}\right) \times 100 = 27.06\%$$

**(ii) Code-Size Overhead (1.5 marks):**

$$\Delta_{\text{size}} = \frac{2.8 - 2.1}{2.1} \times 100 = 33.33\%$$

**(iii) Suitability Comment (3 marks):**

- **Time check at 50 MHz:** 1 cycle = $\dfrac{1}{50 \times 10^{6}} = 20$ ns.
- Original execution time: $8500 \times 20\,\text{ns} = 170\,\mu s$.
- Optimized execution time: $6200 \times 20\,\text{ns} = 124\,\mu s$.
- Deadline: $10\,\text{ms} = 10\,000\,\mu s$. Both versions **comfortably meet** the deadline (margin $\approx 98.5\%$).
- **Code-size check:** New size 2.8 KB ≤ 32 KB Flash ⇒ Fits comfortably.
- **Conclusion:** Optimization is **suitable and recommended**; it saves $46\,\mu s$ per invocation with negligible memory impact.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks on Code Optimization questions:**
> 1. **Confusing local vs global scope** — CSE applied inside one basic block is local; across CFG is global. Partial credit lost if scope is not specified.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention trade-offs** — Saying "loop unrolling is good" without "but increases code size" loses 1–2 marks on 7-mark questions.
> 3. **Skipping the loop-control cycles in unrolling analysis** — Many students compute only body cycles and forget branch/increment, producing an inflated speedup.
> 4. **Mis-stating Amdahl's Law** — Writing $S = 1 + f/s$ instead of $S = 1 / [(1-f) + f/s]$ is a 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **No example code** — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for a worked code snippet; theory alone is incomplete.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**
>
> - **Definition ready:** Optimization = faster / smaller / lower-power with same semantics.
> - **Three pillars:** Time, Space, Power — always trade-off, never all three at once.
> - **Know all 19 techniques** listed in §2.2 by name and trigger condition.
> - **Loop optimization is the single most-tested sub-topic** — master LICM, unrolling, fusion, tiling.
> - **Strength reduction table (must memorize):** `x*2 → x<<1`, `x/2 → x>>1`, `x*2^k → x<<k`, `pow(x,2) → x*x`.
> - **Amdahl's Law** formula: $S = 1 / [(1-f) + f/s]$.
> - **Peephole** operates on **assembly**, not IR.
> - **DCE** removes code that does not affect output (unreachable + unused assignments).
> - **Inlining** = faster call, larger code; **Outlining** = smaller code, slower call.
> - **For 32-bit embedded targets**, `int` multiply = 1 cycle only on Cortex-M3/M4 with hardware MUL; on Cortex-M0 it takes ~32 cycles — strength reduction is critical.
> - **Compiler flags to remember:** `-O0` (none), `-O1` (basic), `-O2` (most), `-O3` (aggressive + unroll), `-Os` (size), `-Ofast` (fast math).
> - **Power formula:** $P = C \cdot V^2 \cdot f$ — reducing CPI directly cuts power.
> - **Speedup must always include loop-control cycles** in KTU numericals.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Efficient Input/Output
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET695 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Efficient Input/Output # Module 4 — Efficient Input/Output ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim-aligned):** > *Efficient Input/Output (I/O) in embedded systems refers to the strategic use of hardware and software mechanisms — polling, interrupts, Direct Memory Access (DMA), memory-mapped I/O, buffering, and power-aware techniques — to transfer data between the CPU, memory, and peripheral devices while optimizing throughput, latency, CPU utilization, and energy consumption.* ### 1.1 Formal Definition In any embedded platform (microcontroller or SoC-based), the processor must continuously interact with **sensors, actuators, communication controllers, displays, and storage units**. Because embedded systems operate under **strict real-time deadlines, limited memory, and constrained power budgets**, the naive approach of letting the CPU handle *every* byte of I/O leads to: - Wasted CPU cycles (busy-waiting) - Missed deadlines (jitter) - Excessive energy drain (continuous clocking) - Poor responsiveness (no concurrency) **Efficient I/O** is therefore the disciplined design discipline that selects the *right transfer mechanism* (programmed I/O, interrupt-driven I/O, or DMA) and pairs it with the *right architectural interface* (memory-mapped vs. isolated I/O) to balance the **three pillars of embedded performance**: $$ \text{Efficiency} \;\longleftrightarrow\; f(\text{Throughput},\;\text{Latency},\;\text{Energy per Byte}) $$ ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital Triage Room" > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy:** > Imagine a **doctor (CPU)** in a hospital emergency ward. Patients (data bytes) keep arriving. | I/O Strategy | Hospital Analogy | Verdict | |---|---|---| | **Polling (Busy-Wait)** | The doctor stands at the door, repeatedly asking *"Anyone here? Anyone here?"* — wasting time even when no patient arrives. | Inefficient | | **Interrupt-driven I/O** | A **nurse** sits at the door. She calls the doctor **only when** a patient arrives. | Smart & responsive | | **DMA** | An **automated conveyor belt** moves patients directly from the entrance to the treatment room. The doctor is freed entirely. | Highest efficiency | **The three pillars of I/O efficiency are therefore: (1) Don't ask — be told, (2) Don't move data yourself — let hardware do it, (3) Don't wake the CPU when you don't have to.** ### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (KTU High-Yield) - **Throughput** — Bytes transferred per second (B/s or Mbps) - **Latency** — Time delay between request and completion (µs / ms) - **CPU Utilization (Ucpu)** — Fraction of CPU time spent on I/O (0 ≤ U ≤ 1) - **Energy per Byte (Eb)** — Joules consumed to transfer 1 byte - **Interrupt Latency** — Delay from IRQ assertion to ISR entry - **Jitter** — Variation in completion time across repeated transfers # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Primary I/O Strategies ### 2.1.1 Programmed I/O — Polling (Busy-Wait) The CPU repeatedly checks a status register bit (e.g., `TX_READY`, `RX_FULL`) until the peripheral signals readiness. **Operational Logic (step-by-step):** 1. CPU writes data into the peripheral's data register. 2. CPU enters a **tight loop** reading the *status register*. 3. Loop continues while the *busy flag* is set. 4. On flag clear → transfer complete, CPU exits loop. **Why inefficient?** The CPU is **locked** in the loop — it cannot execute any application code, schedule another task, or enter a low-power mode. Polling wastes **100% of CPU cycles** devoted to that I/O. ### 2.1.2 Interrupt-Driven I/O The peripheral asserts an **Interrupt Request (IRQ)** line when it needs service. The CPU is free to execute other code; the IRQ triggers an **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**. **Operational Logic:** 1. CPU configures the peripheral's interrupt-enable bit. 2. CPU executes application code. 3. Peripheral raises IRQ → CPU saves context (PC, PSW, registers). 4. ISR runs: handles data, clears the IRQ flag, returns. 5. CPU restores context and resumes prior code. **Why better?** CPU idle time is now available for useful work. However, every byte still costs an interrupt (overhead ≈ 10–100 cycles per ISR entry/exit). ### 2.1.3 Direct Memory Access (DMA) A dedicated **DMA controller** moves blocks of data between peripheral and memory **without CPU intervention**. The CPU is interrupted only **once** (at the end of the block, or on half/full completion). **Operational Logic:** 1. CPU programs the DMA controller: source address, destination address, byte count, transfer mode. 2. CPU issues **one** start command. 3. DMA engine takes ownership of the bus and performs the block transfer. 4. On completion, DMA asserts a single interrupt to the CPU. **Why best?** The CPU overhead is amortized over the **entire block**. For transferring N bytes, the interrupt count drops from **N** (interrupt I/O) to **1** (DMA). This is the most efficient strategy for high-throughput peripherals (UART, SPI, ADC, memory-to-memory copies). ## 2.2 Memory-Mapped I/O vs. Isolated I/O | Aspect | Memory-Mapped I/O (MMIO) | Isolated I/O (Port-mapped) | |---|---|---| | Address space | Peripheral registers share the **same** address space as RAM | Separate I/O address space | | Instructions used | Any load/store (`LDR`, `STR` in ARM) | Special IN/OUT instructions (x86) | | Used in | ARM, RISC-V, most RISC MCUs (STM32, ESP32) | Legacy x86, 8051 | | Flexibility | High — pointer arithmetic works | Limited — dedicated instructions | | Address space stolen from RAM | Yes | No | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Almost all modern embedded systems (ARM Cortex-M, RISC-V) use **Memory-Mapped I/O exclusively**. The term *"I/O address"* simply means *"a memory address that aliases a peripheral register."* ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | CPU Utilization in Polling | $U_{cpu} = 1$ (during transfer) | dimensionless | | 2 | CPU Utilization in Interrupt I/O | $U_{cpu} = \dfrac{C_{isr}}{T_{byte}}$ | dimensionless | | 3 | CPU Utilization in DMA (block of N bytes) | $U_{cpu} = \dfrac{C_{setup} + C_{isr}}{N \cdot T_{byte}}$ | dimensionless | | 4 | Throughput | $S = \dfrac{N_{bytes}}{T_{total}}$ | B/s, Mbps | | 5 | Average Transfer Time (DMA) | $T_{xfer} = T_{setup} + (N-1)\cdot T_{byte} + T_{isr}$ | seconds | | 6 | Energy per Byte | $E_b = \dfrac{P_{cpu}\cdot t_{cpu} + P_{idle}\cdot t_{idle}}{N}$ | J/byte | | 7 | Interrupt Latency | $L_{irq} = t_{detect} + t_{vector} + t_{save}$ | cycles / µs | | 8 | Bandwidth (memory bus) | $BW = f_{bus} \cdot W_{bus}$ | bytes/s | | 9 | Polling vs Interrupt — break-even | $T_{poll\;loop} = T_{isr\_overhead}$ | seconds | | 10 | DMA efficiency gain | $\eta_{DMA} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{N}$ (asymptotic, large N) | dimensionless | **Where:** - $C_{isr}$ = CPU cycles consumed by one interrupt service (entry + exit + body) - $T_{byte}$ = time interval between consecutive bytes arriving at the peripheral - $N$ = number of bytes in the DMA block - $C_{setup}$ = CPU cycles to configure the DMA controller - $P_{cpu}$, $P_{idle}$ = power consumed when CPU is active vs. idle ## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility - **Sensor data acquisition (ADC):** DMA streams thousands of samples from ADC → RAM, freeing the CPU for DSP. - **Display refresh (LTDC, SPI LCD):** DMA feeds frame-buffer pixels to the display continuously. - **Audio codecs (I²S):** Double-buffered DMA streams audio with zero CPU cycles per sample. - **Storage (SD card via SDMMC):** Block transfers in MB/s range impossible without DMA. - **Low-power IoT (BLE, LoRa):** Sleep-mode + interrupt wakeup = years of battery life. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of CPU Utilization for the Three I/O Strategies ### 3.1.1 Setup and Notation Let: - $T_{byte}$ = time between two consecutive bytes that the peripheral produces (or consumes) - $C_{isr}$ = total CPU cycles to enter, execute, and exit the ISR - $C_{poll}$ = CPU cycles per polling loop iteration - $C_{setup}$ = CPU cycles to initialize the DMA channel - $N$ = number of bytes in one DMA block - $f_{cpu}$ = CPU clock frequency The **fraction of CPU time** spent serving the peripheral is what we define as $U_{cpu}$. ### 3.1.2 Polled (Busy-Wait) I/O — Derivation In pure polling, the CPU is *always* executing the check loop while the transfer is in progress. Therefore, during the active transfer window, **every** clock cycle is consumed by I/O handling: $$ \begin{aligned} U_{cpu}^{poll} &= \frac{\text{CPU time spent on I/O}}{\text{Total transfer time}} \\ &= \frac{N \cdot T_{byte}}{N \cdot T_{byte}} = 1.0 \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** The CPU is **100% occupied** by I/O. No other task can run. Polling is justified only for *sub-microsecond* events where interrupt setup itself is more expensive. ### 3.1.3 Interrupt-Driven I/O — Derivation The CPU is interrupted **once per byte** (for a byte-stream peripheral). Each interrupt consumes $C_{isr}$ cycles, and bytes arrive every $T_{byte}$ seconds. So the fraction of time the CPU spends inside ISRs is: $$ \begin{aligned} U_{cpu}^{int} &= \frac{\text{Cycles spent in ISR per byte}}{\text{Cycles per byte interval}} \\ &= \frac{C_{isr}}{T_{byte} \cdot f_{cpu}} \end{aligned} $$ **Numerical example:** Suppose $f_{cpu} = 168\,\text{MHz}$, $T_{byte} = 1\,\mu\text{s}$ (1 Mbyte/s link), $C_{isr} = 200$ cycles. $$ U_{cpu}^{int} = \frac{200}{1 \times 10^{-6} \times 168 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{200}{168} \approx 0.00119 $$ So the CPU spends only **~0.12 %** of its time serving the link — excellent. The other 99.88 % is free for application code. ### 3.1.4 DMA — Derivation With DMA, the CPU configures the channel once (cost $C_{setup}$) and is interrupted once at the end (cost $C_{isr}$). The block contains $N$ bytes, each separated by $T_{byte}$. The total transfer time is roughly $N \cdot T_{byte}$. The CPU's fractional occupancy is therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} U_{cpu}^{DMA} &= \frac{C_{setup} + C_{isr}}{N \cdot T_{byte} \cdot f_{cpu}} \end{aligned} $$ **Comparing the three for a 1024-byte block, $C_{isr}=200$, $C_{setup}=100$, $T_{byte}=1\,\mu s$, $f_{cpu}=168$ MHz:** $$ U_{cpu}^{poll} = 1.000 $$ $$ U_{cpu}^{int} = \frac{200}{168} \approx 0.00119 $$ $$ U_{cpu}^{DMA} = \frac{100 + 200}{1024 \times 1 \times 10^{-6} \times 168 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{300}{172{,}032} \approx 0.00174 $$ **Important:** For *byte-rate* peripherals (every $T_{byte}$ matters), interrupt I/O can have **lower** per-byte overhead than DMA when the block is small. For *burst/block* peripherals, DMA wins by orders of magnitude because it **amortizes** $C_{setup}$ and $C_{isr}$. ### 3.1.5 Asymptotic DMA Efficiency Gain As $N \to \infty$: $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_{DMA} &= 1 - U_{cpu}^{DMA} \\ &= 1 - \frac{C_{setup} + C_{isr}}{N \cdot T_{byte} \cdot f_{cpu}} \\ &\xrightarrow{N \to \infty} 1 \end{aligned} $$ This shows the **scalability** of DMA: a single setup overhead services arbitrarily large transfers. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Polling vs. Interrupt vs. DMA | Dimension | Polling | Interrupt I/O | DMA | |---|---|---|---| | CPU cycles/byte | Very high | Low | Near zero | | Determinism | High (no ISR jitter) | Medium (latency varies) | High (predictable) | | Power (CPU active) | Worst (always on) | Medium (interrupt bursts) | Best (CPU sleeps) | | Power (peripheral) | Worst | Good | Best | | Code complexity | Lowest | Medium | Highest | | Latency to first byte | Lowest | Medium | Medium | | Throughput ceiling | Limited by CPU | Limited by ISR rate | Limited by bus/memory | | Best for | Sub-µs events, GPIO | Byte streams, control | Block transfers | ## 3.3 Power-Efficient I/O Derivation A device in **run mode** consumes $P_{run}$ watts; in **sleep mode** $P_{sleep}$ watts (typically $P_{sleep} \ll P_{run}$). If the CPU must wake $k$ times per second to service I/O, with each wake lasting $t_w$ seconds: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{avg} &= (1 - k \cdot t_w) \cdot P_{sleep} + (k \cdot t_w) \cdot P_{run} \end{aligned} $$ For a periodic sensor sampled at $f_s$ Hz with DMA + sleep: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{avg} &\approx P_{sleep} + f_s \cdot (C_{wake} / f_{cpu}) \cdot (P_{run} - P_{sleep}) \end{aligned} $$ This equation is the foundation of **battery-life estimation** in IoT nodes (e.g., a BLE beacon lasting 5 years on a CR2032 cell). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — I/O Subsystem ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Application Code] --> B{CPU Core
ARM Cortex-M} B --> C[System Bus
AHB/APB] C --> D[Memory
SRAM / Flash] C --> E[DMA Controller] E --> C C --> F[Peripheral Bridge] F --> G[UART] F --> H[SPI] F --> I[I2C] F --> J[ADC / DAC] F --> K[GPIO] F --> L[Timers] M[IRQ Controller
NVIC] --> B G --> M H --> M I --> M J --> M K --> M L --> M classDef cpu fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef mem fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef dma fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef peri fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef irq fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 class B cpu class C,D mem class E dma class F,G,H,I,J,K,L peri class M irq ``` **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows show the **data path**, while the IRQ controller feeds asynchronous events back to the CPU. Notice that the DMA controller sits *on* the bus and can talk to both memory and peripherals **without** passing through the CPU. ## 4.2 Flowchart — I/O Strategy Selection Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Need to Transfer Data] --> B{Data rate?} B -->|Very low /
sub-microsecond| C[Use Polling] B -->|Medium /
byte stream| D{Is data in blocks?} D -->|No, per-byte| E[Use Interrupt-driven I/O] D -->|Yes, block| F{Is DMA available?} F -->|Yes| G[Use DMA with
double buffering] F -->|No| H[Use Interrupt
+ ring buffer] C --> Z[Done] E --> Z G --> Z H --> Z classDef startNode fill:#f9cb9c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px classDef decision fill:#b4a7d6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px classDef action fill:#a2c4c9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px classDef doneNode fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px class A startNode class B,D,F decision class C,E,G,H action class Z doneNode ``` ## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Interrupt-Driven vs. DMA Transfer (1024 bytes) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant CPU as CPU Core participant MEM as Memory (RAM) participant DMA as DMA Controller participant PER as Peripheral (UART RX) Note over CPU,PER: === DMA Mode (efficient) === CPU->>DMA: 1. Configure (src, dst, N=1024, mode=circular) CPU-->>CPU: Execute application code... PER-->>DMA: Byte stream ready (1024 bytes) DMA->>MEM: Block copy (no CPU) DMA-->>CPU: 2. Single IRQ (transfer complete) CPU->>DMA: 3. Clear IRQ, restart Note over CPU,PER: === Interrupt Mode (less efficient) === CPU-->>CPU: Execute application code... loop 1024 times PER-->>CPU: IRQ per byte CPU->>MEM: Store 1 byte end CPU->>CPU: Final housekeeping ``` This sequence diagram makes the **fundamental efficiency insight** visually obvious: the DMA flow has *one* interrupt for the whole block, while the interrupt flow has **N** interrupts (one per byte). At 1024 bytes, DMA has 1024× fewer context-switches. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## **Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)** ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define Direct Memory Access. Mention any two advantages of using DMA in embedded systems.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Direct Memory Access (DMA)** is a hardware mechanism that allows peripheral-to-memory (or memory-to-memory) data transfer **without continuous CPU intervention**. The CPU configures the DMA controller once — source address, destination address, byte count, and trigger — and is interrupted only upon transfer completion. **Two advantages:** 1. **CPU offloading** — frees the CPU to execute application code while data moves in the background. *(1 mark)* 2. **Higher throughput** — block transfers at bus speed, not limited by ISR execution rate. *(1 mark)* 3. **Lower energy per byte** — CPU can sleep during transfer. *(1 mark — any two)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between memory-mapped I/O and isolated I/O.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Memory-Mapped I/O | Isolated I/O | |---|---|---| | Address space | Shared with RAM *(1 mark)* | Separate *(1 mark)* | | Instructions | Normal `LDR`/`STR` | Special `IN`/`OUT` (x86) *(1 mark)* | --- ## **Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]** **(a)** With the help of neat diagrams, explain the three different I/O strategies used in embedded systems: **polling, interrupt-driven I/O, and DMA**. Compare them on the basis of CPU utilization and latency. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A UART produces one byte every 1 µs. The CPU runs at 100 MHz, the ISR consumes 250 cycles per entry, and the DMA setup consumes 150 cycles (with one final ISR).
