Calculate the CPU utilization for: (i) interrupt-driven I/O, (ii) DMA transferring a block of 2048 bytes. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 marks** **Polled I/O (Busy-Wait):** - CPU writes a byte to UART's TX register, then **loops** reading the status register. - Loop continues while the *TX-busy* flag is set. - CPU is locked 100% of the time. *(1 mark)* **Interrupt-Driven I/O:** - CPU enables the UART's TX-empty interrupt and **returns to the application**. - UART raises IRQ when ready → CPU saves context, runs ISR to write next byte, returns. *(1 mark)* **DMA-based I/O:** - CPU configures the DMA channel once: source buffer, UART TX register, byte count, peripheral trigger. - DMA engine autonomously moves each byte to the UART's data register. - CPU is interrupted **once** when the entire block finishes. *(1 mark)* **Comparison Table:** | Metric | Polling | Interrupt I/O | DMA | |---|---|---|---| | CPU utilization | ~100 % | Low | Very low (block) | | Latency to first byte | Lowest | Medium | Medium | | Power (CPU active) | Worst | Medium | Best | | Code complexity | Low | Medium | High | | Best use | Sub-µs GPIO | Byte streams | Bulk transfers | *(3 marks for table + brief commentary. 1 mark for the diagrams — a flowchart similar to the one in SECTION 4.2.)* --- #### **Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 marks** **Given:** - $T_{byte} = 1\,\mu s$, $f_{cpu} = 100\,\text{MHz}$, $C_{isr} = 250$ cycles, $C_{setup} = 150$ cycles, $N = 2048$. **(i) Interrupt-driven I/O:** *(3 marks)* $$ \begin{aligned} U_{cpu}^{int} &= \frac{C_{isr}}{T_{byte} \cdot f_{cpu}} \\ &= \frac{250}{1 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 \times 10^{6}} \\ &= \frac{250}{100} = 2.5 \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall — saturating utilization:** $U_{cpu} = 2.5 > 1$ means the **CPU cannot keep up** with the data rate using interrupts alone. The system is *overloaded* — this is precisely when DMA is mandatory. **(ii) DMA for 2048-byte block:** *(4 marks)* $$ \begin{aligned} U_{cpu}^{DMA} &= \frac{C_{setup} + C_{isr}}{N \cdot T_{byte} \cdot f_{cpu}} \\ &= \frac{150 + 250}{2048 \times 1 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 \times 10^{6}} \\ &= \frac{400}{204{,}800} \\ &\approx 0.00195 \; \text{(i.e., 0.195 \%)} \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** DMA drops the CPU load from **over 250 %** (unsustainable interrupt) to **under 0.2 %** — a >1000× reduction. This justifies DMA for any high-rate peripheral. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** **(a)** Explain **memory-mapped I/O** in detail. How is it different from port-mapped I/O? List any four features of memory-mapped I/O. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the role of **double buffering** in DMA-based data transfer. Why is it important in real-time embedded systems? Illustrate with an example of a continuous audio sample stream at 44.1 kHz on a 168 MHz Cortex-M4. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 marks** **Memory-Mapped I/O (MMIO):** Peripheral control and data registers are mapped into the **same address space** as program memory and data memory. The CPU accesses them with ordinary load/store instructions (e.g., `LDR r0, [r1]` where `r1` holds a peripheral's address). *(2 marks)* **Difference from Port-Mapped (Isolated) I/O:** *(3 marks)* | Feature | MMIO | Port-Mapped | |---|---|---| | Address space | Unified | Separate I/O space | | Instructions | Normal loads/stores | Special `IN`/`OUT` | | Address decoding | Single bus decoder | Two decoders (memory + I/O) | | Used in | ARM, RISC-V, MIPS | 8086, 8051 (legacy) | | Pointer arithmetic | Works | Does not work | **Four features of MMIO:** 1. Unified address space — same `LDR/STR` works for RAM and peripherals. 2. Compiler-friendly — C pointers dereference registers naturally. 3. Wider data width — any access size (8/16/32-bit) supported. 4. No dedicated I/O bus — saves silicon pins and decoding logic. *(2 marks for features.)* --- #### **Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 marks** **Double Buffering in DMA:** *(3 marks)* In double-buffering, **two memory buffers** (Buffer A and Buffer B) are configured for a DMA stream. While the DMA controller is **filling** Buffer A, the CPU is **processing** Buffer B. When DMA finishes Buffer A, the roles **swap automatically** (via "half-transfer" + "transfer-complete" interrupts). This guarantees: - **Zero-copy** continuous streaming — no data loss. - **No manual intervention** between buffer flips. - **Deterministic latency** — CPU has exactly one buffer's worth of time to process. **Audio Example (44.1 kHz, 16-bit stereo = 44,100 × 4 bytes/s ≈ 176 KB/s):** *(4 marks)* - CPU at 168 MHz, DMA bus ≈ 21 MB/s (sufficient headroom). - Each buffer of 1024 samples = 4096 bytes. - Time to fill one buffer = 1024 / 44100 ≈ **23.2 ms**. - CPU gets 23.2 ms to run DSP/effects on the *other* buffer. - DMA + I²S interrupt half-transfer swaps roles seamlessly. **Why critical in real-time systems:** 1. Prevents **buffer underrun** (which would cause audio clicks/glitches). 2. Decouples **producer** (DMA) from **consumer** (CPU) speed. 3. Enables **predictable scheduling** in hard real-time kernels (FreeRTOS, RTX). > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall — Common mistakes students make:** > 1. **Confusing MMIO with DMA** — they are *orthogonal*. MMIO is the *address-decoding* method; DMA is the *transfer* method. They can be combined. > 2. **Forgetting to clear the interrupt flag** in the ISR — causes the ISR to re-enter immediately (infinite loop / system hang). **Always** read the status register that clears the flag, or write a `1` to clear it (as in many ARM NVIC designs). > 3. **Not disabling interrupts inside the ISR** when modifying shared buffers — leads to race conditions. Use `__disable_irq()` / `__enable_irq()` or a lock-free ring buffer. > 4. **Mixing up $C_{isr}$ units** — must be in **cycles**, then divide by $T_{byte} \cdot f_{cpu}$ (in seconds × Hz) to get a *dimensionless* fraction. Watch the unit cancellation carefully. > 5. **Ignoring the "buffer-flip" interrupt** in circular DMA — students often enable only the transfer-complete IRQ, missing the half-transfer IRQ that *enables* double buffering. --- ## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] **Three I/O strategies**: Polling (CPU 100 %), Interrupt I/O (CPU per byte), DMA (CPU per block). - [x] **DMA** is a *bus master* that moves data autonomously; CPU is interrupted **once** per block. - [x] **Memory-Mapped I/O** = unified address space, normal `LDR/STR`, used in ARM/RISC-V. - [x] **Isolated I/O** = separate address space + `IN/OUT` instructions (legacy x86, 8051). - [x] **CPU utilization (DMA)** = $\dfrac{C_{setup} + C_{isr}}{N \cdot T_{byte} \cdot f_{cpu}}$ — key formula for KTU. - [x] **CPU utilization (Interrupt)** = $\dfrac{C_{isr}}{T_{byte} \cdot f_{cpu}}$ — if this exceeds 1, the system is overloaded. - [x] **Double buffering** = two buffers swapped via half-transfer + transfer-complete IRQs. - [x] **Power efficiency** = CPU can sleep while DMA runs; $E_b$ drops dramatically. - [x] **Always clear the IRQ flag** in the ISR (often done by *reading* the data register). - [x] **Polling** is acceptable only for sub-µs, deterministic GPIO events; otherwise prefer interrupts or DMA. - [x] **DMA is mandatory** for high-throughput peripherals: SDMMC, USB, LCD, ADC, I²S audio. - [x] **Three pillars of efficient I/O**: (1) Don't ask — be told, (2) Don't move data yourself, (3) Don't wake the CPU unnecessarily.

topics.title: Testing and Debugging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET695 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Testing and Debugging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧠 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Testing** is the systematic process of executing an embedded system (hardware + firmware) under controlled conditions to verify that it meets its specified functional, performance, and reliability requirements and to *identify defects* prior to deployment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Debugging** is the iterative diagnostic process of *localizing, isolating, and removing* the root cause of a defect (bug) discovered during testing or field operation, often using specialized tools such as JTAG probes, logic analyzers, in-circuit emulators, and on-chip debug modules.

In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, testing and debugging are evaluated under **CO4 (Design and test embedded systems using modern tools)** mapped to **Bloom's Level: Analyze (L4) and Evaluate (L5)**.

The two disciplines are tightly coupled but distinct:

| Aspect | Testing | Debugging |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Find defects / verify correctness | Locate and fix defects |
| **When** | Pre-deployment, repeatedly | After a defect is known |
| **Outcome** | Pass/Fail verdict, test report | Patched code, root-cause analysis |
| **Tools** | Test harnesses, harnesses, simulators, HIL | JTAG, oscilloscope, printf, GDB |
| **Metric** | Code coverage, defect density | Time-to-fix, MTTR |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Car Workshop" Intuition

Imagine you have just manufactured a car at a factory. Before shipping it to the customer:

1. **Testing** = The inspection bay. The car is driven on a treadmill (dynamometer), brakes are slammed, airbags are deployed — *you are looking for problems*.
2. **Debugging** = The mechanic under the hood. Once the inspection bay reports "the left indicator is flickering," the mechanic opens the wiring harness, traces the wire with a multimeter, finds a loose connector, and solders it back. *You are fixing the problem*.

> [!TIP]
> **Student Memory Hook:** *Testing tells you "something is wrong." Debugging tells you "what is wrong and how to fix it."* In KTU board exams, mixing these two terms in a definition question costs easy marks.

## 1.3 Why Testing & Debugging is Uniquely Hard in Embedded Systems

Unlike pure software, embedded systems have **three extra dimensions** that make testing & debugging fundamentally harder:

1. **Hardware-Software Co-existence** — Bugs may originate from either silicon, firmware timing, electrical noise, or their interaction.
2. **Real-Time Constraints** — A bug that appears only at 1 ms intervals is invisible to a slow debugger.
3. **Limited Observability** — You cannot easily "print to screen"; you may have only a few GPIO pins to view internal state.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often treat embedded testing as identical to desktop software testing. Examiners specifically test whether you understand the **real-time, resource-constrained, hardware-coupled** nature of embedded debugging.

## 1.4 Core Categories of Embedded Testing

The widely accepted taxonomy (used in industry and adopted in KTU syllabus references) is:

- **Unit Testing** — Individual functions/modules in isolation.
- **Integration Testing** — Interaction between software modules and between software ↔ hardware.
- **System Testing** — Complete embedded product against its specification.
- **Acceptance Testing** — User/customer validation against the SRS document.
- **Regression Testing** — Re-running old tests after every change to ensure no new bugs.

Embedded-specific additions:

- **Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) Testing** — Plant simulator + real ECU.
- **Software-in-the-Loop (SIL) Testing** — Plant model + simulated processor.
- **Processor-in-the-Loop (PIL) Testing** — Plant model + real processor running on dev board.

## 1.5 Core Debugging Toolchain (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **JTAG (IEEE 1149.1)** is the **de facto standard** for embedded boundary-scan and debug. KTU examiners expect you to know that JTAG provides 5 pins: **TDI, TDO, TMS, TCK, TRST**.

Additional essential tools:

- **GDB + OpenOCD** — Open-source software debugger with on-chip bridge.
- **Logic Analyzer** — Captures multiple digital signals over time.
- **Oscilloscope** — Views analog/digital waveforms (timing bugs).
- **In-Circuit Emulator (ICE)** — Replaces the actual MCU with a hardware clone having full visibility.
- **On-Chip Debug (OCD)** — Built into modern MCUs (e.g., ARM CoreSight, SWD).
- **Printf/Semihosting** — Cheap, intrusive, slow.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The V-Model for Embedded Testing (KTU Exam Favorite)

The **V-Model** is a development lifecycle in which each development phase has a corresponding testing phase on the opposite arm of the "V". It is explicitly referenced in KTU module outcomes for verification & validation (V&V).

```
Requirements   ⇄  Acceptance Testing
   ↓                ↑
System Design   ⇄  System Testing
   ↓                ↑
Module Design   ⇄  Integration Testing
   ↓                ↑
Unit Design     ⇄  Unit Testing
   ↓                ↑
             Coding
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU loves this:** The V-Model is the cleanest way to map *each level of design* to *a specific level of testing*. Board questions frequently ask: "At what stage is integration testing carried out?" Answer: *After module-level coding, against the module design document.*

## 2.2 Testing Levels — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Unit Testing
- Tests the smallest testable parts (functions, classes, ISR handlers).
- Done by the developer.
- Uses **stubs** (simulated lower-level modules) and **drivers** (calling code).
- In embedded C, unit testing frameworks: **Unity, CMocka, CppUTest**.

### 2.2.2 Integration Testing
- Tests interaction between modules.
- Two strategies:
  - **Big-Bang** — Integrate all at once, test the whole. (Faster, but bug localization is poor.)
  - **Incremental** — Add modules one by one. Two flavors: **Top-Down** (uses stubs for lower modules) and **Bottom-Up** (uses drivers for higher modules).

### 2.2.3 System Testing
- Tests the complete integrated product.
- Subsumes: *performance, stress, security, recovery, usability, compliance*.
- Performed in a lab environment using HIL rigs.

### 2.2.4 Acceptance Testing
- **Alpha testing** — In-house, simulated user environment.
- **Beta testing** — Limited external users in real environment.

### 2.2.5 Regression Testing
- Re-execution of previously passed tests after a code change.
- Maintains **test suite** with version control.

## 2.3 Debugging Techniques — Hierarchical View

### 2.3.1 Static Debugging
- **Code review / Inspection** — Manual line-by-line review (most cost-effective defect remover historically).
- **Static analysis** — Tools like **Lint, PC-Lint, Coverity, MISRA-C checkers** analyze code without executing it.
- **Model checking** — Formal verification of state machines (e.g., SPIN, NuSMV).

### 2.3.2 Dynamic Debugging
- **Print statements (printf debugging)** — Inexpensive, but alters timing — *dangerous in real-time systems*.
- **Interactive debugger** — GDB, IAR, Keil µVision — breakpoints, watchpoints, step-through.
- **Logic analyzer / Oscilloscope** — External hardware probes.
- **In-Circuit Emulator (ICE)** — Gold standard but expensive; full memory & register visibility.
- **On-Chip Debug (OCD)** — Built into the MCU; uses JTAG/SWD to control the core.

### 2.3.3 Advanced
- **Trace** — ARM Embedded Trace Macrocell (ETM), Instruction Trace Macrocell (ITM).
- **Profiling** — gprof, perf, OZone.
- **Assertion-based** — `assert()` in code; catches invariant violations early.

## 2.4 JTAG — Deep Working (KTU High-Yield)

JTAG (Joint Test Action Group, IEEE 1149.1) works on the principle of a **boundary-scan chain**.

**Architecture:**
- Each IC pin has a **boundary-scan cell** (a multiplexer + flip-flop) inserted between the core logic and the physical pin.
- These cells are linked in a serial chain throughout the board.
- The **TAP (Test Access Port) controller** — a 16-state FSM — drives the chain based on TMS signals clocked by TCK.

**Five mandatory pins:**
- **TCK** — Test Clock
- **TMS** — Test Mode Select
- **TDI** — Test Data In
- **TDO** — Test Data Out
- **TRST** — Test Reset (optional)

**Operations possible via JTAG:**
1. **Bypass** — Skip a chip.
2. **Extest** — Test inter-IC wiring.
3. **Intest** — Test internal logic of the chip.
4. **Sample/Preload** — Snapshot pin states.
5. **Idcode** — Read device identifier.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are examinable for problems involving test metrics, complexity, and reliability.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Statement Coverage} \; (S_c) &= \frac{\text{Executed Statements}}{\text{Total Statements}} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
\text{Branch Coverage} \; (B_c) &= \frac{\text{Executed Branches}}{\text{Total Branches}} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
\text{Path Coverage} \; (P_c) &= \frac{\text{Executed Paths}}{\text{Total Paths}} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
\text{Defect Density} \; (D_d) &= \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{KLOC}} \\[4pt]
\text{Cyclomatic Complexity} \; (V(G)) &= E - N + 2P \\[4pt]
\text{MTTF} &= \frac{\text{Total Operational Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}} \\[4pt]
\text{MTBF} &= \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR} \\[4pt]
\text{Availability} \; (A) &= \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
\text{Test Effectiveness} &= \frac{\text{Defects Found by Tests}}{\text{Defects Found by Tests + Defects Found in Field}} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $E$ | Edges in control flow graph | dimensionless |
| $N$ | Nodes in control flow graph | dimensionless |
| $P$ | Connected components | Usually $P = 1$ for a single function |
| $D_d$ | Defect Density | defects per KLOC |
| MTTF | Mean Time To Failure | hours |
| MTTR | Mean Time To Repair | hours |
| MTBF | Mean Time Between Failures | hours |
| $A$ | System Availability | $\%$, target typically $99.999\%$ (five 9s) for telecom |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick Question Alert:** Students often confuse **MTTF** (non-repairable, e.g., a satellite chip) with **MTBF** (repairable, e.g., a server). Examiners love this distinction.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (ISO 26262)** — Every ECU must pass HIL tests with > 95% branch coverage.
- **Aerospace (DO-178C)** — Software Level A requires **MC/DC (Modified Condition/Decision Coverage)**.
- **Medical Devices (IEC 62304)** — Mandatory V&V documentation for FDA approval.
- **IoT/Consumer** — OTA firmware updates require robust regression testing to avoid bricking devices.

## 2.7 Debugging Real-Time Systems — Special Considerations

> [!WARNING]
> **Stop-the-world debuggers break real-time behavior.** When you halt the CPU at a breakpoint, interrupts pile up, timers overflow, and the system state mutates. The bug you were hunting disappears.

**Solutions:**
- **Watchpoints** — Halt only when a memory location changes value.
- **Data breakpoints with trace** — Record without halting.
- **Real-Time Trace (ETM, TraceX)** — Continuous execution history with timestamps.
- **Statistical profiling** — Sample PC periodically with a high-priority timer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Cyclomatic Complexity (McCabe)

**Problem:** Given the following C code for an embedded temperature controller, compute the cyclomatic complexity using the formula $V(G) = E - N + 2P$.

```c
int control_loop(int temp) {
    int mode = 0;
    if (temp > 100) {           // Decision 1
        mode = 1;
    } else if (temp < 0) {      // Decision 2
        mode = 2;
    } else {
        mode = 3;
    }

    switch (mode) {             // Decision 3
        case 1:  turnOnFan();   break;
        case 2:  turnOnHeater();break;
        default: logStatus();   break;
    }

    for (int i = 0; i < 10; i++) {  // Decision 4
        if (i % 2 == 0) {           // Decision 5
            sendData(i);
        }
    }
    return mode;
}
```

**Step 1: Count the decision points (predicate nodes)**

The number of decisions $D = 5$, so the number of edges in the simplified McCabe formula is:

$$
V(G) = D + 1
$$

Substituting $D = 5$:

$$
V(G) = 5 + 1 = 6
$$

**Step 2: Verify using the flow graph formula $V(G) = E - N + 2P$**

Build the control flow graph (CFG):

- Nodes $N = 11$ (each statement block).
- Edges $E = 16$ (each arrow including back-edges from the for loop).
- Connected components $P = 1$ (single function).

$$
V(G) = 16 - 11 + 2(1) = 7
$$

Wait — discrepancy! This is because the `if-else if-else` chain contributes 3 edges from 2 decision nodes, while my count above used a simplified $D + 1$ that ignores one branch. **The correct, exact value via McCabe's original definition (counting edges from predicate nodes) is $V(G) = 7$**.

**Step 3: Interpretation**

> [!NOTE]
> $V(G) = 7$ implies that to achieve **100% path coverage**, exactly **7 independent test cases** are required. Industry rule: any function with $V(G) > 10$ is considered high-risk and should be refactored.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Statement & Branch Coverage

**Problem:** A test suite runs against the following code. Test data used: $T_1 = 50$, $T_2 = 150$, $T_3 = -10$. Compute Statement Coverage and Branch Coverage.

```c
void check(int T) {
    int flag = 0;
    if (T > 100) {
        flag = 1;
    }
    if (T < 0) {
        flag = 2;
    }
    if (flag == 1) {
        printf("HOT\n");
    }
}
```

**Step 1: Identify total statements and branches**

Total statements $S_{\text{total}} = 7$ (declarations + 3 `if` blocks + printf).

Total branches: each `if` has 2 branches (true/false), so $B_{\text{total}} = 6$.

**Step 2: Trace test cases**

| Test | T > 100? | T < 0? | flag == 1? | Statements Executed | Branches Covered |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $T_1 = 50$  | F | F | F | 1,2,3,5,7 | F,F,F |
| $T_2 = 150$ | T | F | T | 1,2,3,4,5,6,7 | T,F,T |
| $T_3 = -10$ | F | T | F | 1,2,3,5,7 | F,T,F |

Union of executed statements: $\{1,2,3,4,5,6,7\} \Rightarrow$ all 7 statements.

$$
S_c = \frac{7}{7} \times 100\% = 100\%
$$

Union of covered branches: $\{T, F, T, F, T, F\} \Rightarrow$ all 6 branches covered.

$$
B_c = \frac{6}{6} \times 100\% = 100\%
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Reliability Metrics

**Problem:** An embedded ECU logs 4800 hours of operation, experiences 3 failures, and each repair takes on average 4 hours. Compute MTTF, MTTR, MTBF, and Availability.

**Step 1: Compute MTTF**

$$
\text{MTTF} = \frac{\text{Total Operational Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}} = \frac{4800}{3} = 1600 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2: Identify MTTR**

$$
\text{MTTR} = 4 \text{ hours} \quad (\text{given})
$$

**Step 3: Compute MTBF**

$$
\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR} = 1600 + 4 = 1604 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Availability**

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\% = \frac{1604}{1604 + 4} \times 100\%
$$

$$
A = \frac{1604}{1608} \times 100\% \approx 99.75\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners love this style of question — combining MTTF, MTBF, and Availability in one calculation. Memorize the relations; do not confuse them.

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — JTAG Chain Length Calculation

**Problem:** Four ICs are daisy-chained on a JTAG boundary scan. The instruction register lengths are 8, 4, 12, and 6 bits respectively. How many TCK pulses are required to shift in a complete instruction?

**Step 1:** The total instruction register length equals the sum of all IR lengths.

$$
L_{\text{IR}} = 8 + 4 + 12 + 6 = 30 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2:** Each bit requires one TCK rising edge to shift, so:

$$
N_{\text{TCK}} = L_{\text{IR}} = 30 \text{ pulses}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> If the question asks about the **data register** instead, use the sum of the corresponding DR lengths.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What Debugging Tool

| Symptom | Likely Bug Type | Recommended Tool | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| Output wrong value once | Logic error in C code | GDB breakpoint + watch | Single-step into function |
| Sporadic glitch every 1 ms | Timing / interrupt race | Logic analyzer + GPIO toggle | Visualize signal vs. ISR entry |
| Hard fault on specific input | Stack overflow or null deref | HardFault handler + map file | Inspect LR, PC at fault |
| Works on dev board, fails in field | EMI / power / connector | Oscilloscope + power probe | Analog waveform analysis |
| Memory corruption over time | Buffer overrun, uninitialized pointer | Static analyzer (Coverity) + MPU | Detect at compile time |
| Code runs but slow | Cache miss / inefficient algo | Profiler (gprof, perf) | Sample PC over time |
| Cannot reproduce in lab | HIL needed | HIL simulator | Repeatable stimulus |
| Multi-core sync issue | Lock contention | Trace + ETM | Post-mortem sequence |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Embedded Testing Workflow (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Requirements SRS] --> B[System Design]
    B --> C[Module Design]
    C --> D[Unit Code]
    D --> E[Unit Test]
    E -->|Pass| F[Integration]
    F --> G[Integration Test]
    G -->|Pass| H[System Integration]
    H --> I[System Test]
    I -->|Pass| J[HIL Test]
    J -->|Pass| K[Acceptance Test]
    K -->|Pass| L[Release]
    E -->|Fail| M[Debug Loop]
    G -->|Fail| M
    I -->|Fail| M
    J -->|Fail| M
    M --> D
```

## 4.2 JTAG Boundary Scan Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph hostPC[Host PC]
        devSW[Debugger Software e.g. GDB]
    end
    subgraph probeHW[JTAG Probe e.g. J-Link]
        tapC[TAP Controller]
    end
    subgraph targetBoard[Target Board]
        bs1[Boundary Scan Cell IC1]
        bs2[Boundary Scan Cell IC2]
        bs3[Boundary Scan Cell IC3]
        bs4[Boundary Scan Cell IC4]
    end
    devSW -->|USB / Ethernet| tapC
    tapC -->|TCK| bs1
    tapC -->|TMS| bs1
    tapC -->|TDI| bs1
    bs1 -->|TDO| bs2
    bs2 -->|TDO| bs3
    bs3 -->|TDO| bs4
    bs4 -->|TDO| tapC
    bs1 -.-> bs2
    bs2 -.-> bs3
    bs3 -.-> bs4
```

> [!NOTE]
> The TAP Controller orchestrates the boundary scan chain through a 16-state FSM. The data flows serially TDI → IC1 → IC2 → ... → IC4 → TDO.

## 4.3 V-Model for Embedded V&V

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    req[Requirements Analysis] --> sysDes[System Design]
    sysDes --> modDes[Module Design]
    modDes --> unitDes[Unit Design]
    unitDes --> cod[Implementation Coding]
    cod --> unitTest[Unit Testing]
    unitTest --> intTest[Integration Testing]
    intTest --> sysTest[System Testing]
    sysTest --> accTest[Acceptance Testing]
    accTest -.-> req
    sysTest -.-> sysDes
    intTest -.-> modDes
    unitTest -.-> unitDes
```

## 4.4 Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) Test Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph realWorld[Real Plant Environment]
        sensor[Physical Sensor e.g. Thermocouple]
        actuator[Physical Actuator e.g. Motor]
    end
    subgraph hilRig[HIL Simulator]
        plantModel[Real-Time Plant Model runs on Simulator]
        ioBoard[Analog / Digital IO Interface]
        faultInject[Fault Injection Module]
    end
    subgraph realECU[Real ECU Under Test]
        mcu[Target Microcontroller]
        fw[Embedded Firmware]
    end
    sensor -.->|simulated signal| ioBoard
    ioBoard --> mcu
    mcu -->|PWM / GPIO| ioBoard
    ioBoard --> actuator
    faultInject -.-> mcu
    plantModel --- ioBoard
```

> [!TIP]
> In HIL, the **real sensor and actuator are replaced by simulation**, but the **ECU under test is the actual production hardware**. This makes HIL the gold standard for ECU validation in the automotive industry.

## 4.5 Debugging Methodology Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sym[Observed Symptom] --> repro[Reproduce Consistently]
    repro --> localize[Localize: Where in code]
    localize --> isolate[Isolate: What condition triggers it]
    isolate --> hyp[Form Hypothesis]
    hyp --> exp[Design Experiment]
    exp -->|Confirmed| fix[Apply Fix]
    exp -->|Refuted| hyp
    fix --> retest[Re-test]
    retest --> regress[Regression Test]
    regress --> close[Close Bug Report]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between verification and validation in embedded system testing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Verification | Validation |
|---|---|---|
| **Question it answers** | Are we building the product *right*? | Are we building the *right* product? |
| **Purpose** | Checks conformance to design specs | Checks conformance to user needs |
| **Methods** | Reviews, inspections, walkthroughs, static analysis | Black-box testing, acceptance testing, HIL |
| **Stage** | During development | After build, before delivery |

> **Mnemonic:** *Verification = specs match; Validation = needs match.* (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for difference, 1 mark for examples.)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**List any three on-chip debugging features commonly available in modern microcontrollers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Hardware breakpoints** — set breakpoints in ROM/Flash without code modification.
2. **Watchpoints** — halt when a memory location or peripheral register is accessed.
3. **Single-stepping** — execute one instruction at a time.
4. **Trace (ETM/ITM)** — record instruction execution history with timestamps.
5. **Register & memory inspection** — read/write CPU registers and memory in real time.

*(Any 3, with brief explanation: 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Choice)

### 📌 Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **V-Model** of testing as applied to embedded system development. Discuss the various levels of testing with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the architecture and working of the **JTAG boundary scan** technique. List the mandatory pins and explain any two JTAG instructions. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — V-Model Explanation (7 marks):**

The **V-Model** is a software development lifecycle in which each development phase on the left descending arm has a corresponding testing phase on the right ascending arm, ensuring that every level of design is verified against a specific level of testing.

**Phases (left arm — development):**

1. **Requirements Analysis** — Functional, non-functional, safety, regulatory requirements.
2. **System Design** — Architecture, hardware-software partitioning, RTOS selection.
3. **Module Design** — Internal module interfaces, data structures.
4. **Unit Design** — Algorithm design for individual functions.

**Coding Phase** at the bottom connects to testing on the right arm.

**Phases (right arm — testing):**

1. **Unit Testing** — Tests individual functions in isolation. *Example:* testing a UART init function.
2. **Integration Testing** — Tests inter-module interaction. *Example:* UART driver + DMA controller together.
3. **System Testing** — Tests the complete product. *Example:* full firmware load on target hardware.
4. **Acceptance Testing** — Customer/end-user validation. *Example:* field trial with beta users.

**Advantages:** Early defect detection, clear traceability between requirements and tests.

**Disadvantage:** Rigid; no early working prototype.

*(Each level explained with an example = 7 marks: 1 mark per level × 4 = 4 marks + 2 marks for diagram/advantages + 1 mark for example.)*

**Part (b) — JTAG Boundary Scan (7 marks):**

**Architecture (3 marks):**

JTAG (Joint Test Action Group, IEEE 1149.1) inserts a **boundary-scan cell** between each IC's core logic and its physical pin. These cells are connected in a serial scan chain throughout the board, governed by a **TAP (Test Access Port) controller** — a 16-state FSM.

**Mandatory pins (2 marks):**
- **TCK** — Test Clock
- **TMS** — Test Mode Select
- **TDI** — Test Data In
- **TDO** — Test Data Out
- **TRST** — Test Reset (optional)

**Working:** The TAP controller sequences through states based on TMS, clocked by TCK. In **Shift-DR** state, data is shifted in via TDI, propagated through all boundary cells, and out via TDO. The cells can either **capture** pin states or **drive** them with test patterns.

**Two JTAG instructions (2 marks):**
1. **BYPASS (4'b1111)** — Places a 1-bit register between TDI and TDO of that chip, effectively skipping it. Used to isolate a chip from the chain.
2. **EXTEST (4'b0000)** — Drives test patterns onto output pins and captures responses on input pins. Used to test **inter-IC connections** (PCB shorts, opens, solder defects).
3. *Optional:* **SAMPLE/PRELOAD (4'b0011)** — Snapshots pin states without disturbing normal operation.

---

### 📌 Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **levels of testing** in embedded systems. Compare **top-down** and **bottom-up** integration testing strategies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **cyclomatic complexity**? For the control flow graph shown below, compute $V(G)$ and state the number of independent test paths required.

```
        [Start]
           |
          (1)
         /    \
       (2)    (3)
       |       |
      (4)-----(5)
       |       |
      (6)     (7)
        \     /
         (8)
           |
        [End]
```

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Levels of Testing (7 marks):**

1. **Unit Testing** — Tests smallest testable units (functions, ISRs). Tools: Unity, Ceedling.
2. **Integration Testing** — Tests interaction between modules. Top-down or Bottom-up.
3. **System Testing** — Tests complete integrated system. Includes performance, stress, security.
4. **Acceptance Testing** — Alpha (in-house) and Beta (external users).
5. **Regression Testing** — Re-runs old test cases after each change.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Top-Down | Bottom-Up |
|---|---|---|
| **Order** | Tests top-level modules first | Tests low-level modules first |
| **Stubs/Drivers** | Requires stubs for lower modules | Requires drivers for higher modules |
| **Earliest visible result** | High-level skeleton works early | Low-level utilities tested first |
| **Fault localization** | Easier for high-level errors | Easier for low-level errors |
| **Best suited for** | Systems with well-defined top architecture | Systems built from hardware drivers up |

*(1 mark per level + 1 mark for diagram + 3 marks for comparison table = 7 marks.)*

**Part (b) — Cyclomatic Complexity (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Cyclomatic complexity $V(G)$, introduced by Thomas McCabe (1976), measures the number of linearly independent paths through a program's source code. It is computed as:

$$
V(G) = E - N + 2P
$$

where $E$ = edges, $N$ = nodes, $P$ = connected components. It also equals the number of decision points plus 1.

**Step 1: Count nodes (1 mark)**

Nodes: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8 → **N = 8**

**Step 2: Count edges (1 mark)**

Edges: (1-2), (1-3), (2-4), (3-5), (4-5), (5-6), (5-7), (6-8), (7-8), (8-End) → **E = 10**

**Step 3: Apply formula (1 mark)**

$$
V(G) = 10 - 8 + 2(1) = 4
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation (2 marks):**

- **Number of independent test paths required for 100% path coverage = 4.**
- Industry rule: $V(G) \leq 10$ for low-risk functions; refactor if higher.
- Each edge from a predicate node adds a new path.

*(2 marks definition + 4 marks calculation steps + 2 marks interpretation = 8 marks... compressed to 7 marks total as per KTU marking.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing Verification & Validation** — A 1-mark cut if definitions are interchanged.
> 2. **Missing the 'P' term** — Forgetting $+2P$ in McCabe's formula costs 1 mark.
> 3. **JTAG pin list** — Writing only 4 pins (forgetting TRST is optional but commonly listed) may lose 0.5 mark. Mention that TRST is optional.
> 4. **Static vs Dynamic confusion** — Static analysis does NOT execute the code; stating otherwise costs 1 mark.
> 5. **MTTF vs MTBF** — If a question mentions a repairable system, you MUST use MTBF, not MTTF.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember 🚀

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Testing & Debugging**

- ✅ **V-Model:** 4 development stages on left ↔ 4 testing stages on right.
- ✅ **Testing Levels (in order):** Unit → Integration → System → Acceptance → Regression.
- ✅ **Integration Strategies:** Top-Down (uses stubs) vs Bottom-Up (uses drivers).
- ✅ **JTAG Pins (5):** TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO, TRST (optional).
- ✅ **Boundary Scan** detects PCB **inter-IC wiring** faults (shorts, opens, solder bridges).
- ✅ **Cyclomatic Complexity:** $V(G) = E - N + 2P$; threshold = 10; independent paths = $V(G)$.
- ✅ **MTTF** for non-repairable; **MTBF = MTTF + MTTR** for repairable.
- ✅ **Availability:** $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$.
- ✅ **HIL:** Real ECU + simulated plant (sensors/actuators virtual).
- ✅ **SIL:** Simulated processor + simulated plant.
- ✅ **OCD (On-Chip Debug)** is built into the MCU — examples: ARM CoreSight, SWD.
- ✅ **Printf debugging is intrusive** — alters real-time behavior; avoid in hard real-time systems.
- ✅ **Static analysis** tools: Lint, MISRA-C, Coverity — work *without* code execution.
- ✅ **Dynamic analysis** tools: GDB, Valgrind, profilers — require code execution.
- ✅ **Code Coverage metrics:** Statement, Branch, Path, MC/DC (highest level, used in DO-178C).
- ✅ **Defect Density:** Defects per KLOC (Kilo Lines Of Code).
- ✅ **Software Standards to remember:** ISO 26262 (automotive), DO-178C (aerospace), IEC 62304 (medical), MISRA-C (safety-critical C).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Verify the Software on the Host System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET695 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Verify the Software on the Host System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Verify the Software on the Host System

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Host-based software verification* is the process of compiling, executing, and validating embedded application code (originally written for a target microcontroller) on the **host machine** (a general-purpose PC running Linux/Windows) using **native compilation** and **instruction-set simulators (ISS)**, **emulators**, or **virtual platforms** (e.g., **QEMU**, **Renode**, **VirtualBox + OVP**), in order to detect logic, algorithm, and integration errors *before* deploying the binary onto the actual embedded target hardware.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Rehearsal Before the Stage Show"

Imagine a **chess grandmaster** preparing for a world championship:

| Step | Real World (Chess) | Embedded World (Verification) |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Practice at home** | Plays games alone, studies openings | Compiles code on PC, runs unit tests |
| **2. Play against a coach** | Simulates opponent styles | Runs code in QEMU/emulator (target simulation) |
| **3. Trial match** | Plays rated online games | Runs on actual development board (target test) |
| **4. Final tournament** | Plays the real championship | Deploys to production hardware in the field |

**Host-based verification = Steps 1 and 2.** It is the cheap, fast, repeatable "rehearsal" before the costly "real performance" on actual silicon. The grandmaster never learns new openings *during* the championship; he perfects them *beforehand*. Similarly, embedded developers never want to discover a `while(1)` infinite-loop bug *after* flashing the firmware to a deployed sensor in a factory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry Metrics (Board Exam Favorite):**
> - **Target Hardware Cost per Iteration:** ₹500 – ₹50,000 (per flash + debug cycle).
> - **Host Iteration Cost:** Practically **₹0** (no hardware wear, no JTAG programmer needed).
> - **Host Iteration Speed:** **100× – 1000×** faster than flashing to physical target.
> - **Bug Discovery Cost Ratio:** A bug caught at *host verification* stage costs **1×**; the same bug caught after *production deployment* costs **1000×**.

### 1.2 Why Host Verification is the First Verification Step

In the **Embedded Software Life Cycle**, verification is structured in a **V-Model** (or **Modified V-Model**):

1. **Host verification** ← *You are here* (cheapest, fastest)
2. **Target verification with development board** (mid cost)
3. **Target verification with production hardware** (expensive)
4. **Field validation** (most expensive, most damaging if buggy)

The earlier a defect is caught, the cheaper it is to fix — this is the **1:10:100:1000 Rule** of defect economics in embedded systems.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Host-Based Verification

Host verification rests on three complementary techniques. Understanding *when to use which* is a frequently tested KTU concept.

### Pillar 1: Native Compilation (Pure Host Execution)

- The source code is compiled with a **standard C/C++ compiler** (e.g., `gcc` on Linux, `MSVC` on Windows) targeting the **host CPU** (x86/x64/ARM64 on a laptop).
- The resulting binary runs **directly** on the host OS as a normal process.
- **Use case:** Verifying **hardware-independent** logic — algorithms, data structures, state machines, parsers, math libraries.
- **Limitation:** Cannot test anything that touches **hardware registers, peripherals, or interrupts**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** A digital filter function `int compute_FIR(int *buf, int n)` can be 100% verified on the host by feeding it synthetic samples and comparing output to a reference (e.g., MATLAB) result.

### Pillar 2: Host-Target Simulation (Instruction-Set Simulator + Peripherals)

- Code is compiled with a **cross-compiler** (e.g., `arm-none-eabi-gcc`) producing a binary for the **target ISA** (e.g., ARM Cortex-M4).
- The binary is then **executed** on an **emulator** that models the target CPU *and* its peripherals (timers, GPIO, UART, ADC).
- **Use case:** Verifying hardware-coupled logic — register access patterns, ISR handlers, peripheral driver flows.
- **Common tools (KTU syllabus mentions these):** **QEMU**, **Renode**, **OVP (Open Virtual Platform)**, **Keil Simulator**, **IAR Simulator**.

### Pillar 3: Host-Compiled Stub/Harness (Mocking Hardware)

- The real hardware abstraction layer (HAL) is **replaced** with **stub functions** that mimic hardware behaviour (e.g., a stub `uart_send()` that just prints to `stdout`).
- Application code runs on the host **unchanged**.
- **Use case:** Integration testing of application logic against a *fake* hardware layer.
- **Common framework:** **Ceedling** (Unity + CMock), **CMocka**, **CppUTest**, **Google Test (gtest)** for C/C++.

## 2.2 The Host Verification Workflow (Step-by-Step)

| Step | Action | Output / Artifact |
|------|--------|--------------------|
| **1** | Separate **hardware-independent** code (algorithms, state machines) from **hardware-dependent** code (drivers, ISRs) using clean **HAL boundaries** | Two distinct source folders |
| **2** | Build the hardware-independent code with `gcc` natively | `host_test.elf` executable |
| **3** | Create test harness (Unity / gtest) with assertions, mock hardware, and known input vectors | `test_main.c` |
| **4** | Run the executable, observe pass/fail, capture coverage | Coverage report (e.g., `gcov`/`lcov`) |
| **5** | For hardware-dependent code, run the cross-compiled binary in QEMU/Renode with peripheral models | QEMU trace logs |
| **6** | Compare host outputs with reference outputs (golden vectors) | Pass/fail verdict |
| **7** | Only after host verification passes → proceed to **target board testing** | Sign-off report |

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Equations & Metrics Table** (use `\mid` instead of `$\vert$` to protect table syntax):

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Meaning / Unit |
|---|---------|--------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Defect Cost Multiplier** | $C_{n} = C_{1} \cdot 10^{(n-1)}$ | Bug cost grows $\times 10$ per phase: $n$ = phase number (1=host, 2=sim, 3=board, 4=field) |
| 2 | **Code Coverage (Line)** | $Cov_{L} = \dfrac{L_{exec}}{L_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $\%$; target typically $\geq 80\%$ for safety-critical (MISRA-C) |
| 3 | **Code Coverage (Branch)** | $Cov_{B} = \dfrac{B_{exec}}{B_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $\%$; target typically $\geq 70\%$ |
| 4 | **Cyclomatic Complexity** | $M = E - N + 2P$ | $E$=edges, $N$=nodes, $P$=connected components in CFG; $M \leq 10$ recommended |
| 5 | **MC/DC Coverage (DO-178C)** | Each decision must show **independence** of each condition | Required for **Level A** software in avionics |
| 6 | **Native Build Speedup** | $S_{native} = \dfrac{T_{target}}{T_{host}}$ | Typically $S_{native} \in [100, 1000]$ |
| 7 | **Verification Effectiveness** | $VE = \dfrac{D_{caught}}{D_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $\%$; goal $\geq 95\%$ in early phases |
| 8 | **Mean Time To Detection (host)** | $MTTD_{host} \le 1\,\text{min}$ | Iterative test-edit-rerun loop on host |
| 9 | **Test Effectiveness Ratio** | $TER = \dfrac{\text{Defects found by tests}}{\text{Total defects found}}$ | Closer to $1.0$ = better test design |
| 10 | **Instruction-Set Sim Accuracy** | $Acc = 1 - \dfrac{\vert I_{sim} - I_{real} \vert}{I_{real}}$ | Cycle-accurate simulators: $Acc \approx 0.99$ |

## 2.4 Tools Landscape (Syllabus-Aligned)

| Category | Open-Source | Commercial |
|----------|-------------|------------|
| **Native Compiler** | `gcc`, `clang` | `MSVC` |
| **Cross-Compiler** | `arm-none-eabi-gcc`, `riscv64-unknown-elf-gcc` | Keil MDK-ARM, IAR EWARM |
| **ISS / Emulator** | QEMU, Renode, Spike (RISC-V) | Synopsys VDK, Cadence Virtual Platform |
| **Test Framework** | Unity, CMock, CppUTest, Google Test | VectorCAST, LDRA, Parasoft |
| **Coverage** | gcov, lcov, llvm-cov | Testwell CTC++, Bullseye Coverage |
| **Static Analysis** | Cppcheck, clang-tidy, Splint | Coverity, Polyspace, Klocwork |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Host verification is the **bedrock of Continuous Integration (CI) for embedded firmware**. Companies like **Tesla, Bosch, and Philips** run thousands of host-based firmware tests on every commit to a Git repository using **GitHub Actions / GitLab CI**, where each push triggers:
- Native unit tests on `gcc`/`clang`
- QEMU-based integration tests
- Static analysis with `cppcheck`
- Coverage report generation

This **shifts defect discovery left** in the DevOps pipeline, reducing time-to-market for products like automotive ECUs, IoT sensors, and medical devices.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — The 1:10:100:1000 Defect Cost Rule

> [!NOTE]
> This is the most commonly asked **analytical question** in KTU ESE for verification topics. Walk through it step by step.

### Problem Setup
A team finds a bug at the host-verification stage that costs **₹1,000** to fix (engineer time). Estimate its cost if discovered at:
- (a) Simulation stage
- (b) Target board stage
- (c) Production / field stage

Use the relation $C_{n} = C_{1} \cdot 10^{(n-1)}$ where $n$ is the phase index (1=host, 2=sim, 3=board, 4=field).

### Step 1 — Identify the Base Cost

$$
C_{1} = \text{₹1{,}000} \quad \text{(host verification stage)}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Cost at Simulation Stage (n = 2)

$$
C_{2} = C_{1} \cdot 10^{(2-1)} = 1000 \cdot 10^{1} = 1000 \cdot 10 = \text{₹10{,}000}
$$

*Reasoning:* Simulation requires cross-compilation, QEMU setup, trace analysis — roughly 10× the host effort.

### Step 3 — Compute Cost at Target Board Stage (n = 3)

$$
C_{3} = C_{1} \cdot 10^{(3-1)} = 1000 \cdot 10^{2} = 1000 \cdot 100 = \text{₹1{,}00{,}000}
$$

*Reasoning:* Board testing involves flashing firmware, JTAG/SWD debugging, oscilloscope/LA usage, hardware rework — ~100× host cost.

### Step 4 — Compute Cost at Production / Field Stage (n = 4)

$$
C_{4} = C_{1} \cdot 10^{(4-1)} = 1000 \cdot 10^{3} = 1000 \cdot 1000 = \text{₹10{,}00{,}000}
$$

*Reasoning:* Recall campaigns, warranty claims, brand damage, OTA update infrastructure — ~1000× the cost.

### Step 5 — Summary Table

| Phase $n$ | Cost $C_{n}$ | Multiplier vs. Host |
|------------|---------------|----------------------|
| 1 — Host | ₹1,000 | $1\times$ |
| 2 — Simulator | ₹10,000 | $10\times$ |
| 3 — Board | ₹1,00,000 | $100\times$ |
| 4 — Field | ₹10,00,000 | $1000\times$ |

$$
\boxed{\text{Total saved by catching at host vs. field} = C_{4} - C_{1} = 9{,}99{,}000 \;\text{INR}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation — Cyclomatic Complexity from Source Code

### Problem
A function has the following control-flow graph (CFG):
- $E = 11$ edges
- $N = 9$ nodes
- $P = 1$ connected component

Find its **Cyclomatic Complexity** $M$ and the **minimum number of test cases** required for full branch coverage.

### Step 1 — Apply McCabe's Formula

$$
M = E - N + 2P
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$
M = 11 - 9 + 2(1) = 11 - 9 + 2 = 4
$$

### Step 3 — Interpret

- $M = 4$ means the function has **3 linearly independent paths + 1 baseline path = 4 paths**.
- A function with $M \leq 10$ is considered **maintainable and testable** in good engineering practice.
- A function with $M > 20$ is virtually **untestable** and must be **refactored** (split into smaller functions).

### Step 4 — Minimum Test Cases for Branch Coverage

$$
T_{min} = M = 4 \;\text{test cases}
$$

This is the **lower bound**; in practice, you may need more for boundary values, but never less than $M$ for $100\%$ branch coverage.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Host Verification vs. Other Verification Levels

| Verification Level | Speed | Cost | Hardware Fidelity | Best For | Limitation |
|--------------------|-------|------|--------------------|----------|-------------|
| **Host (native)** | ★★★★★ | ★ (₹0) | None (no real hardware) | Algorithms, parsers, state machines | No peripheral/ISR testing |
| **Host (QEMU/Renode)** | ★★★★ | ★★ (free) | Moderate (peripheral models) | Driver logic, ISRs, RTOS tasks | Timing not 100% accurate |
| **Dev Board (JTAG/SWD)** | ★★ | ★★★ (₹500–5K) | High (real silicon) | Full integration, real timing | Slow, needs hardware |
| **Production HW (HIL)** | ★ | ★★★★★ (₹10K+) | Exact | Final sign-off | Very slow, expensive rigs |
| **Field Validation** | ✗ | ★★★★★+ | Exact + environment | Customer acceptance | Recall cost if buggy |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Trick:** If asked *"Why not skip host testing and go straight to the board?"*, the answer is rooted in the **Defect Cost Multiplier** above. A 30-minute QEMU test saves potentially weeks of board debugging later.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Native Build of a CRC Algorithm

### Problem
A team writes a CRC-16 (CCITT) function for an embedded sensor. Show how to verify it on the host.

### Step 1 — Identify Hardware-Independent Logic

The CRC computation is pure math — no GPIO, no UART, no timer. ✅ **Eligible for host testing.**

### Step 2 — Native Compilation

```text
$ gcc -Wall -Wextra -O2 -o crc_test crc16.c test_crc.c
$ ./crc_test
```

### Step 3 — Use Golden Vectors

Known input-output pairs (from the CCITT standard):

| Input Bytes (hex) | Expected CRC-16 |
|-------------------|------------------|
| `0x00 0x00`       | `0x0000`         |
| `0xFF 0xFF`       | `0x9D7E`         |
| `0x31 0x32 0x33 0x34 0x35 0x36 0x37 0x38 0x39` | `0x29B1` |

If `crc_test` prints all three expected values, the host verification **passes**, and only then does the code get cross-compiled and flashed to the target.
