# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is host-based verification of embedded software? Mention any two advantages.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Host-based verification is the process of **compiling and executing embedded application code on a general-purpose computer (the host)** instead of the target microcontroller, in order to detect logic and algorithmic defects early in the development cycle.

**Two advantages (1.5 marks each):**
1. **Speed:** Compile-test-debug cycles are **100×–1000× faster** on the host because no flashing, JTAG handshaking, or hardware reset is required. Iteration time drops from minutes to seconds.
2. **Cost:** Host verification requires **only a PC and free tools** (gcc, QEMU). It eliminates the per-iteration cost of wearing out flash memory on the target and consuming JTAG probe licences.

*(Examiner's note: Award full marks even if student lists "ease of automation in CI/CD pipelines" or "no need for physical hardware" as advantages.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between native compilation and cross-compilation in the context of host verification. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Native Compilation | Cross-Compilation |
|--------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **Target** | Host CPU (x86/x64/ARM64) | Embedded target CPU (ARM Cortex-M, AVR, RISC-V) |
| **Compiler** | `gcc`, `clang`, `MSVC` | `arm-none-eabi-gcc`, `avr-gcc`, `riscv64-elf-gcc` |
| **Runs on** | Host machine directly | Target hardware OR ISS (QEMU/Renode) |
| **Use in host verification** | Pure algorithm and logic testing | Driver, ISR, and peripheral testing via simulation |
| **Output binary runs on** | The same machine it was built on | A *different* ISA, requiring emulation or hardware |

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for at least two contrasts, 1 mark for clarity and example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### `Question A (14 Marks)` — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of host-based verification of embedded software. With a neat block diagram, describe the workflow of host-based verification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the limitations of host-based verification. Explain how these limitations are overcome by Instruction-Set Simulators (ISS) such as QEMU. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**Definition (2 marks):**
Host-based verification is the technique of **compiling, executing, and validating embedded application code on the developer's host machine** (a PC running Linux/Windows) rather than on the actual target microcontroller. The goal is to identify and fix **hardware-independent defects** — algorithm errors, state-machine bugs, control-flow mistakes, and integration issues — *before* flashing the firmware to costly embedded hardware.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
   ┌──────────────┐
   │ Embedded C   │
   │ Source Code  │
   └──────┬───────┘
          │ (clean HAL boundary)
          ▼
   ┌─────────────────────────────┐         ┌──────────────┐
   │ Hardware-Independent Code  │────────▶│  Native GCC  │
   │ (algorithms, state machine) │         │  Compile     │
   └─────────────────────────────┘         └──────┬───────┘
                                                ▼
   ┌─────────────────────────────┐         ┌──────────────┐
   │ Hardware-Dependent Code     │         │  Host Run    │
   │ (drivers, ISRs)             │         │  (.exe)      │
   └──────────────┬──────────────┘         └──────┬───────┘
                  │ (cross-compile + stubs)        │
                  ▼                                ▼
   ┌─────────────────────────────┐         ┌──────────────┐
   │   QEMU / Renode Emulator    │         │  Compare to  │
   │   (or stub-mock harness)    │         │  Golden Vec. │
   └──────────────┬──────────────┘         └──────┬───────┘
                  │                                │
                  └──────────┬─────────────────────┘
                             ▼
                  ┌──────────────────┐
                  │ Pass / Fail      │
                  │ Coverage Report  │
                  └──────────────────┘
```

**Workflow Steps (3 marks — list and explain):**
1. **Architectural separation:** Partition source into hardware-independent and hardware-dependent modules via a clean HAL boundary.
2. **Native build:** Compile hardware-independent code with `gcc` for the host CPU; the resulting ELF runs directly.
3. **Test harness:** Use Unity/gtest with mock hardware stubs to drive inputs and assert outputs.
4. **Run + Compare:** Execute on host, compare against golden/reference vectors.
5. **Iterate:** Fix defects, recompile, retest — typically a **few seconds per cycle**.
6. **Sign-off + Escalation:** Only after all host tests pass, cross-compile the full project and proceed to board-level testing.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Limitations of Pure Host Verification (3.5 marks):**

1. **No real hardware interaction:** Native compilation cannot test GPIO toggling, ADC sampling, UART framing, or timer-driven interrupts.
2. **No timing fidelity:** The host CPU runs at GHz speeds, while the target runs at MHz; real-time deadlines and ISR latency cannot be measured.
3. **No peripheral register map:** Real microcontrollers have memory-mapped registers at specific addresses; the host has none of these.
4. **Endianness & word-size mismatches:** A 32-bit operation on x86 behaves differently than on an 8-bit AVR.
5. **No RTOS behaviour:** Task scheduling, context switches, and semaphores cannot be reproduced without a target-aware environment.
6. **No electrical/power behaviour:** Cannot detect brown-out, EMI, or current spikes.

**How QEMU / ISS Overcomes These (3.5 marks):**

- **QEMU** emulates the **target CPU's instruction set** (e.g., ARM Cortex-M3) and provides a **machine model** that mimics memory-mapped peripherals.
- Developers can **load cross-compiled firmware** (`firmware.elf`) directly into QEMU and observe peripheral-mapped I/O, simulating UART prints, GPIO reads, and timer interrupts.
- **Tracing tools** (`-d exec`, `-d in_asm`, `-serial stdio`) allow inspection of executed instructions, register states, and serial output.
- QEMU supports **semihosting**, where the emulated firmware can `printf` back to the host terminal — bridging the host-target gap.
- **Limitations of QEMU itself:** It is *cycle-approximate*, not cycle-accurate; for hard real-time validation, a cycle-accurate simulator (e.g., Synopsys VDK) or actual silicon is still required.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> A very common mistake students make is writing *"QEMU replaces the target hardware."* This is **wrong** — QEMU is an **emulator**, not a replacement. It models CPU behaviour and peripheral registers but cannot reproduce real electrical, timing, or environmental behaviour. **Always use the term "emulator" or "ISS" in your answer**, not "replacement."

---

### `Question B (14 Marks)` — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** With relevant examples, explain the role of **stubs, mocks, and harness frameworks** in host-based verification. List any two popular C/C++ unit-testing frameworks used for embedded host testing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A defect is found at the host verification stage. The cost of fixing it at this stage is measured as **₹800**. Using the 1:10:100:1000 defect cost rule, calculate the cost of fixing the same defect if it were discovered at: (i) the simulator stage, (ii) the target board stage, and (iii) the field-deployment stage. Also compute the **total savings** achieved by catching the bug at the host stage. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Stubs (2 marks):**
A **stub** is a *minimal* dummy function that replaces a real hardware function. It returns a hard-coded value. Example: a stub for `uint32_t read_adc(void)` always returns `512` so that the calling code can be tested without real ADC hardware.

**Mocks (2 marks):**
A **mock** is a *smarter* stub that **records** how it was called (number of times, with what arguments) and can be programmed with **expectations**. Example: a mock for `uart_send()` verifies that the application called it exactly 3 times with the bytes `'H','i','\n'`.

**Harness (1.5 marks):**
A **test harness** is the **driver code** that sets up inputs, calls the function under test (FUT), and asserts expected outputs. Frameworks automate the harness, providing macros like `TEST_ASSERT_EQUAL(expected, actual)`.

**Two Popular C/C++ Unit-Testing Frameworks (1.5 marks):**
1. **Unity + CMock** (ThrowTheSwitch/ThrowTheSwitch) — the de-facto standard for embedded C; lightweight, header-only, works with `gcc` on the host out of the box.
2. **Google Test (gtest) + Google Mock (gmock)** — the standard for C++ projects; integrates with CMake, supports fixtures, death tests, and parameterized tests.
   *(Acceptable alternatives: CppUTest, Ceedling, CMOCKA, VectorCAST — award full credit.)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Given:** $C_{1} = \text{₹}800$ (host stage). Formula: $C_{n} = C_{1} \cdot 10^{(n-1)}$.

**(i) Simulator stage (n = 2):**

$$
C_{2} = 800 \cdot 10^{(2-1)} = 800 \cdot 10^{1} = 800 \cdot 10 = \text{₹}8{,}000
$$

**(ii) Target board stage (n = 3):**

$$
C_{3} = 800 \cdot 10^{(3-1)} = 800 \cdot 10^{2} = 800 \cdot 100 = \text{₹}80{,}000
$$

**(iii) Field-deployment stage (n = 4):**

$$
C_{4} = 800 \cdot 10^{(4-1)} = 800 \cdot 10^{3} = 800 \cdot 1000 = \text{₹}8{,}00{,}000
$$

**Total Savings Achieved by Host-Stage Detection:**

$$
\text{Savings} = C_{4} - C_{1} = 8{,}00{,}000 - 800 = \text{₹}7{,}99{,}200
$$

**Result Table (1 mark for clear tabulation):**

| Stage $n$ | Fix Cost $C_{n}$ |
|------------|-------------------|
| 1 — Host | ₹800 |
| 2 — Simulator | ₹8,000 |
| 3 — Board | ₹80,000 |
| 4 — Field | ₹8,00,000 |
| **Savings** | **₹7,99,200** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes in this Question:**
> 1. Using $10^{n}$ instead of $10^{(n-1)}$ — off by a factor of 10, costing 2 marks.
> 2. Forgetting units (₹) — loses 0.5 mark for "lack of professional presentation."
> 3. Computing the difference as $C_{4} - C_{1}$ correctly but **forgetting to mention it in the final answer sentence** — partial deduction.
> 4. Writing "1000×" without working — examiners give **0 marks** for numerical answers without derivation in KTU 2024 scheme.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- [x] **Host verification = PC-side testing of embedded code** *before* flashing to the target.
- [x] Three pillars: **Native Compile, ISS/Emulator, Stub-Mock Harness**.
- [x] **Native compile** uses `gcc/clang` → runs directly on host → only for hardware-independent code.
- [x] **Cross-compile + QEMU/Renode** → runs emulated target ISA → for drivers and ISRs.
- [x] **Stubs** return fixed values; **Mocks** record calls and verify expectations.
- [x] **Defect cost formula:** $C_{n} = C_{1} \cdot 10^{(n-1)}$ — memorize and practice 2-3 numericals.
- [x] **Cyclomatic complexity** $M = E - N + 2P$ — minimum test cases = $M$.
- [x] **Coverage targets:** Line $\geq 80\%$, Branch $\geq 70\%$, MC/DC for safety-critical (DO-178C Level A).
- [x] **Common tools:** gcc, clang, QEMU, Renode, Unity, CMock, gtest, gcov, lcov, CppUTest.
- [x] **Host verification is the cheapest and fastest** verification level — used first in the V-model.
- [x] **Limitations of host testing:** no real timing, no real peripherals, no electrical behaviour, no RTOS without an emulator.
- [x] **QEMU is an emulator, NOT a replacement** for the target — examiners will deduct marks if you say otherwise.
- [x] **Terminology to use in answers:** "emulator", "ISS", "instruction-set simulator", "host-based", "cross-compile", "native compile", "stub", "mock", "harness", "golden vector".
- [x] **Industry context:** Embedded CI/CD pipelines run **thousands** of host tests per commit; "shift-left" testing is the modern norm.
- [x] **Viva question to expect:** *"Can you host-test an ISR?"* — **Answer:** *"Pure host cannot, but with QEMU/Renode emulation of NVIC and peripherals, ISR entry/exit and handler logic can be verified."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Verify the Software on the Embedded System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET695 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Verify the Software on the Embedded System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Software Verification on an Embedded System** is the disciplined, multi-stage process of evaluating the embedded firmware to ensure it correctly and reliably implements its intended functions, meets all specified requirements, and operates without fault on the target hardware. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, software verification is formally defined under the *V-Model of Embedded Development* as a parallel, downward-verification activity performed at every abstraction layer — from unit-level C/assembly routines to fully integrated firmware on the production board.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Verbatim):**
> *"Verification is the process of evaluating the embedded software to determine whether the products of a given development phase satisfy the conditions imposed at the start of that phase."* — IEEE Std 1012 Verification & Validation Standard

The verification process is decomposed into three principal pillars as per the KTU syllabus:

| Pillar | Focus | Tool Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Simulation** | Host-PC execution of code in a virtual model of the hardware | QEMU, Keil Simulator |
| **Emulation** | Execution on a hardware-mimicking device (ICE) | Lauterbach TRACE32 |
| **On-Target Debug** | Live execution on the actual microcontroller via debug probes | JTAG, SWD, BDM |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Car Assembly Line" Analogy:**
> Imagine you are building a car in a factory. Before sending the finished car to a customer, the quality team performs checks at *every station* — engine bench-test, brake dynamometer, road test, and finally a customer-simulation drive. You **do not** just test the car at the end of the line. Similarly, in an embedded system, you do not simply "flash the code and hope it works." You **verify** the software at each stage — first on a PC simulator (engine bench), then on an emulator (dyno), and finally on the real board (road test). This staged verification is the only way to catch bugs early when they are cheap to fix.

### 1.3 Why Software Verification is Non-Negotiable in Embedded Systems

Unlike desktop applications, embedded software interacts with **physical hardware and real-time constraints**. A subtle bug in a thermostat could allow a heater to overheat; a glitch in a pacemaker firmware is life-threatening. Hence the KTU 2024 Scheme mandates explicit testing pedagogy under **Course Outcome CO4**: *"Design, implement, test, and debug embedded software using appropriate tools and methodologies."*

Key physical/system metrics that verification aims to validate (and which the board examiner expects students to know):

- **Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET)** — measured in microseconds (µs)
- **Stack/Heap Memory Footprint** — measured in bytes
- **Real-Time Response Latency** — measured in milliseconds (ms)
- **Power Consumption Profile** — measured in milliwatts (mW)
- **Register-Level Peripheral Behavior** — measured via logic-trace toggle counts
- **Code Coverage** — expressed as a **percentage (%)** of paths/branches executed

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Verification Pyramid (Hierarchical Testing)

Embedded software verification follows a strict bottom-up pyramid. Each level builds confidence before ascending.

#### Level 1 — Unit / Module Testing
- Tests individual C functions or assembly routines in isolation.
- Driven by the source code (white-box testing).
- **Tools:** Keil µVision Debugger, IAR Embedded Workbench, GCC + GDB on host.
- **Goal:** Verify that the function returns the mathematically correct output for boundary, typical, and stress inputs.

#### Level 2 — Integration Testing
- Tests inter-module communication (e.g., sensor driver ↔ main scheduler ↔ UART TX).
- Verifies data flow, register interactions, and timing handshakes.
- **Tools:** Host-target stubs, hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) benches.

#### Level 3 — System / On-Target Testing
- The complete firmware is flashed onto the real microcontroller.
- All peripherals, sensors, actuators, and communication buses are exercised.
- **Tools:** Oscilloscopes, Logic Analyzers, JTAG probes, in-circuit emulators.

#### Level 4 — Acceptance / Field Testing
- Final validation against customer specifications and regulatory standards (e.g., **ISO 26262** for automotive, **IEC 62304** for medical).
- **Tools:** Statistical stress testing, HALT (Highly Accelerated Life Test).

### 2.2 Verification Techniques (KTU-Favoured Taxonomy)

The KTU board repeatedly tests the *taxonomic distinction* between these techniques:

**A. Static Verification (No Code Execution)**
- **Code Review / Walkthroughs** — manual inspection.
- **Static Analysis** — tools like **PC-lint**, **Cppcheck**, **Coverity** parse code without running it to find unreachable code, uninitialized variables, and array out-of-bounds.

**B. Dynamic Verification (Code is Executed)**
- **White-Box Testing** — tester has full knowledge of internal code structure. Focuses on **branch coverage** and **path coverage**.
- **Black-Box Testing** — tester views software as a sealed module. Focuses on **equivalence partitioning** and **boundary value analysis**.
- **Grey-Box Testing** — hybrid: partial knowledge (e.g., knowing the public API but not internals).

### 2.3 Hardware-Assisted Debugging & Verification Tools

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Block — Memorize this list verbatim:**

| Tool | Function | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Simulator** | Software model of CPU + memory | Host-PC debugging without hardware |
| **In-Circuit Emulator (ICE)** | Replaces target MCU with emulator pod | Legacy 8051 / 68HC11 systems |
| **JTAG Probe** | Boundary-scan + run-control via TAP | ARM Cortex-M (SWD), all modern MCUs |
| **Logic Analyzer** | Captures multi-channel digital waveforms | Bus protocol verification (SPI, I²C) |
| **Oscilloscope** | Single/dual-channel analog/digital | PWM duty, ADC waveform, glitch hunt |
| **ROM Monitor** | Small resident program in target ROM | UART-based GDB stub debugging |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Statement Coverage | $C_s = \dfrac{S_{executed}}{S_{total}} \times 100\%$ | % | % of C statements run |
| 2 | Branch Coverage | $C_b = \dfrac{B_{taken}}{B_{total}} \times 100\%$ | % | % of if/else branches taken |
| 3 | MC/DC Coverage | $C_{mc} = \dfrac{I_{indep}}{I_{total}} \times 100\%$ | % | Modified Condition/Decision (DO-178C) |
| 4 | WCET Bound | $T_{wcet} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{BCET,i}$ | µs | Sum of basic-block worst times |
| 5 | Memory Footprint | $M_{foot} = M_{code} + M_{data} + M_{stack} + M_{heap}$ | bytes | Total flash + RAM used |
| 6 | CPU Utilization | $U_{cpu} = \dfrac{T_{busy}}{T_{period}} \times 100\%$ | % | Real-time schedulability check |
| 7 | Timing Margin | $\Delta T = T_{deadline} - T_{wcet}$ | µs | Slack before deadline miss |
| 8 | Pull-up Resistor (I²C) | $R_{p} = \dfrac{V_{DD} - V_{OL}}{I_{OL}}$ | Ω | Bus integrity check |
| 9 | Sampling Rate (Nyquist) | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Hz | ADC verification criterion |
| 10 | Defect Density | $D_d = \dfrac{N_{defects}}{KLOC}$ | per KLOC | Code-quality metric |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Memorization:** **S-B-M** → **S**tatement, **B**ranch, **M**C/DC — the three coverage levels KTU examiners cycle through.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (ECU software):** Verification must achieve **100% MC/DC coverage** to comply with **ASIL-D** of ISO 26262.
- **Avionics (DO-178C):** Level A software requires the most rigorous verification — every possible decision outcome must be tested.
- **Medical Devices (IEC 62304):** Software Safety Class C demands unit-level + integration-level verification with documented traceability.
- **IoT Consumer Devices:** Verification focuses on power profile, OTA update integrity, and wireless coexistence.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Computing Branch Coverage from Test Cases

Consider the following canonical KTU-style C pseudo-code fragment for an embedded temperature controller:

```
if (temp > HIGH_LIMIT) { heater = OFF; alarm = ON; }
else if (temp < LOW_LIMIT) { heater = ON; alarm = OFF; }
else { heater = maintain; alarm = OFF; }
```

**Step 1 — Identify the total number of branches.**

The construct has three decision points: `temp > HIGH_LIMIT`, `temp < LOW_LIMIT`, and the implicit `else`. In KTU coverage theory, each `if` has **two** possible outcomes (True/False). With two nested `if`s, the *branches* are:

$$
B_{total} = 2 \times 2 = 4 \;\text{branches}
$$

**Step 2 — Design test cases to exercise each branch.**

| Test Case | Input `temp` | `temp > HIGH` | `temp < LOW` | Branches Taken |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| TC1 | $95^\circ C$ (above HIGH=80) | True | False | B1-T, B2-F (skip) |
| TC2 | $10^\circ C$ (below LOW=20) | False | True | B1-F, B2-T |
| TC3 | $50^\circ C$ (in range) | False | False | B1-F, B2-F |
| TC4 | $80^\circ C$ (boundary) | False | True (if LOW=80) | B1-F, B2-T |

**Step 3 — Count the executed branches.**

After running all four test cases, all four branches have been exercised at least once.

**Step 4 — Apply the branch-coverage formula from the Cheat Sheet:**

$$
C_b = \dfrac{B_{taken}}{B_{total}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{4}{4} \times 100\% = 100\%
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result for the KTU answer.**

> *"Branch coverage of 100% confirms that every decision outcome in the thermal controller's logic has been triggered by at least one test input, satisfying Level 2 of the verification pyramid."*

### 3.2 Derivation — Computing WCET for a Simple Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)

**Step 1 — Decompose the ISR into basic blocks.**

Suppose an ISR that services a UART RX interrupt consists of three basic blocks:
- **B1:** Save context (push registers) — takes $8$ clock cycles.
- **B2:** Read UART data register — takes $12$ clock cycles.
- **B3:** Restore context (pop registers) and `RETI` — takes $10$ clock cycles.

**Step 2 — Sum the worst-case basic-block times.**

$$
T_{wcet} = T_{BCET,B1} + T_{BCET,B2} + T_{BCET,B3}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
T_{wcet} = 8 + 12 + 10 = 30 \;\text{clock cycles}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to time using the CPU clock frequency $f_{cpu}$.**

For a CPU running at $f_{cpu} = 50\,\text{MHz}$, the clock period is:

$$
T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f_{cpu}} = \dfrac{1}{50 \times 10^6} = 20\,\text{ns}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the absolute worst-case execution time.**

$$
T_{wcet,\,abs} = 30 \times 20\,\text{ns} = 600\,\text{ns} = 0.6\,\mu s
$$

**Step 6 — Compare against the deadline.**

If the UART byte-arrival rate imposes a deadline of $T_{deadline} = 1.04\,\mu s$ (9600 baud), then the timing margin is:

$$
\Delta T = T_{deadline} - T_{wcet} = 1.04\,\mu s - 0.6\,\mu s = 0.44\,\mu s
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Expectation:** Whenever you compute WCET, **always** state the assumption that the values are *worst-case* (cache miss, pipeline stall, interrupt disabled, etc.) and not average-case.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Verification Tool

| Verification Need | Best Tool | Justification | KTU Board Tip |
|---|---|---|---|
| Algorithm logic on host PC | **Simulator** | Zero hardware cost, repeatable | Always mention in Part A answers |
| Real peripheral timing | **Logic Analyzer** | Captures actual bus waveforms | Mention channel count, sample rate |
| Stepping through C code on board | **JTAG/SWD Probe** | Hardware run-control + memory peek | Name the TAP states if asked |
| EMI/ESD induced faults | **Oscilloscope** | Single-shot transient capture | Mention bandwidth ≥ $2 \times f_{signal}$ |
| Legacy 8051 firmware | **ICE** | Pod replaces the masked MCU | "ICE is obsolete but still asked" |
| Code-level static defects | **PC-lint / Cppcheck** | Parses without execution | Use the word "static" in answer |

### 3.4 Verification Lifecycle Mapping to the V-Model

The KTU 2024 Scheme treats verification as the right arm of the *V-Model*:

- **Unit Design ↔ Unit Test** — verify each function.
- **Integration Design ↔ Integration Test** — verify module handshakes.
- **System Design ↔ System Test** — verify the full firmware on hardware.
- **Acceptance ↔ Field Validation** — verify against customer need.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for the V-Model right arm:** **UIS A** → **U**nit, **I**ntegration, **S**ystem, **A**cceptance — left to right arm of the V.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Embedded Software Verification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Requirement Spec] --> B[Source Code Written]
    B --> C{Static Analysis Passes?}
    C -- No --> C1[Fix Defects]
    C1 --> B
    C -- Yes --> D[Unit Test on Simulator]
    D --> E{Unit Tests Pass?}
    E -- No --> E1[Debug via GDB / Keil]
    E1 --> D
    E -- Yes --> F[Integration Test on Emulator]
    F --> G{Integration OK?}
    G -- No --> G1[Trace Bus via Logic Analyzer]
    G1 --> F
    G -- Yes --> H[Flash to Target Board]
    H --> I[On-Target System Test]
    I --> J{JTAG Probe Pass?}
    J -- No --> J1[Use Oscilloscope for Glitches]
    J1 --> I
    J -- Yes --> K[Acceptance / Field Validation]
    K --> L[Release Sign-off]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — JTAG-Based On-Chip Debug Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PC[Host PC running GDB / Keil] -->|USB / Ethernet| PROBE[JTAG / SWD Probe]
    PROBE -->|TDI TDO TMS TCK| TAP[Test Access Port on MCU]
    TAP --> BSCAN[Boundary Scan Cells]
    TAP --> ICE[On-Chip Debug Logic]
    ICE -->|Halt / Step / Reg R-W| CORECPU[Core CPU]
    CORECPU --> MEMS[Flash and RAM]
    CORECPU --> PERIPHS[Peripherals GPIO ADC UART]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Verification Pyramid (Hierarchy of Tests)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Acceptance Testing] --> P2[System Testing on Target]
    P2 --> P3[Integration Testing]
    P3 --> P4[Unit Testing on Simulator]
    P4 --> P5[Static Analysis and Code Review]
    style P1 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P2 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P3 fill:#FFE9B0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P4 fill:#FFF1B8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P5 fill:#FFF8C8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Black-Box vs White-Box Testing Perspective

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BB[Black Box Testing]
        BB1[Inputs from Equivalence Classes] --> BB2[Firmware as Opaque Module]
        BB2 --> BB3[Outputs Match Spec or Not]
    end
    subgraph WB[White Box Testing]
        WB1[Source Code Visible] --> WB2[Branch / Path / MC-DC Coverage]
        WB2 --> WB3[Internal Variable and Register Inspection]
    end
    style BB fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px
    style WB fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define software verification. How is it different from software validation in embedded systems?*

**Model Answer (Board Key Points, 3 Marks):**
- **Verification (1 mark):** Process of evaluating the embedded software to determine whether the products of a given development phase satisfy the conditions imposed at the start of that phase. Answers the question: *"Are we building the product right?"*
- **Validation (1 mark):** Process of evaluating the final embedded software to ensure it meets the customer's intended use in the real-world environment. Answers the question: *"Are we building the right product?"*
- **Example (1 mark):** Verification → unit-testing a UART driver returns correct baud-rate divisor values. Validation → connecting the board to a PC and confirming the terminal displays the expected string in the field.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three hardware tools used to verify embedded software on the target system. Mention one specific use of each.*

**Model Answer (Board Key Points, 3 Marks):**
- **JTAG Probe (1 mark):** Used for run-control debugging — halting the CPU, single-stepping, and reading/writing memory/register contents via the Test Access Port (TAP).
- **Logic Analyzer (1 mark):** Used to capture multi-channel digital waveforms across SPI/I²C/UART buses and verify protocol timing on the actual hardware.
- **Oscilloscope (1 mark):** Used to observe analog/digital waveforms of PWM outputs, ADC inputs, and capture single-shot transient glitches that disrupt embedded software.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing verification with validation** — costs at least **2 marks** in the 14-mark answer.
> 2. **Naming a tool without stating its function** — examiners deduct 1 mark for every orphaned tool name.
> 3. **Skipping the coverage-formula derivation** — in coverage-based questions, you must write the formula and substitute values.
> 4. **Omitting "worst-case" qualifiers** in WCET questions — explicit assumption statement is mandatory.
> 5. **Using the term "ICE" for modern ARM MCUs** — ICE is largely obsolete; prefer "JTAG/SWD probe" for Cortex-M.

---

#### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]*

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the different levels of testing in the embedded software verification pyramid. Mention the appropriate tool for each level. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Level | Description | Tool | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Unit Testing** | Individual C/assembly functions tested in isolation on a host-PC simulator. Verifies boundary, typical, and stress inputs. | Keil Simulator, QEMU | 2 |
| **2. Integration Testing** | Inter-module interactions verified — drivers, RTOS tasks, communication stacks. | Emulator (QEMU), HIL benches | 2 |
| **3. System Testing** | Full firmware on the actual microcontroller board; all peripherals exercised under real timing. | JTAG probe, Logic Analyzer, Oscilloscope | 2 |
| **4. Acceptance Testing** | Final validation against customer specs, regulatory standards (ISO 26262, IEC 62304). | HALT chambers, field-trial kits | 1 |

**Board-Examiner's Voice (1 mark for the closing summary):**
> *"The pyramid progresses from cheap, host-side, code-level tests to expensive, hardware-intensive field tests, ensuring defects are caught at the earliest possible stage — minimizing engineering rework cost."*

---

**Q. (b)** Consider the following embedded C function that decides the speed of a DC motor. Compute the **statement coverage** and **branch coverage** achievable with the three test inputs given below. Show all derivations.

```c
void setMotor(int speed) {
    if (speed > 100) {
        motorForward(MAX);
    } else if (speed < 0) {
        motorReverse(MAX);
    } else {
        motorStop();
    }
}
```

Test cases:
- TC1: `speed = 150`
- TC2: `speed = -10`
- TC3: `speed = 50`

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Count total statements (1 mark).**
There are **5 statements**: the function call, two `if` conditions, the two inner calls, and the `else` body call. Functionally, the *executable* statements are 3 (the two `if` bodies plus the `else` body):

$$
S_{total} = 3
$$

**Step 2 — Count executed statements across the three test cases (1 mark).**

- TC1 (`speed = 150`): executes `motorForward(MAX)` → 1 statement.
- TC2 (`speed = -10`): executes `motorReverse(MAX)` → 1 statement.
- TC3 (`speed = 50`): executes `motorStop()` → 1 statement.

All three distinct statements are covered. Hence:

$$
S_{executed} = 3
$$

**Step 3 — Compute statement coverage (1 mark).**

$$
C_s = \dfrac{3}{3} \times 100\% = 100\%
$$

**Step 4 — Count the total branches (1 mark).**
Each `if` is a decision with two outcomes. Two `if`s ⇒ four branches.

$$
B_{total} = 4
$$

**Step 5 — Count the executed branches (1 mark).**
- TC1: `speed > 100` → True; `speed < 0` → not evaluated (short-circuit). Branches covered: **2**.
- TC2: `speed > 100` → False; `speed < 0` → True. Branches covered: **2** (one new, one duplicate).
- TC3: `speed > 100` → False; `speed < 0` → False; else → True. Branches covered: **2**.

Union across all test cases: **B1-T, B1-F, B2-T, B2-F** = 4 branches.

$$
B_{taken} = 4
$$

**Step 6 — Compute branch coverage (1 mark).**

$$
C_b = \dfrac{4}{4} \times 100\% = 100\%
$$

**Closing Statement (1 mark):**
> *"Both statement and branch coverage reach 100% with the three chosen test inputs because the inputs are well-spaced across the three equivalence classes: above HIGH (>100), below LOW (<0), and within range [0, 100]."*

---

#### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]*

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of JTAG-based on-chip debugging. How does it help in verifying embedded software? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark).**
JTAG (Joint Test Action Group, IEEE 1149.1) is a 4-wire serial protocol (**TDI, TDO, TMS, TCK**) that provides boundary-scan testing and on-chip debug access to the CPU core, memory, and peripherals.

**Step 2 — Architecture components (3 marks).**

- **TAP Controller** — a 16-state finite state machine that interprets TMS to navigate the scan chain.
- **Instruction Register (IR)** — selects the operation (e.g., BYPASS, IDCODE, SAMPLE/PRELOAD, EXTEST, DEBUG).
- **Data Registers (DR)** — boundary-scan cells, bypass register, and the device-identification register.
- **On-Chip Debug Logic** — integrates with the CPU core to support halt, single-step, breakpoint, and watchpoint operations.
- **Host Probe** — external hardware (e.g., J-Link, ULINK) that drives the TAP signals from the PC's debugger IDE (Keil, IAR, GDB).

**Step 3 — How it helps in software verification (2 marks).**

- **Non-intrusive run-control** — halt the CPU at any C source line, inspect all registers, and step through firmware.
- **Memory read/write** — patch RAM/Flash contents live to test alternate code paths without re-flashing.
- **Real-time trace** — capture program-counter history via ETM (Embedded Trace Macrocell) to verify timing.
- **Boundary-scan chain test** — verifies solder-joint integrity of the MCU on the PCB before software is even loaded.

**Step 4 — Block diagram reference (1 mark).**
Refer to the diagram in Section 4.2 of these notes (Host PC → Probe → TAP → Boundary Scan / On-Chip Debug → CPU/Memory/Peripherals).

---

**Q. (b)** A UART ISR on an ARM Cortex-M4 (clock $f_{cpu} = 72\,\text{MHz}$) consists of three basic blocks with worst-case execution times of $14$, $19$, and $23$ clock cycles respectively. Compute the **absolute WCET in microseconds** and the **timing margin** if the byte-arrival deadline is $2.0\,\mu s$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Sum the basic-block worst-case times (1 mark).**

$$
T_{wcet,\,cycles} = 14 + 19 + 23 = 56 \;\text{cycles}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the clock period (1 mark).**

$$
T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f_{cpu}} = \dfrac{1}{72 \times 10^6} = 13.8889\,\text{ns}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to absolute time (1 mark).**

$$
T_{wcet,\,abs} = 56 \times 13.8889\,\text{ns}
$$

$$
T_{wcet,\,abs} = 777.78\,\text{ns} \approx 0.778\,\mu s
$$

**Step 4 — State the worst-case assumption (1 mark).**
> *"The values 14, 19, 23 cycles are worst-case estimates assuming the worst possible memory-access latency (Flash wait-states, cache miss) and maximum interrupt latency on the Cortex-M4 NVIC."*

**Step 5 — Compute the timing margin (1 mark).**

$$
\Delta T = T_{deadline} - T_{wcet,\,abs} = 2.0\,\mu s - 0.778\,\mu s = 1.222\,\mu s
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the result (2 marks).**
- The ISR comfortably meets its deadline with a positive margin of $1.222\,\mu s$.
- The **CPU utilization** for this single ISR is:

$$
U_{cpu} = \dfrac{0.778}{2.0} \times 100\% = 38.9\%
$$

> *"This means the UART ISR alone consumes only ~39% of its allotted time slot, leaving ample headroom for higher-priority interrupts. The verification PASSES with a healthy safety margin."*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must memorize before exam:**

- **Verification vs Validation:** Verification = "building the product right"; Validation = "building the right product." Don't confuse them.
- **Three Pillars of Verification:** Simulation, Emulation, On-Target Debug.
- **Four Levels of the Testing Pyramid:** Unit → Integration → System → Acceptance (mnemonic: **UIS A**).
- **Three Coverage Types:** Statement $C_s$, Branch $C_b$, MC/DC $C_{mc}$ — formulas must be written out, not just stated.
- **Five On-Target Debug Tools:** Simulator, ICE (legacy), JTAG/SWD probe, Logic Analyzer, Oscilloscope — name + function for each.
- **JTAG Pins (4-wire):** TDI, TDO, TMS, TCK — IEEE 1149.1 standard.
- **WCET Computation Rule:** Sum of worst-case basic-block cycle counts × clock period. Always declare the worst-case assumption.
- **Nyquist Sampling Rule:** $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ — relevant for ADC verification.
- **Mnemonic "S-B-M"** for coverage levels; **"UIS A"** for test pyramid.
- **Standard Real-World Standards:** ISO 26262 (automotive), DO-178C (avionics), IEC 62304 (medical) — examiners love these buzzwords.
- **Black-Box vs White-Box:** Black = no internal visibility, uses equivalence classes; White = full source visibility, uses branch/MC-DC coverage.
- **Pull-up Resistor (I²C):** $R_p = \dfrac{V_{DD} - V_{OL}}{I_{OL}}$ — common exam formula.
- **Static vs Dynamic Verification:** Static = no execution (lint, code review); Dynamic = execution required (unit tests, integration tests).
- **Always state the assumption** (worst-case, room temperature, nominal VDD) — examiners reward this with 1 bonus mark per question.
- **In a 14-mark answer**, include at least one formula, one diagram reference, and one closing interpretive statement.
